NSF grants have previously helped fund the University’s efforts to build the world’s most powerful quantum computer and, as of fiscal year 2021, provided around 12% of the Duke Lemur Center’s funding. Prior to the April 9 hold, Duke had not received a single new NSF grant since October 2025, according to a federal tracker of government spending. The foundation's grant-awarding process was already subject to significant delays due to the fall’s government shutdown and the Trump administration’s delayed release of the foundation's budget. However, Nature said that grants to Duke, alongside the three other targeted universities, were stalled for longer than other institutions. They reported that it took the foundation’s Office of Award Management an average of 10 days to finalize grants overall, but for the targeted institutions, an average of 91 days. This is not the first time Duke’s federal funding has been in jeopardy. Last summer, the federal government froze $108 million in National Institutes of Health funding to Duke after two federal agencies launched investigations into the University and Health System for alleged racial discrimination. The status of that money remains unclear. Duke has also experienced losses in funding from the cuts imposed by the Trump administration to the tune of hundreds of millions of dollars . Some canceled grants have completely gutted certain centers and institutes within the University and Health System. The School of Medicine recently announced that it had received nearly $78 million less in federal funding than at this point in the fiscal year before the Trump administration took office.

title: ‘It takes a village to trap an ion’: A look inside the Duke Quantum Center
author: Brenton Wang
text: As Duke has spent the past years publicly investing in and debating artificial intelligence, the University has simultaneously focused on an entirely different form of technological innovation: quantum computing. The Duke Quantum Center (DQC), located in downtown Durham in the Chesterfield building, is the only “vertical” quantum computing center in the entire world. The center — led by Kenneth Brown, Michael J. Fitzpatrick distinguished professor of engineering — is the brainchild of leading quantum scientists at Duke. Principally, the center has the unique capability to create a quantum computer from programming to assembly. Brown co-founded the center in 2021, alongside several other experts, and assumed the position of director in 2026. Under his leadership, the center has strengthened ties to Duke’s Deep Tech , the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and North Carolina State University and its researchers have started to earn accolades . Brown said his “long-term dream” for the center is to move it out of central Durham and onto the Duke campus, so that undergraduates can interact with it more often. Scientists working at the Duke Quantum Center. Quantum computing represents an alternative to digital computing — the current standard for the world’s devices. Almost all devices, from most supercomputers to the iPhone, are digital: they encode information in bits, which take on the value 0 or 1. Even if a digital computation works quickly, the process ultimately requires solving a complex computational problem by following pre-defined logical steps until one works, or essentially approximating the problem to find a sufficient solution. The method works effectively for daily tasks, but can be insufficient for cutting-edge research involving heavy computation. By contrast, quantum computers rely on quantum bits, known as qubits, which can be put into superpositions of quantum states: both 0 and 1 at the same time. This mixture underlies the potential of quantum computers. If engineered a certain way, a quantum computer solves a problem by amplifying the correct solutions and dampening the incorrect ones, possibly computing in seconds what may have taken a classical computer millennia. Or at least, in theory. Brown described the quantum mechanical effect as “subtle” and could easily fall apart with “noise” — in other words, the components of an environment that dampen the correct solutions. “Until the beginning of the 20th century, human beings had been around a long, long time and never noticed anything quantum mechanical,” Brown said. Optics and electronics for stabilizing the frequency of the lasers used for manipulating barium ions at the Duke Quantum Center. But then, scientists began to achieve “sufficiently precise measurement of things” and “run into real physics mysteries” that could only be answered with quantum mechanics. These answers, along with the properties that DQC seek to exploit, are so elusive that science did not notice them until the early 1900s. Di Fang, assistant professor of mathematics and a faculty member of DQC, compared the current state of quantum computing to being able to play a sheet of music, but only on a toy piano. “There are already many things you can do with it,” Fang said. But ... in order to play Mozart, we hope to have a full piano.” However, Fang said the “toy piano” stage is still better than where quantum computing was around a decade ago. Then, quantum algorithms were well known, yet no one had a quantum computer to execute them, as if piano pieces were written before pianos were even invented. In the meantime, Fang, alongside other faculty members and students at DQC, are still forging ahead, writing new and grander pieces through innovative technology. Alice, Bob and Cleo are a chatty trio, sending hundreds of messages to each other per second. But they are not a circle of friends in a group chat. Instead, they are a set of hulking machines, specifically trapped-ion quantum computers, built for data transmission experiments. Each machine weighs hundreds of pounds and is usually hidden under cloaks in a shared computational room. Ashish Kalakuntla, a doctoral candidate studying physics, explained that Alice, Bob and Cleo have qubits contained in barium or ytterbium ions. Then, the DQC uses lasers to control and manipulate these ions, allowing them to emit photons, physical light signals that represent an easier method of communicating qubits from one machine to another. According to Kalakuntla, the trio once achieved a 97% fidelity rate and were able to entangle 250 photon pairs per second. Typically, researchers at DQC are only able to collect a photon from a given ion around 2% of the time, so only a constant bombardment of photons compensates for these difficult odds. Although other researchers have worked on similar technology, the DQC’s development of the three-machine trapped-ion network marks an unprecedented achievement in the quantum computing world. Even some of the most prestigious labs working on networks have only one or two traps. "Cleo," a trapped-ion quantum computer. But, DQC has more than just Alice, Bob and Cleo. Emma Stavropoulos, a physics doctoral candidate, said that many Duke community members may not realize the different expertise required for the experiments done at DQC. “We have people working on theory, lasers and optics, radiofrequency engineering, electronics, chip design and fabrication, cryogenic systems, noise and stability, and control software,” Stravropoulos said, as examples. Stavropoulos has been working on a project around quantum simulation, or QSIM. QSIM is a quantum analog simulator that helps solve Hamiltonian systems, which provide instructions for quantum computers. The Hamiltonian problems she aims to solve involve dozens of particles bouncing against one another, each with a unique velocity and acceleration. The particles can overwhelm a classical computer, causing it to fail or produce incorrect results. The usage of the Hamiltonian system allows a “high degree of control,” according to Stavropoulos. The experiments she and her team conduct to improve the length and coherence of simulations help further enhance already uniquely powerful computer systems. A maze of lasers, at the center of QSIM. DQC, which started with five faculty members in 2021 and has since grown to 15, has already attracted quantum theorists from around the country. Fang said the center was one of the main reasons she took a position at Duke. “The interdisciplinary aspect of Duke is amazing,” Fang said. “... The Duke Quantum Center is really unique in that regard.” Ian Von Wald, program director at DQC, said the center aims to further grow its community through creating a fund for graduate students, hosting talks and reintroducing a summer school for people interested in quantum computing. As Stavropoulos put it, “it takes a village to trap an ion.” Microfabricated trap for holding atomic ions at the Duke Quantum Center (credit: Sandia National Laboratories). Beyond community-building, the center still aims to make greater contributions to the field beyond its existing breakthroughs. For example, the center is working toward creating a 256-qubit quantum computer within seven years, and received a $1 million grant for the project in 2024. Once completed, the quantum computer would be the most powerful of its kind. Brown said he looks forward to venturing into uncharted territory with this new computer, which he anticipates will be so powerful that it can perform tasks that no classical computer can. Someday, quantum computing could yield advances in drug discovery, digital privacy or solar panel technology, and perhaps even daily uses for an average person. Fang noted that it remains unclear which type of hardware will ultimately lead to a robust, scalable and cost-effective quantum computer. Public and private labs worldwide are pursuing different types of quantum computers, and DQC is currently pursuing the trapped ion and neutral atom approach, two methods of many. Perhaps the breakthrough quantum computer will be forged through a different method from a superconducting qubit to a photonic qubit or quantum dot. But maybe, according to Fang, “everyone has their own style of piano." Editor's note: This story was updated Saturday to correctly reflect Di Fang's title as assistant professor of mathematics and that DQC is pursuing both the trapped ion and neutral atom approach in its efforts. It was then updated Monday to correctly reflect Fang's belief that while it is unclear which method of quantum computing might produce a "robust, scalable and cost-effective" computer, she did not "stress caution" about DQC's potential for a breakthrough as well as to correctly reflect some of the science around the Alice, Bob and Cleo computers. The Chronicle regrets the error.

title: Duke Health settles class-action suit for $3.7 million after patients alleged personal data shared with Meta
author: Ella Moore
text: A court granted preliminary approval to Duke University Health System’s $3.7 million class-action settlement following a lawsuit alleging the healthcare network shared patient information with tech company Meta. Over 800,000 individuals could be eligible for payouts and must file for payment by Aug. 16. Patients who logged into the Duke MyChart portal or MyDuke Health mobile app at least once between Feb. 18, 2019 and June 17, 2022, are members of the settlement class and are eligible for payment. The settlement will become effective pending final court approval. According to the court-approved settlement website , the settlement does not include any admission of wrongdoing by DUHS, and the court has not ruled that DUHS committed wrongdoing either. Duke Health officials wrote in an email to The Chronicle “the matter is subject to a court-approved settlement process, and Duke Health has no comment.” The class-action lawsuit was first filed in 2022 by lead plaintiffs Kim Naugle and Afrika Williams. It came after a news report by The Markup alleged that the platform Meta Pixel, a tool that tracks website traffic and activity, sent Facebook information about patients’ health conditions, medications and demographics such as sexual orientation. The Markup reported that pixel tracking was found on DUHS’s appointment scheduling page, alongside 32 other top hospitals across the nation. Williams and Naugle’s lawsuit alleged that DUHS transferred patient data between the private patient portal Duke MyChart and Facebook. DUHS has previously denied these allegations. “DUHS has investigated the use of the Meta Pixel on our website and patient portal and has determined that DUHS did not transmit any of its patients’ protected health information to Meta,” a spokesperson for the health system wrote in a November 2022 statement to MedCityNews. The pixel tool has been the subject of dozens of class-action lawsuits related to collecting legally protected patient information and sending it to Meta for use in targeted advertising on Facebook. DUHS initially moved to dismiss the case in November 2022, arguing that neither plaintiff provided specific examples of medical information shared with Facebook, nor “how she was possibly harmed” by the alleged data transfer. In a March 2024 ruling , U.S. District Judge Joi Elizabeth Peake dismissed some of the lawsuit’s claims but allowed others to stand. Williams and DUHS ultimately participated in two mediation sessions in December 2024 and February 2025, from which a settlement agreement emerged. The court preliminarily approved the $3.7 million settlement in March 2026. Part of the settlement fund will be allocated to attorney’s fees and a service award for the lead plaintiffs. The rest will be distributed to affected persons who receive approval for payouts. Class members must submit their claim form by Aug. 16 via an online form or by mail to the settlement administrator. The mailing address for claims is made available on the online form. The settlement administrator, appointed by the court, will determine the final amount distributed to each member once the settlement receives final court approval and becomes effective. If claim members choose to exclude themselves from the settlement, they must notify the settlement administrator via mail by July 20 and can subsequently retain any legal claims they may have against DUHS. If claim members do not notify the settlement administrator that they are opting out, they will remain bound to the guidelines of the settlement, which include releasing the right to bring legal action against Duke Health in the future regarding this case. To voice opposition to the settlement, claim members must file an official written objection with the court, DUHS counsel and defendant’s counsel by July 20. Objection does not preclude members from submitting a settlement claim. Members of the public can attend the final settlement approval hearing, which will take place Aug. 27 at 9:30 a.m. at 324 W. Market St. in Greensboro. During the hearing, the court will make a final determination of whether the settlement is “fair, reasonable and adequate and in the best interests of the settlement class,” according to the settlement website.

title: ‘We can tell the story better’: Mary Frances Luce on Fuqua’s future
author: Luca Gragnoli
text: Mary Frances Luce officially became the first female dean of the Fuqua School of Business on April 1. Previously, she had been serving as the interim dean since August 2024, when Bill Boulding, former dean and J.B. Fuqua professor of business administration, stepped down. To learn more about her motivations for undertaking the deanship as well as some of her priorities and ambitions for Fuqua, The Chronicle spoke with Luce last month. Recognized as a pioneer by Chron15 in 2025 for paving the way for female leadership, Luce is also the first Fuqua alumna in the University’s history to serve as dean. Luce graduated from Fuqua in 1994 with a doctoral degree in business administration with a concentration in marketing after receiving her bachelor’s degree from Ball State University in 1989. She has been a member of the faculty for over two decades, but described her time as a doctoral student at the school as a “formative” first job. Following a decade of teaching at the Wharton School of Business at the University of Pennsylvania, Luce joined the Fuqua faculty in 2004. In addition to being dean, she holds the title of Robert. A Ingram professor of business administration. Throughout her many years at Fuqua, Luce taught students in all of the school’s 10 degree programs and served as a senior associate dean of faculty for six years. She also developed a strong connection and understanding of the business school, which encouraged her to accept the role of dean. “My long history with this place and my deep knowledge of this place are more an asset than a hindrance,” Luce said. Luce shared that what brought her back to Fuqua in the first place was the school’s collaborative culture and intellectual environment, which she said community members commonly referred to as “Team Fuqua.” “This culture of deep collaboration — trying to solve the big problems for organizations, for business and for society, and really working together across boundaries — was very attractive to me,” Luce said. Luce said that she is not the only one who feels this way. Many prospective, current and former students have shared with Luce that their decision to apply and attend Fuqua was based on their appreciation for its collaborative spirit. Despite being a relatively “young school,” having only been founded in 1969, Luce said that Fuqua also uniquely excels in academic research, describing the institution as a “research-based business school.” Luce has experience in the research realm, having previously served as an associate vice president in Duke’s Office for Research and Innovation. “We look a lot more like some of the social science departments in [the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences] than somebody might think about a business school,” Luce said. She highlighted Fuqua’s health sector management program, in particular, as a point of passion. Luce described the program as interdisciplinary, a “huge draw for excellent students,” and a place where faculty across various academic disciplines can collaborate and teach students. “I think that’s really Fuqua at its best,” Luce said. Luce believes that the “integrated story” of Fuqua, an institution built on combined excellence in research and collaboration, must be communicated to prospective students and employers. During conversations with students, Luce found that many applicants picked Fuqua after attending Blue Devil weekend — a two-day welcome for admitted students. On those days, students speak with faculty and alumni and learn what collaboration means at Fuqua, according to Luce. However, Luce wants students to learn that lesson even earlier. “We can tell the story better,” Luce said. “We don't have to rely on people coming to Blue Devil weekend.” Although Fuqua’s excellence is nationally recognized, the institution has seen its own share of challenges in the past year as the University engaged in broader strategic realignment efforts. During her two years as interim dean, Luce had to engage in cost-cutting, an even bigger concern for Fuqua, since most of its revenue comes from tuition and business schools are currently facing "enrollment pressures," according to Luce. With the cost-cutting, Fuqua had to cut some activities and pause a program. The main concern for Luce was implementing the required cost-cutting while ensuring that students were not overly impacted by the measures. Although the faculty grew smaller, Luce said maintaining support for faculty-driven research remained a priority as it serves as “long-term investments.” “We can't afford to have a class of students with a terrible experience out there saying I didn't get what I was promised at Fuqua; we can't afford for our faculty to pause their research, because they need to remain thought leaders,” Luce said. Cost-cutting at the University led to the loss of about 5% of Duke’s full-time staff through voluntary buyouts and involuntary layoffs , though it is unclear how many were based in Fuqua. Luce admitted that “every staff member” at the school has a “harder job now than they did before,” and that it has been a challenge for community morale. Despite the difficult decisions, she had an overall optimistic reflection on Fuqua’s future after strategic realignment. “I think we made some good reorganization moves that are going to make us more efficient,” Luce said. Looking to the future, Luce hopes to integrate the school more with Duke’s broader intellectual community and be more intentional about finding connections to undergraduates in Trinity and the Pratt School of Engineering. “We're literally sitting on the edge of an intellectual gold mine, and we should be contributing to that as much as we can,” Luce said. “And we should also be learning from that.”

title: Duke professor Jungsang Kim awarded South Korea high honor for quantum computing work
author: Seyun Park
text: Duke professor Jungsang Kim was awarded South Korea’s Changjo Medal, the highest class within the nation’s leading honor for scientists and engineers, the Order of Science and Technology Merit. Kim, who works on quantum computing at Duke, is the Schiciano family distinguished professor of electrical and computer engineering and the co-founder of quantum computing company IonQ. Kim received the medal April 21 from South Korean Prime Minister Kim Min-Seok in a ceremony in Seoul. “I grew up in Korean culture. I appreciated all the education and mentoring that I received during my early days, that put me on a passion for becoming a scientist,” Kim said. “... It was very special to go back to the origins, where all my parents, my siblings and my family are.” Kim also serves as an associate director of the Duke Quantum Center, where his work primarily focuses on trapped atomic ions in quantum computers. He uses the trapped ions in a vacuum and then manipulates them with lasers, using the electrically charged atoms to store information. Over the past 30 years, his goal has been to make quantum computing commercially viable. “Trapped atomic ions are a leading platform for first-generation quantum computers, but they are also fundamentally scalable to more powerful general purpose devices in future generations,” Kim wrote, alongside Duke Quantum Center Director Kenneth Brown and associate director Christopher Monroe, in a 2016 paper about the concept. Last year, Kim and his team marked an important milestone in the journey toward scaling up quantum computing. In November 2025, the U.S. Patent Office granted their patent for a “quantum computer based on manipulation of ion chains.” The new patent built on previous trapped-ion computing by initializing multiple ion chains in one trap. “It's something that I've been working on for over the last two decades of my career,” Kim said. “... We took that kind of fundamental science, started building systems that can actually do more useful computing, taking it all the way to commercially viable systems ... [and that was what] I think the award was recognizing.” Kim was born and raised in South Korea, but then moved to the U.S. in 1992 for his doctorate at Stanford University. He later became an assistant professor at Duke in 2004. In 2015, Kim co-founded IonQ with Monroe as a Duke-based startup that has since gone public. He served as the company’s chief technology officer until 2024 and was named Duke’s chief science and technology strategist for the provost in February 2025. He also currently serves as a professor of physics. “Jungsang’s receipt of the Order of Science and Technology Merit is a distinction of the very highest order,” Jerome Lynch, Vinik dean of the Pratt School of Engineering, said in a press release. “His pioneering work to make trapped-ion quantum computing scalable and commercially viable has moved an entire field forward.” Compared to artificial intelligence and other emerging technologies, quantum computing is in a nascent stage. But Kim predicts quantum computing is on the rise. “Have we actually seen the ChatGPT or the large language model level of breakthrough in quantum? I don't think we've seen that yet, but I don't think that's very far away,” Kim said. “I think in the next few years, some of those early applications of useful quantum computers will very likely happen.” Kim will be among those leading the charge as the development of quantum technologies continues. “I think quantum is a very speculative area with very high risk, but also very high reward,” Kim said. “I would very strongly encourage young students who have a lot of career ahead of them to take some risks, jump into the fields that nobody else is working on and try to see if they can be a part of creating that future.”

title: Chron15: Duke's icons, leaders, pioneers in 2025-26
author: Ella Moore
text: Chron15 seeks to recognize the individuals or groups that define what it means to be a member of the Duke community in 2025 and 2026. This year in particular marked a time of resilience. The University lost many members through cost-cutting and new immigration policies, while others learned how to continue supporting the community through a rapidly changing higher education environment. Through it all, the icons, leaders and pioneers nominated by Chronicle readers and selected by a committee of Chronicle staff pushed our community forward, whether through advocacy, athletic achievement, thought-provoking discussion, innovative research or new University programs. These individuals have united us, inspired us and spent their time at Duke making our campus a better place. There are many more individuals and groups that have made invaluable contributions to this University and its community, though The Chronicle can only name 15 of them. We, and our readers, thank countless more for all they do to make our University a home we can be proud of. Ella Moore chaired the Chron15 2025-26 committee. She is a Trinity junior and a news editor of The Chronicle’s 122nd volume.

title: 2025-26 Chron15: Duke's icons
author: Staff Reports
text: Duke’s icons are the people everyone knows — for their kindness, passion, talent or intellect. They are the people who light up the classrooms, dining facilities and residential quads, or who you see plastered across the television in Krafthouse. Taina Mair has been the three-year point guard for Duke women’s basketball and was the unquestioned leader of the bunch this year. She led her team to its second straight ACC Tournament win and Elite Eight appearance. Mair’s best came when it mattered most; in the ACC championship against Louisville, she scored 19 points with a remarkable 12 rebounds, winning the ACC Tournament’s Most Valuable Player award. In the NCAA Tournament’s Sacramento 2 Region, she scored a combined 43 points against LSU and UCLA. “She’s a winner ... She does everything that you would want, and she’s unafraid of the moment as well. So I’m just fortunate enough to coach her,” head coach Kara Lawson said of Mair . Mair spent three years in Durham, and after a lessened role in the 2024-25 campaign, she often played long, grueling minutes as the sole primary ball handler this year. Lawson and the team frequently singled Mair out as a consistent performer and energy-setter even in the November and December nadir of this season. Mair’s facilitation prowess was evident as she tied Duke legend Chelsea Gray’s record for assists in a single season with 201. As the 14th pick in the 2026 WNBA Draft, Mair was the first Blue Devil selected in the first round since 2018. - Chronicle Staff Reports Transferring to Duke men’s basketball from Syracuse after his sophomore season, Maliq Brown served a two-year term as the Blue Devils’ premier defensive weapon, developing into a fan favorite for the Duke faithful in the process. The Culpeper, Virginia, native acquired a series of honors during his time in Durham, including ACC Defensive Player of the Year and ACC Sixth Man of the Year during his senior season. “It’s almost like a different game [Brown] plays,” guard Caleb Foster said of his two-year teammate. “I’ve never played with somebody with such an impact on the team.” With his calm demeanor and 7-foot wingspan, the 6-foot-9 forward stood as the anchor of the pair of 35-win Blue Devils teams, showing to everyone who watched what it meant to be part of a team. And in his final game in Cameron Indoor Stadium — senior night against North Carolina — Brown gave Blue Devil fans one last masterclass, recording his highest totals in points and steals in a Duke uniform with 15 and five, respectively, on 6-of-9 shooting across 30 minutes, cementing his legacy as the model role player for Blue Devil basketball. - Chronicle Staff Reports Some spots on campus just have a way of drawing people in. Chef's Kitchen — the rotating restaurant on the second floor of WU — did exactly that this year, and then some. Home to instant classics like Smashburgers and Don's Classic Subs, the lines that snaked around the second floor became a familiar sight around lunch time, and a reliable sign that something yummy was waiting at the end of them. Students generally didn’t mind the wait and the food was always worth it. The hardworking employees nearly always had to stay beyond the slated closing time of 3 p.m. to finish out the line of students, which they did with grace. The Chronicle is grateful for their diligence and altruism. Beyond the lunch rush, Chef's Kitchen also hosted cooking classes, giving students a chance to slow down and actually learn something in the kitchen, headed up by Chef John Eisensmith. And unlike a lot of dining options, it never felt like it was unnecessarily gouging your food points. Chef's Kitchen struck a rare balance: delicious, unique food you can’t get anywhere else on campus, fair prices and a buzz around it that made it one of the most talked-about spots on campus this year. Here’s to hoping whatever comes next lives up to the standard it set. Please bring Don’s Classic Subs back. - Chronicle Staff Reports When you’ve outlasted five university presidents and built a career longer than many of your colleagues’ entire lives, you become as much a part of the institution's history as anyone else can claim to be. Thus, it’s near-impossible to imagine Duke without Victor Strandberg, but after 60 years, the English professor has taught his final class. Arriving in Durham in 1966, the Massachusetts native brought to the South his wealth of knowledge on literature of all sorts. Although he certainly had his favorite authors, his ability to passionately and effectively teach everything from the Bhagavad Gita to William Faulkner was what made him such a beloved and cherished instructor. His teaching style consisted of lectures and tests rather than the open-table discussions and critical essays English curricula have opted for over the years, yet nothing about the love for literature he instilled in his students felt outdated. Duke, not just the English department, is losing a foundational member of its community. Students and faculty will sorely miss Victor Strandberg and wish they could learn more from him. And after 60 years, that’s all you can really ask for. - Chronicle Staff Reports Jaden Small, stagename JDon!, a junior and rapper from the Bronx. In any given year, the signed artists of Small Town Records (STR) may sound like a classic “man walks into a bar joke,” or maybe like a Durham-based Breakfast Club. During a Small Town Records-sponsored performance, you could find a freshman funk-jazz group, a singer-songwriter-physicist or a rapping summer analyst. Despite Duke not having an official school of music, STR has gifted students a place to perform. Housed in the basement of the Bryan Center, the student-run record label holds yearly auditions to sign a small-but-mighty selection of student artists. Providing signees with the resources to produce and perform their own music, STR artists can be found opening LDOC, jamming in Coffeehouse and performing at Devine’s. The organization also welcomes students beyond the stage. Through STR’s executive team, non-performers gain experience in roles like artist marketing and band management. For more technology-savvy folks, executive positions expand into committees like sound engineering and song production. And for those who love to jam, the record keeps a roster of instrumentalists in their house band, ready to provide back-up to their signed artists. - Chronicle Staff Reports

title: 2025-26 Chron15: Duke's leaders
author: Staff Reports
text: Duke’s leaders are the people who champion the University community during good times and bad, inspiring others with their emphasis on values and progress. The leaders featured on this year’s Chron15 list are community members who used their power for good as they shaped the University’s path forward, often even as they faced their own trials. These leaders worked tirelessly to improve the Duke experience for every member, whether through research, student organizations or everyday work shifts. Duke prides itself on being a leading research institution, and no one knows this better than Jennifer Lodge, vice president for research and innovation. She oversees the University’s $1.2 billion annual research portfolio and helps to connect Duke to external partners in research and commercialization. As the University’s chief research officer, Lodge has helped faculty and staff to navigate federal uncertainty from frozen research grants and canceled funding. When four federal agencies threatened to impose a 15% cap on facilities and administrative (F&A) cost reimbursement — which would have cost Duke to the tune of $194 million annually — Lodge was at the forefront of a national effort to protect funding for overhead expenses. Lodge served as a subject matter expert on a national team to produce a new research funding model, known as the Fiscal Accountability in Research (FAIR) model, that would improve transparency and help support the actual expenses, direct and indirect, that factor into research. The 26-person team represented a mix of universities, academic medical centers, independent research institutes and the private sector, with backing from 10 national organizations. Lodge’s contributions have helped to provide Duke with some sense of relief from what had once been identified as the University’s biggest financial concern . Since unveiling the FAIR model in July, it has received support from members of Congress and many in the scientific community. - Chronicle Staff Reports When asked to point to the face of student life at Duke this year, most Blue Devils would likely direct you to Trinity Class of 2026 alumna Kaitlyn Williams. From her position at the helm of several prominent student organizations, Williams has forged countless connections between communities in and around campus in service of her mission to support her peers. As president of the Duke University Union, the University’s largest student organization that serves as an institutional hub for arts, media and programming, Williams was the engine behind many of the campus events that made the 2025-26 academic year memorable for students. She oversaw the operations of DUU’s 18 committees and helped launch its newest in spring 2025: (DUU)rham, which seeks to help burst the colloquial “Duke bubble.” Notably, she also served as president of SPIRE Fellows, a program designed to enhance equity in STEM disciplines by supporting students who have historically faced barriers to access and opportunity; Williams’ consistent and empathetic mentorship was widely credited with boosting the program’s success. Williams will retain her ties to Duke in the coming years — both informally through the many close relationships with faculty, staff and students she has cultivated in her time on campus and formally as the University’s newest undergraduate Young Trustee . - Chronicle Staff Reports Since 1990, the Duke Human Vaccine Institute has worked to develop vaccines and treatments for some of the world’s most prominent emerging infections. Its state-of-the-art facilities make it one of the leading vaccine institutes in the country and help it to manufacture critical mRNA therapies, vaccines and other cell-based products. The institute has been home to several groundbreaking advancements in HIV and AIDS research. Over a series of three federally supported projects spanning 21 years, investigators have identified many key characteristics of HIV and begun to develop a series of vaccines that can trigger an antibody response and neutralize the virus as it mutates — an approach that DHVI Director Barton Haynes has described as “brand new” in the fields of immunology and vaccinology. In spite of the Department of Health and Human Services’ sweeping cuts to HIV research in 2025, DHVI has continued to advance its vaccine development efforts through multiple trials of different vaccine candidates, with plans to pursue alternate funding opportunities in the future. - Chronicle Staff Reports Duke’s housekeeping staff has faced unprecedented adversity this year due to the University’s cost-cutting initiatives and the Trump administration’s immigration policy. For Margoth Erazo, a Honduran immigrant and Duke housekeeper for 23 years, her future is in jeopardy after she lost her job following the Trump administration’s revocation of her Temporary Protected Status. After Duke restructured its University Environmental Services schedules in November, housekeepers were forced to rebid for shifts and building assignments. East Campus Housekeeper Nahshon Blount called the process a “survival of the fittest game,” where seniority is prioritized. Some staff were left without a fixed schedule and faced random building assignments. While facing these challenges, the tireless work and resilience of the staff members of Duke housekeeping continue to make campus a safer and cleaner place. In whichever building they are servicing, Duke housekeeping’s 230-plus-person staff has made the student experience more enjoyable. Cleaning up after gamedays and parties, their work across campus, while sometimes not fully appreciated, is vital to the day-to-day operations of the University. The Chronicle recognizes the staff’s unwavering dedication to their work and flexibility shown this year. - Chronicle Staff Reports Michael Ramos, Trinity ‘26, has spent four years at Duke doing what comes naturally to him: showing up for people. A double major in public policy and international comparative studies, Ramos brought the perspective of a “fronterizo” raised in the San Diego-Tijuana border as the son of Mexican immigrants to everything he touched on campus. As co-president and later president of Beyond Borders, he pushed the student advocacy group beyond Duke's walls to build real ties with Durham organizations like Siembra NC and Somos Durham. During the fall Border Patrol raids, he raised awareness on campus about students’ rights and worked within the Durham community to advocate for the protection of Latino individuals and families. When Luis Juaréz was removed from his bus driver position at Duke due to his Temporary Protected Status ending, Ramos organized a GoFundMe that moved the community to action, raising over $17,000 to help pay for Juaréz’s legal fees. As a Hart Fellow, he turned his attention to Yaqui indigenous culture and the meaning of home amid displacement, a personal question to him. Ramos has been a consistent champion for first-generation and Latino students throughout his time here, work that earned him the Samuel DuBois Cook Society Undergraduate Student Award and has left a lasting, personal impression on campus advocacy. - Chronicle Staff Reports

title: 2025-26 Chron15: Duke's pioneers
author: Staff Reports
text: Duke’s pioneers are the people and organizations who push the University forward, relentlessly reimagining every aspect of our community with boundless energy and passion. They question the status quo and refuse to accept anything that is unjust or unsatisfactory. The pioneers of this year’s Chron15 facilitated difficult conversations, founded academic programs that transformed the Duke experience and sparked community-driven change across campus and the world. Eric Mlyn, distinguished faculty fellow at the Kenan Institute for Ethics and lecturer at the Sanford School of Public Policy, retired this year. During his over two decades at the University, Mlyn pioneered the Robertson Scholars program, DukeEngage and DukeEngage Gateway, which have since served hundreds of students. Throughout his time on Duke’s campus, Mlyn has been tirelessly dedicated to expanding his students’ worldview beyond themselves. Because of Mlyn, students have had the opportunity to travel to over 20 different locations in the United States and around the world to participate in community projects such as building the longest bridge in Estwatini, teaching English at refugee schools in South Korea and working to empower youth in Brazil. Outside of his work connecting students to community engagement, Mlyn has maintained a focus on how higher education can continue to contribute to democracy, whatever its challenges. Through his leadership of projects at the Kenan Institute on Democracy and Higher Education, Mlyn has encouraged students and faculty alike to advance their perspectives on the potential of higher education. During the spring semester, Mlyn led a “Higher Education at a Crossroads, Reckoning with the Rise of Illiberalism” project, supporting student research into the subject in preparation for a May 22-23 conference. Though the spring semester was Mlyn’s last in teaching, he will continue to lead the “Democracy in Peril: Paths to Renewal” Duke Engage in DC program over the next few years. - Chronicle Staff Reports Cassidie Carmen Bates, Sanford ‘26, has made a lasting impact on Duke’s campus by advancing grief-informed support, research and community engagement during her time at the Sanford School of Public Policy in ways that did not previously exist. Recognizing the need for more open and accessible conversations around loss, she launched new grief support initiatives for students, helping bring grief awareness into broader campus dialogue and policy discussions. She delivered a TEDxDuke Talk focused on the importance of bereavement leave policies being informed by both the lived expertise of grievers and subject-matter data on how grief affects the brain and body. Cassidie also co-established Duke’s first-ever Grief Panel, bringing together undergraduate, graduate, medical, divinity and other students to share personal experiences of loss and explore how Duke can become a more supportive and grief-aware community. In addition to her research and advocacy, Cassidie established Duke’s partnership with Grieftastic Inc. and led a campus book talk featuring nationally recognized authors and leaders in the grief space. Through her research, leadership, and community-building efforts, she has created meaningful change at Duke while helping elevate the conversation around grief support in academic settings. - Ariel Keller, Sanford '26 Christopher Coome, a postdoctoral fellow with the Sanford School's Civil Discourse Project and an intellectual historian by trade, is the beating heart of Duke's efforts to combat negative polarization and revitalize serious political discourse. Arriving on campus in spring 2025, he has spent the lion's share of his time in Durham facilitating dialogues across political differences through civil discourse roundtables and political philosophy seminars. In less than two years, he organized and facilitated over 30 such events, oftentimes supported by both Duke Democrats and College Republicans. He has taught hundreds of Duke students about everything from the philosophies of Hegel, Diogenes, and Paine to more basic, practical things like "how to disagree agreeably with someone." Coome also serves as the faculty advisor for The Lemur: Duke's Big Ideas Magazine, the principal co-sponsor for most Civil Discourse Project events. When Coome is not working in the Civil Discourse space, he plays an active role in growing the campus' community of intellectual conservativism — a community which was essentially nonexistent before he arrived. In less than two years, Coome has taught a course on the history of conservative political philosophy, supported conservative clubs and led a reading group for the Duke Law Federalist Society. Coome plans to expand upon his work with the Civil Discourse Project as he teaches the course Public Policy 202S, Political Polarization, in the fall. His most recent book, “The Foundations of Re-Enchantment,” was published in December 2025. - Sherman Criner, Trinity '26 Nimmi Ramanujam has built a career upon standing up for women’s health. Now, she’s being recognized on the national stage for the impact of her work. Ramanujam is the Robert W. Carr Jr. distinguished professor of biomedical engineering in the Pratt School of Engineering, where she has spent decades researching how to address disparities in outcomes for breast and cervical cancer patients. She is also the founder and director of Duke’s Center for Global Women’s Health Technologies. Ramanujam has developed technologies that make cervical imaging more affordable and less invasive, helping to improve diagnostic outcomes for women across the globe. In 2024, she received the Department of Defense’s Breast Cancer Research Innovator Award, alongside a $10.4 million grant to support her work on early detection of breast cancer and reducing the burden of care for patients by decreasing the number of required treatment visits. In February, Ramanujam was elected to the National Academy of Engineering, one of the highest awards given to engineers. She told The Chronicle at the time that she hoped the achievement would bring attention to women’s health research beyond her individual contributions to the field, saying “it’s nice to see that this is being brought to the forefront ... that what we’re doing is considered meaningful and impactful.” - Chronicle Staff Reports The Chronicle previously spotlighted Nina King as a pioneer after she became the first woman and the first Black person to serve at the helm of Duke’s athletic department. In her past five years as the Blue Devils’ athletic director, King has spearheaded efforts to keep the University’s 27 varsity sports teams competitive in a new landscape of revenue sharing, NIL governance, conference realignment and media rights. Since assuming her role in May 2021, King has overseen the hiring or promotion of 11 head coaches — including football’s Manny Diaz, baseball’s Corey Muscara, women’s soccer’s Kieran Hall and fencing’s Omar Elgeziry. Duke has won 16 ACC Championships under her leadership, including four during the 2025-26 academic year, and made deep runs in NCAA Tournaments across multiple sports. With college athletics quickly evolving, Duke has found new sources of revenue, including a first-of-its-kind streaming deal between the men’s basketball program and Amazon Prime Video. Multiple athletics facilities, including Jack Coombs Field, Taishoff Aquatics Pavilion and the Krzyzewski Center for Athletic Excellence, are midway through significant renovation projects. “The standard for Duke, if it has this logo on it, is to be excellent in all things,” Diaz said at the 2025 ACC Kickoff. “Nina King has done a phenomenal job of making sure that all of those programs are poised to be successful in the future.” - Chronicle Staff Reports

title: The Chronicle's guide to making the most of a Durham summer
author: Yashraj Vishwakarma
text: Although Bryan Center Plaza, the Brodhead Center and the rest of Duke’s campus may be less lively during the summer, there is plenty to do in the Bull City. The Chronicle has curated a guide to Durham’s summer offerings, ranging from cultural events to culinary exploration. For film enthusiasts, the Carolina Theatre screens a mix of the latest releases, as well as classics as part of its Retro Film Series. Through its centennial programming , the theater will also organize a wide variety of events in the summer that will continue throughout the year. Individual tickets are typically $12 per double feature, before taxes and fees. The Durham Performing Arts Center has also scheduled numerous musicals over the summer, including “Hamilton” from May 21 to June 14 and “The Notebook” from July 14 to July 19. DPAC will also feature other types of performances, including from composer and pianist Max Richter, who will perform his orchestral pieces with the American Contemporary Music Ensemble. Although Duke students are always free to visit the Nasher Museum of Art, Durham is also home to the 21c Museum Hotel, which doubles as a contemporary art gallery . The museum is currently home to a mix of permanent installations and temporary exhibitions. Additionally, those in Durham for the summer can engage with the city’s arts community by visiting the Scrap Exchange or the Golden Belt campus . In particular, Golden Belt hosts a monthly art showcase and sale, starting at 6 p.m. on the third Friday of every month. If you’re passionate about music, local staples such as Motorco Music Hall , the Fruit and the Pinhook have many events scheduled, featuring local artists and themed events centered on specific genres and musicians, such as “Debí Tirar Más Fiestas: The Bad Bunny Party” on June 20 at Motorco. For those who really do not want to leave campus, DukeArts is hosting “Arts on East,” a series of free concerts at 7 p.m. every Wednesday from May 27 to June 24 on East Campus. Durham’s restaurants, bars and cafes have been lauded as among the best in the South, with both casual and fine-dining venues serving a diverse range of cuisines. For those seeking popular options such as Thai cuisine, Thai@Main Street and Thaiangle are both well-priced restaurants in downtown Durham with menus featuring both traditional and Asian-fusion dishes. Pizzeria Toro , Mother & Sons Trattoria and Cucciolo Osteria are also located in downtown Durham and provide excellent Italian fare, although they are more expensive. Durham also hosts a variety of cuisines, including at Goorsha in the Brightleaf District, which serves traditional Ethiopian fare for excellent value. South of West Campus, visitors can also stop by Guglhupf , a restaurant and bakery serving a blend of traditional brunch items alongside German dishes like schnitzel and bratwurst. Despite being one of the largest cities in North Carolina and a central part of the Research Triangle, Durham also has numerous attractions for nature lovers. Although the Al Buehler Trail and Sarah P. Duke Gardens on campus are open to students year round, there are also other places worth exploring throughout Durham. Eno River State Park is free to visit, although camping and event permits require additional fees. The park and the affiliated Eno River Association often organize activities, such as wood-whittling workshops. For those seeking a more active outing, the 22.6-mile-long American Tobacco Trail runs through Durham, Wake and Chatham counties and is open to both pedestrians and cyclists. Located just a short walk away from East Campus, Durham Central Park is also a great place to spend some time outdoors. From 8 a.m. to 12 p.m. on Saturdays and 3 to 6 p.m. on Wednesdays, park goers can find the Durham Farmer’s Market , showcasing local vendors. The park also hosts a number of events , including some recurring ones such as yoga from 6 to 7 p.m. every Tuesday. Whether students are looking for live music, new restaurants or outdoor activities, Durham offers a wide range of ways to stay busy throughout the summer months.

title: The Chronicle’s guide to navigating Duke summer services
author: Lila Cohen
text: Whether enrolling in an academic summer session, participating in a Plus (+) Program or interning locally, students staying at Duke over the summer will retain access to many campus services. The Chronicle compiled a list of seasonal updates. Multiple school year staples — including Bella Union, JB’s Roasts & Chops, The Chef’s Kitchen and Pitchfork’s — will be closed for the summer season. Most Brodhead Center restaurants will serve food from 11 a.m. to 7 p.m, while Cafe and Panera will operate from 7 a.m. to 3 p.m. on weekdays and from 8 a.m. to 3 p.m. on weekends. The Skillet will be closed until June 2 and Marketplace until June 14. Some Brodhead Center locations will close mid-July for the remainder of the summer. The most updated details on specific hours of operation for all dining locations can be found at the Duke Dining website . Available dining locations will accept summer session food points, credit and debit card payment and Flex points during the summer. Student Health is open throughout the summer on Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday and Friday from 8 a.m. to 4:30 p.m., and Thursday from 9 a.m. to 4:30 p.m. Service will be paused for a brief 12-12:30 p.m. lunch break each weekday. To schedule an appointment, students should call the Wellness Center at 919-681-9355, and select option one for non-urgent visits or option two for same-day appointments. Non-same day visits can also be scheduled online via the Duke My Health portal. More information about on-campus health services and other resources can be found at the Duke Student Health website . Summer modified hours for Wilson Recreation Center are 5:30 a.m. to 9 p.m. on weekdays and 9 a.m. to 6 p.m. on weekends. Red Mango will be closed Friday through Sunday and open Monday through Thursday from 12 p.m. to 8 p.m. Brodie Recreation Center is open from 7 a.m. to 9 p.m. on weekdays and 9 a.m. to 6 p.m. on weekends. The East Campus pickleball courts will be closed for the duration of the summer as part of the Lilly Library renovation. The Outdoor Adventures climbing wall and Taishoff Aquatics Pavilion will also remain closed until fall 2026 for renovations. Brodie Aquatics Center and the Outdoor Adventures equipment rental desk will operate on a limited schedule through Aug. 23. Group fitness classes will also continue throughout the summer. Members and students must sign up in advance on the MyRec portal . For more information, visit the Duke Recreation & Physical Education website . A valid parking permit from the Duke Parking & Transportation Services Office is required to bring a car onto campus. Most academic year permits do not expire until mid-August. Duke Transportation will run the CSF shuttle for the summer, which serves stops along the regular C1 and Swift shuttle routes, including Blue Light Living, Swift Avenue and East and West Campus. Service is available from 7 a.m. to 9 p.m. on weekdays and 7:30 a.m. to 9 p.m. on weekends. The TransLoc app continues to provide real-time bus tracking during the summer. Duke Vans, which can be ordered via the TransLoc app, operate Monday through Saturday from 6 p.m. to 1 a.m. Vans can be ordered until 12:30 a.m. to on-campus locations and off-campus locations within 1.5 miles. More information about Duke Vans can be found at the Duke PTS website . Library services are available to all Duke students during the summer. Perkins and Bostock Libraries are open from 8 a.m. to 7 p.m. Monday through Thursday, 8 a.m. to 6 p.m. on Friday and 1 p.m. to 5 p.m. on Sunday. Lilly Library, initially scheduled to reopen in summer 2026, will likely remain under construction through the fall semester of the 2026-27 academic year. The reopening may extend into spring 2027. Students will have access to the Student Mailbox Center, which is located in the Bryan Center, throughout the summer. Package pickup remains available on weekdays from 8:30 a.m. to 5 p.m., and individual mailboxes are accessible daily from 7 a.m. to 11 p.m. Students can send outgoing mail from the Duke Technology Center, which is located in the bottom level of the Duke Store. According to the Duke Mail Services website , the DTC will be open over the summer from 8:30 a.m. to 5 p.m. on weekdays and 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. on Saturdays. Store hours may vary . Beginning May 20, most campus buildings will require a physical or mobile DukeCard for entry. Facilities such as the Brodhead Center, Bryan Center and libraries will maintain their usual access schedules. More information can be found here .

title: Harvard capped A’s. Duke faculty and students are skeptical
author: Lila De Almeida
text: Harvard University will soon cap the number of A grades professors can give to undergraduate students, after faculty overwhelmingly voted in favor of a proposal to combat grade inflation in the past few days. Although the policy could set an example for Harvard’s peer institutions to revise their own grading policies, those at Duke are apprehensive. The most notable provision, approved by Harvard faculty in a 458-201 vote, is a 20% cap on A grades, with an allowance of up to four additional A’s for each class. For example, starting in fall 2027 at Harvard, a maximum of six students in a 10-person seminar, or 24 students in a 100-person seminar, could receive A’s. There is no cap on any lower letter grades. The Harvard proposal emerged after an October 2025 report from Harvard’s Office of Undergraduate Education found that over 60% of grades earned by Harvard undergraduate students are A’s, up from about 25% 20 years ago. Duke has seen similar trends, although the University has not indicated any current plans to significantly change its grading practices. Data from the Office of the University Registrar showed that at least 33% of Duke’s student population earned a 4.0 GPA in the fall 2024 semester. As a result, cutoffs to earn Latin honors and Dean’s List/Dean’s List with Distinction have risen, with 2026 graduates in the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences requiring a 4.0 GPA to earn a summa cum laude designation. Yet, some Duke faculty and an undergraduate student are unsure that Harvard’s approach to grade inflation is necessary. Greg Samanez-Larkin, professor of psychology and neuroscience, said he awards A’s to many students and stands behind that choice. “I want as many people as possible in my class to be a very high performer, and if they’re willing to give me effort, I’m going to give them a ton of effort, and we’re going to work together,” he said. To Samanez-Larkin, it makes sense that so many Duke students receive A’s given the competitive nature of admissions. “We’ve taken the top sliver of a distribution of student talent, and now we’re gonna put it in 100 layers? That just makes no sense to me,” he said. So, as long as students reach Samanez-Larkin’s own “standard for excellence,” they will receive an excellent grade, noting he gave some students C’s and D’s this semester as well. Still, Samanez-Larkin said he can see the argument for A grades being reserved for when students go beyond reproducing what they learn in class and instead are “more creative ... push boundaries and ... surprise us with what they do.” Nick Carnes, Z. Smith Reynolds distinguished professor of public policy studies, argues that grade inflation is only natural; he likened grades to a currency, which are always prone to “inflationary pressure.” “I really appreciate what Harvard is trying to do,” Carnes said. With grades compressed at the top of the distribution, he thinks it’s difficult to distinguish between good, great and exceptional performance among students. He thinks Harvard’s new approach attempts to “reset the baseline for what is a good student.” Despite that optimism, Carnes has concerns over how a policy like Harvard’s would be implemented. “You might truly have a seminar with 10 amazing students who all do exemplary work. I’ve had classes like that at Duke,” he said. But with a limited number of A’s to award, there may be “tension” between professors trying to accurately reflect student performance and a university-imposed cap. Senior Ananya Misra has seen grade inflation firsthand, but she’s also not convinced it requires action. “I’ve definitely had some classes where ... someone else’s A feels like it wouldn’t be the same A as mine. But I think for the most part, we all work really hard to get the grades that we get,” she said. “... I don’t think it’s as big of a problem as Harvard is making it out to be.” If Duke wants to prevent grade inflation, Misra said the University should not emulate Harvard’s approach. First, she predicts that grade inflation would simply shift to A-minus’s in the long run, without actually impacting learning. “Rationing A’s is not improving education. It’s just making it harder for people to get the grades that they’re working hard for,” she said. Misra is also concerned about how grade caps might increase competition among students “especially in discussion-based seminars or classes with problem sets.” “I feel like people would not want to help each other out,” in contrast to the “very healthy, friendly environment,” Misra has observed in most of her Duke classes. Carnes echoed Misra. “Anything that discourages students from working together, I would have some reservations,” he said. Regardless, Misra is skeptical that Harvard’s grade cap will be permanent. She pointed to how Princeton University’s grade deflation policy, which had also placed a cap on A grades, was overturned in 2014 after faculty criticism. “Harvard is going to institute this ... student stress is going to skyrocket ... and then they’re gonna reverse it like Princeton did,” she said. A February survey found that 85% of Harvard undergraduates opposed a cap on A’s.

title: ‘An impossible deck of cards’: Durham City Council begins budget planning amid decrease in property tax revenue
author: Ella Moore
text: Durham City Manager Bo Ferguson presented the Fiscal Year 2026-27 budget amid what he described as “unprecedented” times at Monday’s Durham City Council meeting. For the first time in recent history, the council will construct a budget while contending with a decrease in property tax revenue from the previous year. Ferguson entirely attributed the loss to a “one-time factor”: major commercial properties successfully appealing their property taxes. As a result, the budget is set to contain a lot of tradeoffs, Mayor Leonardo Williams said, adding that the circumstances surrounding the budget are an “impossible deck of cards.” Despite the dip in property tax revenue, the council will not raise property taxes this year, and Ferguson expects revenue growth in future years. He emphasized that the city must practice “careful stewardship” while deciding funding allocations, given a smaller tax base, inflation and budget cuts. “This year demanded difficult choices ... but because of these choices, we are still able to deliver meaningful progress on the priorities that matter most,” Ferguson said. As Durham manages the decrease in property tax revenue, the North Carolina General Assembly might be making it harder for municipalities to raise property taxes. As part of a budget framework announced last week, legislators plan to send a constitutional amendment to voters in November asking them to permit the legislature to place a cap on local governments’ property tax rates. The total proposed budget is $766.1 million, an increase from $722.2 million last year. Ferguson’s budgeting strategies included cost-cutting, investments from community partners and direct financial commitments to social causes. During his presentation, Ferguson called on Duke to contribute to Durham’s work to reduce homelessness. Ferguson’s plan sees the Durham community jointly investing $13 million toward the effort in its first year, including $9 million in direct rental assistance. Ferguson asked community partners, including Durham County, Durham Continuum of Care and the University, to join the city’s $4.55 million year-one investment. According to an impact report by Urban Ministries of Durham, there has been a 300% increase in unsheltered homelessness in the city between 2018 and 2024. As of April 2026, the city’s Homelessness Data Snapshot reported over 1,400 people experiencing homelessness in Durham. “Our long-term goal is simple,” Ferguson said. “When someone becomes homeless in Durham, they obtain housing within 30 days.” Through the Duke HomeGrown initiative, Duke committed to creating down payment assistance for first-time homebuyers as a part of its plan to invest $203 million in Durham and the wider Triangle. The University’s initiative did not include specific tenets on homelessness, but Duke has reportedly created over 1,000 affordable housing units since 2000 and invested $22 million into home ownership initiatives supported by Habitat For Humanity, the Durham Housing Authority and other partners. Ferguson also noted plans to raise the minimum wage for city employees from $21.90 to $25.09 per hour. The increase of 14% reflects Durham’s largest minimum wage increase for city employees ever, according to Ferguson. Duke, the second-largest private employer in North Carolina and the largest employer in Durham, has also faced demands to raise its minimum wage to $25 an hour. On March 4, the University and Duke University Health System announced they would raise their minimum wage to $20 an hour beginning July 1. Neither has indicated plans for further raises. Ferguson presented several projects under Durham’s Capital Improvement Program, ranging from water and sewer projects to sidewalk repairs, as well as the addition of 16 positions to the fire department. The city will also maintain fare-free buses in the next fiscal year. On the cost-cutting front, Ferguson said that the city had removed nine positions, four of which were vacant, and that those affected had already been notified and would be assisted in finding new employment. The city also plans to redirect $927,000 in funding away from community partner organizations. “No one is going to be fully happy with this budget,” Williams said. “... We have a lot of work to do.” The council will hold budget work sessions May 27 and May 28 and then host a public hearing June 1. The council will make its final decision on the upcoming budget June 15.

title: NCGA budget agreement includes $208 million for Duke-UNC children’s hospital
author: Mia Taubenblat
text: Legislative leaders in the North Carolina General Assembly agreed last week to approve $208 million for a planned Duke Health and UNC Health children’s hospital in Apex, North Carolina. The agreement resolves one of many sticking points between the State House and State Senate that have prevented the legislature from passing a budget for over a year . The hospital funding agreement came as part of a broader budget framework announced by Republican Senate leader Phil Berger and Republican Speaker of the House Destin Hall. The hospital, North Carolina Children’s, will be the state’s only standalone children’s hospital, housing 500 beds, an ambulatory surgical center, a medical office building and a children’s behavioral center. According to a Duke Health press release , the project aims to “provide a higher volume of highly specialized pediatric care, enabling families from across the state to remain in North Carolina when seeking complex care.” “We are grateful to our state leaders for their continued support of North Carolina Children’s and our shared goal of creating a brighter, healthier future for our children,” wrote Duke Health officials in a Monday email to The Chronicle. “We continue to make progress toward opening the state’s first independent, dedicated children’s hospital, and remain on track to break ground in late 2027.” The state legislature allocated approximately $320 million to UNC Health in 2023 for the project. Construction is set to begin in 2027 and is expected to take around six years. The hospital is a $2 billion to $3 billion endeavor, with a significant portion of funds coming from fundraising efforts. Duke Health officials explained that large public-private projects like North Carolina Children’s typically evolve over multiple legislative cycles. The project will be funded over time through a combination of state and health system support, as well as philanthropy. In 2025, the State Senate reauthorized $103.5 million left unspent from the original 2023 grant, but the State House did not. Berger has been a major supporter of the hospital, with the Senate first proposing an additional $535.5 million for the project in their 2025 budget, an investment since abandoned. However, in the House, Hall previously opposed funding for the hospital, challenging its necessity given other pediatric care options throughout the state. He recently questioned whether it was worth building North Carolina Children’s instead of supporting rural hospitals and healthcare. The $208 million agreed on for North Carolina Children’s is being reallocated from another project, North Carolina Care, which aimed to build rural health centers. The project was authorized by the legislature in 2023 and was intended to be a partnership between UNC Health and East Carolina University’s health system, but it never made progress. The agreement announced by Berger and Hall last week also addressed other issues that had led to a budget impasse, mainly pay raises and taxes. Under the proposal, teacher starting salaries will rise to $48,000 a year from $41,000. Republican lawmakers also approved small one-time bonuses for teachers to compensate for a lack of pay increases over the past year. On average, annual teacher salaries will increase by 8%. State law enforcement officers will receive a minimum raise of 13%, with a 20.3% raise for Alcohol Law Enforcement officers, and state employees will receive a 3% raise. Democratic Gov. Josh Stein applauded the planned salary increases, but wrote in a press release that “the proof, however, will be in the pudding.” Hall and Berger also announced that the individual income tax rate will drop from 3.99% for the 2026 tax year to 3.49% for the 2027 tax year. The 3.49% rate will remain constant through the 2029 tax year, then reduce to 3.24% for the 2030, 2031 and 2032 tax years and decrease again to 2.99% for 2033 and 2034. After that, tax cut triggers — which lower individual income tax rates when revenue benchmarks are met — will return . The use of tax cut triggers was a central area of dispute between the State House and State Senate, with Berger favoring the trigger model and Hall opposing it. The budget framework also included a proposal to place two constitutional amendments on the ballot in November to determine the future of individual income tax rates and local property tax rates. The first amendment would cap the individual income tax rate at a maximum of 3.5%, half of the current cap of 7%, which voters approved in 2018. The second amendment voters will consider would grant the legislature the authority to cap local governments’ property tax rates. Two other constitutional amendments will appear on November ballots separate from the budget agreement, asking voters to codify the “right to farm” and the “right to work.” “Both chambers want to deliver additional tax relief to the citizens of North Carolina,” Berger said during the press conference. The future of the budget, though more promising, still remains uncertain. During their press conference, Hall and Berger said they expect a vote on a budget bill as early as mid-June. However, none of the agreements are yet in writing. Hall acknowledged that there are still outstanding issues to be resolved, but maintained during the press conference that he’s “confident that with these big issues largely behind us, the rest of that stuff will be able to [be] dealt with by chairs.” Compounding the challenges, constitutional amendments require a three-fifths majority in both the State House and the State Senate before they can reach the ballot. In the House, Republican lawmakers need the vote of at least one Democrat or unaffiliated representative to reach that supermajority. Some Democrats, including Stein and House minority leader Robert Reives, voiced disapproval of the individual income tax rate cap, with Stein calling it a “financial straightjacket” in a press release . Hall postponed planned votes on the amendments Tuesday. Stein does not have the power to veto a constitutional amendment, but he does have the power to veto the budget. The legislature can override a gubernatorial veto with a three-fifths supermajority in both chambers, but again, Republicans would need a crossover vote in the State House.

title: Meet Sophi Balerdi, the latest Duke alumnus on CBS’ ‘Survivor’
author: Sarah Diaz
text: Although dozens of students spend their spring semester surviving finals, early wake-ups and enduring chilly temperatures while tenting for Duke’s basketball game against North Carolina, survival has taken a more literal form for a select few alumni. In Duke’s history, six graduates have competed on CBS’ “Survivor,” which challenges contestants to be the last “survivor” standing while living in the wilderness, scavenging for food and ensuring their fellow contestants do not vote them off the island. These Duke “survivors” include Kelly (Goldsmith) Shriner, Trinity '01; Keith Sowell, Trinity ‘20; Molly Byman, Law School ‘20; Daniel Strunk, Trinity '14; Jesse Lopez, Graduate School ‘22; and Sophi Balerdi, Fuqua ‘21. Balerdi, the most recent alumni to compete on “Survivor,” placed second on Season 49. In anticipation of Wednesday’s Season 50 finale, The Chronicle sat down with Balerdi to hear about her “Survivor” journey. An avid viewer of the show since she was 7 years old, going on “Survivor” was a lifelong dream for Balerdi. Balerdi first began watching “Survivor” with her grandmother, a Cuban immigrant, who used the show to learn English. When Balerdi grew up, she wanted to give her grandmother a “special season”: one featuring her granddaughter. Unfortunately, Balerdi’s grandmother died a few years before she ended up going on the show. “I still made it my mission to go out there, because I felt like it would be a really cool experience of trying to connect with her,” Balerdi said. After making it onto the show, Balerdi said she felt like her grandmother was with her the whole season. When Balerdi was cast, it felt like “winning the lottery,” with selection being largely dependent on how directors believed contestants would interact with one another. In her application and interviews, she discussed her culture and told the story about her grandmother, which she thinks helped her stand out from other potential contestants. Going into the show, Balerdi did not expect to place second; she cared more about saying she “met Jeff Probst.” But, she soon realized that she had potential to go further than she initially thought. During the first few weeks of the season, players are divided into tribes, which must perform well in competitions to avoid going to tribal council and voting one of their members out. Balerdi started out on what was dubbed “the disaster tribe,” which was sent to tribal council every week at the beginning of the season. Acknowledging her inability to make significant physical contributions, Balerdi decided she had to adopt a different strategy. “I knew I was going to have to leverage my ability to connect with people,” she said. “... I was able to show them that loyalty and connection with me was more valuable than any weight that I was not pulling in challenges.” Balerdi believes that her experience at the Fuqua School of Business helped teach her how to build these strong connections. Growing up in a Cuban family in Miami, she had been surrounded by people of similar backgrounds for most of her life. However, coming to Duke was her “first experience outside of the Cuban Miami bubble,” allowing her to develop friendships with people from places all over the world. As the “only Hispanic person” in Season 49, Balerdi needed to connect with people of many different cultures. “I don't think I would have done so well had I not had my Duke experience,” she said. After being swapped to a different tribe, Balerdi needed to establish relationships with new players. It was in this second tribe that she found her allies Savannah Louie and Rizo Velovic, who she would work with for the rest of the season. From navigating her “disaster tribe” to maintaining her trio alliance, Balerdi described her “Survivor” experience as “playing from the bottom” — which in her opinion was much more fun. “You have less to lose,” she said, “and we just try to cause as much turmoil as possible for the other side.” Ultimately, the trio made it to the final four, with Louie winning the season. Looking back at her season, Balerdi highlighted her friendship with Velovic, who was characterized as a major strategic force in the show. Although she felt that her “Survivor” story was presented accurately on television, she wished they showed her relationship with Velovic to a greater extent, explaining that many times she and Velovic worked “hand in hand together.” As a whole, these friendships with Louie and Velovic resulted in Balerdi’s most treasured memories from her “Survivor” experience. She reminisced on a reward her trio received, spending a luxurious night away from their tribe with an unlimited amount of Mexican food. “It felt like we were at a sleepover with our friends,” she said. “... We were just talking in the complete darkness about real life. ... It was such a wholesome fun night for us as friends that us three will probably never forget.” Following the season’s end, Balerdi’s friendships have only grown as she has deepened her connections with Louie and Velovic, while also meeting alumni from other seasons. In particular, she’s become close with Dee Valladares, a fellow Miami resident and the Season 45 winner. All three of Balerdi’s friends returned to play again on the 50th season of “Survivor.” Balerdi’s pick to win the season, Emily Flippen, was eliminated in the May 6 episode, and Louie and Valladares were also eliminated. But, Balerdi is still cheering on Velovic as the season enters its finale. Editor's note: This story was updated Tuesday morning to correctly reflect that six Duke graduates have competed on "Survivor," not four. The Chronicle regrets the error.

title: After Duke revokes ‘lifetime’ email promise, alumni scramble to untangle online accounts
author: Claire Cranford
text: For nearly three decades, Duke offered its graduates what it called a “lifetime” benefit: a permanent email forwarding address, @alumni.duke.edu, that would follow them wherever they went. It wasn't an inbox, but a redirect. Any email sent to the address would automatically bounce to whatever personal account the alumnus had on file, which they could update at any time. If they changed jobs, cities or email providers, the @alumni.duke.edu would stay the same. Alumni built their professional and personal lives around it accordingly. Then, a little over two weeks ago, the University announced it was ending the service. According to Tracey Temne, Duke’s associate vice president for marketing, communications and stewardship, the decision was made after “careful consideration” and to “ensure we are providing the best services to our alumni.” Alumni were given until July 31 to transition away from the email addresses. In some cases that was an address they had used as their sole professional contact since the late 1990s. Alumni flooded university inboxes and the Duke subreddit with complaints, prompting Duke to extend the deadline to Jan. 31, 2027.
