Temne said that the extension was given “to allow alumni ample time for this transition.” But for some of those alumni, the extension missed the point entirely. “I'm grateful the feedback was heard. But more time to accept a broken promise is not a resolution,” wrote Aaron Roberts, D.Phil ‘15 and M.A. ‘16, in an email to Duke administrators and The Chronicle. Bill Howell received his alumni email forwarding address in December 1999 after completing his Masters in Business Administration at the Fuqua School of Business. “Lifetime is permanent. End of discussion. You made a commitment to thousands of thousands of students,” Howell said. Howell used his Duke alumni email as his primary address for nearly 27 years. It is tied to every organization he has joined, every subscription he has held and every professional contact he has made. Since the announcement, he said, he has been discovering roughly 10 new accounts per day that he now has to change, not including years-old contacts he may never be able to update. “I don't even know how to track down all the places where it's been used,” he said. Juan Batlle, Trinity ‘98, had a similar experience. He used the address through successive institutional affiliations — the National Institutes of Health, medical school, residency training in Boston — precisely because it would be stable when everything else was changing. “An email forwarding address was like a lifeline,” Batlle said, comparing it to the era before cell phone number portability, when changing carriers meant changing numbers. He listed the accounts tied to his Duke address: scientific journals where he has published papers, his Apple ID, bank accounts and even alumni-interviewing correspondence, for which his Duke email helps assure students that a real alum is contacting them. “I don't even know how many there are, because there's no central way to keep track of them,” Batlle said. For many alumni, the stakes of this decision go beyond inconvenience. Laurel Coons, Graduate School ‘17, called the alumni mailboxes a “public identity endpoint” in a formal letter to University leadership. Scientific publications, legal records, resumes, archived databases, healthcare portals, financial accounts and password recovery systems routinely carry these alumni email addresses as permanent points of contact. Once the address goes dark, alumni may never know about missed publication inquiries, legal notices or financial alerts from contacts who have no way of knowing the address is no longer valid. “That harm is not fully fixable after the fact,” she wrote. According to Duke Alumni’s FAQ page on the email change, the word “lifetime” did not guarantee it would be offered in perpetuity. “While described as a ‘lifetime’ benefit, the service has always been subject to University policies, technological feasibility and security requirements, which evolve over time,” the FAQ reads. The University’s explanation for discontinuing the service centers on deliverability and security. According to the FAQ, external email providers have adopted increasingly restrictive forwarding policies, causing messages to be rejected unpredictably and impeding reliability. But some alumni are skeptical. Howell, who served as the Chief Information Officer or in top information technology positions for organizations including the RAND Corporation, Integer Holdings, Eli Lilly and Aurora Health, said the security argument has validity but does not entirely hold up. Roberts named a specific fix in his letter to Duke. Cloudflare Email Routing, he wrote, “provides fully authenticated, SPF/DKIM/DMARC-compliant email forwarding at no cost per user,” referencing three core protocols used to authenticate email communications. Alternatively, Batlle proposed that the alumni association could acquire the domain and attach it to a Gmail Workspace, where Google assumes the security burden and individual alumni pay a small annual fee. Duke did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment on these alternatives. For Howell and many other alumni, the revocation of the email felt like the University going back on its word to alumni. The University has adopted the slogan “Forever Duke” as a statement of its ongoing commitment to alumni, and the announcement of the email discontinuation was signed off with the same phrase. “The irony is just painful,” Howell said. “The lifetime email is going away. Just remember, we're ‘Duke forever!’” He also said the episode has shaken his confidence in Duke’s institutional commitments more broadly, including its commitment to donors. Howell said he has removed Duke from his will as a result of the decision. On Reddit, other alumni have publicly pledged to withhold donations. Even modest amounts, Howell noted, would likely exceed whatever the University is saving by discontinuing the service. The Chronicle reached out to Duke's Office of Information Technology, Alumni Engagement and Development and University leadership with specific questions, including the number of alumni affected, whether cost was a factor in the decision and whether alternative forwarding solutions had been evaluated. Duke's response did not address those questions.

title: Students reflect on free speech, antisemitism concerns after suspension, reinstatement of Duke SJP chapter
author: Sarah Diaz
text: Duke’s Students for Justice in Palestine chapter recently made national headlines after an Instagram post by the group led to a monthlong suspension and subsequent reinstatement by the University. For Duke students, the SJP saga resulted in a mix of relief and frustration, with students expressing concerns about the state of free speech and antisemitism on campus. Student Affairs first suspended SJP on March 24 after the Office for Institutional Equity received student complaints about an Instagram post promoting a general body meeting. The post featured an illustration by artist Emory Douglas, originally published in the newspaper The Black Panther in 1970, depicting two pigs — one labeled "U.S. Imperialism" holding the torch of liberty and the other labeled "Zionism" holding the Star of David. Almost a month later, OIE determined that the post did not violate the University’s Policy on Prohibited Discrimination, Harassment and Related Misconduct; Student Affairs restored the organization’s status the following day. Some students criticized the University’s handling of the issue, arguing that the initial suspension was an intrusion on free expression. Junior Shreya Karumuri said that although the University “boasts this institutional neutrality,” it failed to uphold that standard by “condemning anti-Zionist speech, but not condemning anti-Palestinian speech.” The University has not yet made an explicit, public commitment to institutional neutrality despite faculty and students showing varying levels of support for Duke to do so. Karumuri pointed to actor and comedian Jerry Seinfeld’s comparison of the “Free Palestine” movement to the Ku Klux Klan at an on-campus event last September as an example of the University’s inequitable handling of situations regarding controversial speech. A University spokesperson said in a statement at the time that “the invitation of speakers to campus does not imply any endorsement of their remarks.” “But then somebody makes an Instagram post that's anti-Zionist and then the organization is suspended immediately,” Karumuri said. Junior Angela Zhao, co-president of Duke’s Climate Coalition, said the suspension and reinstatement of SJP is best thought of as part of a broader restriction on student activism given the University’s current “precarious situation ... with the Trump administration.” She discussed how activist groups have to follow a “two-week protest registration protocol,” requiring student groups to treat protests like any other campus event and register with the University at least two weeks before the planned date, under Duke’s Pickets, Protests and Demonstrations policy . Zhao described this as a “major roadblock” for her club and in line with a broader “surveillance” that Duke is exerting over student organizations like SJP. Duke’s PPD policy has received scrutiny from students for allocating certain student groups “different (or less desirable spaces)” for demonstrations compared to others. Last month, a faculty committee called for an overhaul of the PPD policy altogether. “I do think there's a little bit of administrative overreach in those aspects,” Zhao said. “While I do understand the purpose of protecting Duke as a university publicly, I think it’s led to a lot less student activism on campus.” In contrast, junior Noah Hamid said that he believes the University made the standard “procedural decision” to temporarily suspend SJP while determining if the post warranted investigation. Although he does not personally agree with Duke’s final verdict to reinstate the group, Hamid believes that SJP’s temporary suspension and subsequent reinstatement should be “celebrated as Duke committing themselves to ... freedom of expression and standards that they’ve clearly set out.” Some Jewish students raised concerns with the way the University justified its reinstatement of SJP. Sophomore Galit Moff said that “by removing the suspension, [Duke is] essentially condoning [SJP’s] behavior.” Moff referenced an April 23 letter addressed to SJP leadership from Sharon Gooding, associate vice president for institutional equity, equal opportunity and compliance, to criticize Duke’s handling of the matter. In the letter, Gooding explained that although SJP’s Instagram post “alludes to antisemitic tropes,” there was “insufficient information to support the existence of a hostile education environment.” Gooding was referring to Duke’s Policy on Prohibited Discrimination, Harassment and Related Misconduct, under which OIE was assessing whether SJP should be investigated for “Hostile Environment Harassment” allegations. Moff disagreed with the idea that “support[ing] the existence of a hostile educational environment” should be the standard for a group not to receive financial backing from the University. “Just because I'm capable of continuing to go to class ... after this post doesn't mean that it's not problematic and should be supported and funded,” she said. Sophomore Sam Steiner did not take issue with the reinstatement of SJP, but he shared similar concerns to Moff about how Duke justified the organization’s return. “If they had said ‘You know what, we actually don't have the right to kick this club right now,’ I would have ... just moved on,” Steiner said. However, he added that “putting out a statement that says alluding to antisemitic tropes doesn't break University policy is a bad way of phrasing things and opens the door for stuff to go downhill.” To Abi Human, a graduate student in the Nicholas School of Environment and president of Duke’s Jewish Solidarity Movement, it was important to distinguish between “being unsafe and being uncomfortable.” Unlike Moff, she felt the post did not present real safety concerns for Jewish students. “Of course it didn't. It was an Instagram [post],” Human said. Duke has substantially changed its institutional equity policies since President Donald Trump took office in 2025, finalizing its set of “inclusive excellence” principles only in December. The new principles coupled with the potential overhaul of the PPD policy leaves the University with steps ahead in how it approaches student activism and speech on campus.

title: Funding cuts gutted the Duke Center for International Development. Now, its director is stepping down
author: Seyun Park
text: Over the course of a year, Duke’s Center for International Development shrank from nearly a dozen full-time faculty and staff to just one. Soon, it will have zero. After the defunding and closure of the U.S. Agency for International Development, compounded with Duke’s own cost-cutting campaign, DCID’s future has become deeply uncertain. As Director Edmund Malesky steps down next week, the center has no clear plan forward. Malesky told The Chronicle he does not know who the next director might be — or if there will even be one. “If there wasn't a commitment here and there weren’t the resources to have a Center for International Development, [then] this is time for me to think about doing other things with my career,” Malesky said, explaining his decision to step down. Now housed in the Sanford School of Public Policy, DCID was established in 1985. For decades, it focused on advancing sustainable development practices across the world through research, education and advocacy. But then, USAID — which funded around two-thirds of the center’s research projects according to Malesky — became one of the first targets of President Donald Trump’s administration. By July 2025, USAID, formerly the world’s largest foreign aid agency, ceased operations entirely. Although Malesky was aware of the Trump administration’s skepticism toward USAID, he was still “shocked” by the federal “animosity” directed at the agency and the speed at which it was dismantled. Some of the DCID projects that USAID funded included quantifying the effects of climate change on internationally displaced peoples and examining political will in anti-corruption efforts. It also helped fund a long-standing and influential project in Vietnam that generated significant economic reform in the country, though the project has since attained private funding. Then, the Duke cuts hit. In March 2025, the University announced its strategic realignment plans, aiming to cut costs in response to a changing federal landscape. Only a few weeks later, The Chronicle reported that DCID had “paused” its operations. Throughout the summer, the center’s scope continued to diminish; faculty and staff who had been with DCID for decades left the center, and most others were moved out and into Sanford’s main organizational structure. By the end of the summer, DCID’s roster dropped from 11 to two full-time staff: Malesky and Executive Director Jonathan Abels. However, Abels soon started another job in August 2025 as managing director of the Duke-UNC Rotary Peace Center, a jointly run program with the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. Although Abels is still listed on DCID’s website as executive director, he now only spends about 20% of his time on DCID work, according to Malesky. In a May 12 email to The Chronicle, Sanford leadership confirmed that DCID “does not have any full-time staff dedicated exclusively to the center.” It said that staff positions were consolidated “consistent with the University’s strategic realignment goals and priorities.” Abels did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment. With the staffing shortages and lack of funding, Malesky said that the March 2025 operational pause never really ended. “It's more complicated than a pause in that we continued to work on the private projects ... but we were doing it on a much smaller staff, and we weren't trying to expand or organize,” Malesky said. “We were basically moving to sunset.” In his 2025 annual report , Malesky wrote that the cuts “have set back decades of progress, threatening the lives and livelihoods of people around the world.” “We lost so much real expertise on how to deal with global disasters, humanitarian relief, post-conflict,” Malesky said. “ ... The people still exist. The human capital is there, but it's not in an organized way that it can be channeled for solving the problems in the way it could before.” Malesky wrote in his 2025 report that he was “confident DCID will emerge from the sawdust of Trump 2025 to continue its mission.” But in April, he was less confident, reflecting on the tall task of returning the center to its former stature. “At this point, given the government funding environment for aid, it would require significant mobilization on Duke's part to raise private funds,” Malesky said. Sanford leadership did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment about whether it plans to fundraise for the center or hire a new director. As Sanford undergoes a leadership change July 1, with JR DeShazo starting his tenure as the Joel L. Fleishman Dean of the Sanford School, Malesky is cautiously optimistic about how the new dean will handle DCID. DeShazo earned a Master's in Development Economics from the University of Oxford and served as a faculty associate at Harvard University’s Institute for International Development. “Things are up in the air a little bit, because there's gonna be a new dean of Sanford ... He might see value in DCID to keep it around,” Malesky said, although he acknowledged he had not spoken with DeShazo. Sanford leadership wrote that “it would be premature to comment on [DeShazo's] goals or priorities." They added that “Sanford remains committed to supporting global development through research, teaching and engagement,” writing that more than 15 Sanford faculty are working on international research projects. Some of DCID’s research will continue, even if the actual center shuts down. DCID’s Southeast Asia Research Group recently acquired a three-year private grant from the Luce Foundation to continue its scholarship. The center is also still a de facto home for Sanford’s Master of International Development Policy, the Duke-UNC Rotary Peace Center and other programs. Malesky hopes that if DCID does have a future in Sanford, it focuses more on large problems such as climate change, artificial intelligence or democratic backsliding, while also adopting a different funding mechanism. “I would want Duke to provide seed money and then have groups of interdisciplinary professors on campus compete for that seed money,” Malesky said. “ ... Instead of letting the aid community tell me what we want to work on, I would let it organically come up from Duke.” As Malesky prepares to depart DCID — with a “lot more gray hair” than when he started — he will oversee one last center event: the Evidence for Governance and Politics Workshop. From May 14 to May 16, scholars will come to Duke’s campus to discuss their research on international development. The DCID website lists the workshop as its only upcoming event.

title: ‘Our Super Bowl’: Durham restaurants reflect on commencement weekend
author: Lila Cohen
text: For Duke graduates, commencement signifies the end of four years of hard work and the start of an exciting new chapter. For the more than 7,700 hospitality workers who call Durham home, graduation brings an influx of business and tourism amid a vibrant, packed weekend. The Chronicle spoke to some of the people behind Duke students’ favorite Durham restaurants to see how the weekend went for them. Last year, commencement weekend was Durham’s fourth-highest visitation weekend, an uptick of 27% from average weekend tourism, according to Jada Harkins Andrews, senior manager of public affairs at Discover Durham. In particular, commencement weekend draws large numbers of out-of-state tourists, Harkins Andrews wrote. As a result, business typically doubles over commencement weekend at restaurants Parizade and Nikos according to Karina Stong, wine director and assistant general manager of Parizade, and Douglas Rodriguez, culinary development officer of Nikos. Jimmy Kim, Trinity ‘04 and Fuqua ‘12, who owns three restaurants in the Raleigh-Durham area — Cucciolo Osteria, Cucciolo Terraza and Cucciolo Famiglia — agreed. For him, graduation weekend is the biggest weekend of the year for his businesses. Parizade served 700 people last Sunday as families celebrated graduation and Mother’s Day, with Nikos serving 700 on Saturday and Cucciolo Osteria serving around 180 to 230 guests each night. To prepare, Cucciolo restaurants started taking reservations for commencement weekend at 9 a.m. on March 1 via email. Just two minutes later, their reservations for commencement weekend were full. For Nikos and Parizade, planning for next year’s commencement weekend starts as soon as graduation ends. Parizade, Stong shared, is mostly booked six to eight weeks before commencement. But she has even received calls up to a year in advance for reservations, as has Nikos. Right after commencement, the Parizade team debriefs on what worked and what did not, with a focus on ensuring next year runs even tighter and more polished to meet customers’ needs. As the year goes on, the team focuses on planning menus, training staff and stocking supplies in preparation for commencement. “You can’t come into this weekend surprised or unprepared,” Stong said. She added that her fellow staff member, Parizade General Manager Igor Gacina, refers to the weekend as “our Super Bowl.” However, with its influx of tourists, commencement weekend also brings its share of challenges. Rodriguez and Stong both reported enduring 16-hour days last weekend, with Stong working from 8 a.m. to 12 a.m. Difficult customers are also more common. Rodriguez can typically count on one hand the number of times per month Nikos has a difficult customer, since he said Durham residents are usually polite. However, for a handful of out-of-town visitors during commencement weekend, “nothing was good enough,” Rodriguez said. Kim shared a similar sentiment, noting that the weekend brings more demanding guests than usual. “We do what we can to the best of our ability,” Kim said. Customers also see significantly increased prices at some Durham restaurants during commencement weekend. Giorgio’s Hospitality Group — which owns 18 restaurants in North Carolina, including Nikos, Parizade, Local 22 Kitchen & Bar and the Nasher Museum Cafe — marks up prices by about 25 to 30% over commencement weekend, according to Rodriguez. Despite the challenges, commencement weekend remains special for Durham restaurants, as they get to share in the joy of graduates and their families. Kim described the weekend as “vibrant” and “gratifying”, noting that the Cucciolo team celebrates their hard work after the Sunday evening shift is over. For Stong, the biggest takeaway from the weekend is how connected students are to the local food scene during their time at Duke. “We get to be part of these really special core memories for students the rest of their lives,” Stong said.

title: Instructure strikes agreement with hackers after Canvas breach hits Duke, thousands of other schools
author: Ella Moore
text: On Monday evening, Instructure — the parent company of the online learning platform Canvas — “reached an agreement” with criminal extortion group ShinyHunters after the group breached the company and threatened to leak stolen information from thousands of schools, including Duke. As part of the agreement, Instructure said they retrieved the stolen data and received “digital confirmation of data destruction” through shred logs. They also said that ShinyHunters informed them that no customers would be “extorted” because of the breach. It is not immediately clear what Instructure provided in return, if anything. On its status page , Instructure said Canvas is fully operational and safe to use, and users have no need to interact with the “unauthorized actor.” Allison Nixon, chief research officer at the New York City-based security consultancy Unit 221B, argued that companies should not pay ShinyHunters in exchange for stolen data, saying that while they have proven capable in communicating effective threats, the group has not always followed through when an agreement has been reached. ShinyHunters first claimed on May 3 that it had breached Instructure and obtained the information of 275 million students and teachers, consisting of “certain identifying information of users ... such as names, email addresses, and student ID numbers, as well as messages among users,” per Instructure’s status page. Instructure said in the Monday evening statement that “core learning data” was not breached. The Duke Information Technology Security Office confirmed May 6 that Duke was among the 9,000 institutions affected by the incident in an email to all faculty and students. Then, in a May 12 email to all faculty and students, Chief Information Security Officer Nick Tripp wrote that the IT Security Office had since "reviewed an interim digital forensics report" authored by a third-party security firm, which "acknowledged" the circumstances leading to the incident and confirmed that ShinyHunters no longer had access to Instructure. Tripp wrote that his office also has "credible evidence" that affected data included names, contact information, Canvas internal usernames, course and enrollment information, course-related data and academic term information. At the time, there is no evidence that grade information was impacted by the breach. In the meantime, Tripp wrote that Duke "has reinforced its digital security" for Canvas integration with other services and continues to monitor the situation closely, promising to share further updates when they become available. He cited guidance from the University's privacy office which says that while students, faculty and staff can continue to use Canvas, they should exercise "serious caution" when messaging or including documentation on the platform that involves sensitive information, such as health details, addresses or dates of birth. Duke OIT has since taken the Canvas incident off of its “active incidents” page . Most Duke students are not currently using Canvas, as undergraduate final examinations concluded May 2, and Summer Term I does not begin until May 13. Instructure reported that the hackers exploited a vulnerability within its “Free For Teacher” environment on Canvas. The company has since disabled the tool — which allows users to create courses even when their institution is not a Canvas customer — pending a full security review. “We know that's disruptive, and we didn't make that call lightly,” Instructure CEO Steve Daly wrote on the company's status page . “But keeping the entire Canvas platform secure has to come first.” Daly also apologized to users affected by the breach and said the company “got the balance wrong” in choosing to focus on fact-finding rather than consistent updates. In response, he said Canvas has now launched a dedicated “Incident Update page” that shares information on active incidents as soon as it is available. “Rebuilding trust takes time,” Daly wrote. “We’re going to earn it back through consistent action and honest communication.” Instructure also announced that the company’s leadership will host a webinar May 13 to provide more information about the cyberattack and the next steps to “harden the system.” ShinyHunters has taken credit for breaching third-party education-technology vendors in past attacks. Earlier this spring, ShinyHunters claimed to have breached Infinite Campus — a K-12 student information system — and the publisher McGraw Hill. Infinite Campus refused to pay the ransom demanded by ShinyHunters. McGraw Hill did not provide public communication on whether they reached an agreement with the group or paid a ransom. In October, the hackers also claimed to have stolen over a billion customer records across dozens of companies through a breach of Salesforce, which contracts with Instructure and McGraw Hill. Editor's note: This story was updated Tuesday evening to include information from an email sent to all Duke students and faculty regarding the incident.

title: A look inside the Lilly Library renovation
author: Seyun Park
text: After two years of demolition, construction and refurbishment, the Lilly Library renovation has reached its home stretch. Despite original projections that the East Campus library could open in summer 2026, Duke officials now predict the reopening could be delayed to as late as the spring semester of 2027. The $64 million project, which started in May 2024 , now boasts a fully built-out addition to the library’s west side, making the new building 56,300 square feet — 75% larger than its pre-renovation size. The new library will introduce additional collaborative workspaces, an Innovation CoLab space and a cafe with an outdoor terrace. The Chronicle was invited on a tour of the unfinished library, led by Project Manager Eddie Hull and Kelley Lawton, head of East Campus libraries. “The original building was very much designed for solo scholarship study,” Lawton said. “In this building, we're retaining that, but we're also gaining ... a lot more variety of spaces than we had before.” Accessing the new Lilly Library will be much easier for first-years, thanks to two new entrances. One, on the west side of the building closest to Brodie Recreation Center, leads from the outdoor terrace directly into the new cafe, while another on the southwest corner connects to the “backyard” area of East Campus. The original entrance, facing Baldwin Quad, opens into the new atrium. The skylights above the atrium were refurbished during the project, but not replaced. Many other elements of the original Lilly Library, first constructed in 1927, were also preserved, including the original windows in the Few and Carpenter Reading Rooms. “We really wanted to try and retain as much of the original as we could, if it was structurally sound,” Hull said. “There’s certainly a magic about being able to say, ‘This is original.’” The Few and Carpenter Reading Rooms had their ceilings refurbished during the project and will soon be restored to their original Gothic design. The entrance area will also include a space for the Thompson Writing Studio, which currently has locations in Perkins Library and the Bivens Building. The library’s cafe will be located behind the atrium and main service desk, in the new part of the building. Plans envision the cafe as similar to Perkins’ Von der Heyden Pavilion, along with outdoor seating for 70. Visitors in search of physical books will no longer find them in Lilly Library’s familiar central stacks — four floors of steel bookshelves squeezed into a three-story building. Instead, the basement of the building will house around 150,000 volumes on new rolling compact shelves. The basement of the library will also contain many of the 16 new collaborative group study spaces, a classroom and a new testing center for students with accommodations. Upstairs, the historic Thomas Reading Room, which features much of the library’s Asian art collection, will also be restored to its pre-renovation look. A new balcony will connect Thomas to the other second-floor reading room. In addition, a 75-seat assembly space overlooking the terrace quad will host lectures, seminars and other events. In total, the expansion and renovation will allow the library to seat 635 people, an increase from 444 pre-renovation. According to Hull, Lilly will serve as a “destination” for students on East Campus. Much of the exterior and shell of the library is now complete, with installation of solar shades on the new glass facade forthcoming. Inside, however, much remains to be done. Hull listed painting the ceilings, finishing the flooring and installing the HVAC system, audio/visual wiring, shelving and more as a sampling of work left to be completed. “You can't do the work I've just described without getting the shell taken care of,” Hull said. “The shell is done. Now, it's just building in the interior to make sure that it's all working right.” The region that will become the cafe terrace surrounding Lilly currently serves as a construction staging area. The tennis and pickleball courts located behind Lilly also recently closed, with the installation of new tennis courts set to begin this summer. Hull said the University aims to receive its Certificate of Occupancy for the space in mid-September, which would signify the completion of true construction work. The next few months before opening would then be used to move in furniture and collections as staff settle into the building. Despite the prolonged timeline, which Hull and Lawton attribute to general construction delays, the duo stressed that the library will be open sometime in the next academic year. Lawton floated the idea that the library could unofficially open for the December reading period before its inauguration in January 2027; however, she said a hard reopening date would depend on construction progress this summer and early fall. Until the library reopens, first-years can continue accessing library resources through the Bishop’s House and Music Library on East Campus.

title: ‘All the steps matter’: Microsoft CFO Amy Hood embraces nonlinear growth in commencement address
author: Abby DiSalvo
text: Microsoft Chief Financial Officer Amy Hood, Trinity ‘94, highlighted the power of adaptability and community in her Sunday commencement address to the Class of 2026. Hood became Microsoft’s first female CFO in 2013 and has more than tripled the company’s revenue in her tenure. President Vincent Price introduced her to a packed Wallace Wade Stadium as a "thoughtful, visionary leader.” But when Hood stood behind a podium to address the thousands of graduates sitting before her, she did not attempt to offer a foolproof path to success. She instead acknowledged the uncertainty that comes with starting a new chapter. “Successful careers are rarely, if ever, a straight line,” Hood said. “... All the steps matter, and it took me decades to figure that out.” When she arrived at Duke, Hood planned to be a biology major, as she was “among the few” who loved organic chemistry. However, by her sophomore year, Hood dropped her pre-med plans and decided to major in economics — not because she loved Keynesian theory, she recounted, but because the economics major required the fewest additional credits to graduate on time. She went on to intern at Goldman Sachs in New York, attend Harvard Business School and return to Goldman Sachs in San Francisco, but she admitted that the path still “didn’t feel right.” So, Hood gave up her job for an internship with the National Park Service, only to end up stamping souvenir booklets at Alcatraz Island, home of the former federal penitentiary. She quit after one day. When she finally landed a job at Microsoft — a company from which she had twice been rejected — Hood missed her first day of work. She had forgotten she had to drive through Oregon on her way from California to Washington, and travel took much longer than anticipated. “Your next step doesn't have to be a perfect one,” Hood said, reflecting on her own journey. “It just has to be an opportunity. Even the ones that now seem silly built confidence or taught me a lesson.” Only a few decades later, Hood was listed No. 16 on Forbes’ 2025 list of the 100 most powerful women in the world. As Microsoft’s Executive Vice President and CFO, she oversaw billion-dollar investments in data centers, championed a path to carbon negativity and spearheaded three major acquisitions: LinkedIn, GitHub and Activision Blizzard. To celebrate her leadership, Duke awarded her an honorary Doctor of Humane Letters degree at the commencement ceremony. Hood said she hoped graduates would lean on the same resiliency that her undergraduate education provided her back in the early ‘90s. Upon reflection, the challenges she overcame at Duke prepared her for eventual success. “The thing that got me through the growing pains wasn't a plan,” Hood said. “It was the ability to keep learning and grinding it out when the plan stopped working, and that started here, in this place.” Hood emphasized that the Class of 2026 already had the “superpower” of adaptability, having survived the COVID-19 pandemic in high school, constantly evolving technology and many difficult classes. She also noted that redirection, although frightening, often opens new doors. “I can't believe I’m saying this to some of the most accomplished young people on the planet, but maybe lower your bar a little,” Hood said. “Be open that something may not be exactly right, but could turn out to be a perfect place to learn about yourself.” Hood then urged graduates to appreciate the community around them. She called her first-year orientation “the best networking event” she ever attended and met her husband, former Duke football player Max Kleinman, Trinity ‘93, as an undergraduate. “These people sitting next to you, the ones who pulled all-nighters, who made you laugh when everything felt impossible, this is your community,” Hood said. “Invest in that. Celebrate them. Be there.” In honor of Mother’s Day, she concluded her address with a final word of advice from her mother, who offered reassurance in tough times with the simple acknowledgement that Hood was “plenty smart.” It was meant as a token of belief, which Hood extended to the graduates before her. “Class of 2026, you are certainly plenty smart,” Hood said. “You'll figure it out one messy step at a time, and trust me, the mess is where all the good stuff happens.”

title: ‘A chance to shape what comes next’: Juliet Peñaranda Pérez and Lucas Bellaiche open Commencement 2026
author: Dylan Halper
text: Trinity senior Juliet Peñaranda Pérez and doctoral candidate Lucas Bellaiche delivered welcoming remarks at the 2026 Commencement ceremony Sunday, encouraging graduates to embrace their differences and reflect on the bonds they forged at Duke. “Class of 2026, this degree is more than a credential,” Peñaranda Perez said. “It is a chance to shape what comes next.” Peñaranda Pérez, the undergraduate speaker, earned a Bachelor of Science in biology with minors in chemistry and global health. She also served as a research assistant in the Hartman Lab and was a Mayo Clinic UPS Scholar. Bellaiche, the graduate speaker, leaves the University with a doctorate in psychology and neuroscience. At Duke, he researched the relationship between imagination and mental health, specifically how creative thinking might benefit emotional regulation. During his time at the University, he also received the Dean’s Graduate Fellowship, James B. Duke Fellowship and a National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship. Peñaranda Pérez began her remarks highlighting her background as a first-generation college student from Puerto Rico. She expressed gratitude for her family, particularly her grandmother, who came to Durham for Commencement on her first flight in over 20 years and “her first weekend off in even longer.” “I stand here as someone who didn't get here alone ... [my family] ensured that while their journey was uphill, mine could be limitless,” Peñaranda Pérez said. Peñaranda Pérez then turned to the idea that, although the Class of 2026 “didn't all start from the same place” and came from a variety of backgrounds, the Class learned how to move forward together. “In a time that feels so divided, we can be the ones who make room: room for different stories, voices, cultures, different ways of seeing the world,” Peñaranda Pérez said. “Because what we build next should not just reflect where we came from, but who we made room for along the way.” Bellaiche began his speech acknowledging the unique nature of the occasion for some graduate students: 2026 marks the centennial year for both The Graduate School and Duke Divinity School. Similar to Peñaranda Pérez, Bellaiche noted the vast diversity among graduate students’ areas of study, saying their “far-reaching excellence” and “intense scholarship” extend from the Duke University Hospital to the Rubenstein Arts Center. Yet, academic differences did not stop graduate students from forming connections with one another, according to Bellaiche. “This meant an interdisciplinary culture where we push science forward because of, not in spite of, our different backgrounds,” Bellaiche said. He described the friendships formed over the “shared stress of March Madness” or trying new ice cream flavors at The Parlour. Most importantly for Bellaiche, he will soon be marrying one of his classmates whom he met during his doctoral program. “Even if you didn't propose to the person sitting next to you in class, I am sure that Duke will forever be a part of all of our lives,” Bellaiche said.

title: Duke honors 3 individuals for outstanding commitment to service
author: Yashraj Vishwakarma
text: Duke Community Affairs and the Center for Community Engaged Scholarship recently presented an undergraduate, graduate student and faculty member with the 2026 Sullivan Award in recognition of their outstanding commitment to service. The honorees’ work included international humanitarian efforts in Gaza, founding an organization dedicated to helping those transitioning out of homelessness and practices of everyday service in Durham and on campus. “This award reminds us that our institution is measured not just by our academic productions but also by how we care for one another and how we engage beyond campus,” said Stelfanie Williams, vice president for community affairs, in an April 23 ceremony honoring the award recipients. The Sullivan Award is named for Algernon Sydney Sullivan, a 19th-century lawyer with a commitment to service. Honorees are nominated to a selection committee, which then provides recommendations to Provost Alec Gallimore who makes the final decision. The faculty award was presented to Dr. David Hasan, professor of neurosurgery and biomedical engineering, for his repeated trips to Gaza amid the Israel-Hamas war to provide lifesaving care to those in need. Despite the personal risk and resource constraints, Hasan, a Palestinian American, mobilized essential medical supplies and treated severely injured patients. He also established the Gaza Children’s Village, a multifaceted initiative aimed at supporting orphaned and vulnerable children in Gaza by providing them with shelter, education, healthcare and rehabilitation. According to their website , Gaza Children’s Village helps over 18,000 children daily. “In moments defined by division, [Hasan] has chosen connection. In the face of suffering, he has chosen action,” said Amber Rogers, senior program coordinator of the N.C. Leadership Forum, a University-run initiative. In his acceptance speech, Hasan described how the pain he witnessed in Gaza motivated his work to support victims and bridge the gap between Israelis and Palestinians. “It's not about the number, it's about the pain. To see that made me committed to one principle, and that principle is never again for both sides, and we work tirelessly for that,” Hasan said. Trisha Dalapati, a doctoral student in the School of Medicine, was presented the award in recognition of her effective leadership of the WellNest Housing Support Program, a student-led initiative that assists formerly homeless individuals with moving into their new homes and integrating into their communities. Since its founding in 2019, WellNest has supported 80 individual families and mobilized over 100 student volunteers. “Trisha has never lost sight of what matters most: creating a sense of home, not just through physical spaces, but through connection, respect and belonging,” Rogers said. In her acceptance speech, Dalapati explained how she learned that there was a critical gap missing for homeless individuals between when they had secured a lease and actually finalizing a move to a new home. WellNest was able to help. “It's remarkable to watch how the WellNest program has grown from just an idea to a self-sufficient student organization with close to 20 student leaders as well as dozens of community volunteers,” Dalapati said. The undergraduate award was presented to senior Jasmin Riley for her dedication to community-oriented initiatives and impactful leadership across various Durham and campus organizations, including Durham Public Schools, DukeEngage and the InventHER and InventSTEM initiatives, which seek to provide underrepresented groups with STEM resources. “Her work reflects profound understanding that meaningful change happens not only through action, but through connection,” Rogers said. Rogers also highlighted Riley’s acts of everyday service, saying she “centers people in everything that she does” and “moves through the world with humility, compassion and unwavering integrity.” Upon accepting the award, Riley gave a speech emphasizing her commitment to bringing communities together since her first year at Duke, noting her leadership on Bell Tower dorm’s House Council. She later became an orientation leader, served on Edens Quad Council and was an active participant in the Mitchell-White living learning community. “Bringing people together and building connection ... was what I felt joy in doing,” Riley said.

title: Trinity positive on financial outlook, approves Durham Tech transfer partnership
author: Abby DiSalvo, Dylan Halper
text: Despite key challenges, the financial outlook for the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences is better than expected, Professor of Sociology Stephen Vaisey said at Arts & Sciences Council’s Thursday meeting. Vaisey, the chair of the council’s budget committee, explained that Trinity’s effort to reduce expenses by 20% in alignment with Duke’s broader cost-cutting initiative remains ongoing. However, the college still plans to conduct a “moderate number” of faculty searches and will make no further reductions in the total number of funded doctoral students next year. “A lot of the worst-case scenarios that we imagined from the Trump administration have not materialized,” Vaisey said. When President Donald Trump first entered office last year, he imposed unprecedented pressure on higher education institutions, including attempts to eliminate vast amounts of research funding. In turn, Duke responded with staff layoffs and other measures to reduce expenses, preparing for a world in which the federal government gave universities less money. A year later, court rulings have blocked some federal policies, and the Trump administration has seemingly slowed down its barrage of new actions targeting Duke. “The bottom line is things are better than we expected,” Vaisey said. Yet, he cautioned, “There's still a lot of work to do.” Vaisey told the council that Trinity has long run an annual deficit of around $20 to $25 million, which compounded the difficulty of cost-cutting. The deficit has created a situation where Trinity needs to think in terms of trade-offs, he said, and faculty should not think there are “pots of money” that can fund desired projects. He also outlined how Trinity’s finances are “at the mercy” of factors outside the college’s control, such as fluctuating financial aid spending. Since Duke practices need-blind admissions, the amount of financial support an incoming class requires can change by tens of millions of dollars from year to year. The Chronicle reported in March that the average institutional grant Duke provides has nearly doubled in size from 2012 to 2024, according to IPEDS data. Trinity specifically pays for students’ financial aid out of its own operating expenses. As a result, 14% of Trinity’s revenue went to financial aid in fiscal year 2025. Despite the unpredictable costs, Vaisey said his committee also considered financial aid to be one of Trinity’s greatest economic opportunities. If the University were able to receive more donations for financial aid that could go to Trinity, then the college could spend the already dedicated money elsewhere. “Getting money for financial aid has the same effect as getting money for an endowed professorship,” Vaisey said. Some of the other revenue opportunities the committee identified were increasing philanthropic donations, the size of the first-year class and the number of transfer students. Duke has already begun its push for more transfer students and fundraising through the Made for This Campaign. Duke admitted 130 more students to the Class of 2030 than in previous classes. In the last admissions cycle, it tripled the number of transfer students admitted compared to before the COVID-19 pandemic. Jonathan Mattingly, Kimberly J. Jenkins distinguished University professor of new technologies, questioned how Duke’s endowment is distributed and how much of the funding actually lies under Trinity’s control. Vaisey noted that while only 9% of Trinity’s revenue came from the endowment, the average rate across Duke’s other nine schools is around 13%. He said that part of the problem lies with donors who tend to “want to put their names on sort of flashy things,” but that the University may also engage in some “steering” by directing donors toward projects outside Trinity. “Most of our donors are Trinity graduates,” Vaisey said. “We are responsible for the experience that probably leads them to want to give money in the first place, so we should be prioritized for that reason.” Arts & Sciences Council also approved a partnership between Trinity and Durham Technical Community College to allow a small cohort of students to transfer into select Trinity degree programs after completing their associate’s degree at Durham Tech. The partnership was approved with 30 in favor and two abstentions. The program will serve low-income, first-generation students identified early in their careers at Durham Tech either through faculty member nominations or by college counselors at their high schools. Duke would then advise the student during their time at Durham Tech to prepare them to enter the University as a junior. “Just knowing what we can do for these students at Duke, the sky's the limit,” said Jenny Wood Crowley, associate vice provost for strategic enrollment management. Over the next year, Trinity plans to see which departments will participate in the program, so that recruitment for a three to five student cohort can begin in fall 2027. The Pratt School of Engineering has already launched its partnership with Durham Tech, with recruitment expected to begin this coming fall. Academic Council also met Thursday, voting to approve the degrees of nearly 7,000 graduates ahead of commencement Sunday. The council then unanimously approved the Appendix I faculty committee report, which called for an overhaul of Duke’s Pickets, Protests and Demonstrations policy. Ed Balleisen, senior vice provost for interdisciplinary programs and initiatives, and Joseph Salem, Rita DiGiallonardo Holloway University librarian and vice provost for library affairs, briefly presented on the future of the Duke University Press. The Press, a nonprofit scholarly publisher affiliated with the University, strives to “support scholars” seeking to make positive change in the world. The department publishes around 150 new books and 60 journals per year. Balleisen highlighted the publisher’s “excellence in disseminating scholarship” and “strategy diversification.” He and Salem outlined several potential avenues for future cost-cutting within the Press: “streamlining” digital collections, pushing for more international opportunities, simplifying book design and strengthening the department’s philanthropic program. The council also awarded juniors Anushka Kumar, Daniel Levin and Emily Song the distinction of Faculty Scholars, the highest honor given by faculty to undergraduates planning to pursue doctoral degrees. The council then went to executive session. On the agenda were the University’s data center plans, in which an anonymous questioner requested that presenters detail “expected resource use,” plans for sustainability and opportunities for University community members to provide feedback on the project. The Duke Office of the Ombuds also gave a presentation on its 2024-2025 report, followed by a discussion of Duke Kunshan University led by Provost Alec Gallimore and Melanie Manion, Vor Broker family distinguished professor of political science and chair of the advisory committee on DKU. The Chronicle has previously covered the University’s uncertainty over DKU’s future.

title: Duke among 9,000 schools affected by Canvas cyberattack
author: Abby DiSalvo
text: Instructure has since struck an agreement with ShinyHunters. Read more here . Update 11:25 p.m.: As of Thursday evening, Canvas was largely operational once again. According to an 11:17 p.m. status update from Instructure, “Canvas is now available for most users.” Update 5:37 p.m.: On the afternoon of May 7, Canvas was once again rendered nonfunctional. According to multiple student newspapers, including The Collegiate Times and The Daily Pennsylvanian , a message from ShinyHunters appeared on user dashboards threatening to release stolen data. “ShinyHunters has breached Instructure (again),” the message read. “Instead of contacting us to resolve it they ignored us and did some ‘security patches.’” The message instructed affected schools or Instructure to consult with a cyber advisory firm and contact the hackers “to negotiate a settlement” to prevent the release of data. “You have till the end of the day by 12 May 2026 before everything is leaked,” the message read. Around 4:20 p.m., the hackers’ message was replaced by a message from Canvas that stated the site was “currently undergoing scheduled maintenance.” At 4:41 p.m., Instructure updated its status page to announce that the company is “currently investigating this issue.” The University did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment about whether it had been made aware of the new developments or if it planned to contact ShinyHunters in time for publication. Most Duke students are not currently using Canvas. Undergraduate final examinations concluded May 2, and Summer Term I does not begin until May 13. Below is the original story. Criminal extortion group ShinyHunters claimed Sunday that it had breached Instructure — the parent company of the online learning platform Canvas — and stolen data from 9,000 schools, including Duke. The Duke Information Technology Security Office first confirmed that Duke was among the institutions affected by the incident in a Wednesday evening email to all faculty and students. ShinyHunters claimed to have stolen information from 275 million students and teachers, consisting of “certain identifying information of users ... such as names, email addresses, and student ID numbers, as well as messages among users,” according to Instructure’s status page . However, Instructure also reported it “found no indication” that passwords, dates of birth, government identifiers or financial information were compromised. In a 5:15 p.m. update on Wednesday, Instructure said the incident was resolved with Canvas fully operational and no indication of “ongoing unauthorized activity.” However, ShinyHunters had previously told The Daily Pennsylvanian that they would leak the contents of the data on May 8 unless contacted by either Instructure or affected schools. Duke OIT still lists the Canvas incident as active on its status page. The Chronicle was not able to independently confirm ShinyHunters’ plans for the data. Chief Information Security Officer Nick Tripp wrote in the Wednesday evening email to faculty and students that Duke's “IT Security Office is closely monitoring this incident and is continuing to assess any effect on the University community.” The cyber incident did not appear to be directed at Duke, but the hackers have targeted institutions such as Harvard University and the University of Pennsylvania in recent months. Tripp did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment about whether the IT Security Office is concerned about ShinyHunters leaking the data or if they are taking additional measures to ensure data privacy. Instructure first became aware of a cybersecurity incident on the evening of May 1. The next afternoon, the company issued an update stating that an investigation with outside forensic experts was ongoing, but that the incident had been “contained.” According to most recent data, Canvas is used by 41% of higher education institutions in North America. The learning management system allows instructors to distribute course content, facilitate discussions, post assignments and release grades. Duke transitioned to the platform over the course of the 2023-24 academic year after previously using Sakai. The North Carolina Department of Public Instruction, which agreed to use Canvas across all state K-12 schools in 2015, was also notified of the breach by Instructure. The Wake County Public School System alerted families and staff of the cyberattack on Wednesday, but the impact on Durham Public Schools is unclear. ShinyHunters has taken credit for breaching third-party education-technology vendors in past attacks. Earlier this spring, ShinyHunters claimed to have breached Infinite Campus — a K-12 student information system — and the publisher McGraw Hill. In October, the hackers also claimed to have stolen over a billion customer records across dozens of companies through a breach of Salesforce, which contracts with Instructure.

title: Duke, Durham police departments investigate robbery on Al Buehler Trail
author: Dylan Halper
text: A robbery occurred Wednesday on the Al Buehler Trail, according to a DukeAlert sent to all students, faculty and staff Wednesday afternoon. Two suspects reportedly stole a victim’s jacket and headphones after shoving them down on the Highway 15-501 side of the trail. The only description of the suspects is that one of them rode a bike. No weapons or injuries were reported. According to a University spokesperson, Duke University Police Department is actively investigating the incident, which was reported to them by the victim this afternoon. They are partnering with Durham Police Department in the investigation. The victim is not associated with the Duke community, according to the spokesperson. The DukeAlert requested that any community members with additional information about the incident contact DUPD at 919-684-2444. The Al Buehler Trail is located on the edge of West Campus near the Washington Duke Inn and behind other athletic facilities. The trail is a popular spot for hiking or running among community members and Durham residents. The most recent reported robbery near campus was in January when DUPD responded to three car break-ins outside of Blue Light Living complex. “We encourage the community to be aware of their surroundings and to report concerning behavior,” the DukeAlert said. This is a developing story and will be updated as information becomes available.

title: Durham City Council imposes 60-day moratorium on data centers
author: Ella Moore
text: Durham City Council unanimously passed a 60-day moratorium on approving data center development in its Monday meeting, with some council members describing the measure as a first step toward more robust legislation targeting the centers. The council was previously expected to consider a two-year moratorium on data centers, but decided to decrease the length after learning that a moratorium beyond 60 days would require approval from a joint city and county planning commission. Council Member Nate Baker, who drafted the moratorium, described discussions surrounding data centers as a “fast-evolving issue that requires time.” In a memo to the city council, Baker said a moratorium was necessary to allow various city departments to research the issue, work with stakeholders and draft the needed amendments for more expansive legislation. “I am concerned that other communities have not given themselves enough time to thoroughly understand this complex issue,” Baker said. Durham residents and environmental activists voiced broad support for the moratorium at the meeting, but many called for a longer freeze on data center development. Speakers frequently cited the same concerns: the environmental impact of data centers and potential subsequent price hikes in energy and utility bills. At peak summer heat, data centers could use up to a minimum of 1 million gallons of reclaimed water per day according to WRAL. Other research has suggested that data centers can use as much power as 100,000 homes, with larger facilities currently being built expected to consume up to 20 times that amount. Duke Energy, which provides power services for the vast majority of Durham, has already proposed an 18% rate increase for customers across North Carolina as they expand into data center development. Leslie St Dre, founder of environmental justice group Community Land and Power and organizer for the Stop Data Centers in Durham Coalition, described a moratorium as an opportunity to pass legislation that closes potential loopholes companies could exploit. Although Baker initially called for a 24-month moratorium in the ordinance, he acknowledged that the longest current moratorium on data centers in North Carolina is 1 year in neighboring Orange County. He also described the measure as a key pathway to give the city more time to “bring forward more robust language for a more robust moratorium.” “I think it is vital that we have no less than a 24-month moratorium on hyperscale data centers in the city of Durham,” Baker said. St Dre, holding a bright orange poster reading “Data Centers = Death,” said the council’s planning commission could not properly develop legislation in under two years that accounts for the many environmental, energy and living concerns surrounding data centers. “Durham residents and every living being deserve the longest, strongest moratorium,” St Dre said. Mimi Kessler, a Durham resident, also criticized the 60-day moratorium as insufficient. She said it amounted to “window-dressing,” arguing Durham does not need any economic stimulus that the data centers may provide, saying the city is already in a housing shortage and struggles to fill vacant jobs. “The moratorium should be here for a much longer period of time; in fact, forever,” Kessler said. Mayor Leonardo Williams and Council Member Shanetta Burris voiced similar sentiments, with Burris describing the 60-day moratorium as feeling like “60 seconds.” “Durham needs to indefinitely have a moratorium on megawatt data centers,” Williams added. Earlier on Monday, Durham County commissioners discussed plans for their own moratorium, indicating that a joint effort to extend the moratorium could be in the works. Council Member Matt Kopac said that parts of Durham County outside the city itself would likely attract more proposals for data centers, and urged those who spoke at the council hearing to bring the same energy at the county level. “I look forward to a joint, unified approach to addressing this issue for our great city,” Kopac said.

title: Class of 2026 commencement preview
author: Staff Reports
text: The Class of 2026 both started and left Duke at periods of fundamental change for the University. During their first year, the Class of 2026 experienced the introduction of QuadEx and Experiential Orientation, and in their senior year, the University underwent a vast cost-cutting program in response to federal pressure on higher education. In the middle of their time at Duke, they celebrated the University's centennial and witnessed a contentious election cycle. The Chronicle is here to celebrate the Class's accomplishments and speak to the experience that was uniquely theirs ahead of Commencement Sunday. Below, you'll find a look back at their years on campus, along with a commencement preview, a gallery of graduation photos and more. The Class of 2026's first year was one of new beginnings — a year marked by the implementation of QuadEx, the first seasons of two head coaches and the lifting of COVID-19 protocols. Sophomore year saw Duke celebrating its 100th year, demonstrations over the Israel-Hamas war and the effects of the Supreme Court’s decision to strike down affirmative action in higher-education. The Class of 2026’s junior year saw a high-stakes presidential election, a monumental basketball season and the beginning of sweeping changes in higher education. Senior year saw Duke engage in a year of cost-cutting in response to federal policy changes, historic wins for the University’s sports teams and the rise of artificial intelligence. By Luca Gragnoli By Ana Despa By Jackson Kitts Duke Law will host Maurice “Mo” Green, North Carolina Superintendent of Public Instruction, and the economics department will hear from Lorie Logan, the president and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas. By Sarah Diaz and Annie Eilers Dianne McIntyre, Cass Sunstein and Abby Wambach will each receive honorary degrees in recognition of their trailblazing careers on May 10. By Seyun Park

title: First Year: 2022-2023
author: Staff Reports
text: The Class of 2026's first year was one of new beginnings — a year marked by the implementation of QuadEx, the first seasons of two head coaches and the lifting of COVID-19 protocols. Duke made national headlines this year. Following the Class of 2022’s graduation, The Chronicle reported that the student commencement speech closely resembled a 2014 Harvard student address. In late August, Duke volleyball player Rachel Richardson reported that she was “racially heckled” during a game at Brigham Young University. The incident resulted in the banning, then unbanning of a fan after the school’s investigation did not find evidence of racial slurs directed at Richardson. The University implemented the QuadEx residential program in the fall of 2022, which saw the introduction of Faculty Fellows and the creation of Quad Arches. The Class of 2026 was the first to experience Experiential Orientation, another QuadEx program, which marked an overhaul in orientation programming that cost $1 million more than the traditional Orientation Week experience. The Chronicle spoke with alumni who advocated for housing reform and examined the differences between QuadEx and other peer institutions’ residential systems. At the same time, students had mixed reactions to the new housing system. Under QuadEx’s “all-gender housing model,” the Baldwin Scholars lost their female-only section. The future of selective living groups on campus remained uncertain. Some chapters of Duke-affiliated Greek life were still recovering from the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic while adjusting to QuadEx, and non-Greek SLGs received conflicting messages from the administration regarding recruitment. The University slowly removed COVID-19 protocols that had become a part of campus life. By late September, masks in classrooms were no longer required. By March, masks on buses were no longer required. In April, Duke ended its COVID-19 vaccine requirement for most students, faculty and staff. As a monumental midterm election season came and went, The Chronicle spoke with candidates, voters and journalists before, during and after Election Day. After all the votes were tallied, the Republican Party fell just one seat short of a supermajority in the General Assembly. In April, North Carolina state Rep. Tricia Cotham changed her party affiliation, shifting the supermajority in the Republican Party’s favor. In December, Duke accepted 800 Early Decision applicants, making the 16.5% acceptance rate the lowest in Duke’s history. Duke admitted 4.8% of its Regular Decision applicants in March. The Class of 2026's first year also saw a new dawn for football culture. Under the leadership of new head coach Mike Elko, the ACC Coach of the Year, the Blue Devils ended the regular season 8-4 and defied expectations to become Military Bowl Champions. In December, line monitors announced a pilot program providing financial aid through tenting supplies. Cameron Indoor Stadium was packed once again, the Blue Devils went undefeated at home and Duke completed a sweep of North Carolina after losing to the Tar Heels in the Final Four the previous year. And despite early road struggles and a controversial no-call against Virginia, the Blue Devils rallied to win the ACC Tournament before losing in the second round of the NCAA Tournament. Women’s basketball enjoyed a special season of its own, falling just short of the ACC regular-season title and earning a No. 3-seed for the NCAA Tournament. Men’s lacrosse won the 2023 ACC title after defeating Syracuse in the Blue Devils’ conference finale. Men’s tennis went to the NCAA Sweet 16 for the first time since 2015 after achieving its best-ever ACC record. Duke Athletics wasn’t the only part of Duke that experienced a changing of the guard. Gary Bennett, vice provost for undergraduate education, was named the next dean of Trinity College. Provost Sally Kornbluth was named the next president of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and will be replaced by Alec Gallimore. Suzanne Barbour joined the Graduate School as its new dean in September. Duke also saw several losses. Peter Nicholas, the Nicholas School of the Environment’s namesake, passed in June. John Burness, former senior vice president for public affairs and government relations, passed in December. Samar Zora, a fourth-year doctoral candidate in the cultural anthropology department, passed away in February. Basketball and baseball legend Dick Groat passed in April. Beloved campus pets Nugget and Peaches died over the summer. As the Bryan Center prepares for renovations that could cost up to $110 million, cultural and identity groups are asking for more equitable space allocations for their organizations. As student groups shared their grievances and sought solutions, the Center for Multicultural Affairs celebrated its 50th anniversary and held its first multicultural graduation. Campus labor unions saw major developments this year. In August, the Duke University Press Workers Union officially won its union. The Duke Graduate Students Union filed for an election with the National Labor Relations Board in March after launching a campaign for formal recognition in September. In response, the University is challenging a 2016 NLRB decision affirming the legal right for graduate students to unionize. This was a big year for climate activism on campus. Duke announced its Climate Commitment, which aims to increase the role of the University in tackling the climate crisis. The Undergraduate Environmental Union — a subcommittee within Duke Student Government — voiced that they believed the Climate Commitment does not go far enough and called for more student input. At the same time, several Duke centers and affiliates have struggled. Duke Student Affairs saw a series of staffing shortages, hampering student group funding and planning for cultural events. The Center for Documentary Studies, a nonprofit affiliate of Duke, has undergone a programmatic overhaul over the past year. A year after the establishment of the Center for Gender Violence Prevention and Intervention, the Center still lacks a permanent director. On the academic side, Duke’s Asian American Diaspora Studies minor saw its first year, while the School of Medicine and the School of Law announced that they would withdraw from US News and World Report’s rankings in January. The Curriculum Development Committee also began thinking about what Trinity College’s new curriculum will look like. Undergraduates chose junior Isaiah Hamilton to be the next DSG president, while senior Sydney Hunt was chosen as the next Undergraduate Young Trustee nominee. Seniors Shreyas Hallur and Qi Xuan Khoo were named Rhodes Scholars.

title: Sophomore Year: 2023-2024
author: Staff Reports
text: Sophomore year saw Duke celebrating its 100th year, demonstrations over the Israel-Hamas war and the effects of the Supreme Court’s decision to strike down affirmative action in higher education. Duke rose to No. 7 in the U.S. News and World Report’s Best National Colleges Ranking, the highest in the University’s history. During the admissions cycle, Duke saw a 28% increase in Early Decision applicants and accepted a record low 12.9%. In the Regular Decision round, Duke admitted another record low 4.1% of applicants, resulting in an overall acceptance rate of 5.1% for the Class of 2028. In January, Duke kicked off its centennial celebrations in Cameron Indoor Stadium, hosting student dance groups, former men’s basketball coach Mike Krzyzewski and actor Ken Jeong, Trinity ‘90. The University also received a $100 million award from the Duke Endowment, which will be used to provide financial aid for students from the Carolinas and underrepresented backgrounds; to increase student support, community engagement and experiential learning initiatives; and to remodel the Reuben-Cooke building. The award is the single largest in Duke’s history. Following the Oct. 7 attacks in southern Israel, protests erupted on college campuses across the country. At Duke, pro-Israel vigils honored the victims of the attacks and demanded a return of the hostages. Meanwhile, pro-Palestinian protesters also demanded that Duke call for a ceasefire and that it divest from its holdings in Israel. In an effort to encourage civil discourse, Provost Alec Gallimore launched the Provost’s Initiative on the Middle East, a year-long initiative hosting an array of experts to discuss the conflict in the region. In November, Durham County hosted its municipal general election, which decided Durham’s mayor, Leonardo Williams, and three new City Council members: Javiera Caballero, Nate Baker and Carl Rist. Williams gave his inaugural State of City address in April, emphasizing the importance of investing in education in Durham. In the March 5 primary election, Durham voters set up a gubernatorial race between Democrat Josh Stein and Republican Mark Robinson. Former president Donald Trump came out victorious over Nikki Haley after both candidates campaigned in North Carolina ahead of Super Tuesday. Duke made national headlines in September after a New York Times Magazine article named Duke among the "least economically diverse" of the elite colleges. In response to the article, President Vincent Price characterized it as portraying the University “in a rather harsh light.” However, students resoundingly agreed with the article’s findings. The University received national backlash again after it announced plans to close its herbarium after over 100 years of operations. According to Duke biology professors, the decision holds serious implications for research in climate change and biodiversity. Following the Supreme Court’s June 2023 ruling ending race-based affirmative action, the University announced the end of the Reginaldo Howard Memorial Scholarship Program, a full-ride scholarship for select Black students. The scholarship will be replaced with the Reginaldo Howard Leadership Program and will be open to all undergraduate students, regardless of race. The University also faced a number of lawsuits, ranging from allegations that the Alice M. Baldwin Scholars program engaged in “reverse discrimination” to a class-action lawsuit charging the University with practicing need-aware admissions, which it settled by paying $24 million. Duke football had a whirlwind of a season this year. Students rushed the field in excitement after the team upset No. 9 Clemson during its season opener at Wallace Wade Stadium, the squad rose to an all-time high No. 16 in the AP Poll and College Gameday came to Duke’s matchup against Notre Dame where students watched the beginning of the end of the Elko Era. After an end-of-game injury for junior quarterback Riley Leonard, the season hit a downward spiral, as a number of players entered the transfer portal following coach Mike Elko’s surprise departure to Texas A&M. Incoming coach Manny Diaz would mark a new era of Duke football. Duke men’s basketball had a season of ups and downs, dropping from its No. 2 preseason ranking after an early loss to the No. 12 Arizona Wildcats. The team eventually found its groove, yet still fell to North Carolina during both match-ups at the Dean E. Smith Center and Cameron Indoor Stadium. Throughout the season, freshman guard Jared McCain dominated both on the court with his stellar 3-point shooting performance and off the court with his TikTok dances — most notably to “2 Days Into College” by Aimee Carty. With the top-ranked recruiting class, the team also experienced a transfer craze, with only two starters remaining for the next year’s roster. The Cameron Crazies experienced changes to tenting in Krzyzewskiville this year with the implementation of a Wellness Week and a decision to raise the “grace” temperature from 25 degrees Fahrenheit to 32 degrees Fahrenheit. The policy changes were made in response to complaints from last year’s tenters that tenting was “too mentally and physically overwhelming.” Duke women’s basketball also experienced an electric season, dancing its way past No. 7 Ohio State in the Elite Eight to earn a spot in the Sweet 16 for the first time since 2018. Duke softball also had itself a season, climbing to No. 1 for the first time in program history in April. Duke field hockey found its way to the Final Four in its Cinderella season, falling in a heartbreaker to Northwestern. Divestment was a hot topic on campus this year. Climate-conscious students built on a decade-long campaign to get the University to divest from fossil fuels, holding multiple demonstrations and publishing a report about fossil fuel-funded research at Duke. The Advisory Committee on Investment Responsibility published a report in February recommending Price and the Board of Trustees against divestment. The Duke community also mourned the loss of some of its members this year. Angela Risi, a second-year master of fine arts student, died in October. Hans Joris Van Miegroet, professor of art and art history, died in February. Joseph Izatt, chair of the department of biomedical engineering and Michael J. Fitzpatrick professor of engineering, died in April. After plans to revise the Trinity curriculum beginning in February 2022, the Arts & Sciences Council approved a new curriculum in April to take effect in fall 2025. The new curriculum places a greater emphasis on the humanities amidst declines in humanities enrollment nationwide. The Mary Lou Williams Center for Black Culture reopened in March following a nearly 18-month closure after a burst chilled pipe in the Flowers Building forced the center to relocate to the Bryan Center. The reopening was originally slated to occur in fall 2023 yet was postponed to spring 2024 due to delays in constructions. Duke continued its construction efforts, announcing plans to begin a two-year renovation of Lilly Library. Renovations were initially slated to start in 2020 but were delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Another one of Duke’s East Campus facilities was also shut down indefinitely, this time due to lead dust being detected in various parts of the Brodie Recreation Center. The University notified 2,800 people who had entered the facility when the lead dust was exposed. In this year’s student elections, junior Heather Raslan pulled out victorious in a field of three candidates vying for Duke Student Government president, replacing senior Isaiah Hamilton. Senior Drew Greene was selected as the undergraduate Young Trustee. On the last day of classes, students celebrated their achievements with a live concert headlined by Swae Lee and performances by Weston Estate and Elley Duhé.

title: Junior Year: 2024-2025
author: Staff Reports
text: The Class of 2026's junior year saw a high-stakes presidential election, a monumental basketball season and the beginning of sweeping changes in higher education. The academic year began in high spirits. In celebration of Duke’s Centennial, singer-songwriter Ed Sheeran gave a rainy Founder’s Day performance to students and alumni in Wallace Wade stadium. Sheeran’s “Castle on a Hill" might have been an omen of the good to come, as the next day, Duke football rallied to a nail-biting last-minute win over archrival North Carolina.
