She pointed out that while peer institutions may have a written culture expressed through their policies, there is also an “operational sort of campus culture.” “I want to make sure that we walk out of here understanding that there are real risks to those members of our community who choose to stand up in front of everyone,” McMahon said. “So I would caution us not to move quickly towards a change.” Academic Council will vote on the Appendix I Committee report at its next meeting in May.

title: 3 in 4 first-years use AI at least weekly, most unsatisfied with new Constellations program
author: Luca Gragnoli, Leena Mirchandani
text: Editor's note: This story is part of a series about the Class of 2029 based on a survey conducted by The Chronicle in January and February 2026. You can read more about our methodology and limitations here or read all our survey coverage here . The Chronicle surveyed the Class of 2029 about their academic habits over their first year at Duke, from their use of artificial intelligence and opinions on the new Trinity curriculum to their academic interests and perceptions of course rigor. The Chronicle asked survey respondents to indicate the major they are most likely to declare, and nearly two-thirds — 63.20% — indicated they are likely to double major. We grouped intended majors by discipline and found that the natural sciences were the most popular discipline at 59.20%, followed by social sciences at 41.60%, engineering at 21.20% and arts and humanities at 11.60%. Another 2.80% of our respondents said they were likely to complete an interdepartmental major or design their own major through Program II. We observed variation by academic discipline in survey responses regarding perceived course rigor and the clarity of AI guidelines. The Class of 2029 reported extensive use of generative AI in academic settings, which was defined as artificial intelligence capable of generating text, images or other media. Nearly three in four respondents said they used generative AI at least once a week to aid in completing their assignments. Students’ AI use seems to be steadily increasing. Last year , three in five students — about 60.27% — reported using AI at least weekly to help with their assignments. The year prior , only about one in five respondents — 21.07% — said the same, though they were surveyed earlier in the academic year, which could have contributed to the disparity. AI usage was slightly more frequent among respondents enrolled in the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences than it was for students in the Pratt School of Engineering — Duke's two undergraduate schools. More Trinity students used AI for assignments at least once a week at 75.01% than Pratt students at 72.72%. But the trend flipped for daily users: One in five Pratt students reported using AI for assignments every day, whereas that figure dropped to 15.31% for Trinity students. Problem sets were by far the most common assignment type where first-years used AI, with nearly three-fourths of our respondents reporting doing so. That’s up significantly from last year, where problem sets also led the pack, but only at 38.84%. Coding tasks and short written assignments like discussion posts tied for second, similar to last year’s results. We asked our 10% of respondents who never use AI for assignments why they choose not to and offered eight possible explanations. Their top concern was that AI produces inaccurate information, followed by AI use being considered cheating, then that the technology could inhibit their learning experience. This year, we asked respondents for the first time whether they thought their professors had clearly communicated their expectations about AI use for their classes. Duke has been reshaping its approach to the technology over the past year, most recently with a report from the AI at Duke steering committee advocating for a “strategic orientation” toward the University’s adoption. However, faculty and student opinions on AI in the classroom remain mixed , and instructors have been encouraged to create their own course policies surrounding use of the technology. Despite Duke’s overarching AI approach remaining in flux, first-years generally thought professors were effective at communicating their expectations for academic AI use. The overwhelming majority of our respondents — 90.80% — said most of their professors had communicated their expectations for AI use in their classes, and over half said all their professors had done so. Students intending to pursue engineering degrees were less likely to have clear AI guidelines from their professors than their peers in other fields, but the disparity was slight. By contrast, respondents studying the natural sciences or pursuing an interdepartmental major were more likely to have clear AI guidelines for their classes. This year, Duke first-years had to choose between two academic programs, FOCUS and Constellations, for the first time. While the FOCUS program has existed at Duke for decades, the Constellations program was piloted this year as part of the new Trinity curriculum . Students enrolled in the FOCUS program live in the same dorm and take two thematically tied seminar classes together in the fall semester. The Constellations program, on the other hand, runs throughout the entire year with a set of three interconnected courses and does not include a living-learning element. All Trinity first-years must enroll in one of the two programs. Our Trinity respondents were relatively evenly split between them, with 50.51% enrolled in Constellations and 49.49% in FOCUS. Both FOCUS and Constellations have been touted by the University as community-building and academic enrichment opportunities. However, our survey revealed a harsh divergence in student satisfaction across the two programs. FOCUS students gave much more positive feedback on their experience compared to their Constellation counterparts. The majority of respondents enrolled in FOCUS reported that the program had enriched their academic and social lives, at 76.29% and 81.45%, respectively. Far fewer Constellations respondents said the same — 40.40% and 49.49%, respectively. If first-years could opt out of these programs, would they? Exactly half of our Trinity respondents said yes. But again, opinions were largely split along program lines. Nearly three-fourths of Constellations students said they would have opted out, while about the same proportion of FOCUS students said the opposite. This disparity could be caused in part by selection bias from differences in program structure. FOCUS is a self-selected program for which students must complete an additional application. Thus, students willing to undertake the effort to apply may be more inclined to participate in first-year seminars to begin with. Constellations, on the other hand, are required for all remaining Trinity students and may capture more students who are less interested in seminar-style courses or students for whom the courses fall out of anticipated degree pathways. Our survey did not ask students about the factors that influenced their feedback, though, so we cannot evaluate this hypothesis with the data alone. Last year, we asked first-year students how the difficulty of their Duke courses in STEM, humanities and social sciences compared to those of their high school. This year, we removed the reference to students’ high school experiences and instead asked respondents to rate the difficulty of their 200-level courses to get more comparative results. A much higher proportion of our respondents said they hadn’t taken any 200-level humanities or social science courses yet at 66.40% and 73.20%, respectively, than 200-level STEM courses at 33.20%. These students were excluded from the remainder of our analysis of first-year students’ perceptions of course difficulty. The remaining first-years tended to find humanities courses easier and STEM courses more difficult, and they were more neutral on social science courses. No one found 200-level humanities classes very difficult. Perceptions of course difficulty in the humanities varied depending on the type of high school attended by students. In general, privately educated students found their college classes easier than their public-school peers, although the difference was starker for social science and humanities classes than for STEM courses. Students from rural communities tended to find their courses more difficult than those from urban or suburban communities, regardless of academic discipline. The Class of 2029 applied to Duke in its fifth year of permitting test-optional applications. Our test-optional respondents — who made up roughly a fourth of the sample — generally found their classes to be harder than their peers who submitted the SAT, ACT or both as part of the admissions process.

title: Tapas section in Brodhead Center to be repurposed into ‘deli concept’ in response to student demand
author: Dylan Halper
text: Those who have been missing the baked baguettes and sliced meats of Don’s Classic Subs may be in luck. Duke Dining plans to repurpose the existing Tapas section located between Il Forno and Sazón in the Brodhead Center — more commonly known as WU — into a “deli concept,” the office confirmed in a Thursday email to The Chronicle. Duke Dining did not share the new station’s name or when it would be installed. The office also did not respond when asked whether the new venue would continue to offer items currently sold at the Tapas station, such as empanadas, stuffed arepas and paninis. “This is not a replacement, but an evolution of the space to better meet student demand,” Duke Dining wrote. Deli-style offerings were “highly requested” by students, according to the office. The Chef’s Kitchen on WU’s second floor recently experimented with a deli pop-up, Don’s Classic Subs, which lasted from mid-March until last Friday. The venue offered eight sub sandwiches while also allowing customers to build their own. According to Duke Dining, the pop-up resonated with students and inspired the office’s decision to expand deli offerings on campus. “This shift was driven directly by student feedback,” Duke Dining wrote. “Through surveys, emails, conversations in [Duke University Student Dining Advisory Committee] and insights from recent pop-ups in the Chef’s Kitchen, Duke Dining identified a clear gap on campus for a deli-style offering.” Duke Dining hinted at the decision on its Instagram story earlier this week, responding to a question about bringing back subs by writing that “good news *may* be coming.” Although the email did not list specific menu items to be offered by the repurposed station, Duke Dining wrote that the deli will remain under the same operator as Il Forno and Sazón “to maintain consistency in quality and operations.” That operator, Brenen’s Restaurant Group, also operates Enzo’s Pizza Co. in Durham. “This change reflects a thoughtful use of an existing space to bring in a concept students have been asking for, while continuing to build on the strength of the current dining program,” Duke Dining wrote.

title: Former Duke Trustee, Ex-Va. Lt. Gov. Justin Fairfax and wife found dead in apparent murder-suicide
author: Claire Cranford
text: Editor’s note: The following article contains mentions of murder, sexual assault and suicide. Justin Fairfax, Trinity ‘00 and former lieutenant governor of Virginia, fatally shot his wife, Cerina, Trinity ‘99, before killing himself in what is being investigated as a murder-suicide. Fairfax County police found the couple dead in their home early Thursday morning. The former Democratic party official had close ties to Duke, including serving on the Board of Trustees as a Young Trustee from 2000 to 2002 and the Sanford School of Public Policy’s Board of Visitors where he served for 10 years from 2009 to 2019. The Board of Trustees serves as governing body for the University, while the Board of Visitors holds an advisory role for Sanford. A Reginaldo Howard Scholar, Fairfax graduated from Duke with an undergraduate degree in public policy studies and French. Fairfax’s connection to Duke grew contentious in 2019, after an alumna accused him of sexually assaulting her when they were undergraduates. The accusation became public after Fairfax was in the news for allegedly sexually assaulting someone at the 2004 Democratic National Convention. In response, then-Sanford Dean Judith Kelley requested he resign from the Board of Visitors. Fairfax denied the allegations of wrongdoing and claimed Duke had mistreated him. According to the News & Observer, he sent a 19-page letter to Duke administrators in 2022 demanding they clear his name and acknowledge mistreatment. “... it is well-past time for Duke to clear my name and rectify the tremendous injustice that I, my family and other loved ones — including countless fellow Duke Alumni and Board Members — have had to endure for the past three and a half years at the hands of my beloved alma mater,” Fairfax wrote in the letter addressed to Kelley, President Vincent Price and then-Vice President and General Counsel Pamela Bernard. The letter came at the tail end of his appointment as Virginia’s lieutenant governor under former Virginia Gov. Ralph Northam, a position he held since 2018 after making an unsuccessful run for state attorney general in 2013. At the time, he was only the second African-American elected statewide in Virginia. Fairfax then ran an unsuccessful campaign for governor in 2021. Justin’s wife, Cerina (née Wanzer) Fairfax, graduated from Duke with a Bachelor of Science in Psychology and minors in religion and chemistry. She ran a family dentistry practice in the Washington area, and from 2010 to 2014, she served on Duke’s Young Alumni Development Council. She met Justin Fairfax during her time at Duke. The killings came amid “a complicated or messy divorce,” according to Fairfax County Chief of Police Kevin Davis. A court filing said the couple had been separated since 2024 and filed for divorce last year.

title: The Class of 2029’s first year: All the reporting from The Chronicle’s annual survey
author: Staff Reports
text: This is The Chronicle’s ninth year of surveying the first-year class. Below are all our articles on the Class of 2029. The Chronicle has also collected coverage on the Class of 2028 , Class of 2027 , Class of 2026 , Class of 2025 , Class of 2024 , Class of 2023 , Class of 2022 and Class of 2021 . This year’s survey team included Data Editor Zoe Kolenovsky, a senior; Editor-in-Chief Ana Despa, a junior; Recess Managing Editor Zev van Zanten, a senior; Photo Editor Amy Zhang, a junior; City News Editor Annie Eilers, a sophomore; and staff writers Leena Mirchandani, Lila Cohen and Luca Gragnoli, all first-years.

title: Who is the Class of 2029? Methodology, limitations and summary statistics from The Chronicle’s survey
author: Zoe Kolenovsky
text: Editor's note: This story is part of a series about the Class of 2029 based on a survey conducted by The Chronicle in January and February 2026. Read the rest of our survey coverage here . The Chronicle is releasing survey data about the Class of 2029. For the ninth consecutive year, The Chronicle surveyed first-year students about their lifestyles, demographics, plans at Duke and more. Questions ranged from standardized test scores and religious beliefs to approximate family income and use of campus resources. The results of this survey will be released throughout the coming week in a series of articles that give a deeper look at the class wrapping up its first year at Duke. The survey was administered from Jan. 26 to Feb. 8. A total of 250 first-year students completed the survey in its entirety. Three $100 prizes were randomly awarded to participants who filled out the entire survey, and another two $100 prizes were randomly awarded to participants who successfully referred a peer to complete the survey. There are 1,750 members of the Class of 2029 according to the class profile , marking a 14% survey completion rate. Last year’s survey was conducted in March and April 2025 and was completed by 13% of the Class of 2028. In total, this year’s survey asked 77 questions, compared to 79 questions last year. Participants saw different questions based on their responses to avoid redundancy. For example, students were only asked about the country they lived in before attending Duke if they indicated they were international admits. We removed a few questions that have not historically yielded significant findings, such as whether students are varsity athletes or what cities international students are from, as well as questions that we determined had lost time relevance. To minimize page jumps in an effort to boost our completion rate, we also removed conditional formatting — which limits visibility to respondents who had answered a previous question a certain way — on many of our questions. Historically, respondents had most frequently dropped out of the survey while switching between sections. For the first time, we asked students how much financial aid they received from sources beyond Duke, as well as whether paying for their Duke education put financial strain on them or their family. We added questions pertaining to the admissions process, including whether students were interviewed, whether their secondary school provided them with a college admissions counselor and their graduating class size. Given the launch of Constellations for first-year students this year, we asked respondents to indicate whether they enrolled in the FOCUS program or Constellations, whether they felt their program enriched their academic and social experiences, and whether they would have opted out if given the choice. Other new questions included whether students supported preferences for legacy in Duke’s admissions process, whether they think professors have clearly communicated expectations about AI use and how many hours of sleep they get per night on average. We only analyzed fully completed responses this year, in line with the prior two iterations of the survey. Jerry Reiter, professor of statistical science and former department chair, previously told The Chronicle that partially completed responses could represent a different population than the fully completed cases, so combining them could misrepresent the survey results. Reiter also told The Chronicle that students’ reasons for opting out of the survey, such as low enthusiasm for Duke, could make the results less representative of the class. He added that students may not have chosen to complete the questionnaire due to time constraints, which could result in the nonrespondents being systematically different than the respondents, skewing the results of the survey. Below, we compare our survey data with the Class of 2029 profile from the Office of Undergraduate Admissions and other official data to show how The Chronicle’s survey responses may differ from the overall class. All summary statistics are rounded to the nearest hundredth, unless they were sourced externally and reported with less precision. Women were overrepresented in The Chronicle’s survey, similar to previous years. In our survey, which included more gender options than the class profile, 58.40% of respondents identified as women, 40.00% as men and 1.20% as nonbinary or genderqueer. Less than 1% did not select a gender identity. In comparison, the breakdown of the full Class of 2029 places women at 52.5%, while men make up 47.5%. As in recent years, The Chronicle’s survey gathered race and ethnicity data differently than Duke’s admissions office. Previously, Duke reported race and ethnicity based on the categories used by the Common Application, Coalition Application and Questbridge application. But this year, the admissions office shifted to the federal Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System method, which places students in a single ethnic or racial category. We allowed students to select multiple identities in our survey, meaning percentages do not add up to 100 and are thus not directly comparable with Duke’s data, which lists “multi-race” as a separate category. As we have in previous years, we also chose slightly different race and ethnicity categories than Duke; our categories align with those used in most federal reporting, as set by the U.S. Office of Management and Budget . In our survey, we included “other” and “Middle Eastern/North African” categories and separated American Indian or Alaska Native students from Native Hawaiian students, whereas Duke does not. Duke groups Pacific Islanders with Asian students, but we grouped Pacific Islanders and Native Hawaiian students together. Duke also lists non-U.S. residents in a separate category, whereas we asked all students to report their race and/or ethnicity regardless of their nationality. In our survey, 48.00% identified as white; 42.80% identified as Asian; 11.20% of respondents identified as Hispanic/Latinx; 8.40% identified as Black or African American; and 4.00% identified as Middle Eastern or North African. Less than 1% identified as American Indian or Alaska Native, and fewer identified as Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander. About 1.60% identified as a race or ethnicity not listed on the survey. According to the official admissions profile, 33% identify as white; 25% identify as Asian, Asian American or Pacific Islander; 11% of first-year students identify as Hispanic/Latinx; 7% identify as Black or African American; less than 1% identify as Native American, American Indian, Native Alaskan or Native Hawaiian. Another 6% identify as multi-race, and 11% are non-U.S. residents. Another 7% did not report a race or ethnicity. Students self-reported Asian and white identities at much higher rates in our survey than in the class profile. Slightly more of our respondents identified as Black or African American than in the class profile. All other race/ethnicity categories were similar. Our question addressing disability reflected the Americans with Disabilities Act’s definition. Less than 1% of respondents identified as having a physical impairment, and 6.40% identified as having a mental impairment. Students could identify as having multiple impairments, and the vast majority — 93.20% — identified as having none. However, our survey may underpresent the number of first-years with disabilities. In spring 2025, a fourth of undergraduate students were registered with the Student Disability Access Office, according to SDAO Director Leigh Millar. She did not have updated figures for the current academic year, or for the first-year class individually. The official Class of 2029 profile states that 89% of students are from the United States and 11% are international students. Our survey had very similar results, with 89.60% self-reporting as domestic students and 10.40% as international students. The Office of Undergraduate Admissions does not publish students’ financial information, but according to the most recent data from the U.S. Census Bureau as of 2024, the median family income in the United States was $83,730. Nearly three in four respondents in The Chronicle’s survey reported an annual family income over $80,000, suggesting that the Class of 2029’s family incomes skew higher than national trends. According to Duke’s data , about half of undergraduates receive institutional financial aid. Kathy Phillips, interim dean of undergraduate admissions, explained that the official figure includes need-based support from the University, along with federal and state sources, merit-based scholarships and athletic scholarships. About 59.20% of respondents in The Chronicle’s survey receive financial aid from Duke, meaning our results overrepresent the proportion of students on financial aid. Pell Grant-eligible students in particular are overrepresented — 30.80% of our respondents said they received a Pell Grant, whereas 22% of the Class of 2029 was eligible for the federal aid award, per the class profile. Two-fifths of our respondents reported receiving financial aid from other sources besides the University, and most of them said that aid represented less than a fourth of their total cost of attendance. Just under half of matriculated students in the Class of 2029 — 48.57% — were admitted either in the Early Decision cycle or through QuestBridge, according to Phillips. The remainder were admitted either during the Regular Decision cycle or accepted off the waitlist. Our survey results were similar to official data. About 48.87% of our respondents were accepted in the Early Decision cycle, 44.80% in the Regular Decision cycle and 6.40% off the waitlist. First-generation students — those whose parents have not earned a bachelor’s degree — are overrepresented in our survey, similar to previous years. Almost a fifth of our respondents, 19.60%, identified as first-generation students, compared to 14% reported in the class profile.

title: DSG senators elect new speaker of the senate, approve judiciary committee members in final meeting of the year
author: Jeremiah Fang
text: Duke Student Government senators met Wednesday for their final session of the year to elect a new speaker of the senate, approve new members to the senate judiciary committee and bid farewell to senior senators. Junior Olivia McConnell, vice president of the academic affairs committee, gave an update that materials for the study sphere project had been ordered. Senior Liv Schramkowski, vice president of Durham and community affairs committee, announced that the committee was “wrapping up” its passport initiative for the semester. The committee will also be partnering with the Project Discover experiential orientation program to expand the program’s reach to incoming first-years. Sophomore Nikhil Sethi, vice president of services and sustainability committee, announced that an e-waste recycling bin would now be available in the Duke Technology Center in the basement of the University Store. The project is in partnership with Triangle Ecycling , a Durham-based business focused on recycling and refurbishing old electronics. Two senators, junior Olivia McConnell and sophomore Major Richmond were nominated for speaker of the senate and addressed the body. McConnell’s platform emphasized her experience as both a vice president and a senator, proposing a return to anonymous voting within DSG as well as a shift from weekly to monthly committee updates. Richmond’s platform focused on increasing engagement within DSG by emphasizing building a stronger sense of community through both social events and informal bonding while increasing cross-committee collaboration. McConnell was elected speaker by a 22–17 vote. Senators approved members of the senate judiciary committee for Session 33. The group is responsible for approving members of the judiciary, overseeing disciplinary issues of senators and serving as the bridge between DSG and the judiciary. The following senators were nominated and appointed as members of the senate judiciary committee: Sophomore Ty Mastrian Sophomore Ryan Jankelowitz First-year Noah Bean Sophomore Cassatt Boatwright Sophomore Violet Weber First-year Daniel Nuñez First-year Anson Stine was nominated and appointed as chair of the committee. Senators voted to approve $500 to cover material costs for a survey initiative led by the Karsh Student Advisory Board tasked with giving students a “platform to communicate campus affordability and financial concerns directly to KSAB.” “[The survey] is what’s going to help tuition go down and financial aid go up,” said Labiba Siddiquei, director of first-generation and low-income affairs and a sophomore.

title: Transfer students support Duke's rising focus on transition resources, still notice support gaps
author: Ryan Kilgallen
text: The choice to transfer schools is not easy. With Duke’s rising transfer student enrollment, students say the least the University can do is facilitate a smooth transition. Last May, Duke admitted nearly 50% more transfer students than in previous years, aiming to roughly double its enrolled class size. The University has also partnered with Durham Technical Community College to guarantee Duke admission for select community college students pursuing associate’s degrees in engineering. Talks surrounding a similar program in the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences remain underway . Current transfer students said that administrative changes have improved their transition to the University, but that Duke still has room to improve as it helps students acclimate to life in Durham. With transfer students being a “huge priority” for Trinity, Dean of Transfer Claire Siburt indicated that the University is strategically engaging campus partners to holistically support incoming transfer cohorts. “We're thinking about how [to] lay a foundation, not just for an increase in numbers, but ... so that we can open ourselves to even more diversity of academic journeys,” Siburt said. Siburt leads the Transfer Student Support Network (TSS), a collection of over 20 offices on campus that meet regularly to discuss the needs of the transfer student community. According to Siburt, Trinity also launched a new transfer office last summer to “build alliances around campus” that can support transfer students through their entire Duke careers. “We’re moving into a space [where] we can really think about how we bolster the community-building events that we already have,” Siburt said. Siburt believes Trinity’s new undergraduate curriculum will improve the transfer student experience. Under the new curriculum, she explained, transfer students have a “prorated system” for arts and sciences requirements that can be adjusted depending on when a student matriculates. Additionally, according to Siburt, the new curriculum allows for greater flexibility as transfer students build their academic plans. Siburt emphasized that the credit transfer process seeks to ensure students have the prerequisite knowledge to succeed in their coursework rather than to check arbitrary boxes. “I see a lot more students thinking about their prior coursework in terms of preparation and in terms of their whole academic journey, rather than, ‘Does it count for this box, or does it count for that box?’” Siburt said. Since transfer students have attended another university before Duke, they possess a unique “comparison factor” that Siburt described as useful at identifying strengths or “blind spots.” “I think we just have a ton of opportunity to be intentional about how we support transfer students and how we foster that transfer student community as it grows, but also as it diversifies,” Siburt said. Many students emphasized the effectiveness of student-led transfer initiatives, like the Project Transfer experiential orientation program, but described mixed experiences with other academic and social elements of the transition to Duke. Junior Bryce Bowman said that Project Transfer provided a perfect welcome, tailored to the needs of a new-to-Duke but not-new-to-college community. In addition to receiving relevant guidance from the orientation program, student leaders helped to build a lasting community during the new transfer students’ first week at Duke. “I immediately felt connected to the upperclassmen who had also transferred before me, and they had good perspectives on what it was like to be a transfer student,” Bowman said. “... The best part about transferring was the way that Duke immediately set you up with students who understood what you were going through.” However, many transfer students also identified room for improvement. Sophomore Dylan Yang noted that selective student organizations on campus will often only accept first-year students or sophomores but not upperclassmen. “It’s not the school’s fault, but all the clubs are built where you get two years to join,” he said. “... We have a lot more ‘burden’ than the first-year students ... and then we have to figure out all these clubs.” Many students found classes at Duke to be challenging, but they also noted that academic support existed for those who asked and were persistent. “You have to take initiative, of course, to reach out to the dean,” said junior Derek Xue. He mentioned that getting help felt “like a kickball game,” where faculty or staff would redirect him to other people, but he could always find help in the end. Transfer students in Trinity also expressed frustration with the process of transferring course credits toward their Duke degree. Yang said the process was “slow and confusing,” which he partially attributed to the small number of staff supporting a large student cohort. In past years, Trinity and the Pratt School of Engineering each designated an academic dean to support the schools' incoming transfer students — around 66 in Trinity and five in Pratt in fall 2025 . Sophomore Amanda Acra, who transferred from Durham Tech, spent two years at the community college but only received credit for six courses. She compared this to the transfer policies at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, mentioning that UNC would have accepted all her credits from her time at Durham Tech. On its website, Trinity says it does not accept any course credits taken outside of a four-year college or university. Sophomore Cayden Hart noted similar challenges due to the limited input he was able to provide about the courses he took before Duke. Transfer students are required to submit course syllabi and transcripts, but they can only provide supplemental information on a case-by-case basis. “Despite me being the person who took these courses and understands them most intimately, I was [mostly] removed from the process,” Hart said. “... It would have been nice to hop on even just a Zoom call with the people that were helping ... because there were certain courses that I didn’t get counted for anything.” Xue, who transferred from a Canadian university, was able to give additional details about the courses he had taken. It greatly improved his experience, which felt more like a “conversation” compared to those of peers transferring from domestic institutions. Xue added that he found Duke to be “pretty flexible” with accepting credits, “as long as you present a good case.” Given the more limited number of Pratt transfers, new engineering students cited easier access to support. Bowman found his credit transfer and academic advising experience “fairly straightforward,” with his biggest challenge simply updating his academic plan when different departments would provide “new information” about course credits. Siburt said Trinity's transfer office has since hired a new project coordinator, who has helped in advising students, evaluating credits and preparing students for their first fall semester. As Duke looks ahead to welcoming future transfer student cohorts, student experiences could help to inform future decisions. But, with potential future partnerships already on the horizon, the role of student voices in decision-making remains to be seen.

title: Student Affairs freezes funding, revokes recognized status for Duke's SJP chapter
author: Ana Despa
text: Duke's Students for Justice in Palestine chapter has been suspended from organizational activity after the Office for Institutional Equity received complaints about an alleged antisemitic Instagram post, setting off a weeks-long dispute between the group and Student Affairs over the authority behind the disciplinary actions. On March 24, Ben Adams, senior associate dean of students for QuadEx, informed SJP leaders that OIE had received complaints regarding a March 13 flyer promoting a SJP general body meeting published on the group's Instagram. According to email correspondence obtained by The Chronicle between SJP leadership and OIE, 10 students had filed complaints. The flyer featured an illustration by artist Emory Douglas originally published in the newspaper The Black Panther in 1970, depicting two pigs — one labeled "U.S. Imperialism" holding the torch of liberty and the other labeled "Zionism" holding the Star of David. In a subsequent email to SJP leadership, Adams wrote that the depiction of the pig holding the Star of David is alleged harassment under the Policy on Prohibited Discrimination, Harassment and Related Misconduct (PPDHRM). Adams, on behalf of Student Affairs, asked the group to remove the post, which it voluntarily did. Then, Student Affairs restricted SJP’s recognized group status and froze its funding. Emails from SJP leadership to OIE show that the post was referred for including “antisemitic imagery,” which may violate the PPDHRM policy. Per the procedures, if the initial assessment, which is currently being carried out, is conclusive, SJP would receive a Notice of Allegations signifying that a formal investigation has been opened. Under PPDHRM, OIE holds the jurisdiction to enact measures against student organizations. And even then, those measures are labeled as “supportive,” which cannot be imposed for punitive or disciplinary reasons and are meant to "restore or preserve a party's access to Duke's programs and activities," the policy states. However, according to the emails, the funding and student group status were revoked by Student Affairs, not OIE. The Chronicle reviewed an email where SJP leadership sent OIE a written account of a meeting where OIE confirmed it did not authorize or initiate the freeze or Instagram post takedown request and that a formal investigation has not yet been opened. OIE did not dispute SJP's account of the meeting. Through subsequent correspondence with Adams, SJP leadership contested the jurisdiction of Student Affairs to impose disciplinary measures. “Duke SJP is committed to engaging with this process in good faith and following proper procedures,” SJP leadership wrote in a March 25 email to Adams. “We hold ourselves accountable to these standards and likewise expect any authoritative body to act within its proper scope and on the basis of clearly established policy.” Adams responded that the Dean of Students office holds independent authority to implement interim measures on recognized student organizations, describing organizational recognition as "a privilege" that carries responsibilities. He said the decision reflected both the current complaints and "the cumulative context of prior conversations" with SJP leadership over the past two years as well as the organization’s social media activity. “The Dean of Students' office makes independent assessments of whether interim measures are warranted to protect the health, safety or welfare of the university community or any member of it,” Adams wrote in a March 26 email. A day later, SJP leadership inquired for specific policies that gave Student Affairs the jurisdiction for the freeze. Adams has not responded to the group's inquiry. He declined The Chronicle’s request for comment on the matter “since it's still under review by Student Affairs.” In response to The Chronicle’s inquiry on the intention of the cartoon in the general body meeting post, SJP leadership said the image “was never intended to be antisemitic” and that criticizing Zionism as a political movement is “not the same as targeting Jewish people.” They added that in the same issue of The Black Panther where the image appeared, it explicitly stated that the struggle is directed solely against what it calls the Zionist regime.

title: Former IMF deputy managing editor Gita Gopinath talks shifting global economy at Duke event
author: Yashraj Vishwakarma
text: Gita Gopinath, Gregory and Ania Coffey professor of economics at Harvard University, spoke with David Rubenstein, Trinity ‘70, about real-world macroeconomics through the lens of a rapidly changing global economy in a fireside chat on April 8. The discussion, moderated by Manoj Mohanan, interim dean of the Sanford School, was part of the Sanford School of Public Policy’s Rubenstein Distinguished Lecture Series . Having previously served as deputy managing director of the International Monetary Fund, Gopinath described the IMF’s founding as rooted in the belief that conflicts between nations could be resolved through greater economic integration. “The more interconnected we are in economics and finance, the more stability we have in the world,” Gopinath said. The talk opened with a discussion of the staggering $39 trillion national debt . Although Gopinath stressed that the national debt is not creating a “crisis at this current moment,” she cautioned that the trajectory of debt growth is unsustainable. According to the Congressional Budget Office , public debt this year sits at 101% of GDP and the number is projected to increase to 120% of GDP by 2036. Also on the topic of fiscal policy, Gopinath questioned the government’s reliance on industrial policy and levying of tariffs against foreign nations. Although she recognized prioritizing domestic production and reviving manufacturing industries at home, she noted their adverse impact on other industries that have lost economic support. “My perspective is that when you favor one industry, you are disfavoring another sector, and that therefore the economy doesn't grow as much,” Gopinath said. Gopinath also disagreed with the federal government’s use of tariffs as a means of reducing the trade deficit, which she said is a consequence of “unbalanced growth strategies.” She added that tariffs are not the solution to the tensions between the U.S. and China because “they just don’t work.” Rubenstein then asked Gopinath about inflation, which has recently accelerated to its highest rate in nearly two years. She attributed inflation to the increasing frequency of “supply side shocks” such as the Russo-Ukrainian war and the most recent Iran war, and their domino effect on oil and gas prices. “I think we're going to be in this world where we will see more volatility in inflation. I don't think that translates necessarily into the average inflation rate drifting up sizably,” Gopinath said. Gopinath also addressed the federal government’s pressure on the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates, as part of a broader question regarding central bank independence. Notably, she emphasized that the Federal Reserve should have independence with operational decisions based on the mandate provided by Congress. She criticized politicians for using their platforms to promise lower interest rates, even though such decisions lie solely with the Federal Reserve. Another point of discussion centered around the role of the dollar as a global reserve currency. In particular, she noted the rise of the renminbi, ruble and most of all, gold, as emerging alternatives, but that the dollar continues to be viewed as the dominant global currency. However, she pushed back against the narrative that a strong dollar is the reason for a rising trade deficit, and the dollar should be devalued to make exports cheaper. Rubenstein and Gopinath also discussed, at length, the prospect of pursuing a doctoral or undergraduate degree in economics. She emphasized the analytical nature of economics, as well as one’s ability to branch out into adjacent fields, whether that is investment banking or public policy. “I think that training in economics would get you to ask the right questions,” Gopinath said.

title: As DKU's future hangs in the balance, Duke leaves questions unanswered
author: Claire Cranford
text: In December 2025, former Executive Vice Chancellor of Duke Kunshan University Denis Simon received a letter. A China scholar of forty years, Simon had been teaching a graduate seminar at Duke on Chinese science and technology policy. The letter said the course would not be continuing. Budget constraints, it explained. But Simon knew the money was there — $9,500, sitting in an endowment. His course had regularly drawn 15 graduate students on average over the past three years and its evaluations were uniformly excellent. He climbed the chain of command. According to Simon, the Provost’s office said it wasn’t their decision. His direct supervisor said he had argued for Simon and lost. The explanations shifted first to budget, then enrollment composition and finally, that there were simply too many courses on China. The discussions seemed peculiar to Simon. Just two months prior in October 2025, Duke released a 30-page global strategy report titled “The World at Duke, and Duke in the World” calling for bold bridge-building, open inquiry and a renewed commitment to international collaboration. But amid the report's sweeping language and geographic priority areas spanning East Africa, South Asia, Southeast Asia, the Middle East and Latin America, one area was conspicuously absent: China. Missing alongside it was a clear vision for DKU, one of the University’s most expensive, politically sensitive and strategically consequential global investments. According to an October interview with Krishna Udayakumar, who chaired the Global Priorities Committee, the future of DKU “was not part of the scope” of what the committee took on. Disagreements about DKU’s identity have persisted both internally and at Duke since at least 2021. Pressures from internal faculty reassessment and the federal government loom large over the future of the joint venture. The inaugural agreement governing DKU and Duke, called the “Cooperative Education Agreement,” expires in 2028. Renegotiations are reportedly underway. Thus far, the University has described the venture as a “generational commitment” that has enrolled more than 2,200 students and produced top-notch faculty. But at least in the most recent months, Duke has been unable to answer critical questions about what the campus is for and what the University is prepared to sacrifice to keep it. The story of DKU begins with an idea that was slightly different from the one actually realized. When Duke first presented the concept of a China-based campus to its Academic Council in 2009, the driving force was the Fuqua School of Business. The vision was a commercial executive master's in business administration program that would provide a foothold in a rapidly growing market. In January 2010, Duke entered into an agreement with Durham’s sister city Kunshan , securing 200 acres and a promise that the local municipal government would fund construction. Ground was broken that September. Then, things began to go slightly awry. The original Chinese partner university fell through. Faculty in Durham raised concerns about academic freedom and brand dilution. Construction was delayed throughout 2012, partly due to the global financial crisis and a sweeping anti-corruption campaign in China that threatened the market for executive education. What had begun as a business school pivoted into an undergraduate liberal arts curriculum partnered with Wuhan University, something with no real precedent in Chinese higher education because of its Western-centered design. The institution opened its doors to graduate students in 2014 and undergraduates in 2018, but when Simon arrived at DKU as executive vice chancellor in 2015, he found an institution whose relationship to Duke's broader ambitions had never been clearly defined. When he reported to Duke's Board of Trustees, they kept asking the same question: How does DKU fit into Duke's larger global strategy? “Those questions were lingering,” Simon said, “and during my five years as EVC, they kept re-emerging at the board level and the operational level.” Donald Taylor, professor in the Sanford School of Public Policy, chaired the University Priorities Committee that issued the report to the Academic Council ahead of its 2016 vote authorizing DKU's undergraduate program. He voted in favor. But at a February Academic Council meeting earlier this year, he said something he had been sitting with for some time. “If I went back and knew everything I knew now, I would have probably been opposed to it,” Taylor said. He was careful to add later in an email to The Chronicle: “That is only clear to me in retrospect. Duke's task now is to make DKU the best that we can.” The Global Strategy report opens with an acknowledgment of "geopolitical uncertainty and fiscal pressures" and articulates three thematic priority areas — health, climate and economic development — aligned with Duke’s broader institutional priorities. But Simon, who had met with Udayakumar roughly two weeks before the report's release, read it with the eye of someone who has designed global strategies at multiple universities and headed up DKU. “The lack of a strategic vision for DKU in a document like this is one of the most conspicuous by its absence,” he said. “My sense was it was more like a pre-strategy document rather than the strategy document,” he said. “It does not highlight critical choices that must be made. It does not specify measurable targets. It doesn't tell you how we're going to take all of these aspirations ... and make them operational.” In a written statement to The Chronicle, Udayakumar wrote that Duke anticipates building on what it has learned from DKU and other partnerships to “inform and strengthen different models of regional and global research and education.” Unlike his statement in October, he did not explicitly reply to The Chronicle’s request for comment on DKU's place in the strategy, but acknowledged that the University’s regions of focus “will evolve over time based on engagement and activities that are faculty- and program-driven.” When Duke entered the joint venture with Wuhan University in 2013, Kunshan was among the wealthiest township-level cities in China , flush with revenue generated largely by Taiwanese manufacturing investment. The city committed to building and funding the campus infrastructure, a significant enough incentive that Duke could enter the venture without paying for construction itself. Since then, the financial assumptions behind the partnership have shifted. Cross-strait tensions between China and Taiwan have driven much of Taiwanese investment out of Kunshan , while local government budgets across China face increasing strain. China's Ministry of Education has not agreed to subsidize DKU, and, according to Simon, Wuhan University does not provide any funding — only faculty and advisory support. “There's not enough tuition generated to pay for the overall operation of the campus,” Simon said. The enrollment numbers tell part of that story. DKU welcomed 490 students to its Class of 2028 in fall 2024, an increase of 100 students from the previous year. The Class of 2029 grew again to 600 students, the largest and most diverse class in school history. Simon said that the decision to admit more than 500 students was driven by a need to generate additional revenue. “Money is not a small issue for this campus,” Simon said, adding that without additional funding sources, “[any] deficit has to be paid by Duke and by the city of Kunshan due to the refusal to subsidize.” Despite financial woes and strategic uncertainty, the Kunshan campus has found some success. In December 2025, the Arts & Sciences Council held a closed meeting on the state of DKU, where a panel of individuals involved in overseeing the Kunshan campus presented updates. The Chronicle obtained a draft of the minutes from the meeting, during which participants were discouraged from taking notes. According to the minutes, panelists concluded DKU primarily as a successful liberal arts institution — a humanities-based institution not included in the global report — rather than the research-oriented and technological enterprise envisioned when DKU first opened its doors. “It is not a mini-Duke,” said Melanie Manion, Vor Broker family professor of political science and chair of the DKU advisory committee, according to the minutes. "It has an innovative, interdisciplinary curriculum. It is highly successful." Noah Pickus, associate provost and dean of academic strategy and learning innovation at DKU, noted that acknowledging the “limits and challenges to what it means to be interdisciplinary” is equally important. Specifically, he pointed to the tension between the institution’s mission and academic expectations from students and parents during the closed session. “There is pressure on for [more traditional things],” he said, according to the minutes. “I believe that DKU succeeds or fails based on the quality of that education and its distinctiveness.” The concerns around the student experience are not new. Around 2021, the Executive Committee of the Arts & Sciences Council compiled a report of over 100 pages documenting concerns about academic freedom, religious freedom, surveillance and student well-being at DKU. Students who spoke to the committee did so anonymously, afraid for their academic standing and futures. The committee submitted 24 specific questions to the administration. The meeting with then-Provost Sally Kornbluth to discuss the findings was canceled after she received them. To the committee's knowledge, the questions were never answered. At the December 2025 Arts & Sciences Council meeting, Professor Mona Hassan, who helped compile the report, cited one student’s reflection that stayed with the committee: "It's not really a university when it's not conducive to learning." Later, President Vincent Price, addressing the Academic Council in March, opted for different language to describe DKU's purpose as having shifted toward “something that really is a liberal arts institution.” On the ground, shaping the school's identity has fallen on a disproportionately young, overextended faculty. There are roughly 100 assistant professors and only about 25 full professors, of whom eight are Duke faculty. According to December 2025 Arts & Sciences Council minutes, Professor of Physics Joshua Socolar, chair of the DKU advisory committee, said faculty teaching and service loads far exceed those of their Durham counterparts. Students feel the strain as well. The campus operates on seven-week mini-sessions designed for interdisciplinary depth, but the structure has been unpopular among students . “There's a lot of unhappiness on the campus right now,” Simon said. “Students don't like the seven-week modular curriculum, and a bunch of other things they don't like, but the curriculum is a big issue.” Professors also pointed to a persistent cultural, geographic and intellectual divide between Chinese domestic and international students and cited Duke’s long-distance control of DKU as another concern. Socolar also pressed on the issue, which cuts to the core of the 2028 renegotiation: What role will Duke actually play in the partnership, if any? Beyond internal tensions, the University faces an even larger obstacle in answering Socolar’s questions: an American political climate increasingly hostile to interaction with China. In May 2025, Reps. John Moolenaar and Tim Walberg — chairs of the House Select Committee on the Chinese Communist Party and the House Education and Workforce Committee, respectively — sent Price a letter alleging that Duke’s partnership with Wuhan University “creates a direct pipeline between U.S. innovation and China's military-industrial complex.” The Chronicle found many claims in the letter to be false or misleading. The letter also named four universities — Georgia Tech, University of California, Berkeley, the University of Michigan and Oakland University — as examples of institutions that had ended partnerships in China, urging Duke to follow suit. Simon does not think Duke should follow those universities out the door. He has spent his career arguing that the security-versus-openness framing is a false binary — that U.S. universities became great precisely because of international engagement. “The notion that somehow every Chinese is an industrial spy,” he said, “is just fallacious.” Legitimate security concerns require targeted, highly selective responses rather than wholesale retreat, he added. Although Simon noted that the Justice Department's China Initiative , which prosecuted dozens of Chinese-American researchers between 2018 and its disbandment by the Biden administration in February 2022, saw nearly every case collapse, he is clear-eyed about the political moment that views any collaboration with China as a national security threat. “Right now, it definitely is [the dominant paradigm],” he said. “It was during Trump one. It became important. And now during Trump two, it's still important.” Amid rising pressure from both the executive and legislative branches, Duke has said remarkably little publicly, despite extensive discussion behind the scenes. When Duke has commented publicly, it has done so with great care. Price's March statement confirmed that renegotiations were underway, that the University remained committed and that the question of committing was “less an if than a how.” What the “how” entails — including funding, academic freedom and potential limits — remains unclear. In a Thursday email to The Chronicle, administrators wrote that “Duke remains committed to DKU and its liberal arts mission, grounded in Duke’s academic standards and values. We appreciate your interest; partnership discussions are ongoing, and we will share updates as plans develop and stakeholders are engaged.” Administrators did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment on the timeline for renegotiating the operating agreement, whether it considers DKU central to its global strategy or if it answered the letter from Reps. Moolenar and Walberg. Administrators also did not comment on DKU’s financial sustainability. Editor's note: This story was updated Wednesday morning to reflect that President Price referred to DKU as a "liberal arts" institution not a "human rights" institution during the March Academic Council meeting. The Chronicle regrets the error.

title: Chronquiry: Why is a statue missing at the Duke Chapel entrance?
author: Luca Gragnoli
text: At its dedication on June 2, 1935 , six statues lined the entrance of the Duke Chapel. Then, in 2017, the statues became five after Duke University President Vincent Price removed one — a statue of Confederate General Robert E. Lee . The Chapel’s entry portal features notable Southern and religious figures, including Girolamo Savonarola, a Renaissance-era reformer preacher, Martin Luther, founder of Lutheranism, John Wycliffe, the first English translator of the Bible, President Thomas Jefferson and Sidney Lanier, a Confederate poet and musician. The position once filled by Lee’s statue remains empty. The statues were first installed in 1932, toward the end of the Duke Chapel’s construction. It remains unclear who chose the six figures lining the Chapel’s entry. As most European-Gothic cathedrals were Catholic and depicted Saints, the Chapel’s sculptors were unsure of who to depict, given the Duke family’s Methodist affiliation. A professor at Vanderbilt was asked for advice on the matter. He suggested six men who would represent “historical figures from the Protestant and Methodist traditions and from the American South,” according to a metal plaque next to the Chapel entrance. Among these six men was Lee , a ‘Soldier of the South’ best known for his role as commander of the Confederate Army during the American Civil War. The son of a Revolutionary War officer, Lee was a graduate from the U.S. Military Academy at West Point and served in the Mexican-American War. He then served as superintendent of West Point in the 1850s. Lee’s estate owned nearly 200 enslaved people, and once Virginia seceded from the Union in 1861, Lee resigned from the U.S. Army and joined the Confederacy. After Lee was chosen to be portrayed at the Chapel, some key University administrators expressed dissatisfaction with the idea. University President William Preston Few and Board of Trustees member William Perkins expressed their feelings in letters exchanged in September 1932. “Do you think there is anything that can be done about the ‘Lee’ statue in the entrance to the Chapel, or should we just accept it as one of the core disappointments of life?” Few wrote to Perkins on Sept. 9. “We will just have to accept it as one of our disappointments,” Few replied on Sept. 13. “I do not believe anybody will ever take it to be Lee.” The removal of Lee’s statue from the entrance to the Chapel followed the deadly Unite the Right rally in Charlottesville, Va., on Aug. 12, 2017. The rally, which included white supremacist and neo-Nazi sympathizers, protested the removal of the statue of General Robert E. Lee from Charlottesville’s Market Street Park, formerly named Lee Park. The rally degenerated into clashes with counter-protesters, resulting in more than 30 injuries. After the initial fight broke out, a self-identified white supremacist deliberately drove his car into a crowd of counter-protesters, killing one person and injuring 35 more. Under mounting pressure from alumni, students and campus organizations, and following the vandalism of Lee’s statue at the chapel, President Price ordered the removal of the figure a week after the rally. Price noted that doing so would make the campus more welcoming for everyone. In a 2017 email announcing the news to the Duke community, Price said his decision "presents an opportunity for us to learn and heal," adding that the statue will be preserved so that students can study Duke’s "complex past and take part in a more inclusive future." At the time, the removal was praised and welcomed by many students and alumni, who expressed their relief in having their desires heard by the administration. In September of 2017, Price convened a Commission on Memory and History to review the future of the empty recess remaining at the Duke Chapel. The Commission concluded that the space should remain vacant for a year so that the University could “engage in a process of education and community engagement,” as the chapel’s entry plaque reads. Following this period, Price announced that the recess would be left vacant as a memory of the past. The Rev. Dr. Luke A. Powery, Dean of Duke Chapel, said that the space might represent "a hole that is in the heart of the United States of America, and perhaps in our own human hearts — that hole that is from the sin of racism and hatred of any kind." At the same time, Price launched the Truth, Racial Healing and Transformation Center, which aims to eliminate social structures that perpetuate racism, and initiated a review of controversial names of facilities and buildings on campus. "At Duke, we have the courage and integrity to engage productively around our disagreements without resorting to incivility or vandalism, instead finding collective strength in open-minded participation and mutual respect," Price wrote in an email in September 2017 announcing these initiatives. In 2018, the Commission on Memory and History recommended that the Carr building — named after Julian Carr, a notable white supremacist who donated the land that would become East Campus — to “Classroom Building.” The name was changed later that year.

title: Trump administration backs off NIH funding cap, easing Duke's biggest financial threat
author: Ana Despa
text: The Trump administration’s legal fight to slash federal research dollars has seemingly subsided. The deadline to appeal a lower court ruling against the 15% cap on indirect cost reimbursement rates from the National Institutes of Health passed last week without action or public comment from the Trump administration. For now, what Duke administrators once called their “biggest financial concern” has receded. The NIH first shared plans to implement a 15% cap in February 2025. The ruling was promptly blocked by a district court injunction. In January, the First Circuit Court of Appeals agreed the cap was unlawful. The Department of Justice had until April 6 to appeal the decision. Indirect costs, also known as facilities & administrative costs, refer to the expenditures needed to maintain laboratory spaces, equipment and other administrative actions. Institutions renegotiate their indirect cost reimbursement rates with the federal government roughly every two to four years. Duke’s rate with the NIH is 61.5%. This means that for every dollar of grant funding issued directly to researchers, 61.5 additional cents are given to the University. Under the Trump administration’s initial desired cap of 15%, Duke was anticipating a loss of $194 million annually, per a press release . The financial threat prompted significant action. In the summer of 2025, the University announced plans to cut $350 million from its expense base over the next five years, citing the uncertainty around the cap as one reason. It has already reduced $229 million this fiscal year by cutting departmental spending and prompting the departure of more than 700 employees. At the School of Medicine alone, which relies almost exclusively on research funding from the NIH, administrators planned a $125 million reduction in spending by 2026. Still, the issue may not be fully settled. Catherine Liao, Duke Health’s vice president for government relations, said in a Friday webinar that the executive branch remains interested in pursuing the cap, potentially through Congress. However, action in the near future seems unlikely with lawmakers focused on the midterm elections. Liao has been meeting with members of Congress to emphasize the importance of federal research funding. For now, she said, Duke’s reimbursement rate appears secure. “We made the point how important those costs are to research,” Liao said. “... We're certainly happy that the court ruling is final and that our costs are current and safe as they are.” The proposed cap raised concerns across higher education, particularly about its potential impact on basic research. Though basic research often lacks immediate commercial payoff, it underpins major medical and scientific breakthroughs. “I think there’s a misunderstanding of what indirect costs are,” said Robert Jenal, the former executive vice chancellor at University of Massachusetts Medical School who helped negotiate its reimbursement rates with the federal government. “The name is kind of a misnomer, almost, because they’re real costs for the university.” The 15% number, Jenal said, seemed arbitrary and would have made basic science research unaffordable for many. He emphasized that coverage of indirect costs is part of a broader partnership between universities and the federal government, and that typically, the reimbursement rate is accurately negotiated to cover those overhead costs. “The government is a partner. You may not have built that building to do research for the federal government if you couldn’t get help supporting it,” he said. Duke publicly condemned the proposal as a “significant challenge” in February 2025 but has since gone quiet. Administrators did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment on whether they are changing their cost-cutting targets now that the threat has subsided. Although the indirect cost reimbursement rate battle has slowed, the Trump administration has continued to outline budgetary reductions at the NIH, which would reduce the awards the agency could give to university researchers. Trump proposed a 12% reduction in the NIH’s overall budget in the FY27 budget request after requesting a 40% reduction last year. However, Liao noted that Congress rejected that request last year — and likely will again. “We’ll be continuing in Washington to see how this Congress acts over the next few months,” Liao said.

title: Submit nominations for Chron15: Duke’s icons, leaders and pioneers
author: Staff Reports
text: Every year, The Chronicle releases a list of icons, leaders and pioneers within the Duke community. We are curating this year's list, and we need your help! The Chronicle is seeking individuals at Duke who are making a difference, creating change, inspiring others, discovering something ground-breaking or simply leading their lives in service and with integrity. Pioneers are people and organizations who push the University forward; leaders are those that use their power for good as they shape the University’s future; icons are the people everyone knows, whether it be for their kindness, passion, talent, intellect or a combination of all three. In previous years, we’ve recognized star athletes , student leaders and campus innovators who have made tangible differences in the Duke community. The Chronicle also accepts group nominations.The 2024-25 Chron15 included Shave and Buzz and Duke Graduate Student Union, for example. If you know anyone who sounds like the description above, submit their name here by the last day of classes, Wednesday, April 22.

title: Despite decrease in NIH grants, School of Medicine sees positive signs for future federal funding
author: Dylan Halper
text: Duke School of Medicine leaders are cautiously optimistic about the school's financial footing, even as it received roughly $78 million less in federal research funding than at this point in the fiscal year before the Trump administration took office. Administrators said in a Friday webinar that National Institutes of Health grants to the University are resuming following the government shutdown in the fall and that it had since resumed staff hiring, moving away from a prior cost-cutting measure . “The last year or so has been really tough, but we are moving in the right direction,” Mary Klotman, executive vice president for health affairs and dean of the School of Medicine, said. The webinar also touched on the school’s plans to implement artificial intelligence more widely across “research, education, patient care and administration.” According to Geeta Swamy, executive vice dean for clinical sciences and research administration, the school has received slightly over 1,100 awards from the NIH since the start of the federal fiscal year on Oct. 1. The total is about 100 less than the number of awards at this time last year. Swamy said that the 1,100 grants totaled to around $78 million less than the same point in FY24, before the Trump administration took office, and around $19 million less than the same point last year, which partially overlapped with President Trump’s tenure. The decrease in federal funding comes as the Trump administration has attempted to limit the amount of grants the NIH awards, often delaying already approved funding. According to Nature, 5,844 NIH grants were suspended or canceled altogether in 2025. Notably, the Trump administration froze $108 million to the school in late July. Administrators did not comment on the status of the $108 million, which represents nearly 19% of the school’s total NIH funding, in the Friday webinar. Swamy said that nationally, higher education institutions have been experiencing the same decreases in funding as Duke. She said the NIH issued slightly over $1 billion less in funding to colleges and universities this year than at the same time last year. “It is good to know that we're trending in the same way as the national picture, but obviously we're going to keep a very close eye on what all of that is looking like,” Swamy said. Despite the overall reduction, Swamy said she expects the flow of federal funding to pick up soon. She noted that after the government shutdown last fall, NIH funds were frozen “for a few months,” but that since January, the school has experienced an uptick in funds. Swamy also said that the school is monitoring research awards for any restrictions attached to them, but has noted many less requirements compared to last year. “We're hopeful that we can continue to see those things continuing the trend that we'd like it to,” Swamy said. Catherine Liao, vice president for government relations for Duke Health, also offered other reasons to be optimistic about increased NIH funding. She explained that although Trump’s FY27 budget request proposes a $5 billion cut to the NIH, approximately a 12% reduction in its overall budget, she expects Congress will not agree to the proposal. Last year, after Trump requested around a 40% cut to the NIH, Congress actually increased funding by about 1%, according to Liao. Liao also said that last week, the Trump administration gave up its legal fight to impose a 15% cap on indirect cost reimbursement rates for research funding from the NIH. The policy was blocked in court in January, but the Department of Justice did not appeal to the Supreme Court by the necessary deadline. The University had previously cited this proposed cap — which would limit the amount of money the University receives for research costs related to facilities or administrative matters — as a significant threat to University funding. “We’re certainly happy that the court ruling is final and that our costs are current and safe as they are,” Liao said. Amid the rosier federal outlook, Heather Hamby, executive vice dean of administration, said that the school would continue to evaluate its costs to be able to engage in “strategic investments.” Last summer, the school announced plans to cut $125 million from its budget to prepare for less federal funding, but Hamby did not provide updates as to the school’s progress toward that goal. However, Hamby did say that there is no hiring freeze in effect. Instead, the school engages with hiring on an “evaluation of request” basis, where a committee assesses the necessity of the hire and whether it is a replacement or new position. “We are not on a freeze, but I do think we've been very cautious, given our economic situation,” Hamby said. She still cautioned researchers to be responsible with how they use their research funding, despite the more positive funding landscape. “We are wanting each of you to plan carefully with your leadership on the best use and the most prioritized use of funds,” Hamby said. “ ... That's kind of our new world.” As the University seeks to embed AI across campus, the School of Medicine is working on its own approach to the technology. “I want to take a moment to encourage our entire community to thoughtfully embrace AI — not as an abstract concept, but as a tool that can help make our work more efficient and allow us to focus more fully on our impact,” Klotman said. To that end, Klotman recently announced the appointment of Christopher Lindsell, former director of data science and biostatistics for the Duke Clinical Research Institute, to serve in a new position as vice dean for data science and AI starting this month. In the two-year role, Lindsell will be tasked with coordinating all efforts on AI across the school by developing “durable practices and investments” to help lead the nation on AI strategy. “Technology is moving quickly, expectations are rising and the pressure to both act and succeed is real,” Lindsell said. Lindsell said that AI has the capability to ease administrative burdens and help doctors engage in better clinical care and decisionmaking. Still, Klotman emphasized that the school does not intend for AI to replace the human interactions at the center of health care. “AI is a tool that should enhance our ability to teach, learn, discover and care for patients, but certainly not distance us from each other,” she said.

title: Jewish Life at Duke’s Host Your Own Seder program facilitates cultural exchange during Passover
author: Lila Cohen
text: From the Rubenstein Arts Center to East Campus residence halls, Seders — religious dinners traditionally hosted on the first and second nights of the Jewish holiday Passover — took place all over campus last week as part of Jewish Life at Duke’s Host Your Own Seder program. Jewish and non-Jewish Seder attendees reflected on their experiences observing Passover at Duke, sharing cultures and traditions. In addition to attending student-hosted Seders, community members also had the option to participate in communal Seders at the Freeman Center for Jewish Life or Chabad at Duke’s Fleishman House. First-year Eli Sobel hosted a Seder in the basement of Gilbert-Addoms residence hall — called “G.A. Down Under” or "GADU" — on April 1. Ahead of his Seder, Sobel created the haggadah, a book containing readings and prayers that explain the story of Passover and the rituals of the meal, by combining historical details with elements from Sobel and his co-host’s personal traditions. Of his 10 Seder attendees, only two were Jewish. “They were all pretty enthusiastic,” he said, describing the non-Jewish attendees. Sobel incorporated a small play about the story of Passover into the Seder, asking all attendees to participate. He served as the play’s narrator and described the play as “the most successful part” of the meal. One attendee, who does not typically celebrate the holiday, decided to try keeping kosher for Passover after attending the Seder. “Opportunities like this don't come around very often, and when they do, it's great to really take advantage,” Sobel said, adding that his friends are “all very big on showing off [their] own individual cultures and religions to each other.” Although JLD’s Host Your Own Seder program typically occurs on the first night of Passover, Hoof ‘n Horn had scheduled a show of its spring production Ragtime for the holiday’s second night, and senior Lily Egol wanted to ensure she could observe the holiday with fellow Jewish peers in the musical theater group. Egol, co-music director and pianist for Ragtime, reached out to JLD about hosting a Seder on April 2. After receiving approval, she organized a shortened Seder before Hoof ‘n Horn’s evening show, encouraging all to participate. “I’ve always wanted to host a Seder,” Egol said. “It was really cool to take some of my family's traditions and bring them to a new group of people and celebrate with friends.” Egol also added that she hopes that Hoof ‘n Horn will continue to host Seders after she graduates this spring. First-year Arielle Misra, who had never been to a Seder before, described it as “really interesting.” Misra learned about the event through Hoof ‘n Horn and decided to attend. “I think people are a little bit scared to go outside of their comfort zone ... It's good that there are a lot of smaller Seders for people to feel more comfortable learning about [Passover],” she said. Egol appreciated the opportunity to share Jewish traditions with a diverse group, noting that a Seder includes explanations of the rituals and history behind the holiday, which naturally makes it more accessible to newcomers. The holiday of Passover began April 1 at sundown and ended April 9 at sundown. Chabad at Duke hosted students for all eight days and announced that 500 people participated in its Seders at the Fleishman House. Students were able to find kosher-for-Passover food at the Freeman Center for Jewish Life for breakfast, lunch and dinner. Other campus locations also offered kosher-for-Passover meals, including at Trinity Cafe, Devil’s Krafthouse, Gothic Grill and Beyu Blue.

title: Here’s how to participate in SHAPE Week on campus
author: Max Tendler
text: Duke Sexual Harassment & Assault Prevention & Education is holding its annual SHAPE Week from Monday through Thursday as part of visibility efforts during Sexual Assault Awareness Month, which takes place every April. Through a series of events and support drives, the week aims to honor survivors, raise awareness about sexual violence and promote support and resources that are available on campus.
