Sophomore Ava Claar, director of outreach for SHAPE, wrote that the week’s events aim to demonstrate the need for “ongoing education, advocacy and collective effort to make Duke’s campus a safer and more supportive place, especially for having difficult but necessary conversations.” In spring 2020, Jacob Jeffries and Eden Schumer, both Trinity ‘22 and former Duke Student Government senators, helped organize the first SHAPE Week in collaboration with over 20 student groups. Notably, it featured a speech by Nadia Murad, recipient of the 2018 Nobel Peace Prize. Since then, Claar said that SHAPE Week has worked to continue “creating spaces for important conversations.” Take Back the Night Walk, the concluding and most visible event of SHAPE Week, began in 2022 and has been held annually since then. At the walk, students march across campus, standing in solidarity with survivors of sexual violence. Here’s how you can participate in the march and other events happening this year. SHAPE events this week From 11 a.m. to 1 p.m. Monday on Bryan Center Plaza, SHAPE is partnering with the Durham Crisis Response Center to discuss the DCRC’s domestic violence shelters and volunteer opportunities. They will also run a “necessities drive,” collecting household and hygiene items for victims. On Tuesday and Wednesday, SHAPE will continue its DCRC donation drive while hosting a poster-making session from 11 a.m. to 1 p.m. on B.C. Plaza to prepare for the annual march. Markers and poster boards will be available on B.C. Plaza for those interested in making signs. SHAPE Week culminates in a pair of events Thursday, beginning with a guest lecture on sexual assault adjudication and ending with the Take Back the Night March. The conversation will take place at 5:30 p.m. in Sanford 004 and feature guest speaker Tamara Lave, Title IX expert and professor of law at the University of Miami, who will discuss how colleges address sexual assault cases on their campuses. Shortly thereafter, at 6:45 p.m., students will march from the Sanford building to East Campus to honor previous sexual assault prevention advocates, support survivors and increase visibility on campus.

title: Roy Cooper champions himself as affordability candidate at Durham rally
author: Max Tendler
text: Former North Carolina Gov. Roy Cooper headlined a rally in Durham on Thursday, promoting his run for U.S. Senate alongside other Democratic elected officials. Cooper, who is running for the Senate seat being vacated by retiring Republican Sen. Thom Tillis, spoke after two hours of speeches all emphasizing the same three ideas: Cooper is for affordability, health care and combating corruption. “North Carolina can't afford any more excuses. We need to make stuff cost less,” Cooper said, alluding to his campaign’s core mantra. Cooper emphasized his disdain for the “chaos” in Washington, and positioned himself as a man of the people, in contrast to the politicians who he believes are letting “working people [be] left behind.” “Going to Washington, D.C. just wasn’t in my plans,” Cooper said, highlighting his long career of public service in North Carolina. “... [But] I couldn’t watch the people of my state be treated this way. Not without a fight.” Cooper announced his run for Senate in July, and resoundingly won the Democratic primary last month after facing minimal opposition. He is a familiar face across North Carolina, having held statewide office for nearly 25 years. Cooper served as state attorney general for four terms from 2001 until 2017, and then as governor until 2025. Before that, Cooper began his journey in elected office in 1987, serving in the N.C. House of Representatives until 1991. Afterward, he was elevated to the N.C. Senate, where he remained for 10 years, becoming majority leader in 1997. During his governorship, Cooper notably worked with the N.C. General Assembly to expand Medicaid which has been credited with providing health insurance to around 720,000 North Carolinians. However, in general, his relationship with the Republican-controlled state legislature was contentious, as he issued over 100 vetoes across his tenure, a record number. He also oversaw the state’s response to numerous natural disasters, including Hurricane Helene in 2024. Throughout the rally, Cooper portrayed himself as an everyman, while framing Whatley as an opportunistic former oil lobbyist who is “out of touch” with North Carolinians. Whatley did serve as a partner at lobbying firm HBW Resources from 2007 to 2022, where he worked on issues related to oil and gas development. From 2008 to 2019, he was also executive vice president for the Consumer Energy Alliance, an advocacy group backed by multiple fossil fuel companies according to Politico . In government, Whatley has a shorter history, serving in the George W. Bush administration as principal deputy assistant secretary for the U.S. Department of Energy from 2001 to 2003 and then as chief of staff to former Republican Sen. Elizabeth Dole, Woman’s College ‘58. Whatley's most prominent role came when he was elected chairman of the Republican National Committee in March 2024, after having served as chairman of the N.C. Republican Party since 2019. He remained in the role until Aug. 2025, when he resigned following his announcement to run for U.S. Senate. Cooper’s speech touched on many of the winning issues in the Democratic Party’s current playbook. He brought Durham residents on stage to talk about how they were struggling to make ends meet, lamenting the high cost of groceries and health care. “And now, with the war in Iran, here comes higher gas prices,” Cooper said. Rallying behind Cooper were several Democratic elected officials, including state Sen. Natalie Murdock and U.S. Rep. Valerie Foushee, both of whom represent Durham. “Roy Cooper has been a trusted partner in the fight for equity and opportunity for all communities,” Foushee said. “... Roy Cooper is running for us. Michael Whatley is running just to help himself and his fellow D.C. insiders and lobbyists.” Also among the lineup was Anita Earls, N.C Supreme Court associate justice, who is running for re-election this November to maintain a Democratic seat on the Republican-dominated court. Cooper endorsed her specifically, stating that her “re-election matters for true justice.” Democratic Gov. Josh Stein vouched for Cooper’s character, describing his experience working under him as senior deputy attorney general. He’s a “good man and a good friend,” Stein said, emphasizing Cooper’s work going after predatory payday lenders and prosecuting criminals. In the four public polls taken since the primary, Cooper has held the lead, ranging from a margin of 3 to 14 percentage points, according to the New York Times.

title: Assemble the Georges: Duke George Society seeks to reshape campus
author: Jackson Kitts
text: A new society has popped up on campus, with a peculiar membership rule. To join, you must be named George. The Duke George Society was founded in February when senior George Nathaniel, and juniors George Donnelly and George Fenimore began hanging up flyers to assemble all the Georges across campus. Chatter spread among Duke’s student body and Fizz upvotes spiked. All of it traces back to the society. “The state of the world right now is probably related to the lack of George that we've seen,” Nathaniel said. “You can look at a graph, you see problems go up, George goes down ... We’re trying to change the world by bringing us together.” What actually occurs behind closed doors at the society’s meetings remains unclear. Members declined to share details. Yet, the society’s leadership insisted that their presence will soon be felt across campus and beyond in a larger way. Their Instagram account is public and has posted content highlighting members’ spring break trips and meetups on campus. “We exist in the public. People know that there is a George society, but what we're up to, don't worry,” Nathaniel said. “We'll make ourselves known to you in due time.” The society hopes that going forward, they will continue to bring together more Georges and reach a day where everyone is named George. “We aim to create a sort of legacy of George,” Fenimore said. “It started with us, but boy, it's not going to end with us.”

title: Duke admissions outlines post-affirmative action strategy, AI limits and transfer push
author: Ella Moore
text: Duke’s Office of Undergraduate Admissions, along with the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences transfer team, outlined a slew of admissions priorities in a presentation to Arts & Sciences Council Thursday, just nine days after the office admitted its Class of 2030. Kathy Phillips, interim dean of undergraduate admissions, told council attendees that the office looks to “support the faculty,” and keep the “goal of the University in mind” through the students they admit. She discussed the admissions team’s approach to diversity metrics and artificial intelligence use, as well as considering students’ academic pursuits and transfer status in their enrollment targets. Phillips summarized the many changes her team has had to contend with in the past two years, including navigating the admissions landscape following the 2023 Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard Supreme Court decision, which deemed the consideration of race and ethnicity in college admissions unconstitutional. Phillips said Duke unequivocally does not consider race or ethnicity — nor “proxies” to these characteristics — in its admissions decisions. Other demographic identities have significance for University priorities, though. She pointed to continued partnerships with QuestBridge, which matches low-income students with limited financial support to elite higher education institutions, and the STARS College Network , which supports small-town and rural students, as key examples of Duke’s work to ensure socioeconomic and geographical diversity in its student body. While the University has been “geographically consistent” in the last few admissions cycles, admitting more students from the Carolinas also remains a priority, according to Phillips. Duke has substantially increased its investment in undergraduates from North and South Carolina in recent years, specifically through its Initiative for Students from the Carolinas . Currently, the University provides full-tuition grants for admitted students from the region with family incomes of $150,000 or less. Phillips said she did not expect the “impending demographic cliff,” an expected decline in high school graduates that has become a point of concern for many colleges and universities, to be a challenge for Duke. She predicted Duke would remain a desirable and competitive choice for future applicants. Duke has seen steady increases in applicants over the last few admissions cycles, including for the Class of 2030, which saw a record-high 55,776 applications in the Regular Decision round. On legacy applicants, Phillips said the admissions office works with the Office of Alumni Engagement and Development to identify priority applicants — more specifically, children of Duke alumni who remain involved in the University through financial contributions or other investments. She emphasized these applicants make up a small number of spots, estimating they fill about 100 spaces — roughly 6% of the first-year class. Phillips described the recent classes of admitted students as steadily more credentialed and outcome-focused but less socially adept. She posited that these characteristics have arisen for a variety of reasons, including that many current students spent some of their time in high school amid a global pandemic and their unique identity as the “technology generation.” On the outcome-focused point, David Malone, professor of the practice of education, observed that students are now coming into his “The University: Why Are We Here?” FOCUS course with a set idea of roughly six professions that will provide a “return on investment” of attending university, which mostly revolved around STEM- and economics-based fields. Phillips explained in response that many students, especially those with low-income, first-generation or international backgrounds, may consider studies in the arts and humanities as “luxury goods” that they or their family may not want to prioritize ahead of a financially safer career path. She described the emphasis on more technical fields as a problem commonly faced by research universities, rather than an issue unique to Duke. While economics and STEM degrees consistently rank among Duke’s most popular majors, Phillips said admitting students with a genuine, demonstrated interest in arts and the humanities remains a priority. She also noted that students with interests in climate and sustainability, as well as civic and environmental engineering, have become a point of focus for the admissions office. Phillips said Duke does not use artificial intelligence for any step of the admissions process outside of organizing applications. She has previously expressed the University’s continued commitment to human review in admissions but qualified that the admissions office is not entirely opposed to its use in the future. “Most universities — Duke included — are eager to implement AI to assist with the objective parts of application evaluation, like reading transcripts to note course selection and grades,” Phillips wrote in a Jan. 14 email to The Chronicle. “The resources for doing so are just now being introduced to the marketplace.” During Thursday’s council meeting, she mentioned she would love to see a tool that helps the office standardize transcripts, as most high schools do not have unified standards for how they calculate grade point averages and implement class rank. Phillips added that while other institutions, such as the nearby University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, use artificial intelligence to assess students’ writing style and grammar proficiency through their admissions essays, Duke rarely sees applicants who do not submit competent essays and thus currently does not feel the need to use AI to analyze or rate essays. Duke stopped assigning numerical scores to the essays submitted by applicants in 2024. In the most recent admissions cycle, Duke revised one of its optional supplemental essay prompts to ask applicants to reflect on their own use of AI, inviting them to describe a situation in which they would or would not choose to use the technology and explain why. Attracting transfers has become an increased priority for Duke, an initiative that Claire Siburt, academic dean for Trinity transfer students, described as a “whole University effort.” Jenny Wood Crowley, associate vice provost for strategic enrollment management, said that transfers provide an additional population for Duke to draw from who can live on West Campus, whose capacity outstrips East Campus by a factor of four, she said. She also cited the mass exodus of juniors who study abroad each fall semester as empty seats that the University can fill with transfers. Duke has made changes to how it operates transfer admissions in recent years. Last admissions cycle, Duke transitioned from Common App to a personalized transfer application, which led to a brief drop in applicants, according to Anne Sjostrom, senior associate dean of undergraduate admissions. Before the COVID-19 pandemic, Duke admitted roughly 20 to 30 students per transfer cycle. The number rose to 90 last cycle, and Phillips said the University hopes to enroll another 90 in the current one. According to Siburt, around 28% of transfers are athletes, while the rest come from a variety of institutions across the country. These students typically have less financial need and pursue a wide swath of academic interests, though Duke targeted students interested in the arts and humanities in the most recent admissions cycle.

title: Duke halts Black Student Alliance Invitational, students warn of impact on enrollment
author: Ella Moore
text: For the first time since the 1980s , Duke’s Office of Undergraduate Admissions will not host the Black Student Alliance Invitational. Current students say the decision was made without their input and worry it could hurt Black enrollment. BSAI introduced prospective first-years to Duke from a Black perspective. Students spent two nights in the East and West Campus dorms, where they were paired with upperclassmen mentors, attended networking events and watched student cultural performances, including an annual stroll show , organized by the historically Black Divine Nine organizations . Under Student Affairs, Duke's Identity and Cultural Center teams and student organizations will instead run a new "Blue Devil Nights" on April 16 and 17 open to all admitted students. Unlike BSAI, “Blue Devil Nights” has no overnight component and no programming specific to Black students. Jenny Wood Crowley, associate vice provost for strategic enrollment management, told The Chronicle in a Thursday email that the decision to pause BSAI was made after a regular review of all University programming to “ensure they align with evolving legal guidance and institutional priorities.” The pause comes at a time of low Black student enrollment. Just 7% of the incoming Class of 2029 identified as Black or African American, according to Duke’s class profile . Current Black students fear that the number will fall further without BSAI. For sophomore Clark Easley, BSAI was “one of the best weeks” he had at Duke. The invitational eased some of the trepidation he felt about attending a university with an “elitist” reputation like Duke and made it feel more like a home than a school. Many of his core friends went through the program as well. “I cannot undercount the impact BSAI has for recruiting and retaining Black students,” Easley said. Nigel Cooper, a first-year student, said BSAI was instrumental in his decision to choose Duke over other elite institutions he was accepted into, including the University of Pennsylvania and Stanford. The upperclassmen support system built through the multi-day BSAI experience was a key perk of attendance. “I only committed to Duke because of BSAI,” Cooper said. Wood Crowley told The Chronicle in her email that Student Affairs “partnered closely” with students to shape this year’s approach to Blue Devil Nights and the broader Blue Devil Days experience. However, student leaders have said that communication on changes to admitted students' events has been limited. Other students expressed widespread frustration with the lack of communication on BSAI’s fate for the recent admission cycle. The Black Student Alliance, the principal organization overseeing BSAI, did not have firsthand information from Duke administrators that the program would not take place this year. “We haven’t had the opportunity to give direct input into the changes that the administration has made,” junior Demetrist Booker said. Students familiar with administrative communications on BSAI, who asked not to be named after being told not to comment publicly, said that until around January, Duke seemed poised to run the invitational as normal. Applications were sent out for BSAI organizers, and students were appointed to the positions. But around late February and early March, students preparing to plan the April event were told BSAI would be changed and their positions as BSAI co-chairs would be renamed to “Impact Ambassadors.” Some students have speculated that the shift may have been in reaction to the Trump administration’s “Compact for Academic Excellence in Higher Education,” which included multiple tenets on preventing discrimination in all university operations, including admissions. The change is also reflective of Duke’s reaction to a broader push from the federal government for higher education institutions to roll back DEI programming. Wood Crowley touted Blue Devil Days, along with the new Blue Devil Nights, as experiences that together “reflect a more comprehensive and student-informed approach to community-building.” Despite these efforts, Booker worries that removing the overnight component and merging programming with Blue Devil Days won’t add the same depth of experience for students. “BSAI has been a mechanism for the last 50 years to encourage not just Black students, but all students ... by not having, or by significantly changing the programming, students are less inclined to come to the school,” Booker said.

title: Who is John Sauer? The Duke alum arguing for ending birthright citizenship at Supreme Court
author: Lila Cohen, Seyun Park
text: A senior government official and Duke alumnus is leading the president’s charge against birthright citizenship — and it’s not Stephen Miller. Dean John Sauer, Pratt ‘97, serves as the solicitor general of the United States. Last week, Sauer’s job brought him to the Supreme Court, where he argued on behalf of President Donald Trump concerning his executive order attempting to end complete birthright citizenship. “Unrestricted birthright citizenship contradicts the practice of the overwhelming majority of modern nations,” Sauer said at the court’s oral arguments for Trump v. Barbara. “It demeans the priceless and profound gift of American citizenship.” One particularly memorable interaction between Sauer and Chief Justice John Roberts went viral online, with Sauer arguing that the U.S. is in a “new world” when it comes to immigration and claiming that “8 billion people are one plane ride away from having a child who's a U.S. citizen.” “It’s a new world. It’s the same Constitution,” Roberts responded. Legal experts generally expect the nine-member court to rule against the president, with conservative justices signaling skepticism through their questions in oral arguments. North Carolina was one of 22 states to sue the president soon after the order was issued in January 2025, and Duke scholars have expressed skepticism of the policy’s legal standing and concern over its impact on American identity, should the ban be upheld. A ruling on the case will likely come toward the end of the court's term in June. The Chronicle took a look at Sauer’s Duke history and his connections to Trump over the years. Sauer did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment through the Department of Justice’s press office. At Duke, Sauer was an accomplished scholar and a student-athlete. He earned degrees in both philosophy and electrical engineering, was inducted into the Phi Beta Kappa academic honors society and graduated summa cum laude. He was also an Angier B. Duke scholar, Duke’s oldest merit scholarship that covers the University’s full cost of attendance and a summer at Oxford University. Sauer was involved with multiple campus organizations at Duke, including the Catholic Student Center and Duke Students for Life. He was also a walk-on member of the wrestling team, eventually serving as team captain. Outside of Duke, he spent summers in his hometown of St. Louis, where he worked with prisoners and taught inner-city children. He appeared in a February 1994 edition of The Chronicle, co-authoring a letter to the editor alongside 17 other members of Duke Students for Life in objection to an editorial that praised then-President Bill Clinton’s health plan, which included funding for abortions. Sauer wrote that given abortion’s alleged unpopularity in the country — he cited a New York Times poll — it would be “tragic and immoral” to force citizens to pay for it. In his senior year, Sauer was named a Rhodes Scholar, becoming the third student-athlete from Duke to earn the prestigious award. His adviser, Ed Mahoney, then-director of undergraduate studies in the philosophy department, praised Sauer at the time for his modesty. “I'm definitely no wiser or more virtuous than anyone else," Sauer said in a 1996 Chronicle article on the achievement. "I'm just a regular guy.” After graduating, Sauer took the aforementioned Rhodes Scholarship to pursue a second bachelor’s degree in theology at Oxford. He later earned a master’s degree in philosophy from the University of Notre Dame and a Juris Doctor from Harvard Law School, where he served as articles editor of the Harvard Law Review and graduated magna cum laude. Sauer went on to clerk for Judge J. Michael Luttig in the Fourth Circuit Court of Appeals, then for Justice Antonin Scalia in the Supreme Court. Both judges were noted conservatives during their terms. Following his clerkships, Sauer served as an assistant U.S. attorney before transitioning to private practice, founding his own law firm in 2015. Between 2017 and 2023, he served as the solicitor general in his home state of Missouri. Sauer is no stranger to the Supreme Court — or arguing for Trump. The solicitor general is the fourth-highest ranking member of the Department of Justice behind the attorney general, deputy attorney general and associate attorney general. The position’s main responsibility is to represent the government in cases before the Supreme Court. So far in Trump’s second term, Sauer has argued in seven of the administration’s biggest cases, ranging from the president’s tariffs, challenged in Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump , to his attempt to fire Federal Reserve chair Lisa Cook, challenged in Trump v. Cook . But Sauer was arguing for Trump in the nation’s highest court even before he was on government payroll. In 2024, when he was a private practicing attorney, Sauer represented Trump in the case Trump v. United States, which revolved around whether the Department of Justice could prosecute Trump for the Jan. 6 insurrection and attempts to overturn the 2020 election. Sauer eventually won 6-3, securing a surprising and controversial verdict from the court, which granted wide latitudes of immunity from criminal prosecution to presidents for actions conducted in office. In 2020, when he was serving as solicitor general of Missouri, Sauer filed a “friend of the court” amicus brief with the Supreme Court, supporting Texas’ effort to overturn the results of the 2020 presidential election. The case was eventually dismissed by the court due to the state’s lack of standing to sue. Over the years, Sauer’s advocacy on Trump’s behalf has made him a personal favorite of the president. Trump praised Sauer, calling him a “deeply accomplished, masterful appellate attorney” in a statement announcing his nomination. Sauer went on to be approved 52-45 in the Senate. Sauer’s loyalty to the president may eventually pay off in a big way for the Blue Devil: Sauer’s name is reportedly in the mix for a coveted Supreme Court spot, should one open up. “He is certainly at the top of my short list for the Supreme Court,” Mike Davis, another one of Trump’s lawyers, recently told CNN.

title: Kaitlyn Williams, Belton Moore named Young Trustee nominees
author: Ella Moore
text: Trinity senior Kaitlyn Williams and Belton Moore, a master’s in business administration and public policy candidate, were selected by the Young Trustee nominating committees and President Vincent Price as Duke’s next undergraduate and graduate/professional Young Trustees, respectively. The nominations will be brought to the Board of Trustees for election on May 9, wrote Margaret Epps, secretary to the Board of Trustees and Price’s chief of staff, in a Wednesday email to The Chronicle. The two Young Trustees will start their terms on July 1 once confirmed by the board. Young Trustees are students who serve two- or three-year terms as one of the 37 members of the Board of Trustees to provide the perspective of “today’s Duke.” Though they spend their first year as non-voting observers, Young Trustees are tasked with ensuring the “long-term health” of the University, rather than advocating for student issues. Williams, originally from Virginia, is a SPIRE fellow pursuing a Program II in inequality in education. She has been heavily involved on campus throughout her time at Duke and described receiving the nomination as a “dream.” Williams currently serves as President of the Duke University Union, the University’s largest student-led programming and media organization with 18 committees. Before assuming the presidency, she helped establish (DUU)rham , which is aimed at fostering relationships “between Duke students and Durham’s vendors, landmarks and rich history.” Williams is also president of Duke SPIRE Fellows , a program focused on “enhancing student success, retention, and engagement in STEM disciplines,” and works as a Green Devils Inter-Institutional Student Sustainability Coordinator, connecting Duke student sustainability leaders with other colleges and universities. “I applied to be a Young Trustee because I believe in the power of higher education and want to help shape its future, starting here at my alma mater,” Williams wrote in a Wednesday email to The Chronicle. “Throughout my time at Duke, I’ve had the privilege of interacting with so many aspects of the university, and I view my time as a Young Trustee as an opportunity to learn about the place I’ve called home for the last four years.” Moore, a native of North Carolina, told The Chronicle that Duke has been “transformative in his life.” As he searched for a way to give back, Young Trustees seemed like a “great opportunity.” Besides pursuing dual degrees in public policy and business as a Rubenstein Fellow, Moore works as an economics tutor at the Sanford School and a research assistant in the Fuqua School of Business. As an undergraduate at North Carolina State University, Moore served in the university’s student government as a senator and vice president of the Native American Student Association. “My parents instilled service as a value, specifically the value of institutions of higher education,” Moore said. Moore’s connection to Duke comes from unique historical ties. His father worked as a tobacco farmer for much of his life, during the two decades when the Duke family oversaw the dominant American Tobacco Company. He is particularly interested in the University’s engagement with North Carolina, especially with “exciting” new programs like the recently announced HomeGrown Initiative. “[Duke’s] investing more into the Triangle, so I’m really excited to plug in there and learn more and see how I can help,” Moore said. This year’s selection of Young Trustees differed from past rounds. Traditionally, finalists faced the student body in a public forum hosted by Board of Trustees Secretary Margaret Epps’ office, where they outlined their goals and qualifications directly to the student body. For 2026, the forum was replaced by private interviews with Epps, a current Young Trustee and the chair of the Board of Trustees' governance committee. Epps expressed confidence in the new process and praised the Young Trustees as “assets to the board,” through their perspectives reflecting the student body.

title: Meet the 2026 Commencement honorary degree recipients
author: Annie Eilers, Sarah Diaz
text: Duke University will honor a pioneering choreographer, a leading legal scholar and a world champion soccer player at its Class of 2026 Commencement ceremony on May 10. Dianne McIntyre, Cass Sunstein and Abby Wambach will each receive honorary degrees in recognition of their trailblazing careers. Dianne McIntyre is the founder of Sounds in Motion, a Harlem-based dance studio and touring company. Her ensemble of dancers and musicians have performed across the United States and Europe for over 20 years, including at major venues like The Joyce Theater, The Brooklyn Academy of Music and The Kennedy Center. Beyond her dance company, McIntyre has mentored and inspired generations of dancers for the past 50 years. Since 1972 , she has choreographed four Broadway shows, 30 regional theatre productions, two feature films and three television productions, among other dramatic pieces. Her work has been commissioned by notable institutions such as the Alvin Ailey American Dance Theater and the American Dance Festival. McIntyre was awarded with the Doris Duke Impact Award in 2016 and was welcomed by the Duke Dance Program for a development residency in February 2022. She has also received additional accolades such as three Bessie awards, a John S. Guggenheim Fellowship and a Kennedy Center Master of African American Choreography Medal. Cass Sunstein is the founder of the program on behavioral economics and public policy at Harvard Law School and a recipient of the prestigious Holberg Prize. He is a leading legal scholar with work spanning behavioral economics, environmental law and political administration. Sunstein currently serves as the Robert Walmsley University Professor at Harvard. This semester he is teaching courses on the intersection of behavioral economics, law and public policy as well as the politics of social change. His current projects focus on the regulatory state, administrative burdens, fake news and freedom of speech. Sunstein has authored New York Times bestselling books “Nudge: Improving Decisions about Health, Wealth, and Happiness” and “The World According to Star Wars.” He has also written hundreds of articles for law reviews and journals across the country. Sunstein worked under the Obama and Biden presidential administrations. From 2009 to 2012, Sunstein served as the administrator of the White House Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs, before serving on the President’s Review Board on Intelligence and Communications Technologies and on the Pentagon’s Defense Innovation Board. In 2024, Sunstein won the Distinguished Public Service medal for his work as the Senior Counselor to the Secretary of Homeland Security under the Biden administration. Sunstein previously served as Chair of the Technical Advisory Group on Behavioural Sciences for Better Health with the World Health Organization. He has also advised officials at the United Nations, European Commission and World Bank and served as an adviser to the Behavioural Insights Team. Abby Wambach is an internationally recognized soccer player, coach and World Cup champion. She made four career appearances in the World Cup, during a period when USA women's soccer began growing in popularity. Her veteran leadership helped Team USA win its third title in 2015, the first since 1999. Wambach and her team came in third place in 2003 and 2007, and were the runners ups in 2011. She also played for the U.S. women’s national soccer team in the Athens 2004 and London 2012 Summer Olympics, racking up gold medals in both. With 184 career goals, Wambach is the leading scorer of all time on the U.S. women’s team. She was inducted into the National Soccer Hall of Fame in 2019. Now retired from soccer, Wambach is an advocate for women’s rights and equality. During the 60th session of the UN Commission of the Status of Women, Wambach spoke against soccer’s gender pay gap. She shared that after winning the 2015 FIFA Women’s World Cup, her team received “17 to 18 times less” compensation than the men’s champions from the year prior. After Wambach — alongside with other players — spoke out for years about the need for wage equality, the U.S. women’s national soccer team reached a $24 million settlement in an equal pay lawsuit in 2022. In 2019, Wambach released her New York Times Best Selling novel “WOLFPACK: How to Come Together, Unleash Our Power, and Change the Game.” She is also the co-founder and partial owner of Angel City FC, the first-ever majority female owned soccer club.

title: Quavo to headline 2026 LDOC concert, along with performances by Slayyyter, WHATMORE
author: Ella Moore
text: Quavo will headline the annual Last Day of Classes concert on April 22, according to a video streamed by the DUU LDOC Committee during its Wednesday evening artist reveal. Additional acts include Slayyyter and the musical band WHATMORE. Quavious Keyate Marshall, who goes by the stage name Quavo, is a four-time Grammy-nominated American rapper and record producer from Lawrenceville, Ga. He is known for his versatile flow, ad-libbing ability and melodic lyrical delivery across both features and original songs. Quavo is widely recognized for appearances on high-charting tracks spanning genres from rap to pop, including DJ Khaled’s “I’m The One,” Justin Bieber’s “Intentions” and Post Malone’s “Congratulations.” He first rose to prominence as part of the American rap trio Migos alongside his nephew Kirsnick Khari Ball, known as TakeOff and fellow Lawrenceville native Kiari Kendrell Cephus, known as Offset. The group first gained traction with the release of the single “Versace” in 2013 before going viral with the 2016 single “Bad and Boujee,” featuring Lil Uzi Vert. The song topped the Billboard Hot 100 for three non-consecutive weeks in January 2017. Migos became the first rap group to spend more than one week at No. 1 on the Billboard Artist 100 chart following the release of its third studio album, “Culture II,” in 2018. The sequel to the group’s breakout album “Culture,” “Culture II,” featured popular tracks such as “Walk It Talk It,” “Stir Fry” and “Motorsport,” all of which charted in the top 10 on the Billboard Hot 100. The group eventually split in 2022. Following the death of Takeoff due to gunshot wounds in November 2022, Quavo became a staunch advocate against gun violence. He founded the Rocket Foundation , which supports community-based programs addressing gun violence. In 2024, the Biden administration’s Office of Gun Violence Prevention presented him with the ‘Humanitarian of the Year’ award for these efforts at the Variety Hitmakers Brunch. Catherine Grace Garner, known by the stage name Slayyyter, is an American singer, songwriter and rapper from Kirkwood, Mo. She began her studio career with the release of the studio album Troubled Paradise in the summer of 2021. Her most recent album "Wor$t Girl in America," released in March, brought her to the top the Billboard Dance Albums Chart. Three songs from the album, “ Yes Goddd ,” “ Crank ” and “ Dance ” debuted in the Top 15 on Billboard’s Hot Dance/Pop Song chart. WHATMORE, consisting of members Cisco Swank, Elijah Judah, Jackson August, Sebastiano and Yoshi T, is a New York City-based band that blends hip hop, jazz and alternative indie. The group has recently gone viral on TikTok for its album, aptly titled “whatmore,” and some have compared the group to the popular hip hop boy band Brockhampton.

title: White House proposal could add political litmus test to research grants
author: Jolynda Wang
text: A recent proposal by the White House could add a political litmus test to the federal government’s evaluation of research proposals and allocation of grants. On May 29, the White House’s Office of Management and Budget proposed a rule to federal grant regulations that would require political appointees to review grant awards and ensure they “demonstrably advance the President’s policy priorities.” The sprawling proposal, if implemented, would prohibit federal funding for projects deemed to promote “anti-American values,” as well as diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives and “gender ideology” that denies that there are two immutable sexes within humans. As written, “the proposed changes risk undoing the highly-effective, merit-based, peer-reviewed systems that have allowed researchers to develop countless breakthroughs,” University Vice Provost for Research Michael Crair wrote in an email to the News, adding that both Yale and the federal government share a commitment to transparency and efficiency in their relationship. “Yale is actively contributing to conversations and advocating for policies that strengthen the U.S. research funding model that is admired and emulated around the world and has, for many decades, produced discoveries that Americans benefit from every day,” Crair also wrote. The federal government is currently holding a 45-day comment period during which the public can review the proposed rules and submit feedback. As of June 8, the proposed rule has received 11,135 comments . Crair wrote that the Association of Public and Land-Grant Universities, the Council on Governmental Relations and the American Association of Universities are “already working to provide feedback.” Yale President Maurie McInnis sits on the board of the American Association of Universities and said in a March interview that she has collaborated with the organization to advocate for a more transparent federal research funding model in recent lobbying efforts . Three Yale professors criticized the proposal in interviews with the News, describing it as a threat to scientific independence and warning that it may slow life-changing scientific progress. “If science can only ask politically comfortable questions, it stops serving the public,” Frederick Altice, a professor of medicine and epidemiology at the Yale School of Medicine, wrote in an email to the News. He noted that requiring approval from presidential appointees could delay funding decisions and “create uncertainty for trainees, staff, and community partners.” Thomas Pollard, a now-retired Sterling professor of molecular, cellular and developmental biology, said in a Zoom interview that he expected these changes, citing President Donald Trump’s cancellation of several grants last year. “I've always said that getting an NIH grant was about as competitive as making a team in the National Football League. The money is very hard to get, and this is going to make it even more difficult to get funds to do the work,” Pollard said. Pollard said the proposal is “horrible” and “immoral,” noting that the changes could push back funding for research on developing cures and treatments. “This is a damn shame because these funds have created endless advances in technology and endless advances in clinical medicine,” Pollard said. Molecular, cellular and developmental biology professor Valerie Horsley also wrote in an email to the News that inserting political review into merit-based funding could “undermine scientific independence and slow research progress.” Horsley added that she has been funded by the NIH since she was a graduate student and that she thinks the proposal could complicate faculty recruitment and jeopardize support for graduate students. “These changes would make the process more unreliable and reduce confidence in the system,” Horsley wrote. “It would also make it harder for Universities to support research because the funding would be unpredictable.” Scott Delaney, a former research scientist at Harvard and co-creator of Grant Witness, a website that tracks federal research grant disruptions, described the proposal as an unprecedented expansion of executive control over the research funding process. He added that the changes could make it easier for future administrations to delay, deny or terminate grants to universities like Yale. “This is the biggest attack on the American scientific enterprise in my lifetime,” Delaney said in a Zoom interview. “We cannot underestimate the impact, the transformative impact that this regulation could have if it goes into effect as it’s proposed.” Continued hurdles, ‘worrisome’ delays Last month’s proposal follows a turbulent period for federal research funding at Yale under Trump’s second term. Approximately 120 grants tied to Yale researchers were affected by federal actions in 2025, including around 50 awards that were terminated and later reinstated. In March, some researchers still receiving federal funding told the News that they were facing complications due to recent policy changes, such as longer application and approval processes for grants. On April 9, the United States National Science Foundation’s Office of Award Management placed a “Future Awards to Organization on Hold” designation on Yale, Harvard, Princeton and Duke, impacting 33 research proposals associated with the universities, according to reporting by the scientific journal Nature . The day after the publication of Nature’s reporting, on May 28, the foundation removed the hold from its database for Yale, Harvard and Duke, with several grants released to Harvard and Duke following the change, Nature stated in an update to the original article. The University has not yet received formal notice from the federal government indicating that new NSF grants were placed on hold, according to Crair. However, he noted that Yale has continued to observe delays in grants being awarded. “What we have observed, and continue to monitor, is that some new grants recommended for funding through standard NSF processes, including peer review, have not been funded in the timeline we would typically expect,” he wrote in his June 4 email. “However, I am glad to report that Yale has received a number of new NSF grants in recent days.” “Allowing agencies to withhold funds, or to pick winners and losers outside of the process outlined in law, is a problematic erosion of the fundamental principle in our Constitution that Congress has the power of the purse,” Cerin Lindgrensavage ’06, a counsel for Protect Democracy, a nonpartisan nonprofit organization that advocates for stronger democracy in the United States, said in a Zoom interview. “The law is what should govern how these funds are used and not any single opinion of a bureaucrat at the OMB or NSF.” Altice wrote that the recent stalling of research proposals adds to his concern about political influence on research funding. The affected proposals have already passed independent peer review and agency-level reviews, he wrote, and they seem to have been delayed only at the award stage. “When funding is held at that stage, outside the normal scientific workflow, it raises serious questions about whether decisions are being influenced by factors other than scientific merit,” Altice wrote. “It is worrisome.” The OMB’s proposal will receive public comments on its website until July 13.

title: New Haven is the only city fighting Trump’s ‘slush’ fund in court. Why?
author: Adele Haeg
text: Since President Donald Trump moved back into the White House, New Haven’s new legal counsel has been busy. The city has repeatedly sued the federal government over the past year and a half, joining six lawsuits and 24 other amicus briefs against the Trump administration. New Haven has argued against funding freezes for environmental initiatives and SNAP benefits , and supported Minnesota in its lawsuit over immigration enforcement in the state. Mayor Justin Elicker has praised a “growing nationwide coalition of local governments” who have taken similar action — but with its latest suit, New Haven stands apart. Last month, the city was the only one to join a high-profile lawsuit seeking to block the president’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization fund” in a Virginia district court, and none of the other suits that have been filed in opposition to the fund have municipal plaintiffs. New Haven joined the lawsuit a month after the Trump administration sued the city for its immigration “sanctuary policies” — in that case, New Haven and Connecticut officials were the only defendants. Soon after filing the lawsuit opposing Trump’s fund, New Haven and its fellow plaintiffs — a fired Jan. 6 prosecutor, a California professor formerly charged with assaulting a federal officer, the National Abortion Federation and a government watchdog group — requested emergency relief to block the federal government from disbursing money through the program. The next day, Judge Leonie Brinkema granted a temporary injunction in the case until June 12, and last Friday, the Department of Justice told Brinkema that the fund was “not going forward.” As of Tuesday, the future of the slush fund remains unclear . Allie Jacobs, the corporation counsel for New Haven, wrote in an email to the News that the city thinks its strategy in the latest lawsuit has been successful so far. However, it is not clear how much of an impact New Haven’s involvement in the suit has had on whether the fund will ultimately move forward. The other lawsuits against the “slush" fund are ongoing, and the program has come under bipartisan fire in Congress. The mayor said he is not pursuing legal action for the purpose of political visibility. “If there were a case that was simply symbolic, I’m not sure we would join,” Elicker said in a phone interview with the News. “It’s about us making sure that we have impacts that help New Haven residents.” “The punchline for a lot of Democratic elected officials is that it may both be the right political move and the right move for ‘small-d democracy’” to sue the Trump administration, Dan Barrett, legal director of the Connecticut American Civil Liberties Union, said in a phone interview with the News. Maybe, he added, “people will look back and say, ‘well, at least somebody tried to fight back.’” How did New Haven get involved in the lawsuit? In May, lawyers from the nonprofit Democracy Forward — which is representing the plaintiffs in the suit — approached Elicker and asked if New Haven would join in suing the Trump administration for setting up their “Anti-Weaponization Fund.” The fund — a result of a settlement with the Trump administration in a legal dispute over his tax returns — was set up to compensate allies of the Republican Party who “suffered weaponization” by the DOJ under former President Joe Biden. Along with the three other plaintiffs, Democracy Forward planned to prosecute the Trump administration for misuse of taxpayer funds and “creating a politically discriminatory process ” in their disbursement, alleging that it would reward only Republicans who claimed weaponization. According to Jacobs, New Haven had “a preexisting relationship with Democracy Forward,” which previously represented plaintiffs suing the Trump administration for blocking SNAP benefits. New Haven was one of eight municipalities who participated in that lawsuit. Elicker said that after hearing from Democracy Forward, he considered his options in conversations with legal counsel, recognizing that there were “pros and cons” of suing. Democracy Forward would cover legal fees, and Elicker said that the suit aligned with “our values as a city.” “We’re defending our residents from what is in my view a real misuse of federal funds and taxpayer dollars,” the mayor added. Elicker said that one risk was that the lawsuit might “attract undue attention” from the federal government with New Haven as the only municipal plaintiff. But for Elicker, that did not mean “New Haven should not join these lawsuits but that more municipalities should join these lawsuits.” When lawyers are compiling lawsuits and “trying to get as many viable plaintiffs as you can find, you notice that each one checks a different box,” Barrett said, speculating that New Haven was brought into the suit because it had previously been sued for its “sanctuary policies.” He said it was not surprising New Haven has developed a relationship with Democracy Forward, adding that it is “common” for municipalities to collaborate with similar nonprofits. “There are certainly situations in which municipalities really have to be on the front lines litigating, particularly with some grant programs that municipalities will directly interact with the federal government over,” Barrett said. “In other cases, it’s less clear, but this probably serves a democratic purpose.” New Haven as an “anti-monarchist” city Apart from the city’s adherence to its “sanctuary policies” and other municipal priorities and systems that Elicker said the federal administration is “dismantling,” Jacobs suggested that it is in New Haven’s character as a city to take legal action in the way that it has been. It is “unusual” for cities to sue the federal government, Jacobs said — especially one like New Haven, with only 135,000 residents. “It takes courage for a smaller municipality to stand up to a much larger, much more well-resourced federal government,” she wrote. Jacobs said she recognized legal and historical imperatives for legal action against the Trump administration. “New Haven has a deep history of anti-monarchist principles, dating back to the 1600s and 1700s when the people of New Haven rebelled against the British Crown,” Jacobs wrote, “and we have been fighting against tyranny and injustice ever since.” City Historian Michael Morand ’87 DIV ’93 likened the city’s opposition to the chief executive to the actions of Roger Sherman, a founding father and the first mayor of New Haven. Sherman believed that a “person or persons ought to be appointed by and accountable to the Legislature only, which was the depository of the supreme will of the Society,” Morand wrote, quoting James Madison. “I’ve gotten a lot of positive feedback from residents about the stance that New Haven has taken at this moment,” Elicker said. The city’s position in the case reflects the discontent with Trump administration policies that have drawn thousands of New Haven residents to protests this year. Though there are “other communities that might not be as supportive to their leadership” taking a confrontational stance, Elicker said he feels he has New Haven’s support. “ We have a good team and we’ll continue to respond to opportunities that come in the future,” Elicker said. “Things are so volatile and unpredictable right now, it’s hard to come up with a specific plan.” Elicker was first sworn into office in January 2020.

title: Former Yale golfer James Nicholas ’19 returns to the U.S. Open
author: Brody Gilkison
text: PURCHASE, New York — Before last year, no Yale golfer had played in the U.S. Open since Bob Heintz ’92 in 1999. However, James Nicholas ’19 secured a spot in the 125th iteration of the tournament in 2025 — and this Monday, he qualified yet again for the 126th in Shinnecock Hills. Nicholas earned his spot during “Golf’s Longest Day,” the day when the United States Golf Association hosts 10 of its 13 U.S. Open final qualifying events. For those not already exempt into the U.S. Open, qualifying typically comes in the form of an 18-hole local qualifier in early May and a 36-hole final qualifying site a week before the championship begins. “It feels great after such a long day,” Nicholas said in an interview with the News on Monday. He added that he has experienced “a lot of ups and downs this season,” but hopes that having qualified for the tournament will help him “finish the season strong.” Nicholas, a one-time winner on the Korn Ferry Tour — the PGA’s developmental tour — sits at 15th place on the tour’s point list and inside the Top 20 mark needed to earn his PGA Tour card for next year. Just days after playing in the BMW Charity Pro-Am in South Carolina, Nicholas flew to New York to play 36 holes in the hopes of earning his way into his second consecutive U.S. Open. The New York site, which was the only site hosted at two separate clubs, was played at Century Country Club and the Golf Club of Purchase, both in Purchase, New York. Nicholas teed up first at the Golf Club of Purchase and took advantage of scorable conditions with a cool –4 round of 68 to put himself in second place after 18 holes. While he was in a good position halfway through the event, Nicholas needed to remain in the top four of a strong field in order to qualify. Among others competing at his site were four-time All-American Ben James of Virginia, as well as seasoned PGA Tour veterans Max Greyserman and Kevin Roy. The second round proved to be much more difficult, as the rock-hard greens and thick rough required accuracy and a strong mental game. Through 27 holes, Nicholas was still two shots clear of the cut line but had to finish strong in order to punch his ticket to the national championship. Standing on the 17th tee, Nicholas was a shot clear of the cutline at –3, leading amateur Christian Cavaliere by one stroke and two strokes ahead of Matt Jones, a two-time PGA Tour winner himself. However, Nicholas misjudged a greenside shot on the 17th, causing him to drop a shot before paring the final hole to post rounds of 68–72 and sit in a tie for the final spot at two-under. Luckily for Nicholas, Cavaliere made a costly mistake on his final hole of the day, a short, scorable par-4 hole. After Cavaliere double-bogeyed the hole, Nicholas qualified for the U.S. Open without needing to play any extra holes. Nicholas will be playing in this week’s Korn Ferry Tour event in Amarillo, Texas, before heading back to his home state to play at Shinnecock Hills next week.

title: Lincoln Center president Mariko Silver ’99 to join Yale Corporation
author: Aria Lynn-Skov
text: Yale graduates elected performing arts executive Mariko Silver ’99 to the Yale Corporation, alumni board of governors chair Jennifer Ebisemiju Madar ’88 announced in a Monday email to voters. Silver will begin her six-year term as trustee on July 1. Silver, who has been president and CEO of the Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts since 2024, prevailed over Michael Singer ’95 GRD ’99 ’00 MED ’02 to replace outgoing trustee Carlos Moreno ’70. According to a Yale alumni fellow election brochure , Silver raised the largest annual funding total in the Lincoln Center’s history during her first year as its president. She was previously president and CEO of the Henry Luce Foundation, a grantmaking nonprofit, and was also president of Bennington College in Vermont. “We are in a moment when people are asking questions about the value of higher education and the value of publicly funded research, but I believe they’ve never been more important,” Silver said in a video included in the official election materials . The Yale Corporation, or board of trustees, includes 10 successor trustees — appointed by the current board — and six alumni fellows. Alumni fellows serve staggered six-year terms, and one is elected each spring. Moreno was elected to the role in 2020 over Maurie McInnis, who was later appointed to serve as a successor trustee from 2022 to 2024 before assuming her current role as University president. This year, eligible voters — including Yale College alumni who graduated at least five years ago and all other University graduates and honorary degree recipients — were able to vote to elect Singer or Silver until May 17. According to the elections brochure , candidates do not traditionally campaign for the alumni fellow role, standing for election “solely based on the information included in the official election materials.” Silver could not be reached for comment as of Monday evening. As president of Bennington College from 2013 to 2019, she brought in the most diverse class in the school’s history, expanded financial aid and tripled the school’s endowment to $51 million, according to Bennington’s website. In her alumni fellow candidacy video, Silver said that her interactions with faculty were core to her experience as a student, adding that the curiosity and dedication of those she encountered on campus as an undergraduate have carried her through her career and her life. “That sense of curiosity that leads to innovation, that leads to new research, that leads to new ways of thinking, and also that sense of stewardship — gathering what we know, marshalling it for good purposes and sharing it with others — all of those things are part and parcel of the Yale mission and it would be an honor to help to bring them forward,” she said. After graduating, Silver worked under the president of Arizona State University and as policy adviser to Arizona Governor Janet Napolitano. From 2009 to 2011, she served the Department of Homeland Security’s Office of International Affairs under the Obama administration. Candidates for the alumni fellow position are currently chosen by the Alumni Fellow Nominating Committee, which reviews candidates nominated by Yale graduates. Up until the 2021 election, candidates could put themselves on the ballot by gathering signatures from 3 percent of eligible voters. The decision to eliminate the petitioning process was unpopular among alumni, and Victor Ashe ’67 and Donald Glascoff Jr. ’67 filed a lawsuit challenging the legality of the change in 2022. The Connecticut Supreme Court declined to hear the lawsuit in March, after it was unsuccessfully argued in the Hartford district court and Connecticut Appellate Court. Singer, who was Silver’s opponent in this year’s alumni fellow election, is a physician-scientist and entrepreneur who serves on the Yale For Humanity Campaign Committee . In his candidacy video , Singer said the campaign serves as a reminder “that we’re a unified body in New Haven but also a global university ultimately looking to serve the world.” The For Humanity fundraising campaign — which describes itself as “guided by our mission to improve the world now and for future generations — launched in 2021 with the goal of raising $7 billion. It is set to conclude this month. In March, McInnis announced Derek Chang ’89 and Philippe Costeletos ’87 as Yale’s newest successor trustees. The two will begin in their roles alongside Silver in July. The Yale Corporation will meet next on June 6 for the final of five meetings this academic year, according to a University webpage .

title: In faculty group survey, professors report self-censorship under Trump
author: Aria Lynn-Skov
text: Faculty members described an environment of decreasing academic freedom and instances of self-censorship under President Donald Trump’s second term in a recent report by Yale’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors. Yale AAUP released its 26-page report on Tuesday, three months after the group launched a campaign calling on Yale to add explicit guarantees of academic freedom to the faculty handbook. The Tuesday report’s recommendations additionally include increased protections for non-tenured faculty, the consideration of a University-wide faculty senate beyond advisory roles and a faculty-elected committee to hear and decide upon academic freedom complaints. “There’s been a lot of talk about the decline in public trust in higher education, but trust on campus is in jeopardy right now too,” Amy Kapczynski LAW ’03, a Yale Law School professor and member of the AAUP executive committee, said in a phone interview. “And without trust that we won’t be disciplined or harassed for our speech, we can’t offer the best education to our students or do our best research or best contribute to public debates.” The group’s survey received 177 faculty respondents from Yale College and “nearly every professional school.” According to the report, 68.4 percent of respondents indicated that their academic freedom has decreased either somewhat or a great deal since January 2025, when Trump began his second term. Yale has 5,842 faculty members, according to its website . When asked in a phone interview about the challenges of conducting the survey, Daniel HoSang, an American Studies professor and the president of Yale’s AAUP chapter, cited “apprehension among faculty” to share their experiences and a lack of access to a centralized list of faculty. 56.3 percent of respondents also reported being “extremely concerned” or “somewhat concerned” that “students might file anonymous complaints about them,” while 44.6 percent reported concerns about being “disciplined by the university for their public-facing speech and engagement.” The survey also asked respondents about actions — related to teaching, research and public engagement — they may have taken since January 2025 “in response to the Trump administration’s higher education directives and actions and in light of the general climate of attacks on higher education.” 32.2 percent of respondents said they “avoided controversial topics in class or lectures,” and 47.5 percent said they “stopped or avoided posting on social media about controversial topics,” per the report. “The conditions in higher ed have really shifted in the last couple of years, and this is important evidence about how faculty are reacting at one institution,” HoSang said of the report. Since Trump’s inauguration, Yale has avoided direct funding cuts from the federal government that peer institutions, including Harvard and Columbia, have faced. In meetings with professors last spring, Yale President Maurie McInnis indicated that she was refraining from public commentary to avoid the federal government’s targeting, according to professors who attended the meetings. McInnis never confirmed these discussions, citing the private nature of meetings. In October 2024, a month before Trump was elected to his second term, McInnis adopted the recommendations of the Committee on Institutional Voice that University leaders refrain from making public statements on significant issues, except in rare cases. Kapczynski said that the audience for the University’s “lay-low strategy” seems to be the Trump administration and those outside of Yale, adding that when universities neither comment publicly on what’s happening in their “broader environment” nor take action to clarify their “commitment to academic freedom,” it “speaks volumes.” “The reality is that action is important in this context because it’s what conveys the sincerity of our commitment to academic freedom,” she said. Tuesday’s report also reads that “While some reporting has taken the view that Yale has successfully defended the institution and its finances from the present administration, our data show that all is not well at Yale.” In March, the office of Provost Scott Strobel established the Committee on the Principles of Academic Freedom, which is set to release a statement that “articulates the principles that guide the academic freedom of Yale faculty,” according to the Provost’s website . Strobel did not respond to questions about whether the committee’s formation was a reaction to the AAUP chapter’s advocacy when asked by the News in March. When asked if the University plans to address the concerns raised and changes recommended in the report, Yale spokesperson Tina Posterli pointed to the existing language about academic freedom in the faculty handbook, but did not directly address the AAUP chapter’s report. “Academic freedom is a crucial, central element of Yale’s purpose and mission. This tenet is explicitly recognized in Yale’s Faculty Handbook, which states that ‘academic freedom and the spirit of free inquiry are fundamental to Yale’s mission,’ and includes procedural safeguards for faculty who believe their academic freedom has been improperly restrained,” Posterli wrote in an email to the News. She also emphasized the work of the Provost’s Committee on the Principles of Academic Freedom, adding that the “committee has begun its work, including distribution of a survey on academic freedom to all members of the faculty and others in teaching and research,” and that it will produce a report by the end of the fall semester. HoSang said that the faculty handbook is updated annually “at the Provost’s discretion” and that introducing additional language into the faculty handbook is costless, can be “easily done” over the summer by the Strobel and McInnis and would not override the authority of the Provost’s committee on academic freedom “because that’s focused on what the statement should be.” He also pointed to the speed at which Yale narrowed its mission statement following a recommendation by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education — two weeks after the committee released its report. “When President McInnis wanted to change the mission statement, we took note of how quickly it was done,” HoSang said. “She felt there was urgency, there wasn’t a big campus-wide review, there was a recommendation from one committee, but no other campus constituents were engaged, and it was done.” “We would hope that they could act on this with the same urgency that President McInnis acted on changing the mission statement at her discretion,” HoSang said. “We assume that they’ll take seriously that many dozens of their colleagues, their faculties as well, have reported that they don’t feel like they can teach and research freely, and that they’ll want to address that problem.” The AAUP sent McInnis and Strobel the report once it was released but has not yet met with members of the administration since the report’s release, according to HoSang. “We’re looking forward to their response, given the widespread endorsement of the campus,” HoSang said, referring to the student support the AAUP’s recent campaign for academic freedom has received. Last month, the Yale College Council Senate and the Graduate and Professional Student Senate passed a joint resolution backing Yale AAUP’s academic freedom demands. Following the Tuesday release of Yale AAUP’s report, leaders from both student organizations emphasized the importance of protecting academic freedom. “Academic freedom is fragile,” Sam Haddad LAW ’26, last year’s president of the Graduate and Professional School Student Senate, wrote in an email to the News. “The Federal Administration has proved that over the past few months. If our university’s focus is truly on the production and dissemination of knowledge, then Administrators must act on this report’s concerning findings.” YCC President Alex William Chen ’28 wrote in an email to the News that the report “emphasizes the unmistakable need for students and faculty to jointly advocate for our shared interests: students can’t learn if teachers don’t feel free to teach.” “The Yale education students deserve is achieved through, and only through, critically engaging with an unfettered range of teaching and thought --- academic self-censorship robs students of exactly that,” he wrote. Yale’s chapter of the AAUP was established in November 2024.

title: Yale fills four out of six open residential college dean positions
author: Jolynda Wang
text: Yale College announced new appointments to four out of six open residential college dean positions. Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis announced on Tuesday the appointments of Ksenia Sidorenko GRD ’14 ’23 as the dean of Berkeley College, Katherine Leach as the dean of Davenport College, Matthew Newman ’08 as the dean of Silliman College and David Gardner as the dean of Timothy Dwight College. The new deans are set to begin their tenures in July. The dean positions for Branford and Grace Hopper colleges still remain open. The recent appointments follow a string of departure announcements from six residential college deans, four of whom said they are taking on administrative roles within the University. Lewis told the News in April that the quick succession of departures was “just a coincidence.” “We have interviewed a lot of great candidates and the people who are joining as deans are experienced administrators and caring people who will make great contributions to their residential colleges and to Yale,” Lewis wrote in an email to the News. The appointments were announced in emails to each college community from Lewis following consultations with heads of college and advisory committees within each college. When asked about the selection process, Lewis wrote in an email to the News that “I can say that I’ve appreciated the input of students, staff, faculty, fellows, and heads.” Newman, the next dean of Silliman College, graduated from Yale as a member of Ezra Stiles College in 2008 and later earned his doctoral degree in Classical Studies from the University of Michigan. He currently serves at Princeton University as assistant dean for studies in Whitman College, one of the university’s seven residential colleges. Newman wrote in an email to the News that the “challenge of a new place” and the “opportunity to shepherd Yale students from matriculation to graduation” drew him to the role, adding that he is excited to teach “at some point” and that his transition to his new position has been as “smooth as such a transition can be.” “I hope students will see my ‘office,’ literal and conceptual, as a space for unabashedly asking whatever questions they have, for finding support in moments of difficulty, and for learning to show themselves (and others) grace as they face the trials of the undergraduate experience,” Newman wrote. Newman will move into Silliman with his partner, Nicole Rodriguez, and two pets, Bernard, their black cat, and Charlie, their dog. Julien Amsellem ’27, who is in Silliman, said in a phone interview with the News that he hopes Newman will be an active figure in the college community by attending student events and interacting with students outside formal office hours. “Deans really do play a large role for students,” Amsellem said. “One of the things we really emphasize is that much like the head of college, the dean is someone who is part of this family within the residential college system.” Sidorenko, the incoming dean of Berkeley College, received two master’s degrees in 2014 and a doctoral degree in 2023, all from Yale. She currently serves as assistant dean for access and engagement at Yale’s Graduate School of Arts and Sciences, where she oversees recruitment and retention initiatives and supports graduate students. Before joining the Graduate School administration, Sidorenko taught courses at Yale and also served for six years as a first-year adviser in Pierson and Grace Hopper colleges. Joining her in the Berkeley dean’s residence will be her Ragdoll cat, Salammbô. Sidorenko’s appointment follows the abrupt departure of former Berkeley dean Bárbara Cruvinel Santiago ’17, who was in her second year on the job. Lewis announced Cruvinel Santiago’s leave over spring break, which was met with student disappointment. Leach, the incoming dean of Davenport College, currently serves as assistant dean of undergraduate students at Dartmouth, where she offers academic and personal advising to undergraduates. Leach previously taught at Harvard, where she also worked as a resident tutor, academic advisor and interim dean of a house. According to an announcement sent to the Davenport community, Leach will be joined in the Davenport dean’s residence by her wife, Jordan, as well as their newborn son, Ellis, and their cat, Giles. “She brings to Yale a deep enthusiasm for residential college life and for supporting students both inside and outside the classroom,” Lewis wrote in his announcement of Leach’s appointment. Gardner joins Timothy Dwight College from Phillips Academy in Andover, Massachusetts, where he currently serves as assistant dean of students and instructor in English. Alexander Medel ’27, a Yale College Council senator for Timothy Dwight and a staff writer for the News, said he hopes his college’s next dean will understand the close-knit culture that defines its community and be prepared to embrace its traditions. “I would love to see somebody who understands what makes TD’s community so special, somebody who’s ready to embrace all the customs, traditions and all the special little things that make TD,” Medel said in a phone interview with the News. The upcoming school year is the last year of Lewis’ five-year term.

title: After Harvard passes grade cap, Yale faculty say ‘bravo’ for first step
author: Jolynda Wang
text: Conversations around grade inflation in the Ivy League turned into action on Wednesday when Harvard announced that 70 percent of its faculty voted in favor of a policy to cap A grades at 20 percent for each course, allowing for up to four additional A’s per class, beginning in fall 2027. Wednesday’s vote marked a major shift by Harvard to confront grade inflation that coincides with similar efforts at Yale, which are in earlier stages. Yale professors, while decrying that grade inflation contributes to student stress, perfectionism and difficulty distinguishing academic performance, have described the issue as a collective action problem, in which stricter grading by individual faculty — and universities — comparatively disadvantages their own students. In a statement after Wednesday’s vote, Amanda Claybaugh ’93, Harvard’s dean of undergraduate education who has spearheaded the school’s effort to curb grade inflation, expressed hope that the vote drives Harvard’s peers to follow its lead. “This is a consequential vote,” Claybaugh wrote. “It will, I believe, strengthen the academic culture of Harvard; it will also, I hope, encourage other institutions to confront similar questions with the same level of rigor and courage.” In interviews with the News, Yale professors expressed support for Harvard’s new grade cap as a meaningful step by a leading university to combat grade inflation. Many still pointed out what they described as imperfections in the system, including a lack of nuance between seminars and lectures, the possibility of a proliferation of A-minuses and a focus away from intellectual growth. Yale Law School professor Sarath Sanga LAW ’14 said “bravo to Harvard” in a Zoom interview, praising its faculty for taking concrete action on grade inflation. Sanga said that although he thinks Harvard’s solution is imperfect, it is preferable to maintaining the status quo of grade distribution, which he described as “beyond terrible and illogical.” “This is Harvard’s attempt to make a baby step toward putting together the best possible pedagogical environment for the students,” Sanga said. “By virtue of making the right step in the right direction and being the first one to do it publicly, I mean, that takes guts, right?” Grade inflation has reached similar heights at Yale and Harvard. At Harvard, after its administration urged faculty to combat grade inflation in October, the share of A’s fell from 60.2 percent in the 2024–25 school year to 53.4 percent last fall. At Yale, 58.3 percent of grades were A’s in the 2022–23 school year — part of a distribution that Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis described as “pretty consistent” over the past four years. Yale College’s class of 2026 set a record-high cutoff for Latin honors this month, with summa cum laude requiring a 3.99 GPA. Harvard’s cutoff for summa cum laude last year was 3.989. In April, Yale’s Committee on Trust in Higher Education released a report that includes remedies for grade inflation. The report recommends that the University registrar “immediately” start including grade percentiles per course in transcripts to reflect the “context for each grade.” It also recommended a “3.0 mean, or some other college-wide standard,” which it acknowledged will require “further conversation among the faculty” and urged that the conversation “begin now.” Yale College’s Standing Committee on Teaching, Learning and Advising — convened by Lewis in conjunction with University President Maurie McInnis — is currently reviewing grade inflation at Yale. In February, Lewis told the News that the standing committee’s members were closely monitoring the grading policies at Harvard, noting that peer universities are “competitors” and that he doesn’t want “an A at Yale to be seen as a lesser A.” When asked about the Harvard vote on Wednesday, Lewis wrote to the News that grading practices “will be a topic of discussion” for Yale faculty in the upcoming school year. Economics professor Ray Fair speculated in a phone interview that if Harvard significantly reduced the number of A grades while Yale continued awarding them at much higher rates, a Harvard A could come to carry more weight than a Yale A. That discrepancy could potentially affect Yale’s reputation and ultimately incentivize the University to adopt similar reforms, Fair said. Fair chaired the University’s ad hoc committee on grading policy, which convened in the 2012–13 and 2013–14 academic years.
