That committee’s proposal recommended a set of grading guidelines as well as that Yale College switch from a letter-grade system to a 100-point scale, which faced fierce student backlash and was ultimately dropped by faculty. “Yale tried in 2014 to be a leader in this, and the faculty rejected that. However, now Harvard accepted something similar, and so somebody has started this process,” Fair said, referring to efforts to combat grade inflation. “One would hope that — if you think grade inflation is a problem — the Harvard move would trigger other peer institutions to do the same thing or do something similar.” Sanga, who helped write the Committee on Trust in Higher Education’s report, described the committee’s recommendations as a “better-designed rule” than Harvard’s grade cap. Including course percentiles and aiming for an overall 3.0 average GPA would provide students with clearer, standardized feedback about their academic strengths while also making transcripts easier for outside readers to interpret, he said. But Sanga recognized that universities often hesitate to act until peer institutions move first, and he noted that Harvard’s prominence could pressure schools like Yale to reconsider their own grading practices. “There’s no denying that Harvard is the American flagship. This is the American global leader in higher education, whether you like it or not,” Sanga said, making note of Harvard’s resources. “It has this kind of leadership status even when it’s not perfect, maybe especially when it’s not perfect. And so I’m looking at what they just accomplished there.” Political science professor Dan Mattingly ’04 GRD ’08 referred to Harvard’s policy as “the least terrible option in a sea of terrible options” to combat grade inflation. He said the policy would reduce professors’ ability to independently assess student work and expressed hope for more “nuanced” reforms. Still, he compared Harvard’s move to “very strong medicine” that will “force people into a new equilibrium.” If more elite universities adopt measures against grade inflation, those reforms will become more “durable,” Mattingly added. Philosophy professor Thomas Pogge also described grade inflation as a “nationwide phenomenon” fueled by competition among universities, noting that prominent institutions like Yale and Harvard have a particular responsibility to lead reform efforts rather than continue escalating grades upward. “Highly visible institutions are much more able to take the first step,” Pogge said in a Zoom interview. Some Yale professors, however, pointed out that Harvard’s new system does not account for nuances between course styles and leaves room for the proliferation of A-minuses. Global affairs professor Ted Wittenstein ’04 LAW ’12 wrote in the email to the News that he was of “two minds” about the new Harvard policy. While he supported the Harvard grade cap for its immediacy and simplicity, Wittenstein raised concerns that a universal cap would fail to account for differences between large introductory lecture courses and smaller advanced seminars. Pogge also noted that Harvard’s policy places no cap on A-minus grades, potentially creating what he described as a “loophole.” Physics professor A. Douglas Stone echoed this concern. “They allow unlimited A-minuses it seems and this seems a huge loophole in that instructors not wanting to upset students can have only two grades, A and A-, and still not really differentiate different levels of achievement,” Stone wrote in an email to the News. “I hope we make changes which require a larger range of grades.” Astronomy professor Charles Bailyn ’81 wrote in an email to the News that he thinks Harvard’s proposal does not go “nearly far enough” to address the underlying problems with modern grading systems, arguing that the traditional scale of A through F remains fundamentally flawed. But Bailyn added that he would vote in favor of any Yale proposal to combat grade inflation — “Anything is better than the current ‘system,’” he wrote. Five professors said in interviews with the News that the broader goal of grading reform should be to return attention to intellectual growth and academic challenge rather than perfecting numbers on a transcript. English senior lecturer Heather Klemann GRD ’08 ’13 said in a Zoom interview that determining a percentage cap on grades can feel arbitrary and emphasized the importance of restoring students’ “love of learning.” Film and Media Studies professor Charles Musser ’73 questioned the fundamental concern about grade inflation and opposed Harvard’s proposed cap A grades, stating that the policy would undermine “academic freedom.” He said in a phone interview with the News that his goal is to inspire his students so that all of them produce strong enough work to receive an A. Musser added that Yale’s highly selective admissions process naturally results in a student body capable of earning excellent grades. Rather than viewing high grades as evidence of inflation, he said, they reflect the academic strength of admitted students. Grading systems should focus on encouraging intellectual growth rather than manufacturing an “artificial” distribution of grades, he noted. According to a 2023 report by Fair, 79 percent of grades in Yale College in the 2022–23 school year were A’s or A-minuses.

title: IN PHOTOS: Commencement 2026
text: VOL. CXLIX New Haven, Connecticut Tuesday, June 16, 2026

title: At Law School Commencement, Boasberg ’85 LAW ’90 stresses moral fiber
author: Sasha Cabral
text: Judge James Boasberg ’85 LAW ’90 spoke at Yale Law School’s 2026 Commencement ceremony on Monday to approximately 240 graduates and their guests. The event, which was held in Payne Whitney Gymnasium’s Lanman Center, followed the University-wide Commencement on Old Campus. Boasberg, the chief judge of the United States District Court for the District of Columbia, has been the subject of a firestorm of criticism from the right for his rulings against the Trump administration — particularly his order halting deportation flights of Venezuelan nationals in March 2025. In keeping with recent years, the Law School did not publicly announce Boasberg as the Commencement speaker in its announcement about the ceremony. In addition to the Commencement speech, the ceremony traditionally features remarks by the dean of the Law School and a speech from a faculty member. The faculty speaker this year was Nicholas R. Parrillo GRD ’01 ’05 ’12 LAW ’04. Former Dean Heather Gerken spoke about her departure from the Law School to lead the Ford Foundation, and current Dean Cristina Rodríguez ’95 LAW ’00 also addressed attendees. After Parrillo’s speech, Rodríguez introduced Boasberg, remarking on his participation in theater and basketball during his time at Yale. She added that the judge has made rulings on topics “at the heart of our democracy.” Last spring, Boasberg made headlines when he pursued a contempt investigation into the second Trump administration over its refusal to comply with his order to cease deportation flights to El Salvador — an order that prompted Trump, along with Republican lawmakers, to call for the judge’s impeachment. Last month , a court of appeals ordered the termination of the contempt investigation, citing the lack of a “clear and specific” order from Boasberg mandating that the Venezuelan nationals not be transferred into Salvadoran custody. Last July, former United States Attorney General Pam Bondi’s office filed a misconduct complaint against Boasberg, alleging that he had attempted to inappropriately influence other judges to raise concerns about the Trump administration’s actions. The complaint was ultimately dismissed by a federal appeals court. Two months ago, Trump characterized Boasberg as “wacky, nasty, crooked and totally out of control” after the judge quashed subpoenas in the Justice Department’s investigation into Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell. Days later, Supreme Court Chief Justice John Roberts rebuked personal criticism of federal judges, saying, “it’s got to stop.” Roberts also criticized Trump’s calls for Boasberg’s impeachment last year. Boasberg did not address political matters in his speech, instead offering advice to graduating students. He advised graduates to seek out occupational opportunities with a “mission” that corresponds with their personal values and reminded them, “your life is larger than your career.” He recounted a pivotal moment from his own career where he realized he did not want to continue in a private practice role that involved denying some individuals’ insurance coverage. Boasberg said he found more fulfillment working for the government. In his speech, Boasberg also praised the instruction he received at the Law School from professors including Harold Koh and Guido Calabresi ’53 LAW ’58, who taught about “heroes in the law” that demonstrated “what committed lawyers can do to improve society.” He cited the work of judges like Thurgood Marshall and Frank Johnson, who championed civil rights and racial equality. Koh said that Boasberg’s speech reflected the judge’s own legacy in upholding the rule of law. “Chief Judge Boasberg is one of our finest graduates and judges,” Koh wrote to the News. “His deeply humane and principled speech both inspired our students and challenged them to stand up for the rule of law, as he himself has so courageously done.” Boasberg acknowledged that students might embark on a career in a large corporate firm for financial stability, but advised them to question constantly whether the job is the right one for them. He warned against the practice of choosing a career for the sake of keeping one’s options open. “But when you are representing a public entity, for example, a municipality, a state, or a federal government, there’s a meaningful difference between isolated disagreements with your client and helping to defend policies that are wholly antithetical to your core values,” Boasberg said. “I encourage all of you to have your own red line. Each of you will put it in a different place.” “If you were asked to cross that red line,” he said, “you must decide whether you can do so and still look at yourself in the mirror, or whether it’s time to start searching for a new job.” Lastly, Boasberg encouraged students to pursue unconventional legal careers, despite the uncertainty that might come with such paths. “You will find Yale Law graduates in an amazing array of positions, and mind-bogglingly varied millieus. But you won’t be one of them if you just sit in your cubicle, hoping that one day, someone will come by and tap you on the shoulder with something interesting,” Boasberg said. “Be imaginative, be creative and don’t be bound by the most traditional options.” During his time at the Law School, Boasberg said he lived at 61 Lake Place with seven other students, including current Supreme Court Justice Brett Kavanaugh ’87 LAW ’90. In his speech on Monday, Boasberg said the group still gathers annually. Last year, the Law School’s commencement speaker was Elizabeth Prelogar, the solicitor general under the Biden administration.

title: Yalies for mental health
author: Paul Johansen
text: Congratulations to the class of 2026! It has been a long and difficult road to graduation, and it’s a uniquely challenging moment in history to begin post-college life. How do I know it’s been a long and difficult road? In 2021, a group of students, alumni and family members impacted by Yale’s mental health policies organized Elis for Rachael. I serve as president of this group, and over the past four years, your fellow Yalies have communicated their trials and tribulations with us regarding mental health services at Yale. We are grateful to those of you who shared painful stories of struggling through the labyrinth of mental health services offered at Yale. We used your feedback to create a 10-point petition, which some of you may recall signing at Yale-Harvard games. And we used this petition as the basis of a landmark class-action lawsuit, which we settled with Yale in August 2023, just as you began your sophomore years. In the settlement, Yale agreed to many of the conditions that student groups had requested of the administration over the previous decade. Those student groups included the Yale College Council twice, Mental Health Justice at Yale, Students Unite Now and the Yale Law School Mental Health Alliance. Yale had previously made no credible changes in response to the requests of these student groups. In response to the lawsuit, however, Yale agreed to the majority of your 10 petition requests: Yale stopped terminating health insurance for students taking a medical leave; Yale eliminated costly, non-medical roadblocks to reinstatement following a medical withdrawal; Yale restructured the refund schedule for tuition, room and board fees; Yale allowed students reasonable access to campus while on medical withdrawal, no longer requiring written permission to set foot on campus; Yale agreed to reduce the minimum duration for medical withdrawals; Yale designated a part-time, non-evaluative administrator to advocate for students considering, currently on or returning from a medical leave of absence; and Yale thus far has ended their practice of rejecting reinstatement applications without any actionable explanations. In addition, Yale now allows part-time study as a reasonable accommodation in “rare” instances. Yale also agreed to establish a process for appealing accommodation denials from Student Accessibility Services. We are not convinced this process is independent from SAS, however. On the other hand, many students still wait unacceptably long to see a therapist. For instance, upon returning from summer vacation, students do not automatically continue seeing their therapist from the previous school year. Instead, they must undergo the placement process all over again, unnecessarily lengthening the time it takes to be matched with a therapist. Yale has not met student requests for a more diverse range of therapists. Yale still does not offer an affordable Preferred Provider Organization option for health insurance. And Yale did not agree to implement annual mental health first-aid training for students, faculty, staff and administrators. Furthermore, none of the settlement’s changes apply to graduate students. Under our August 2023 settlement with Yale, the University agreed to a three-year monitoring period during which alleged violations of the new policies could be brought quickly before a judge. That period ends Aug. 28, just two months from now. Although we have generally been encouraged by Yale’s implementation of their new policies, any future erosion will become significantly harder to challenge once judicial oversight expires. We note that the class of 2026 is the last set of students who can remember suffering through Yale previous punitive policies. One of the largest unresolved issues remains federal law. Under current regulations, universities that provide medical care may classify student medical records — ordinarily protected under HIPAA — as “educational records” governed instead by FERPA, which provides substantially weaker privacy protections. We have spoken with Massachusetts’ Senator Ed Markey, a longtime mental health advocate and one of HIPAA’s original authors, about closing that loophole. We were very encouraged to see the Princeton Review include three Ivy League schools — Columbia, Dartmouth and Princeton — on its “2026 Mental Health Services Honor Roll.” Yale was notably absent. The schools recognized this year have “shown a strong commitment to their students’ mental health and wellbeing.” We look forward to the day when Yale earns similar recognition through sustained, rigorous and genuinely student-centered mental health support for both undergraduate and graduate students. Only then will Yale’s policies truly help future classes get to their own finish lines. PAUL JOHANSEN is president of Elis for Rachael and an adjunct professor of mathematics at Berkshire Community College in Pittsfield, Massachusetts. Paul graduated from Yale in 1988 as a member of Ezra Stiles College. He can be reached at findus@elisforrachael.org .

title: On rights and responsibilities
author: Michael Ndubisi
text: Today, on Old Campus, the University’s president will stand before the graduating class for the 325th time and admit them, by tradition, to “all the rights and responsibilities of their degrees.” Some version of those words will be spoken at thousands of commencements across the country, but Yale’s carries particular weight. Where 133 miles north, Harvard will welcome its graduates into “the fellowship of educated men and women,” Yale’s conferral reflects a distinction once made to elevate the obligations of learning and has since diffused into the shared ceremonial language of American commencements. It is a small but telling example of how Yale has long set the terms for American higher education. This April, in another attempt to shape the landscape of higher learning, Yale released a 58-page report on why public trust in universities has collapsed. It identifies real failures in costs that have made elite education a luxury and admissions processes that perform meritocracy while, in reality, rewarding wealth . To its credit, the committee that wrote the report confronts the crisis of higher education with unusual candor. But what it cannot bring itself to examine is the role Yale played in spreading those failures everywhere else. Nor can it reckon with what has become of the graduates it sends into the world. Yale's career infrastructure delivers 46% of its work-bound graduates into finance, consulting and technology. That pipeline concentrates them in five cities across four coastal states. They work in industries that have done more than almost any other to narrow the geography of American opportunity, to hollow out the places they came from and to make the kind of upward mobility Yale claims to represent harder for everyone else. For the rural or first-generation student whose family sacrificed for the degree, that pipeline is not a promise kept. For the country that subsidizes Yale's tax exemption, it is a negative return on its investment. For the faculty who came to contribute to something larger than credential production, it is a quiet embarrassment. And the trust report does not mention any of this. Yale’s report was still circulating when emails obtained from San Francisco’s mayor’s office revealed that the University’s engineering dean had been developing a business plan for a satellite campus in the Bay Area. Yale’s spokesperson says there are no current plans. The emails suggest the conversation is further along than that. The move would follow Vanderbilt’s recent acquisition of the financially insolvent California College of the Arts, in what one Atlantic writer called the national-chain model of undergraduate education. The schools with the resources to expand are doing so toward the cities where talent and capital have already concentrated. While Yale measures its national impact on a state-by-state map , counting clinical trials and patents, its expansion strategy points in the same direction as its graduates . Just days after the trust report’s release, Yale quietly revised its mission statement, excising its commitment to improving the world and educating aspiring leaders. What remains is the preservation and dissemination of “knowledge,” in a narrowed ambition that happens to position the University perfectly to become a national leader in artificial intelligence. Researchers writing in this year’s Business and Society journal warned that generative AI threatens to degrade our collective knowledge ecosystem through what they call “ epistemic pollution ” or the dissemination of low-quality, misleading and fabricated information at scale. And the University that just committed itself to preserving knowledge is racing toward the industry most likely to corrupt it. The report identifies Yale’s predicament as a trust problem, but it is more precisely a resentment problem. Americans increasingly see elite universities and their graduates as participants in a system designed to concentrate wealth and opportunity for a small class of people who already possess most of both at their expense . A university that has revised its mission to withdraw its vision from the world beyond its walls, tracks its impact in patents while half its graduates funnel into extractive industries and is considering expanding toward the same coastal cities those graduates already cluster in is not answering that resentment; it is confirming it. Most of us will leave Old Campus for the last time after commencement with some sadness. There will be tears for the end of our shortest, gladdest years of life. But the conferral of our rights and responsibilities is not a farewell. It is a beginning. Gone are the days of the University for God and for country. But a University that exists only for Yale is not much use to the country that sustains it. So if the institution no longer takes the obligations embedded in the words of our commencement ceremony seriously, then we must. We know the rights: Yale clubs, library access, reunions, The Game and others. The responsibilities are real, too, and they are ours now as well. MICHAEL NDUBISI is a former editor of the Yale Daily News’ Opinion desk and writer of the fortnightly column “A More Perfect Union.” He is a graduating senior in Saybrook College majoring in History and Political Science. He can be reached at michael.ndubisi@yale.edu .

title: Consider our legacy
author: Emily Hettinger
text: We made it. After four years of essays, problem sets and a million choices to make about extracurriculars and internships and social groups, all that’s left is to design quirky hats and enjoy the momentous occasion of our commencement with our friends and family. But in the midst of all this joy, I think we should make one final commitment. We need to grapple with the fact that we are the beneficiaries of a deeply broken and unequal system. And as the class of 2026, we must use the privileges of our education to do something about it. It needs to be said: Yale has a wealth problem, uncomfortable as it may be to acknowledge. According to the New York Times , Yale accepts more students from the top one percent of the United States’ income distribution than the entire bottom sixty percent combined. Daniel Markovits ’91 LAW ’00 says it well in his 2019 book, The Meritocracy Trap: The wealth-based admissions system we have concentrates wealth and power in the hands of a small few, at the expense of political unity, upward social mobility for most Americans and the life satisfaction of even the most successful among us. But, to me, the deeper risk is that the disproportionate wealth at Yale denigrates the very idea of the liberal arts education. There are many reasons to choose a liberal arts education, but probably the main value of the liberal arts, especially in this moment, is that they can, allegedly, prepare citizens to be critical thinkers. In her 1963 book, Eichmann in Jerusalem , philosopher Hannah Arendt describes evil as banal, that most people who do evil things are not evil people but just people following orders. Critical thinkers, in my view, are the ones who say no to banal orders to do evil things. Or at the very least, stand up for what is right, even when it is easy to stay silent. For a school that claims a devotion to the liberal arts, I am concerned by our acceptance of the banal orders we are confronted with. Yale sends a disproportionate number of its students into fields that bring wealth and prestige to their workers, often at the expense of the common good. Places where it is someone’s job to cut costs in United States detention centers, turbocharge opioid sales and invest in fossil fuels. Is this how we want to be remembered? I think things would be different if we reimagined who a Yale education was for. For middle and working class students, a college education, much less an elite college education, is not inevitable. Paying for a school like Yale as a middle class family usually means pinching every penny for years, often to end up in serious debt . And coming to Yale from an underfunded public school often means being the only person from your high school at Yale, which can be an incredibly lonely and daunting experience. Taken together, the opportunity cost to attend Yale for non-wealthy students is quite high. As such, the very choice to attend a fancy school where life is put on hold for four years, just to think and grow, is grounded in the critical thought that the liberal arts are meant to cultivate. For these students, a Yale education is a service to something much larger than themselves, an undertaking on behalf of the community that sacrificed much to make the dream possible. If we had more students for whom this education carried a strong sense of community responsibility, maybe more people would dedicate themselves to the many problems in our world that need solving. We, as a generation of Yalies, have the power to change the definition of a Yale student, to utterly transform our admissions system into one that prioritizes the public good over wealth. But in order to do that, we need to acknowledge that regardless of how we all arrived at Yale, we leave with far more to personally gain from the status quo than we ever will from a reformed system. All of us will have access to opportunities that very few are afforded, simply because of our Yale diploma. All of us who choose to have children will be the parents of Yale legacies who will have an explicit advantage in the Yale admissions process. And even if legacy preference goes away, our kids will have disproportionate access to fancy private schools, music and sports lessons, test prep and all sorts of other things that will look good on college applications. If we change the system, we directly jeopardize our own children’s chances of following in our footsteps at Yale. So, to my peers in the class of 2026, what do we want our legacy to be? Though it will involve personal sacrifice, I think we ought to lead a transformation of Yale’s culture to prioritize the public over the self. We can do better than agnostically educating a disproportionately wealthy student body to develop skills used for personal benefit instead of the public good. In a country with increasing distrust in elites , it is our duty to do better. As my parting contribution to the News, I ask the class of 2026 to do one thing: Pull the ladder up behind you. Reject the structures that we will soon benefit from and call for something better, even though it may cost your future children the opportunity to study at Yale. Commit to organizing against legacy admissions at our university, reinvest the thousands of dollars you might have spent on private school tuition into your local community, surround yourself with people who did not receive an Ivy League education and demand that Yale serve the public good over its own interests. If we succeed, we will be remembered for something much more than clever hats on Class Day. EMILY HETTINGER is a senior in Pierson College studying Psychology and Education and a staff columnist for the News. Contact her at emily.hettinger@yale.edu .

title: The death of college exploration
author: Ryan Wang
text: If you read my application to Yale, you would probably guess that I spent my bright college years flitting between economic research seminars, physics labs and a political campaign office. Instead, I spent my first week of classes grinding through applications for the Yale Student Investment Group or the Yale Undergraduate Consulting Group. By the end of sophomore year, I had climbed the ranks of the consulting group and was running its client acquisition team. Academically, I dropped my physics major and rarely strayed outside of economics and mathematics classes. Sometimes, I mourn for the kid who eagerly submitted his Yale application. I suspect I am not alone. In his 1852 treatise “ The Idea of a University ,” the English theologian John Henry Newman argued that a college education should cultivate the intellect broadly, not provide vocational training. Newman was not alone in his position. Yale’s own 1828 report on academic instruction declared: “Our object is not to teach that which is peculiar to any one of the professions; but to lay the foundation which is common to them all.” Yet for many of us Yalies, this vision has not materialized. Somewhere between 1828 and today, the dream of spending our college years exploring our passions and career interests remains only that — a dream. Exploration is dead. The question is: who killed it? My first suspect is one familiar to most Yalies: our future employers and graduate schools. In the past two decades, the college internship has devolved from an act of exploration into a prerequisite for entry. During my time at Yale, top consultancies have steadily moved recruiting timelines earlier. The proliferation of sophomore summer internships and pre-professional clubs pressures new students to dedicate their underclassmen years to breaking into the industry. My friends in other fields tell the same story. The aspiring investment banker needs a boutique stint before junior year, the software engineer needs a project portfolio to capture the attention of tech companies, the mechanical engineer needs lab experience before finishing their core requirements and the future policymaker needs a D.C. internship before their first political theory seminar. Yalies interested in further academic study face the same curse. Matriculant GPAs at top medical and law schools have crept up consistently, while aspiring doctors now spend thousands of undergraduate hours on clinical experience and research to remain competitive. Students drawn to doctoral studies face National Science Foundation and National Institutes of Health fellowship applications demanding research experience dating to freshman year. Career preparation has superseded exploration across campus. Yet, can our future employers and schools be fully to blame? I have a second suspect for the murder of exploration: us. When younger Yalies seek career advice over dining hall meals, I first ask if they have found their guiding purpose. The conversation usually plays out like this: “What is your north star?” “Right now, I’m locked in on securing that McKinsey internship.” “Yes, but what’s your end goal? Your own guiding mission?” “...I’m not sure yet. Maybe law school?” The ambition that carried us to New Haven is easily hijacked by arbitrary measures of success established by the student community. Within weeks of arriving, first-years are exposed to what “success” looks like in each realm of campus: “You should apply to YSIG if you’re interested in finance. They have a ton of alumni at Goldman Sachs.” “If you want a shot at a T14 school, you should have a 4.0 and a law journal publication.” Rather than charting their own paths, Yalies often co-opt a communal definition of success and work tirelessly to achieve it. First-semester me — the kid that raved about particle physics and economic policy in his Yale application — sleepwalked my way up the ranks of a consulting club without ever asking if that was what I had really wanted. So, who killed college exploration? Both suspects are culpable. Employers and schools impose aggressive recruitment timelines and stringent requirements, and ambitious students eagerly rise to the challenge. This hyper-competitive applicant pool encourages firms to raise their standards again, fueling an arms race. Consequently, higher education produces a generation of over-prepared, under-explored graduates. Students determined to succeed within a single field never discover what they truly want, arriving at 22 in a job they chose at 19 for reasons that were never really their own. Is there a way to break the cycle? Employers and graduate schools should ask which of their requirements still measure genuine ability, and reckon with what they lose by screening out candidates who spent college actually exploring. Absent institutional change, I find it naive to tell Yalies they should simply ignore post-graduate pressures. Instead, I recommend “strategic exploration:” invest seriously in at least two fields you actually care about, fulfill your distributional requirements early to explore the whole range of classes Yale has to offer, and ask whether your ambitions are genuinely yours or inherited from the students around you. I broke out of autopilot at the end of junior year. I rediscovered my love for economics, reneging on a full-time consulting offer to pursue academic research. The world today rewards the straightest path, yet there is a quiet tragedy in arriving at a destination you never chose for yourself. The kid you described in your Yale application is still in there — go find them. RYAN WANG is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Economics and Mathematics. After Yale, Ryan will be working at the MIT Economics Department as a research fellow. Contact him at ryan.wang@yale.edu .

title: What is Yale for?
author: Matias Guevara Ruales
text: Why does one come to Yale? One reasonable answer is money. Just four years out of college, Yale alums earn almost double what the typical college graduate does, and the median lifetime return of coming here was recently estimated at 3.4 million dollars. Still, one could be forgiven for thinking that a Yale education ought to offer more than the material benefits that might accrue from the degree. You may have heard Dean Lewis make some such distinction during a public appearance at some point, and I echo it here because my concern is precisely one of Yale’s main selling points to prospective students: the idea that when you decide to enroll here, you’re signing up to get a “liberal education.” The phrase is familiar to all of us at American higher-learning institutions, but what has it come to mean, amid the onerous demands of our results-oriented culture? Can we still recognize in it the suggestion that there are at least two parts to the value of a college education that matter, only one of which produces “results” in the traditional sense? The first of those is plainly the accreditation of technical skills embodied by the diploma, which paves the way for the superior earnings elite schools are famous for. The second part, often called the liberal arts, is harder to discern. It has to do with the development of students as people — their personal flourishing, we might have said once, though that word now carries a sentimental ring that may itself be a symptom of the diminishing status of the liberal arts in our culture. But the liberal arts have a use, too. They are, in the words of the recent report by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education, “the foundational wisdom that will serve [students] throughout their lives.” No virtue has ever advertised a payoff in strict cost-benefit terms, yet “wisdom” does properly characterize the part of our education that makes us critical thinkers, generous friends and conscientious citizens. These attributes are crucial to any individual life, and to any free society, but in recent years we have begun to surrender them. Ultra-competitive job markets have meant that a growing number of students are allocating their time so as to maximize early-career outcomes at the expense of pursuits once considered worthwhile, like taking challenging courses outside of their majors or joining non-professional clubs. Even within the areas directly related to their post-graduate goals, students must often turn to artificial intelligence, dig up class materials from previous semesters or employ other workarounds that free up time for professional development but defeat the most basic point of academic work: to learn. Underlying this trend is the belief that wasting time on pursuits not easily translatable into attractive resume entries can be fatal for long-term success. But recent research says otherwise. A literature review about the highest levels of human achievement, published last year, analyzed data from top performers across different fields — from Nobel laureates to Olympic champions to the the world’s best chess players — and found that what distinguished them from their sub-elite peers was that the former showed a more gradual progression toward excellence and a larger amount of multidisciplinary practice early on. That is, they took the time to explore many different interests before they specialized and became best-in-class. The world is not organized to facilitate this kind of exploration. Universities emphasize early displays of merit over lifelong achievement, while corporations use their deep pockets to lure young graduates into highly specialized jobs. And on a cultural level, too, we tend to think of doing things simply because we’re passionate about them as somehow amateur in the pejorative sense. But doing things for their own sake should not demote our pursuits to the status of hobbies. On the contrary, when we treat a hobby with enough seriousness we elevate it to the category of a passion, and the power to do this lies, in no small part, with each of us. What would happen if we approached our education as an experience in itself valuable? For one, our efforts might begin to seem worthwhile even without anybody’s stamp of approval, and we, in turn, might become more resistant to failure. When we looked in the mirror and saw that we had played our cards wrong, squandered our time, disappointed the expectations of parents and friends, we would not give up, because our work would not be just a means, but an end in itself. Why does one come to Yale? Yes, to be successful. Yes, to have a good income. But we also come here to be students, become better persons, and find the thing, or things, worthy of our lives’ work. We come here, in short, to flourish. The role of administrators in protecting that sacred soil of our personal flourishing, the university, is paramount. Yale could do more to fight the utilitarian trends that endanger it presently. But we, too, have a part to play in reclaiming the privilege of a truly liberal education — a phrase whose roots lie in the idea that to be educated is to become more free. What could be more profitable than that? MATIAS GUEVARA RUALES is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Humanities and Economics. He can be reached at matias.guevararuales@yale.edu .

title: Unilever research facility set to harness New Haven biotech scene
author: Evelyn Ronan
text: The consumer goods company Unilever will invest $270 million in a new research and development center in downtown New Haven, the company announced in a press conference last Thursday. The center is set to open at 2 Church St. in 2029. At the conference, which was livestreamed , Mayor Justin Elicker and Gov. Ned Lamont SOM ’80 heralded the investment as a sign of growth for New Haven and the state. Elicker lauded the city’s “booming” bioscience sector, which Unilever hopes to harness in studying “skin biology, the microbiome and the skin-brain axis,” according to a press release. In a statement emailed to the News, Leo Aquino, the company’s senior vice president of research and development, cited New Haven’s “ecosystem” of universities and quantum computing investment as a reason for the move. The center will apply “AI-powered, digitally integrated science” to product development and will house “formulation, fragrance, packaging, consumer insights, clinical testing” all “under one roof,” Aquino wrote. The Church Street location is set to employ around 300 workers. The Unilever development news arrives amid a steady flow of biotech developments in New Haven, funded by both Yale and the state. In September, Connecticut announced a $50 million grant to facilitate biotech growth in the city, alongside separate grants for startups QuantumCT and BioCT, which received $10 million and $3 million from the state, respectively. The building at 2 Church St. is set to be constructed by Winstanley Enterprises, a commercial real estate developer that has acquired properties across New Haven, CT Insider reported . In 2013, Winstanley Enterprises began construction on a tower at 100 College St. which now houses Yale’s psychology and neuroscience departments and Alexion Pharmaceuticals, a biopharmaceutical company. In 2024, the group finished construction on a building at 101 College St. which also houses Alexion, as well as biotech lab facilities. Both are located about a quarter of a mile from 2 Church St., where the building housing Unilever is set to stand. “New Haven places us at the center of some of the world’s leading medical schools and research institutes — including Yale — and we fully intend to build on those relationships as the center develops,” Aquino wrote. Becca Schelling GRD ’29, the director of content for the Yale Biotech Club, suggested that the Unilever investment could make the city a more attractive destination for recent graduates. The club supports student engagement with the local biotech sector by partnering with companies through externship and informational programs, Schelling said in a phone interview. Having more biotech companies in downtown New Haven “makes it easier” for the club to “facilitate those interactions with students,” she said. Although Schelling said that she had “not seen” members of the club staying in the city after graduating, she guessed that “if the opportunities presented themselves in New Haven, people would take them.” The Connecticut Department of Economic and Community Development will contribute a $10 million loan to support the construction of the 2 Church St. building, part of an effort to retain jobs for Connecticut residents, CT Insider reported. The new lab will replace Unilever’s longtime research and development lab in nearby Trumbull, Connecticut. David Schleicher, a Yale Law School professor who focuses on local government and property law, wrote in an email to the News that Unilever could have relocated out-of-state, like General Electric did in 2016 when it left Fairfield, Connecticut. A News article from the time described criticism that painted General Electric’s departure as an example of “the state’s chronic inability to grow its high-tech industry to full potential.” Ward 7 Alder Christine Kim ’99 wrote in a Monday email to the News that an “anchor tenant” of Unilever’s scale would mean “daytime population, foot traffic for our small businesses, and energy on blocks that have had vacancies.” Kim’s ward includes 2 Church St. as well as a cluster of nearby Winstanley bioscience developments. Kim added that she is keeping an eye on the “community impacts” of the center. “These are world-class scientists and engineers, many with families, who’ll need housing in an already-tight market. I don’t want our growth to price out the residents who built this city,” she wrote. Kim’s conversations with constituents about the future Unilever center have yielded both “genuine excitement about jobs and a livelier downtown” and “real questions about housing costs, parking, who gets hired, and what the public puts in versus gets back,” she wrote, noting that many are still “taking it in.” Aquino did not comment on which future roles would be filled by Unilever employees currently based in Trumbull and which would be filled through local hiring, but he noted that “pinpointing an area that worked for talent retention and attracting future talent” was one of the “key elements” in choosing the New Haven location. When asked about affordability concerns, Elicker wrote that New Haven is a “growing city” and is “committed to growing inclusively.” Elicker also pointed to the city’s goal of constructing 10,000 new housing units by 2035, a goal which New Haven is “well on our way” to achieving, he wrote. The 2 Church St. building will contribute millions of dollars in tax revenue to help support municipal services, Elicker wrote, adding that constructions at 100 College St. and 101 College St. contributed “more than the annual budget for the Parks Department or New Haven Free Public Library” in taxes to the city last year. Schleicher wrote that he did not think Unilever’s plans would substantially alter the city’s housing market, noting that Yale University and Yale New Haven Hospital each employ more than 10,000 employees, much more than Unilever’s expected staff of 300. Unilever will share the 2 Church St. space with biotech startup QuantumCT, according to the New Haven Independent .

title: Witnessing ICE arrests and nonprofit cuts, locals decry Trump policies
author: Nellie Kenney
text: A group of New Haveners, clad in bright colors and carrying paper cranes, sang out advice for resisting Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers at a recent May Day rally on the New Haven Green: “Don’t say anything, don’t sign anything. Don’t let them trick you into opening the door.” Local activists — like these members of Singing Resistance New Haven, a chapter of the national organization — have mobilized in response to sweeping federal policy changes and their local effects since the beginning of Donald Trump’s second presidential term. New Haven advocates described an atmosphere of anxiety amid both the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown and federal cuts to social services, which have jeopardized local access to food. The “biggest thing, both for attorneys and for non-citizens living in the U.S.” is the “uncertainty and how quickly things are changing,” Maureen Abell, an immigration attorney at the New Haven Legal Assistance Association, said in a phone interview last Friday. Federal immigration officers have arrested at least five people in New Haven this year as part of the president’s ongoing mass deportation operation . ICE arrests increased by around 300 percent in New England in the first seven months of Trump’s presidency, according to data from the Immigration Research Initiative. Abell pointed to cases of federal immigration officers arresting people while leaving court or at green-card interviews . Although these arrests comprised “a small percentage of cases,” she said, the practice is “still having a huge effect through how afraid it is making people to engage with the system.” ICE detained a woman from the Hill neighborhood last June and a Wilbur Cross High School student the following month. The student, Esdrás Zabaleta-Ramirez, was later released following a ruling by a federal immigration judge. In January 2026, ICE arrested three people in downtown New Haven and another person in a New Haven courthouse . In March, federal agents arrested a nursing student at Southern Connecticut State University, Keyla Vasquez-Zuniga, who was granted bond last week. Those arrests have sparked an array of local protests. In July 2025, over a hundred people gathered at Wilbur Cross to protest Zabaleta-Ramirez’ detention. Teachers at the school led a letter-writing campaign attesting to the student’s schoolwork and character. Last month, students and activists rallied at Southern Connecticut State University, calling for Vasquez-Zuniga’s release. New Haven has also been the site of protests against ICE activity at the national level. In response to the deaths of Renée Good and Alex Pretti this January during the Trump administration’s crackdown in Minneapolis, locals formed the New Haven chapter of Singing Resistance and organized four demonstrations , each with at least a hundred attendees, in January and February. As organizers gather to protest immigration enforcement actions, nearby city organizations continue to grapple with federal funding cuts and locals describe increased food insecurity due to Trump-era policy. Integrated Refugee and Immigrant Services, a local nonprofit that aids refugee resettlement, closed its New Haven office in April 2025 after its funding was slashed by the federal government. The organization was also forced to lay off 30 percent of its staff, Maggie Mitchell Salem, the nonprofit’s executive director, told the News in February. In 2024, Integrated Refugee and Immigrant Services had a budget of over $20 million, most of which was provided by the federal government, Mitchell Salem said in a phone interview last Friday. In 2025, following federal funding cuts, its budget dropped to around $11 million, and this year, it hovers around $7 million, Mitchell Salem said — a number which will continue to decrease as funding to support Afghan arrivals “sunsets” in September. Mitchell Salem called the fallout of federal cuts “painful,” adding that for many immigrants, the organization’s New Haven location was one of the “first places they went to receive support and services” in the United States. “We’re having to make some very tough choices, and we have to figure out what the people we serve need most,” she said. “What, reasonably, can IRIS do through our food pantry, through our array of program services? And then what do others do, perhaps better than us? And how can we all work together to de-duplicate, to make sure that the most essential services remain available while having to cut back on others?” The Trump administration’s policy has altered the nonprofit’s partnerships as well as its funding, Mitchell Salem said. In October, the nonprofit announced that it would not receive refugees from the United States Refugee Admissions Program after the Trump administration moved to prioritize the resettlement of Afrikaners, a white minority group in South Africa. “We have never really questioned who the federal government was sending. And now we are,” Mitchell Salem said. Other local nonprofits have felt the impacts of federal actions through their effects on the populations that these organizations seek to help. Steve Werlin, the executive director of New Haven’s Downtown Evening Soup Kitchen, wrote in an email to the News that the greatest effects of federal policy “on the food assistance side” have been changes brought on by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. The act, which Trump signed last July, tightened the work requirements around eligibility for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, restricted eligibility for non-citizens, made changes to benefit calculations and shifted payment responsibilities onto states, according to the National Conference of State Legislatures. “Many of the people we serve have lost eligibility completely, or have seen their monthly allocations through SNAP decreased,” Werlin wrote. He also noted blocked funding to the Emergency Food and Shelter Program, which had been “pumping millions of dollars of food assistance funding directly to community providers across the country.” Werlin hypothesized that the “atmosphere of fear” created by immigration crackdowns has “made it more difficult for people to access food.” Kimberly Hart, a member of the board of directors for the Loaves and Fishes Food Pantry, has seen a “drop in migrants coming to the pantry,” she said. Hart said that changes to the SNAP program “disrupt” the lives of “have-nots.” SNAP was “working very well” before the changes, she said. 6,626 New Haveners — about 17.4 percent of the city’s total voters — voted for Trump in 2024, according to the News’ coverage at the time.

title: New Haven fights back against an antagonistic Trump administration
author: Evelyn Ronan
text: Since the beginning of President Donald Trump’s second term, New Haven has been targeted by the federal government as part of its crackdown against progressive and sanctuary cities. The city has responded with a series of coalition lawsuits and amicus briefs opposing the federal government, with local and state officials seeking to distance their political priorities with those of the Trump administration. In March, the federal government sued New Haven back, alleging that the city’s policies impede cooperation between local authorities and federal immigration enforcement. New Haven has joined five lawsuits and 24 amicus briefs since Trump took office, challenging policies related to immigration enforcement, federal funding, municipal authority and cuts to social welfare programs. In January 2025, the federal government released a memo which froze roughly $3 trillion in nonprofit and local government funds that provide the essential short-term cash flow allowing cities like New Haven to maintain regular operations. The motion was rescinded just as Mayor Justin Elicker was condemning the action at a Hartford press conference. A month later, the city signed an amicus brief alleging that the National Institutes of Health’s research funding cuts would destabilize the scientific research ecosystem and pose threats to early-career researchers, lamenting the “slash-and-burn philosophy” of the Trump administration regarding crucial funding, according to previous interviews with the News. Mayor Justin Elicker offered his first major commentary on the federal blows last February at his annual State of the City address, where he briefly alluded to the federal targeting of sanctuary cities like New Haven and disapproved of the Trump administration’s aims to defund diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives. Soon after, New Haven joined several Democrat-led municipalities in a lawsuit arguing that Trump’s crackdown on illegal immigration violated several clauses that protect municipal autonomy and unjustly targeted cities like New Haven for their “sanctuary” status. Last March, when announcing his proposed budget for fiscal year 2026, Elicker emphasized the difference between New Haven’s political posture and that of the Trump administration. Elicker also criticized the unpredictability of the Trump administration’s financial cutbacks, which he said makes it difficult for municipalities to draw up spending plans. He noted that New Haven is exercising “fiscal caution,” and that public entities like New Haven Public Schools rely on funding from the federal government. A month later, New Haven signed on to another lawsuit, which alleged that recent funding freezes to environment-related initiatives — including the termination of $20 billion in grant funding, $30 million of which was designated for New Haven — were illegal. That March, Elicker lamented the federal government “turning its back on cities” despite binding agreements in an interview with the News. Later that year, in October, New Haven joined another lawsuit which challenged the Department of Homeland Security’s updated terms and conditions for grant recipients. New regulations required recipients to agree not to “operate any programs that advance or promote DEI, DEIA, or discriminatory equity ideology .” The lawsuit coincided with last year’s New Haven mayoral race. Facing a Republican challenger , Elicker continued to emphasize New Haven’s “progressive values.” In a debate , he called the city the “most welcoming” in the world, alluding again to its sanctuary status. Allie Jacobs, the city’s primary legal counsel, said that New Haven weighs the benefits of joining the legal fight against the federal government against the fact that doing so could invite more scrutiny from Trump. “We assess whether the city, its interest, or its residents would be significantly adversely impacted by a particular law, policy, or judicial decision,” Jacobs wrote in an email to the News. “Sometimes that takes the form of joining a lawsuit if we have standing, sometimes that takes the form of an amicus brief, and sometimes that takes the form of other advocacy approaches.” When announcing New Haven’s participation in the lawsuit against the DHS, Elicker said that he was not concerned with drawing extra attention to New Haven. “I hear from some of my mayoral colleagues out there that they don’t want to make their cities a target,” Elicker said at a press conference at the time. “Now is a time that we should not be hiding. Now is a time that we should be standing up and fighting for the values of our community, both because at some point we’re all going to be a target anyway, but more importantly, because this is the right thing to do.” Days after New Haven joined the lawsuit against the DHS, Elicker contrasted the city’s balanced budget with the financial gridlock at the federal level that had resulted in a shutdown. “We in New Haven are continuing to do things differently by providing transparent, reliable, and stable budgeting,” he said at a press conference on the city’s fiscal outlook. “It’s what our residents deserve.” Two weeks later, New Haven joined a lawsuit filed by a coalition of municipalities, charities, religious organizations, business and unions against the Trump administration’s decision not to fund Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, or SNAP, benefits in the wake of the government shutdown. In response to the federal government’s cuts to SNAP funding — which Elicker condemned as “cruelty” that “blocked food from the table of 30,000 New Haveners” in a November interview with the News — Connecticut set aside $500 million for an emergency relief fund that subsidized food stamps during the government shutdown. In December 2025, New Haven joined a fifth lawsuit against the federal government, led by the Public Rights Project, over the federal government’s attempt to claw back over $12 billion in local funding designated for transit and public healthcare programs. The federal government declared that states would need to change policies on DEI initiatives, immigration, and abortion to obtain frozen funds. Aside from the slew of lawsuits, New Haven has joined in the national effort against the federal deputization of municipal governments and a national crackdown on immigration. This January, New Haven signed onto an amicus brief backing Minnesota in its lawsuit against the officials who authorized the federal government’s mass deportations. Elicker doubled down on his effort to distance New Haven ideologically from the Trump administration at the swearing-in ceremony for city officials this year, emphasizing New Haven’s values of “tolerance,” “respect for diversity” and “compassion for our most vulnerable.” He said that the Trump administration’s haphazard funding cuts force city officials to remain vigilant. Now, a year and a half since his first major action against the federal government, Elicker has continued to define his own ideological posture, as well as New Haven’s, as antithetical to Trump’s. In his State of the City address this February, the mayor doubled down on his previous criticism of the Trump administration, calling it an “endless dumpster fire of chaos and cruelty.” Following the dispatch of federal agents to carry out Operation Broken Trust, an Immigration and Customs Enforcement roundup that resulted in the arrest of 65 immigrants across Connecticut, the Trump administration last month filed its own lawsuit against New Haven. The Department of Justice complaint argued that New Haven’s policies illegally restrict local authorities’ collaboration with federal immigration enforcement, disregard federal law and threaten public safety. The suit, which also named Elicker, Connecticut Gov. Ned Lamont SOM ’80 and Attorney General William Tong as defendants, criticized New Haven’s enforcement of the Trust Act , which prevents the “deputization of local and state law enforcement for immigration enforcement.” A 2025 “Year in Review” message from Tong criticized the Trump administration’s “cruel, dangerous, and hurtful agenda,” adding that his office would not relent in its protection of Connecticut families. Rep. Rosa DeLauro, who represents New Haven in Congress, blasted Trump for “pursuing politically motivated lawsuits targeting our state for not carrying out ICE’s extreme enforcement agenda.” “President Trump’s actions are hurting working families, making it harder for them to afford basic necessities,” DeLauro said in a statement sent to the News by a spokesperson. “We must fight back, and New Haven and Connecticut are leading the way by challenging the Trump administration’s actions in court. Many of these cases have already been successful and restored funding to our communities.” In a phone interview with the News, Elicker said the city had been “prepared all along for a possible lawsuit” but emphasized New Haven’s compliance with federal law. “We can see that similar Trump lawsuits across the nation have already been thrown out,” the mayor said. “Trump is trying to force us to settle and carry out immigration policy, but that is not our job,” Elicker added. Several of the city’s legal challenges have already produced major rulings. One federal judge blocked the Trump administration from withholding federal funding from sanctuary jurisdictions, siding with New Haven, and another ordered the restoration of roughly $31 million in frozen environmental grants owed to the city, though the Trump administration has appealed portions of that ruling. Some legal disputes remain unresolved, including the litigation over DHS grant conditions and broader fights surrounding immigration enforcement. Elicker seems to have no intentions of backing down. He said that his approach differs from those of his colleagues across Connecticut. “Some have spoken out, but some have remained under the radar, and I don’t think that’s the right thing to do at this moment,” Elicker said. “We have to fight for what we believe in.” The Department of Justice does not currently have a permanent attorney general. Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch contributed reporting.

title: Wooster Square and walkability encourage seniors to stay in New Haven
author: Tajrian Khan
text: As seniors prepare to leave Yale’s campus and enter the workforce, a handful of them are choosing to remain in the Elm City next year. In a survey sent out to the class of 2026 by the News, 13 out of 115 respondents who said they will remain in the United States — or 11.3 percent — said they plan to live in Connecticut. According to data published by Yale’s Office of Career Strategy, around 12 percent of the class of 2025 stayed in Connecticut, compared to an average of 14 percent in the years since 2016. Rory Bricca ’26 says he hopes to stay in New Haven next year to teach music lessons at the Neighborhood Music School, as well as audition for choirs at the Yale Institute of Sacred Music and work on his own compositions. Bricca was born at the Yale New Haven Hospital and lived in Hamden until he was 10 years old, when his family moved to Tucson, Arizona. But he has “fond memories” of New Haven, he said, “especially East Rock and Wooster Square.” About a month ago, Bricca said he had a “gut feeling” that he wanted to stay in New Haven. “I’m hoping to just have a year where I can stay in this area and stay connected to the relationships I’ve built,” he said. Bricca is also excited by New Haven’s performing arts scene. “Nowhere else in the world could I be going to a friend’s musical or theater performance every week,” he said. “That’s just super fun.” Victor Nguyen ’26 plans to stay in New Haven next year to continue his research at a Yale School of Medicine neuroscience lab. “My current lab aligns with my research goals, and I didn’t want to have to start back at square one in a new location,” Nguyen said. At the same time, he thinks New Haven offers a “nice contrast” to his hometown of Fort Smith, Arkansas. When applying to colleges, he said he wanted to move to a city with a “fast living pace” he is excited to spend at least one more year in New Haven after four years at Yale. Jae-Hee Bae ’26 grew up in Dayton, Ohio, and first came to the east coast as a Yale student four years ago. But now that New Haven feels “comfortable and familiar,” she said, she is not ready to leave. “I think there’s a consensus that people want to leave New Haven after graduation,” Bae said. “But when you’re graduating, there’s a lot of change that happens and having some constant really relieves the pressure of all that change.” She loves that New Haven is “more pedestrian-friendly” than suburban Ohio and that residents are engaged in their communities. “I like that there are a lot of protests that happen on the Green, and there are a lot of events like the 5Ks and the Cherry Blossom Festival,” Bae said. “It shows that organizers really care about making it feel like home.” Like Nguyen, Bae is staying next year to work on mRNA research at the Yale School of Medicine’s Neugebauer Lab and apply to medical schools in the meantime. Many Yale students take a “gap year” before applying to medical school, and for those interested, research opportunities at Yale are “straightforward,” Bae said. “I feel comfortable knowing a handful of my friends are staying here,” Bae added. “I’m super excited to stay involved with a community that I’ve developed a relationship with.” Gia Cook ’26 will be working as a Woodbridge Fellow at Yale’s Office of Public Affairs and Communications. “I’m super-duper excited to be in New Haven,” Cook said. Originally from a suburb of Detroit, Cook said she lived in the “same little yellow house in the same street” her entire life until she came to Yale. “It’s been incredible living here, I don’t really have any dislikes,” Cook said, adding that she appreciated being “somewhere walkable” and having “access to such vibrant nightlife.” Yale University has been the largest employer of graduating students every year since 2016, when the Office of Career Strategy started publishing data on postgraduate plans.

title: After increased pledge, seniors split on whether Yale pays city enough
author: Chantal Eulenstein
text: Beginning next fiscal year, Yale will boost its annual payment to New Haven. Graduating seniors differ on whether they think the increased contribution is appropriate. Two months ago, University President Maurie McInnis and New Haven Mayor Justin Elicker announced an updated town-gown agreement that will see the University give its home city more than $230 million over the next seven years. A survey conducted by the News found that a plurality of graduating seniors — just under 50 percent of 147 respondents — feels that Yale’s new voluntary contribution is either “too little” or “somewhat little.” Elicker said in March that the deal “represents the largest voluntary payment” from any American university to a municipality. Yale will pay New Haven $29.9 million in the next fiscal year, and contributions will increase to $33.6 million by 2033. These sums represent a substantial increase from the previous agreement, under which Yale paid some $23 or $24 million annually. The new payment schedule followed months of negotiations between Yale and New Haven, and the agreement was announced as the class of 2026 looked ahead to graduation. Just over 44 percent of senior respondents said that the new commitment is “appropriate,” and nearly 11 percent said it was “too much” or “somewhat much.” “I think it’s important for Yale to give back to the city it is built in,” Estelle Balsirow ’26 wrote in an email to the News, adding that the survey results suggest that “a lot of seniors agree with that sentiment.” Other seniors were taken aback by the proportion of their classmates who felt that Yale’s contribution to New Haven was excessive. “I found it surprising that nearly 11 percent of people thought that Yale was giving too much money to New Haven,” Colin Kim ’26 wrote in a text to the News. “Yale as an academic institution siphons so many resources like land from the surrounding area, so I personally feel like the school should give more to rehabilitate the structural harms that it has caused to the city.” Yale owns over 850 acres in New Haven, according to CT Insider . Thanks to its status as a nonprofit, the University does not pay property taxes on any buildings that are used for academic purposes. That exemption is protected by an 1834 agreement between Yale and Connecticut. Efforts to push Yale to increase its payment ramped up in the months before the new deal was announced. In January, leaders of Yale’s Local 33, 34 and 35 unions joined city leaders and activists to demand that Yale up its annual contribution to $110 million. And in early March, nearly 500 people attended an online “solidarity summit” organized by New Haven Rising in which speakers denounced Yale for not contributing more. The University is New Haven’s second-largest taxpayer. The new town-gown agreement preserves an existing arrangement in which Yale makes payments equivalent to full property taxes for three years after purchasing a property for academic use, before reducing those payments by 10 percent each year. New initiatives announced alongside the updated agreement in March include a fund to support local nonprofits and a commitment to exploring the possibility of using vacant University land to address Connecticut’s affordable housing crisis . Some seniors believe that Yale’s volunteer contribution to New Haven could increase further. “I’m wondering what determines whether Yale is giving ‘too much,’” Kim wrote, “since I personally think that the quality of education that Yale provides would not suffer at all from the school giving more money to New Haven. In the end, isn’t that all that matters for answering the question of whether Yale pays New Haven ‘too much’ or ‘too little’?” For some seniors, attending Yale meant becoming a New Haven resident — which means that their alma mater needs to give back. “I hope that a stronger relationship from this agreement will form for both Yalies who call New Haven a temporary home and residents who will live here with their families for years to come,” Balsirow wrote. In the University’s first voluntary contribution agreement, signed in 1990, Yale pledged to give around $1.5 million to the city each year.

title: Even after monthslong scandal, ex-chief remembered for community ties
author: Evelyn Ronan
text: It hasn’t been a career-high year for Karl Jacobson. In January, Jacobson, then the chief of the New Haven Police Department, abruptly resigned from his post as chief and later turned himself in on an arrest warrant for two charges of larceny. He allegedly embezzled a combined $85,500 from two city funds, one intended for paying confidential informants in the narcotics unit and the other for the city’s Police Activity League. According to an affidavit, Jacobson bet more than $4 million on online gambling platforms during the time he was allegedly embezzling the funds. In April, the chief pleaded not guilty to the two larceny charges against him. But even as his department moves on from him — on May 5, the city swore in David Zannelli, who was among the three assistant chiefs who confronted Jacobson with allegations of embezzlement — New Haveners remembered his tenure as an era of progressive strides on community engagement. “He had the closest relationship to affected communities of any chief I’ve seen,” Paul Bass ’82, the founder of the New Haven Independent and longtime reporter on local policing, said in a phone interview. “I’ve been shocked at how little I’ve seen of that personal scandal reverberating at a larger scale.” Bass elaborated that the grievances about Jacobson allegedly stealing funds were limited to him and did not taint the department at large. In a phone interview with the News, Mayor Justin Elicker said he didn’t doubt that Jacobson “cared about New Haven.” But he struck a colder tone, emphasizing that the transparency-focused approach that Jacobson championed also extended to his removal. “It’s important to understand the fact that holding Jacobson accountable builds more trust in the community,” Elicker said. “When we talk about accountability, this is what we mean.” According to Bass, it wasn’t policy shifts that marked Jacobson’s term, but his overall “de-emphasization of arrest” and a focus on community ties. “A good policy doesn’t work if you don’t have trust of the community, if you haven’t been in people’s kitchens and living rooms during tough times and helped them navigate crisis,” Bass said. Zannelli, an 18-year veteran of the NHPD, has suggested he wants to continue Jacobson’s legacy of strengthening collaboration between the police and local community. Jacobson’s people-first approach took center stage in the wake of Randy Cox’s arrest . New Haven police arrested Cox on June 19, 2022. He was handcuffed and placed in a police car without a seatbelt . The officer driving the car, Oscar Diaz , made a hard stop, which caused Cox to slam into the front of the vehicle. In body cam footage released promptly after the incident, Cox could be heard saying that his neck felt broken. Diaz called for medical assistance but continued driving instead of waiting for paramedics to arrive and attend to Cox, who was then dragged face down into a holding cell, according to the police report . He could not move and repeatedly tried to get the officers to stop moving him. Officers in the footage can be heard saying Cox was “faking it” and doing “extra s—” before they dragged him out of the vehicle. The officers’ “reckless” treatment of Cox, a New Haven Police Department Internal Affairs report found, led to his ultimate paralysis. Cox is now in a long-term care facility . Jacobson moved to release bodycam footage of the incident and address the officers’ violation of conventional policy immediately after the incident. In March 2023, after the release of the Internal Affairs report, Jacobson called for the dismissal of the involved officers, who were fired in August of that year. “I think the community has asked us to be transparent and swift,” Jacobson said while announcing his choice to call for the officers’ dismissal, according to News coverage from the time. “We have put a lot of thought and investigation into this decision. We want to make sure that everything is done right, so that through this hearing, justice can be done.” Bass speculated that if Jacobson had handled the incident differently, “we would have the potential for riots.” “He had such a great connection with the people who were understandably upset,” Bass said. “He and the mayor got so much good information right away, rather than being defensive, which another chief would’ve been.” Michael Lawlor, a former state representative and a professor at the University of New Haven’s Henry C. Lee College of Criminal Justice and Forensic Sciences, said that Jacobson reached out to Cox’s family immediately after the incident. “That instinct to do something very uncomfortable immediately is a significant attribute of Karl,” Lawlor said. Jacobson later instituted a new policy in reaction to the Cox incident dubbed “ medical Miranda ,” where officers transporting prisoners are required to ask if they need medical attention. Jacobson also co-signed a judge’s decision not to expunge the records of the officers involved in Cox’s arrest. Diaz, the officer driving the police car during the arrest, was later reinstated under Jacobson’s leadership. “He was big on procedural justice,” Lawlor said, describing it as a central aspect of Jacobson’s policing philosophy. “If you’re a cop and you’re dealing with a citizen, it’s extremely important to explain what you’re doing. He really instilled among the rank-and-file cops the importance of this.” Lawlor said Jacobson also emphasized policing strategies that acquainted officers with particular neighborhoods — such as walking beats, in which officers patrol neighborhoods on foot rather than in a car. “When I came to New Haven, I became a better cop and a better person because I walked a beat,” Jacobson said in a WNHH Community Radio interview six months into his term as chief. 15 years on, he still maintained community ties from that time, Jacobson said in the interview. Lawlor said he considers Jacobson’s community-focused approach to be the crux of his term as police chief. “If you’re gonna be a police chief anywhere, you have to be sort of a politician,” Lawlor said.“A lot of cops have a tunnel vision approach to their job, but as a chief, you have to deal with the community, you have to explain things, and you have to be comforting. Karl really had that skillset.” Bass, the longtime reporter on local policing, contrasted Jacobson’s controversy to a 2007 NHPD scandal in which a group of top cops stole thousands of dollars of reserved police department funds , prompting the departure of then-chief Francisco Ortiz. The NHPD appointed James Lewis soon after in 2008, a chief who had never before worked in New Haven and took an “aggressive” approach to policing reminiscent of NHPD’s militaristic crackdown in the 1980s. Lewis was responsible for 20 arrests in his first week as chief. Bass said the fact that the city hired Zannelli “from within,” instead of hiring an outsider, “means this is so different from 2008,” adding that Zannelli maintained his predecessor’s community-focused approach to policing. “Zannelli has really good relationships with people from working in different neighborhoods,” Bass said. “He’s built up an extraordinary level of goodwill and trust in the community.” Karl Jacobson was sworn in as police chief on July 7, 2022.

title: Yale reinstates SAT, ACT requirement after six years of flexible policy
author: Leo Nyberg
text: First-year and transfer applicants to Yale College will be required to submit either their SAT or ACT scores beginning this fall. The change, which Yale announced in a Wednesday evening press release after updating its webpage on standardized testing, reinstates a pre-pandemic mandate after six admissions cycles in which SAT and ACT score submissions were not required. Yale axed the requirement for undergraduate applicants to submit standardized test scores in 2020, then required the submission of either SAT, ACT, International Baccalaureate or Advanced Placement scores under a test-flexible policy beginning in 2024. “Academic excellence is the foundation of the Yale College experience, and, likewise, is the core component of our admissions process,” Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis said in the press release. “SAT and ACT scores are strong predictors of a student’s future Yale academic performance, and, when considered thoughtfully as part of a whole person review, they can help identify well-prepared candidates, especially those from socio-economically disadvantaged backgrounds.” The return to a test-mandatory policy follows a recommendation from the Presidential Council on Yale College Admissions, a nine-member group formed by University President Maurie McInnis in fall 2025 whose membership includes Lewis, other Yale administrators and former New York governor George Pataki ’67. The council’s work follows previous efforts by Yale College to review its admissions processes, including after the Supreme Court ruled in 2023 that affirmative action is unlawful. The group also “considered recent executive orders and guidelines from federal agencies” in its work, according to a Yale webpage . Brown, Cornell, the University of Pennsylvania, Dartmouth and Harvard all require SAT or ACT scores for undergraduate applicants. Princeton will require that applicants report scores from one of the two tests beginning with those seeking enrollment in fall 2028, while Columbia does not require standardized test score submission. According to the undergraduate admissions website , 90 percent of Yale first years who enrolled last fall submitted either SAT or ACT scores.
