Around two-thirds of those students submitted an AP or IB score, and 61 percent submitted scores from multiple exams. “Academic strength is the admissions committee’s first and most important consideration when evaluating candidates,” Jeremiah Quinlan ’03, the dean of undergraduate admissions, said in the press release . “This central pillar of our work has remained constant, even as our policy on tests has changed in recent years.” Quinlan, through a spokesperson, declined to comment beyond his quotes in the release. The council’s review of admissions policies and procedures concluded at the end of the 2025-26 academic year. According to its website , the group issued guidance “to prevent misrepresentation on applications and increase transparency across the admissions process—a focus shared by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education.” The decision to reinstate mandatory SAT or ACT score submission also followed the Committee on Trust in Higher Education’s recommendation in its April report that the University clarify admissions standards. The report floated the requirement of minimum SAT scores for admission or a Yale-specific entrance exam. The Trust Committee identified hyper-selective admissions as one of three “immediate” factors causing declining public trust in Yale and higher education. “When selective admissions seem so inexplicable — or, worse, tilted in ways that benefit the already advantaged — it should come as no surprise that many Americans do not trust the process,” the committee’s report reads. “We recommend that the university embrace a standard of candor: It should only use criteria for admission that it is willing to describe publicly and defend openly.” Following the report’s release, University President Maurie McInnis wrote in a message to the Yale community that she had accepted many of the committee’s findings and referred its recommendations on admissions to the Presidential Council on Yale College Admissions. In a May interview with the News, McInnis said she did not give the council a deadline for when the group should make recommendations. Yale College accepted 4.2 percent of applicants in its most recent admissions cycle.

title: Introducing the Yale Daily News mobile app
author: The Yale Daily News
text: The Yale Daily News is pleased to announce that our new mobile app is now available to download on the iOS App Store. On the app, you can read our latest news articles and opinion columns, listen to our podcasts, play our games and browse our archives. Readers can enjoy our coverage in dark mode, view their reading history and save articles to read later. App users will also be the first to learn about breaking news and top stories, through push notifications. The app was developed by the News’ technology directors Midhun Sadanand ’27 and Raymond Hou ’27, with Seth Goldin ’26 and Yassin Mudawi ’28, in the “Advanced Software Engineering” course during the spring 2026 semester. Sadanand, Hou and Goldin previously led the redesign of the News’ website , which launched in January. With gratitude for their effort, we’re excited to invite you to read and listen to our coverage on the app. You can download it here.

title: Dramat makes ‘fetch’ happen in riotous rendition of ‘Mean Girls’
author: Cameron Nye
text: Ah, Commencement! The time when graduating Yalies gaze upon the sea of white folding chairs on Old Campus and look back on four prosperous academic years. Friends and family fly in from across the globe to celebrate the students who are ready to break out of their blue and white chrysalides and spread their beautiful, corporate wings. It’s a time of rejoicing and reflection for most, but for the Yale University Dramatic Association, Commencement means one thing and one thing only — a fast, frantic and full-throttle musical production. In the downtime between finals and Commencement weekend, an ambitious group of thespians work for ten days to produce a show in celebration of the year’s theater season and the seniors that will go on to bigger, brighter stages. This year, director Will Sussbauer ’27, producer Jack Dozier ’27, music producer Lyndon Chen ’28 and stage manager Rena Kawasaki ’27 teamed up to present a musical based on one of this generation’s cultural pillars — “Mean Girls.” If you haven’t seen Tina Fey’s original 2004 satiric film, close out of this tab and go watch it. It’s 97 minutes of unadulterated Y2K drama and witty dialogue that remains quotable and iconic even 22 years later. After receiving critical acclaim, “Mean Girls” was turned into a musical, hitting Broadway in 2017 and bringing Fey’s beloved characters into the age of social media. The musical includes all the quips and digs that made the original iconic, and stays true to the film’s plot: 16-year-old Cady Heron moves from Kenya to the United States and, after years of being homeschooled, realizes the lions of her youth did not prepare her for the beastly monsters of high school. She’s initially befriended by two social outcasts but is quickly noticed by the Plastics, a gaggle of popular girls who are North Shore High’s apex predators. Cady is thrown into the maws of popularity, and quickly turns from naive newcomer to manipulative mean girl. The musical gained mostly positive reviews and then gained a frighteningly dull film adaptation in 2024. The production, which I saw Friday night, opened to a friendly audience of supportive suitemates, weepy parents and reminiscent peers. The next day, Dozier informed me via text message that the production had become the “highest grossing show in recent history” staged by the Dramat. A very fetch development. Kudos to them! Staying true to the 2017 production, the University Theatre has been transformed into a minimalistic high school — desks come and go as classes change and cafeteria tables are wheeled off once the lunch bell has rung. The back of the stage remains mostly illuminated a fittingly vibrant shade of pink, backlighting slits of drywall decoupaged in a postered collage of song lyrics, inspirational quotes and Uno reverse cards. The rendition incorporated some changes to the show’s book. Lyrics that contained homophobic slurs and problematic language were tweaked, creating a more inclusive environment without taking away from the show’s intended cruelty. Sussbauer did incorporate some more campy choices that leaned into the melodramatic undertones of the comedic musical. Damian Leigh, the show’s resident flamboyant homosexual, cheekily directs his “sexually active band geeks” line to the pit orchestra, most of whose members give toothy grins and wave. Meanwhile, during Regina George’s fiery revenge tune “World Burn,” her notorious hot pink burn book — which contains The Plastics’ nasty notes about their peers — is lowered down like a clunky weapon of mass destruction. Papers then begin cascading down from the rafters onto the stage, picked up by an anxious and enraged ensemble who has learned that their business has been aired to the entire school. It’s choices like this that make “Mean Girls” a gleeful riot, leaning into the absurdity of stringent social hierarchies in which gossip literally rains from the sky while remaining grounded in the lockered halls of a midwestern high school. That balance is further maintained by the performances of the shows’ cast. The Plastics are brought to life by Hadley Murphey ’29 as the tyrannical Regina George, Sasha Fedderly ’27 as the anxious, antsy Gretchen Weiners and Nneka Moweta ’27 as the delightfully dimwitted Karen Smith. It’s clear Murphey studied at the “Reneé Rapp School of Regina George Portrayals,” because her mix of conceited “cursive” singing — heard in “Geeoorggggeeeeehhhh” — and piercing vibrato is a ferocious ode to Rapp’s performance both in the 2024 movie and on Broadway. Meanwhile, Moweta’s impeccable comedic timing elevates Karen from just another “dumb blonde” to a girl who understands the unspoken workings of high school social hierarchies. The social rejects-turned-narrators — comprised of the jaded Janis Sarkisian, played by Olivia Ridley ’26, and the showy “too gay to function” Damian brought by Sebastian Gervase ’27 — drive the show with grungey “F YOU” anthems and electric numbers reminiscent of classic Broadway showtunes. Gervase’s numbers, in particular, feature showstopping choreography — fluttering jazz hands and showy kicks that infuse an intoxicating intricacy to the production, all courtesy of choreographer Sakura Grashow ’28. Caught in the middle of these two worlds is Cady Heron, brought to life by Amelia Gallegos ’29. She captures Cady excellently, portraying the bright eyed, naive math nerd with remarkable earnestness before embodying her character’s curdle into a conniving, calculated bully. Her bright soprano lends itself well to festive, ingenuous numbers like “It Roars” and “Stupid With Love,” in which her developing crush on hunk Aaron Samuels, played by Abram Knott ’27, fills her with “calcu-lust.” With only ten days to become a fully fledged musical, “Mean Girls” accomplished a great deal. Many of the characters were fully realized with tics and quirks that captured the itchy awkwardness of teenage angst. The production, however, was hampered by its technical inconsistency. Even from a seat relatively close to the stage, lines and runs were lost as microphones crunched and sputtered. The lighting wavered, too, with spotlights often scrambling to catch up to actors already speaking in shadow. With more time, undoubtedly, these wrinkles could be ironed out, but for the rehearsal period it was afforded, “Mean Girls” is practically a triumph. What this cast and crew was able to pull off in a short amount of time celebrates the commitment thespians give to their craft — which in turn honors the seniors who have given their all to the University Theatre they’ve called home for the past four years. “Mean Girls” makes sense as a Commencement musical because its story has already cemented itself in the cultural imagination as funny and sharply quotable. But beneath the pink plastic surface is a tale about insecurity and the exhausting performance of trying to be liked. Those questions do not disappear after high school — or even college. We still navigate cliques, self-comparison, friendship fallout and the pressure to package ourselves into something more impressive than we feel. At a moment meant to celebrate moving forward, “Mean Girls” offers a reminder that growing up is about learning how to be kinder and more honest — a fitting sendoff for Yale’s seniors before they contemplate acquiescing to the Regina Georges of the world. “Mean Girls” will run until May 17.

title: Seniors reflect on their experience within Yale’s music scene
author: Gemard Guery
text: Every fall, many first years at Yale weigh how they’ll take part in one of the University’s largest selling points, the music scene, through everything from a capella rush to Yale Symphony Orchestra auditions. Now on their way out, four seniors spoke to the News about their participation in Yale’s music scene, its impact on their Yale experience and what’s next for them. The singer-songwriter Zaida Rio Polanco ’26 has been passionate about music for as long as she can remember. An environmental studies major from White Plains, New York, Polanco grew up with a dad and brother who were both singer-songwriters, contributing to Polanco’s decision to do the same. Polanco grew up watching her dad play for weddings and her brother, who is 11 years older than her, play drums for her dad’s band. “There was always music playing in the house, and they let me find my own genre that I liked,” Polanco said in an interview with the News. Polanco was writing songs when she was as young as three years old. Her dad ran the songwriters club at her high school, and Polanco would perform at open mics as early as third grade alongside high school aged students. By the time she entered Yale, Polanco had already released an album with 10 songs. Polanco was drawn to Yale’s theater and acting scene when she first arrived at Yale but knew she wanted to continue singing and songwriting. “I just knew I wanted to keep going, no matter where I ended up,” said Polanco. “I just got lucky I was able to go to a school that had a student record label that had a bunch of people willing to help bring this music to life.” Polanco was a member of the Shades of Yale for her first three years at Yale and a frequent participant in theater on campus . She was also an active part of 17o1 Records, the student record label that helped Polanco release many of her own projects. For a while, Polanco was reluctant to pursue a career as an artist, but her time at Yale showed her that it is possible. “I’ve met people that turned this pipe dream of going into this career as an artist into something that could actually happen,” Polanco said. “I love the fact that I can hear my best friends performing at Chi Psi from my bedroom window. There are not many places you can do that, be able to have that experience where you’re literally living in a small little bubble of performing artists. I’ll miss that a lot.” While she enjoyed her experience as an environmental studies major, it is not what she hopes to pursue professionally. Following commencement, Zaida plans to pursue music in Los Angeles while working in marketing with a company that has a creative entry point. She also hopes to continue auditioning for theater and film. The bassist For Thara Joseph ’26, music has always been an important part of her identity. A bassist from Jacksonville, Florida, Joseph began playing the piano regularly in elementary school. “Every day, I knew I had at last ninety minutes of playing per day,” said Joseph in an interview. “That really started in sixth grade, and it stayed consistently until I graduated, so it was very intensive in that way.” Courtesy of Thara Joseph Joseph attended a magnet arts program for both middle school and high school, where her passion for music intensified. She was a part of her city symphony orchestra and youth symphony orchestra, where she played the French horn, and in her senior year of high school, she was accepted into a jazz combo through the Jazz Education Network, which connects young musicians with professionals in the field. But as Joseph was preparing to come to Yale, she anticipated putting music on the back burner, focusing on academics instead and seeing where Yale would take her. “I didn’t know if I was going to be able to play and practice at the same frequencies that I had been for many, many years,” Joseph said. But when Joseph found herself unable to practice playing music every day during the First-Year Scholars program, she said she realized how little she enjoyed that experience. In fact, during the program, Joseph went to a local pawn shop and bought a bass bundled with an amp so she could practice. “Looking back, even that moment should have clued me into how important music was as a core part of my daily routine and how hard it would have been to just drop it,” Joseph said. Joseph has been a bassist for the Yale Jazz Ensemble during the entirety of her undergraduate experience, playing in musical productions like “The Wiz” and “For Colored Girls.” She has regularly gigged professionally with graduate students at the Yale School of Music, and has performed for events held through Yale’s Schwarzman Center and opened for celebrities such as Cécile McLorin Salvant and John Legend. Originally, Joseph came to Yale planning to double-major in both global affairs and music. Instead, she tested out classes across many different fields and departments until she landed on Ethics, Politics and Economics. She said her interdisciplinary major, alongside her music experience, opened her eyes to the nature of Yale’s liberal arts education. “I realized I don’t have to choose one path here. I feel like I’m the type of person where I want to have my hands in many different pockets,” Joseph said. “Because of all that time I was able to still invest in my craft, it really had me making that shift into considering pursuing music in a more serious and intentional way.” Joseph says she will miss Yale’s vibrant community the most from her music experience as she leaves Yale. “You get to meet so many different people, and a lot of people, I don’t think I would have ever crossed paths otherwise,” said Joseph. “There’s such an emphasis on collaboration here when it comes to the arts, where it’s music that feels good, it sounds good, and it’s enjoyable. I’ll miss that a lot.” Joseph hopes to someday work in the more executive side of the music industry. “There’s a need for more artists as industry leaders, so that we can increase artist advocacy and bridge what can sometimes be misunderstandings between creatives and executives,” Joseph said. Following graduation, Joseph will be staying in New Haven as a Chauncey Fellow, where she will be developing a service-based arts project. She plans to continue gigging throughout her time as a fellow. The Whiffenpoof For John Colbert ’26, the Yale journey was unlike what he originally anticipated. A humanities major, he originally matriculated in fall 2020, as a member of the class of 2024, but took a gap year due to the pandemic to get more out of his Yale experience. Colbert played the drums often in his hometown of Montclair, New Jersey, gigging with jazz bands through his time in middle school and high school. He also participated in an after school and summer program where local musicians help youth create their own ensembles. Colbert’s older sister attended Yale and recommended that he try out for Yale’s Tangled Up in Blue — or TUIB — a folk music group. While Colbert’s sister was not a part of the group, he said she always enjoyed watching them perform. “It was a big event for her and her friends to go to every year. She was like, ‘You should do this.’ Colbert said in an interview with the News. “So then I auditioned, and that’s been a big part of my time at Yale. That’s really what made me take singing more seriously as something that I did here.” Colbert eventually served as the musical director of TUIB during his junior year, in addition to participating in bands and theater on campus. He also became a member of the Whiffenpoof class of 2025, where Yale seniors take gap years to perform worldwide, something he said he had no intention of being a part of originally. It wasn’t until a friend from TUIB encouraged Colbert to audition after sharing her own experience that Colbert considered becoming a Whiffenpoof. “The idea of being a professional amateur musician or an amateur professional was kind of appealing to me,” Colbert said. “I spent so much time doing music stuff here that I was like, I really wouldn’t mind doing essentially just that for a whole year.” Colbert enjoyed his time as a Whiffenpoof, which allowed him not only to have a fun experience but also gave him the opportunity to reflect on what he wanted his life after Yale to look like. For Colbert, music was also a means of finding his community at Yale. “Being involved in music here was a wonderful way to be engaged with campus life,” he said. “I think playing music together is a really interesting and unique way to form bonds with people and it means a lot to me.” As Colbert prepares to leave Yale, he said he already feels nostalgic about his time in the music scene and how it became a serious endeavor. “In some ways, the things that I’ll miss about playing music here are the things that I’ll miss about not being as young as I am now,” he said. “I’ll miss the strange, infinite possibility of college that is so, so alluring when you’re here.” Following commencement, Colbert hopes to move to New York and continue playing with his band, Dog Boy, which he began during his time at Yale. There, he hopes to engage in the city’s art scene while he continues searching for a job separate from his creative endeavors that he finds fulfilling on a personal level. The concert pianist Paul Ji ’26 has been a well known and distinguished musician for years. A philosophy and music double major from France, Ji has been playing the piano since he was 4 years old. Ji participated in many international competitions when he was younger, winning almost all of them, which further fueled his passion for music, specifically the piano. “I did the Steinway competition for seven years, consecutively, and fortunately, I won all of them and that motivated me to continue,” he said in an interview. “But at one point, I decided that music was much more than competitions, and there was so much joy to be found in connecting with people through music.” Courtesy of Paul Ji When Ji was 15, he recorded his first album after winning a national competition in France. Ji’s second album, the one he recorded while at Yale, will be coming out in early 2027. Much of Ji’s Yale experience was finding the perfect balance between his professional piano career and his academic interests. “I very much appreciate the liberal arts, and I really wanted to delve deep into philosophy,” he said. “I also felt that it would help my music, that it would give me more insights and more intellectual experience.” The majority of his sophomore year was devoted to learning the work of Polish composer Frédéric Chopin. Ji’s next album, recorded in Sprague Hall, consists of the full 24 preludes by Chopin, accompanied by other pieces. This year, Ji had the opportunity to perform with the Yale Symphony Orchestra as the winner of the William Waite competition , where he played Chopin’s second concerto, a romantic piece he performed on Valentine’s Day. Ji’s talents have brought him all over the world throughout his time at Yale. During his sophomore year, he performed his debut recital in Paris at the Théâtre des Champs-Élysées. Since then, he’s played in Vienna in spaces where famous musicians such as Beethoven have performed. While Ji wasn’t a consistent part of any music group, his experience continuously demonstrated to him how strong the musical community at Yale is. He often participated in graduate piano classes in Sprague Hall and would play alongside experts in the field. “Meeting musicians who are also very intellectually driven and invested in many, many different fields has been a gift,” Ji said. Ji hopes to continue to build his career as a concert pianist while simultaneously finding solutions and strategies to make classical music more accessible. “It’s really such a great art that can help people transcend, and I think more people deserve to know it,” he said. Following graduation, Ji will be pursuing his master’s of music in piano at the Juilliard School.

title: Under Bundy, drama school went tuition-free and saw a cultural shift
author: Kiva Bank
text: The office of James Bundy DRA ’95, who is retiring from his posts at the David Geffen School of Drama and the Yale Repertory Theatre, is set to be cleared out by the end of June. But for now, the room’s blue walls are still covered with framed posters from student thesis productions Bundy has seen throughout his 24-year-long tenure — the longest of any Drama School dean to date. “The theater is filled with mementos,” Bundy, who has served in his role as dean and as the Yale Rep’s artistic director since 2002, said in an interview with the News. His office is laden with keepsakes, each object hinting at a piece of his life and work at Yale. Beneath his coffee table sits a “Shakespeare Lexicon and Quotation Dictionary,” a nod to the “Acting Shakespeare” undergraduate course he will continue to teach after stepping down this summer. Nearby is a gold-painted leg signed by an Estonian clown who performed at the Yale Rep this past season, and on Bundy’s desk sits a Funko Pop gifted to him by Nancy Yao, the assistant dean of student life at the drama school. The figurine is styled in his likeness — bald, wearing clear-framed glasses — and the diploma it holds echoes Bundy himself, who earned his MFA in directing from the School of Drama in 1995. “The school completely changed the direction of my career, and opened up a world of opportunities,” he said. Prior to his appointment to the Drama School, Bundy served as the artistic director of Great Lakes Theater Festival, as well as the associate producing director of the Acting Company in New York City and as managing director of Cornerstone Theater Company. Posters for two different student productions of “Hedda Gabler” — the last play that Bundy himself directed at the Yale Rep while serving as its artistic director — hang in his office. During his tenure at the Rep, he also directed productions of Shakespeare’s “Hamlet” and “All’s Well That Ends Well.” Liz Diamond, the chair of the directing program at the David Geffen School of Drama, told the News she is “a little bit in denial” about the change in leadership at the school. She said was glad to see Bundy “pour his heart and soul” into the Yale Rep’s production of “Hedda Gabler.” “I was really moved by the work,” she said in a phone interview with the News. “I’m sure that was partly because I knew it was his last production as dean and artistic director.” Diamond said she first met Bundy in fall 1992, when he was one of four students in her directing seminar. She described him as “surpassingly conscientious,” an “indefatigable researcher” and a “wonderful thinker.” Catherine Sheehy DRA ’92 ’99, who is currently the chair of dramaturgy and dramatic criticism at the Drama School and a dramaturg at the Yale Rep, was a first-year faculty member when Bundy was in his final year as a student. She said she can still recall his 1995 thesis production of “Three Sisters.” “He created these images that were really strong,” Sheehy said. “I can still see Protopopov in his white suit, sitting and smoking.” She said that Bundy’s training as an actor enabled him to understand players better as a director, and that in her mind, “he was always sort of an old soul.” Sheehy recalled that Bundy balanced his studies at the drama school with his responsibilities as a husband and a young father. “I remember him wheeling around his kids at the time,” she said. Bundy said that his time as a student at the Drama School aided him when he took his place at its helm, helping inform his understanding of the school’s potential. When he stepped into the deanship, Bundy said that both the search committee that had appointed him and then-University president Richard Levin ’72 ’74 were clear in communicating their priorities for the school. He said there was a general agreement to rank financial aid as the top priority, followed by faculty compensation and new facilities. “24 years later, we still haven’t broken ground on that building, but we're close,” Bundy joked, referring to the Dramatic Arts building set to be completed in 2029. The seven-floor structure will house the Yale Repertory Theater, the David Geffen School of Drama and the undergraduate Theater, Dance and Performance Studies department in one space. “James willed this building into being,” Sheehy said. “James has had to have extraordinary persistence. The administration has changed. The president has changed, the provost has changed more than once.” At the same time, Bundy has been driven by his desire to decrease student indebtedness, which he said was a major problem when he first began his tenure. According to Bundy, debt for School of Drama students in 1982 averaged $10,000, or roughly $34,000 in today’s dollars, and the average debt for the class of 2005 was around $45,000, or $75,000 today. The average student today graduates from the school with about $16,000 in debt — around a 50 percent net reduction from 1982 and an 80 percent net reduction from 2005. Sheehy said that when she graduated from the drama school in 1992, her student loan payment was $575 per month, which she characterized as “enormous.” She said that the financial aid program did not offer much grant aid at the time, but that when Bundy arrived as dean, he immediately started working to increase financial aid. Diamond said that the first full faculty meeting under Bundy’s leadership marked a change from the previous dean’s style. “We really didn’t have very lively faculty meetings under the previous dean,” Diamond said, but under Bundy, “we began with a physical warm-up led by the acting teacher who taught movement.” “For the first time in history, faculty and staff of the drama school were jumping up and down together and sweating,” Diamond said. She said that when Bundy led a discussion to determine the school’s priorities during the first five years of his tenure, the desire to increase financial aid for students was unanimous amongst faculty. “He pursued that goal assiduously, and it took the better part of nearly 20 years,” Diamond said. She described Bundy’s skills as those of “remarkable systems analyst,” which enabled him to establish a plan to go tuition-free. In 2021, nearly twenty years into Bundy’s deanship, entertainment mogul David Geffen donated $150 million to the Drama School. The gift allowed the school to become tuition-free for all full-time students in perpetuity, and the school was renamed in Geffen’s honor. Before the donation, Bundy oversaw a need-based student aid program, according to Sheehy, and also played a key role in establishing 70 of the school’s 91 named scholarships. In 2008, Bundy helped facilitate funding for living stipends and summer support for playwriting students through a bequest from playwright Eugene O’Neill and Carlotta Monterey, which he said made Yale’s playwriting program competitive with other fully-funded MFA programs. As a result, all students in the playwriting program — of which there are three per year — receive stipends to cover their living expenses during both the academic year and summer months, in addition to full-tuition scholarships. Chantal Rodriguez, associate dean of the Drama School, said in a phone interview with the News that Bundy’s dedication “to decreasing the overall indebtedness of alumni” has formed part of his legacy. During the first year of the pandemic, the Drama School saved roughly $3 million in production costs because it did not put on any plays. The school then used the excess funds toward tuition and living stipends to offer a fourth year for students in the classes of 2021, 2022 and 2023 free of charge, Bundy said. Diamond also praised Bundy for his dedication to establishing a shared leadership model among the associate and assistant deans, which promotes collective decision-making. “You cannot make theater if you are a person who insists on flying solo,” Diamond said. “It’s a form of practicing for civilization, and I think that the fact that James, in his own practice as a leader of our school and theater, strove to lead collaboratively, was remarkable.” Bundy also brought about a cultural shift at the school, according to Sheehy. For years, the acting program followed a longstanding tradition of admitting 10 men and six women each year, based on the belief that classical repertory, particularly Shakespeare, required more male actors, Sheehy said. But students argued that the policy was both discriminatory and no longer beneficial to their training, and Bundy removed the quota. Evan Yionoulis ’82 DRA ’85 was recently announced as Bundy’s successor. The outgoing dean characterized Yionoulis as his former mentor, as he worked as her teaching assistant during the summers of 1993 and 1995 when she taught acting for a Yale program. Yionoulis, who previously served as the chair of acting at the drama school, also served on the search committee when Bundy was hired, he said. “She asked me, to the effect of, ‘How would you want people to describe your leadership style?’” he said, recalling the time when Yionoulis was involved in his selection. “I said that I would want to lead with my heart.” Diamond said Bundy is known to read the application of every single incoming student as well as memorize their names and proper pronunciation before orientation. As of last fall, the school enrolled 240 students, according to the Office of Institutional Research. “That is just an example of his deep commitment to this community, and it makes everybody step up,” Diamond said. Bundy also hosts an annual barbecue in the backyard of his house in Westville, a tradition he said started when he moved into the home with his family around the time his deanship began. He said he recalled thinking, “Oh, the whole school could fit in this backyard.” “It’s a nice way for people to be together at the outset,” he said. “We’ve had 10,000 guests at our house over the course of the decades, and that’s felt like a really meaningful experience.” In April, Diamond said, the school was able to return the favor and host a farewell party in celebration of Bundy with all students, faculty and staff. “There were a gazillion balloons that were shaped like hearts, which has extra meaning for a dean who had at least two open heart surgeries during his tenure,” she said. Sheehy said that Bundy’s “open-heartedness” and “open door” policy as a dean will be hardest to replace. “I always think that it’s a sign of good character if you’re willing to be a fool for love,” Sheehy said. “James is willing to be a fool for his love of this institution, and he’s willing to be humble in that way and enjoy a joke and see the funny side.” Bundy graduated from Harvard College in 1981.

title: Bulldogs return to NCAA tournament after second-place Ivy finish
author: Eli Ratner
text: Yale women’s crew will return to the national stage after showing strength at last weekend’s Ivy League championship and earning an at-large bid at Tuesday’s NCAA selection show. No. 7 nationally-ranked Yale will make its fifth straight appearance at the tournament with its fifth-seeded first varsity eight, sixth-seeded second varsity eight and seventh-seeded first varsity four crews. With a national title on the line in Gainesville, Georgia, 22 teams will take the water. The Bulldogs’ first varsity eight won the national title last year, and the team narrowly finished second to Stanford. This year, Yale’s first varsity eight boat, the defending national champion, will compete in Heat 4, facing off against the University of Massachusetts, Rutgers University, Princeton, the University of Miami and the University of Rhode Island. The second varsity eight boat will take on Boston University, the University of Miami, the University of Virginia, Oregon State University and Jacksonville University in Heat 3, and in the first varsity four, Yale will compete in Heat 2 against the University of Texas, the University of California, Berkeley, Boston University and the University Michigan. Tuesday’s at-large bid follows the Bulldogs’ strong showing at the Ivy League Championship last weekend. All Yale crews medaled, with the first varsity four and second varsity four taking home gold, the first varsity eight, second varsity eight and third varsity eight winning silver and the third varsity four making a bronze finish. With 82 points, Yale won the team points title for the second consecutive year, narrowly surpassing Princeton by two points. The Tigers, who won the conference championship, earned the Ivy League’s automatic bid. In addition to Yale, two other Ivies, Brown and Columbia, also earned at-large berths. Now, the Bulldog crews look to avenge the narrow loss to the Tigers at the conference championship and return to New Haven with new hardware, cementing their position as one of the nation’s top programs. The Elis will hit the water on Friday, May 29.

title: Yale heads to regionals after beating Brown, capturing Ivy Pennant
author: Justin Leahy
text: The Yale Bulldogs advanced to the NCAA regionals after defeating the Brown Bears 7-5 on Sunday afternoon in the final round of the Ivy League Tournament. With the win, Yale concluded a 3-0 sweep of the double-elimination tournament at George H.W. Bush ’48 Field. This year’s rise to regionals marks the Bulldogs’ first since 2017 and seventh in program history. The Bulldogs entered the tournament having won a dramatic second consecutive Ivy regular season title. Entering their last regular season series of the year against Dartmouth earlier this month, they needed a three-game sweep to take the title. They did just that, winning the regular-season Ivy title and earning the right to host the Ivy League Tournament. To reach the tournament championship game on their home turf, Yale knocked off last year’s champions, Columbia, 12-3 on Friday and this year’s runner-up, Brown, 5-2 on Saturday, before defeating Brown again on Sunday afternoon. “All the things this team has accomplished and worked through this year have all led to this,” captain Davis Hanson ’26 wrote in a text message to the News. “It’s even more special that we all get to go compete together for a championship.” Hanson emphasized the Bulldogs’ strong mindset heading into the tournament after Yale had dropped two of three to Brown and lost or tied every game versus Columbia in the regular season. “With Brown and Columbia, we really adapted the mentality that we owe those teams our best performance,” Hanson wrote in a text message to the News. “Those are two really good teams this year, and they got the better of us in the regular season, but everyone on our team wanted to play them again in the playoffs. We wanted to show them what we truly are.” The lean, light-throwing righty Daniel Cohen ’26 started for the Bulldogs on Sunday. Although Cohen had been dominant all season, compiling a sub-5 ERA, he struggled in his first two innings, giving up three runs and failing to record a sixth out. Though Yale was down 0-3 heading into the bottom of the second, Yale pieced together a rally. Garrett Larsen ’27, Kaiden Dossa ’27 and Chris DiPrima ’27 provided clutch back-to-back-to-back hits, eventually leveling the score at 3-3. After Cohen was pulled in the second inning, Teo Spadaccini ’27 came into the game. Spadaccini served up nearly six innings of one-run control, setting a near-impeccable tone for the afternoon. The Bulldogs went on to score four more unanswered runs, backed up by clutch doubles from Hanson, Dossa and Jack Dauer ’28. When Spadaccini faced a little trouble at the end of his performance, Ben Winslow ’27, a reliever who has been dominant all year, came in to mop up, instead walking two straight batters. Jack Ohman ’28, last season’s Ivy League Pitcher of the Year who had started on Friday, then came on in relief. He quickly forced three outs, walking only one batter, limiting the bases-loaded, no out damage to just two runs and helping Yale keep their lead. The next inning, Ohman closed out the game in dominant fashion, securing the Bulldogs the game and the Ivy League title. Winning the Ivy League meant especially more for Hanson, who tore his ACL in practice before the start of last season. “To me, personally, this game means everything,” Hanson wrote in a text message to the News before the championship contest. In Friday’s first game of the tournament, Yale faced No. 4 ranked Columbia. During the regular season, Yale struggled versus Columbia, going 0-2-1 in New York. However, Yale stood firm. Ohman tossed five innings of two-run ball on the mound. Ohman also stranded six runners and struck out five, often hitting speeds of 96 and 97 miles per hour, with many MLB scouts watching in the stands. After Ohman’s two runs, the Bulldog offense put up a concerted effort to back up their ace. With two outs, DiPrima hit a bases-clearing double, pushing the Bulldogs ahead 6-2. Spadaccini stood sharp on the mound in relief, retiring all nine batters he faced to carry Yale through the eighth inning. Yale then added four more runs in the bottom of the eighth to close out the 12-3 win. Dossa, an ALl-Ivy honoree, reached base four times, stole two bags and extended his on-base streak to 35 games. Dossa kept his perfect 28-for-28 stolen base record for the year. Another All-Ivy honoree, Larsen, who led the Ivy League in batting average this year (.389) added an RBI double in the eighth and also scored three times. In game two on Saturday, Tate Evans ’26 — who was named as the new Ivy League Pitcher of the Year on Thursday, succeeding Ohman — threw seven innings of one-run ball against the third-ranked Brown Bears. Evans tossed a career-high 123 pitches and struck out five Bears. During the regular season, Yale had lost two of three against Brown. But again, Yale was able to overcome the odds. The game was tied through the first five innings. However, the offense proved strong yet again. In the sixth inning, Larsen legged out an infield single, swiped second, then moved to third on a sacrifice bunt by DiPrima. Dauer then lined a double down the line, driving in Larsen and giving Yale the lead. To end the game, the workhorse reliever Winslow closed out the final two innings, securing the 5-2 win. The victory marked Evans’ seventh win of the season, placing him on top of the conference. Pablo Rodriguez ’27 summed up the Bulldogs’ no-quit mentality in a text message to the News. “Every pitch, the ball might not always fall our way, but when we stick with it and grind out every at-bat, eventually we’re going to get the results we want,” Rodriguez wrote. Yale will learn its NCAA Tournament regional assignment on May 25 after the bracket is revealed on ESPN2.

title: Seniors led first playoff run in history, four victories over Harvard
author: Brody Gilkison
text: The bar for making a splash in Yale’s football history is high. The Bulldogs have 18 college football national championships, the most in the history of the sport. Walter Camp, class of 1880, known colloquially as the father of football , headlines a deep list of accomplished Yale alumni in the sport’s chronology. But the seniors still managed to leave their mark on the program throughout a career that ended with Yale’s first playoff run in history. After joining a team that had gone 5–5 in its previous season and ended its year with a heartbreaking loss to Harvard, the now-seniors saw a stark contrast during their rookie season. Although Team 149 dropped its first game of the season to Holy Cross, it went on to win eight of its final nine games, including a 19-14 victory over Harvard, and became the Ivy League champions. Among the only members of the class of 2026 to get meaningful playing time on Team 149 were Josh Pitsenberger ’26 and Inumidun Ayo-Durojaiye ’26, both of whom would become foundational pieces on future Yale teams. In the now-seniors’ sophomore season, Yale’s 150th football team took the field looking to defend their conference title. The Bulldogs got off to a slow start, losing their first two games to Holy Cross and Cornell. But from there, the Bulldogs won six of their next seven games, entering the week of The Game with a 6–3 record. At The Game, Yale took down the rival Harvard Crimson to win the Ivy League Championship for the second year in a row. With the win, the class of 2026 were now two for two in conference championships — and against the team from the North. The 2024 season brought adversity, with two early conference losses handicapping Team 151’s chances of winning its third straight Ivy League championship. However, the Bulldogs persevered, fighting all sixty minutes of every game that season. This time around, Team 151 entered the week of The Game with one goal and one goal only. Yale, with a record of 6–3 overall and 3–3 in conference play, was mathematically eliminated from winning the conference title. The only thing left to do in the 2024 season was to take down the Crimson for the third year in a row. In an absolute barn burner that Yale led the entire game, the Bulldogs upset the Football Championship Subdivision No. 17 Harvard, winning 34-29. Among the top moments of the game was Pitsenberger’s unforgettable 39-yard touchdown catch and score , where he seemingly juked out half of Harvard’s defense en route to the end zone. It was the class of 2026’s third straight victory over the Crimson. The following offseason was unlike anything Ivy League football had ever seen before. In a shocking announcement , the Ivy League adopted a rule change allowing its teams to play in the postseason. This meant that, for the first time ever, the conference champion would receive an automatic berth in the Football Championships Subdivision playoffs following the end of the regular season. Yale, a program that has long held the record for most national championships, was given a chance to compete for their first title since 1944, when the Ivy Group Agreement was created. With the stakes now as high as they had ever been before, Team 152 entered the 2025 season led by team captain Josh Pitsenberger. The Bulldogs started strong, with two straight wins to kick off the season. Then, Yale fell 31-13 to Lehigh University, led by then-coach Kevin Cahill. The following week, Yale’s defense surrendered a last-second game-winning field goal to Dartmouth to fall to 2–2 for the season. The Bulldogs bounced back a week later with a 40-point win over Stonehill College to right the ship. From there on, it was smooth sailing for Team 152 as they rattled off four consecutive victories over Ivy League opponents to set the stage for the 141st playing of The Game. No. 8 Harvard entered the final week of the regular season with a perfect 9–0 record, the heavy favorite to be the inaugural Ivy League automatic qualifier. But Yale, with just one conference loss up to this point in the season, still had good reason to keep its Ivy title hopes alive. A win over Harvard would have put both teams at 6–1 in conference play, with the head-to-head tiebreaker then favoring Yale. Yale stripped Harvard on the first play from scrimmage, deep in Crimson territory, and never looked back. A quick score and another field goal put the Bulldogs up 10-0 nine minutes into the first quarter. A few drives later, Pitsenberger punched the ball in for the first of his three touchdowns on the day to put the Elis up 17-0. While Harvard finally managed to put points on the board, in the following drive, wideout Nico Brown ’26 hauled in a 64-yard touchdown pass, sending the Bowl into pandemonium. Yale maintained its lead throughout the game, and the Bulldogs secured their third Ivy title in four years. In addition to their fourth-straight win over the Crimson, Yale had also punched their ticket to their first-ever Football Championship Subdivision playoff appearance. The first-round playoff matchup saw Team 152 traveling to take on Youngstown State University. Down 42-14 halfway through the third quarter, the Bulldogs pulled off the unthinkable. Overcoming a 0.2 percent win probability, Yale came all the way back to knock off the Penguins 43-42 . In an instant classic, the Bulldogs won their first playoff game in school history. The following week, Yale’s Cinderella story came to a close with a close road loss to the eventual champions, Montana State University. Many of the Team 152 seniors have since found new homes. Pitsenberger recently signed as a priority undrafted free agent with the Houston Texans, while Ayo-Durojaiye signed with the Hamilton Tiger-Cats of the Canadian Football League. With eligibility still remaining, many other seniors found new homes with high-major FBS programs as well. Michael Bennett ’26 recently departed to join the Oregon Ducks, while Brown, Ezekiel Larry ’27 and Wilhelm Daal ’26 all left to join ACC programs in Stanford University, the University of Virginia and Duke University, respectively. Center Leo Bluhm ’26 is headed to Rice University, defensive lineman Ejiroghene Egodogbare ’26 committed to the University of Houston and safety Josh Tarver ’26 transferred to Tarleton State University to round out the Texas crew.

title: Seniors leave behind four years defined by resilience
author: Walter Royal
text: The five members of the class of 2026 made an immediate impact for Yale women’s soccer. In the now-seniors’ first season, the team jumped five spots in the standings to finish third in the Ivy League. The rejuvenated team boasted a 7–0–1 home record in 2022, with a season-opening 0-0 draw with Hofstra University being the only blemish to their undefeated defense of Reese Stadium. 2023 represented a step back. While their nonconference season showed promise, with the team’s offense displaying further improvement, the hot boots went cold in conference play, finishing the Ivy slate 0–5–2. However, the Bulldogs bounced back in the class’s junior year, posting a winning overall record and picking up a pair of conference victories. This season started out bright, with a 4-0 win over Hofstra University in the home opener. Things went south from there, but the Yale squad showed promise, rallying for a stunning 1-0 midseason upset over the eventual Ivy-champion Princeton Tigers on the road. Though the team missed the postseason once more, the Princeton win provided a crowning achievement for the team’s senior class that graduates after persevering through adversity.

title: As seniors cross finish line, a ‘proud’ record to look back on
author: Walter Royal
text: The class of 2026’s final season was one to remember for Yale’s cross country team. Last fall, the team finished fifth place in regionals, the highest in recent memory, for the second straight year. Owen Karas ’26 and Kenan Pala ’26 also qualified for NCAAs, becoming the first Bulldog men to do so since 2023. Captain Braden King ’26 recalled the team’s collective excitement after the regionals race in an interview with the News. “It’s such a weird mix of feelings and emotions when you just finished the hardest race ever in the 10K,” King said. “You find out your two best friends qualified for nationals, and it’s just super overwhelming. A lot of people hugging each other, a lot of tears.” Entering New Haven as a particularly highly recruited class, expectations for the class of 2026 were elevated. King said he is proud of what the class has achieved together. “When I look back at our time and how we’ve all developed, I’m super proud of what we built here and what we’ve continued,” he said. “We’ve really helped push this program further, and I couldn't have asked for a better group of fellow seniors to do that with.”

title: Seniors saw team through years of steady improvement
author: Eli Ratner
text: The four seniors of Yale field hockey — Captain Poppy Beales ’26, Lauren Venter ’26, CC Wolf ’26 and Maddy Zavalick ’26 — brought the program to new heights. The class of 2026 arrived in 2022 alongside a new head coach, Melissa Gonzalez, and played its way into a new era for Yale field hockey. Since their freshman season, the Elis have steadily progressed, moving up from a 7–9 record in 2023 to 10–6 in 2024, before their most successful 12–6 campaign in 2025. This fall, the Bulldogs advanced to the NCAA tournament for the first time ever. The Elis went 12–4 in the regular season, earning a 4–3 record in a competitive Ivy League before falling short against Princeton in the Ivy League tournament. The Bulldogs finished the season 12th in the NCAA Division I rankings. The excellence of the class of 2026 began before their senior season. Early in her career, Beales emerged as one of the team’s most accomplished players, starting every game she has played since her sophomore year and earning All-Mideast Region and All-Ivy First Team honors in each of the past three years. Following her successful senior campaign, she was dubbed a second-team All-American. Zavalick also played a key role on the team throughout her career, starting 12 of 15 games her sophomore year, before returning to start all games as a senior.

title: 91 percent of senior class has used AI for schoolwork, News survey finds
author: Hari Viswanathan
text: This year’s graduating seniors were first years when OpenAI released ChatGPT. As the first class with the opportunity to use large language models like ChatGPT during every year of their college career, most seniors have now incorporated artificial intelligence into their lives to assist them for various purposes, from aiding with problem sets to researching theses. According to an anonymous survey conducted by the News and filled out by 172 seniors, 91 percent of the class of 2026 have used AI for schoolwork. Only 9.1 percent of students surveyed said they had never used AI for schoolwork. A majority of students — 67.5 percent — reported using AI sometimes, often or very often. More than 75 percent of respondents said they have used AI in a problem set. About 64 percent of respondents said they have used AI to write a paper, while 48.5 percent of the surveyed seniors said they used AI to write their senior theses — slightly less than the 51.5 percent that said they did not. Joey Tan ’26, who is a computer science major, described AI as a “supplementary tool” that she utilizes alongside other forms of technology. “I’ve used it to look up things that I want, like a more in-depth answer compared to Google, for instance,” Tan said in a phone interview. “Or I’ll do a mix of Google searching and AI.” She said that she thinks one of the most evident impacts of AI is on office hours, adding that students can now use AI, instead of tutors and undergraduate learning assistants, to teach them content and help them through problems. “It’s kind of unfortunate, because I made so many of my computer science friends in office hours,” Tan said. “It was, like, a really important community for me. It was how I found people that take upper class electives with me later on.” Aviv Melamud ’26, another computer science major, said that using Claude — the large language model developed by Anthropic — to work through problems is like having a virtual teaching assistant. “It basically killed office hours,” Melamud said in a phone interview. Who is using AI? Male respondents were more likely to report that they used AI for schoolwork than female respondents. While 16.7 percent of male respondents said they used AI “very often” for schoolwork, only 1.6 percent of female respondents said they did. According to the survey results, students majoring in the sciences were most likely to report AI use for schoolwork, followed by those in the social sciences, those in interdisciplinary majors and those in the humanities. Brian Moore ’26 noted that in the humanities, AI can serve a similar function as writing partners in the Poorvu Center for Teaching and Learning, improving things like grammar, syntax and diction. “I tend to see this as polarizing. There’s a lot of folks who won’t touch AI with a 10-foot pole, and others who will readily adopt it,” Moore, who is majoring in humanities, said in a phone interview. Melamud said he thinks AI will not render STEM classes obsolete. He compared the experience of learning computer science with AI to that of learning math with the aid of a calculator. Professors will have to grapple seriously with the advancement of AI technology, Moore said, but he added that there are creative ways in which professors can weave AI into class assignments and pedagogy. “I guess, in some ways, AI is prompting us to re-examine what the philosophy of education is, right?” Moore said. ChatGPT was released on Nov. 30, 2022.

title: With high hopes, Yale continues to invest in Upper Science Hill
author: Sara Agrawal
text: One corner on campus has experienced a particularly expensive transformation over the past four years. The University had poured hundreds of millions into the development of Upper Science Hill, with the aim of setting Yale apart in the fields of physics, the applied sciences and engineering. University leaders have consistently emphasized their hopes to place Yale at the forefront of cutting-edge scientific fields like quantum computing. “The USHD reflects a long-term commitment to securing Yale’s leadership in scientific discovery and innovation in order to contribute meaningfully to addressing the world’s present and future challenges,” Michael Crair, Yale’s vice provost for research, wrote in an email to the News, referring to the Upper Science Hill Development project. In a February interview with the News, Yale President Maurie McInnis identified investing in the physical sciences and engineering as the “top priority” of the University, and in her end-of-year message to the Yale community, she singled out the University’s “strides in reimagining Science Hill.” When Linda McMahon, the United States’ secretary of education, toured Yale in April, she called the construction on Upper Science Hill Construction “incredible.” Yale’s 2023–24 financial report stated that out of the University’s $614 million in capital spending, which includes “renovations” and “acquisitions and new construction,” 12 percent — around $75 million — went into biological and physical sciences. That figure rose to over $190 million — 29 percent of $661 million — in the 2025 fiscal year . When asked why he thought Yale was investing so heavily in engineering and physical sciences, physics professor Karsten Heeger emphasized the importance of Yale’s frontier scholarship in those fields as well as the humanities. “Yale has always been leading in various fields, including the humanities, for example,” Heeger, who is also the director of the Yale Wright Laboratory for experimental nuclear physics, particle physics and astrophysics, said. “Science and engineering and technology is absolutely essential for society and our modern world. If we want to be a leading research university, we have to be cutting-edge in those fields.” The development of Upper Science Hill is a decade-long project that has been planned in two phases. According to Heeger, the first phase, which began in 2022 and broke ground in 2024, is set to be completed this fall. This phase involves the construction of a new Advanced Instrumentation Development Center and replacing an old part of the Wright Lab. Heeger said that the renovation will bolster the Wright Lab with “state-of-the-art laboratories that will give us the vibration isolation, the environmental control, all of the things that we need, the infrastructure for cutting-edge research.” That transformation will provide new research opportunities for both undergraduate and graduate students, Heeger added. The second phase of the Upper Science Hill development project will see the completion of a new Physical Sciences and Engineering Building, or PSEB, Heeger said. “The idea was to build a building complex that brings together research groups from physics, physical sciences, and the engineering departments and mainly with the focus on quantum science and material science,” he said. Crair wrote that the building complex will foster “cross-disciplinary collaboration.” In total, the building will house around 50 research groups, according to Heeger. Heeger also said that the physics department has made new appointments in quantum science and atomic, molecular and optical physics, but that exact final number “remains to be seen.” The development of Upper Science Hill follows a 2018 report of the University Science Strategy Committee, or USSC, which states that “tomorrow’s leaders need a solid grounding in STEM so they can understand the world and shape its future” and outlines investments the University might make to improve STEM at Yale. The report suggested that the University invest in graduate students, advance instrumentation, update core facilities and promote diversity in the STEM pipeline. The report also emphasized that the School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, or SEAS, would play a vital role in the future of science at Yale and “must be fully integrated into the fabric of the University.” In 2022, following another report from a SEAS strategic planning committee in 2021, the University announced major investments in applied sciences and engineering and the separation of SEAS faculty from the Faculty of Arts and Science. A focus on quantum Quantum science has been one of the focuses of Yale’s investments in engineering and applied science. The field, which studies the smallest units within atoms that act both as particles and as waves, is used both in physics and in computing and was one of the “five ideas of priority investment” highlighted in the 2018 USSC report. Speaking about the research in Wright Lab, Heeger said that “quantum science is intrinsic to all what we do.” Shaun Pexton ’26, the co-president of Yale Undergraduate Quantum Computing, called quantum the “shining beacon of Yale engineering” in an interview with the News. Pexton said that he’s noticed growing attention around quantum during his time at Yale as the “race” of quantum technology has made its way into “political conversations” and as Yale has gained recognition as a place where “a lot of cool stuff is happening.” In her end-of-year message, President McInnis said that the new facilities on Science Hill will “supercharge quantum science at Yale.” The Yale Quantum Institute will be relocated from Hillhouse Avenue to the PSEB, according to its website . In the message, President McInnis also mentioned professor Michel Devoret, who won a Nobel Prize in 2025 for his work in quantum physics , and QuantumCT , a nonprofit launched in 2022 by Crair and Pamir Alpay, the interim provost of the University of Connecticut. McInnis’ wrote in the end-of-year message that through quantum technology, “we will make our home state more attractive to start-ups” and “create a talented workforce in this area.” When asked why he thought the University was heavily investing in and promoting quantum, Pexton said that technological development, especially in AI and quantum, have become indicators of a university’s advancement. “Yale wants to be at the forefront, and they are at the forefront,” Pexton said. The construction on Upper Science Hill is located at 268 to 280 Whitney Ave.

title: After admin banner ban, YSE grads don anti-ICE t-shirts in class photo
author: Michelle So
text: Since the school’s founding in 1900, graduates of the Yale School of the Environment have taken an annual class photo behind Marsh Hall, where the original Yale Forest School was located . For over a century, these photos have captured the school through class size increases, the emergence of color photography and shifts in student body demographics. In recent years, the class photos have become a platform for School of the Environment students to make broader political statements. Members of the class of 2024 posed with pro-Palestinian protest materials, according to members of class of 2026, before the school updated its class photo protocol to prohibit signs and banners. This year, some members of the class of 2026 organized an unofficial class photo in which students donned t-shirts that depicted melting ice cubes. “The shirts were meant to be symbolic and hold two meanings: climate change is melting ice, and abolish ICE,” Riki Bertoldi ENV ’26, one of the photo’s organizers, wrote in a text to the News. On April 29, after the graduating students lined up for their official photo, nearly all of them stayed behind to change into the shirts and pose for a new photo, according to Bertoldi. She said that the idea for the unofficial photo had been conceived “months before.” Emily Kaufman ENV ’26 wrote in an email to the News that past students have presented more “explicit messages” in their class photos. “I really loved the class of 2024 photo when we saw a lot of protest signs, and I believe there was a big banner that said, ‘War is an environmental issue,’” Adrian Huq ENV ’26 said in a Zoom interview. “That really resonated with me as a peace advocate and anti-militarist sort of climate activist.” Huq said that administrators discouraged protest-related photoshoots after the 2024 display. Paige Stein, the school’s chief communications and marketing officer, wrote in an email to the News that in order to “emphasize the collective, inclusive nature of our school community,” the School of the Environment updated its “class photo protocol.” “Official photos are now taken without banners or signs,” Stein added. “The School of the Environment adheres to the university’s wider policy on freedom of expression.” According to the website of the office of the secretary and vice president for university life, the University prohibits large “banners held by multiple individuals” at outdoor events, but does not specify a policy prohibiting signs or banners in official photos. This year, as the annual class photo neared, Huq and Bertoldi said they worked to navigate around this updated policy change. “We decided to get creative and use textiles instead as a medium to express a collective statement in a way that still felt visible and meaningful to our community,” Bertoldi said. According to Bertoldi, Kaufman led much of the planning and coordination, while Brandon Hoak ENV ’26 created the initial design, produced the stencil and led the screen-printing process. “We made sure to communicate our plans with both international students and the broader class and asked whether people would feel comfortable with the shirts being worn during the official class photo,” Bertoldi wrote in an email. “Some students expressed discomfort, so we decided to take the shirt photo separately after the official class photo.” Bertoldi said she and other organizers invited other students to join the unofficial photoshoot through a WhatsApp group chat called “YSE Class of 2026,” which included every member of the graduating class. Students who wished to opt out were given the option to. According to Kaufman, the photo’s organizers “felt it was important to continue the tradition in a more subtle yet still powerful way” after they had elected not to wear the shirts during the official photo. “Through the drawing of melting ice, we wanted to show that environmentalism cannot be separated from the injustices currently facing immigrant communities and that YSE will continue our longstanding tradition of speaking out despite administrative pushback,” Kaufman said. Marsh Hall is located at 360 Prospect St.

title: Yale is building 263 new wells for geothermal energy
author: Michelle So
text: Natural gas provides roughly half of Connecticut’s energy, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. While burning the fuel produces half the emissions as does burning coal, natural gas is not as green as one might think. Any carbon-based form of energy produces carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases when combusted for energy. Greenhouse gas emissions are responsible for trapping heat in Earth’s atmosphere, which contributes to global warming. Yale has committed itself to achieving zero emissions by 2050, a goal towards which the University has slowly been advancing. Alternative forms of energy, including geothermal technologies, may be the ticket there. “Yale is moving toward geothermal energy as part of the university’s commitment to achieve a zero‐emissions (zero‐carbon) campus by 2050,” Andy Bromage, the sustainability communications officer for the Yale Office of Facilities, wrote in an email to the News. In order to achieve that goal, Bromage said, drilling is underway to install 263 geothermal wells on Upper Science Hill. The new plant will support buildings including the Yale Science Building, the Sloane Physics Laboratory, the Sterling Chemistry Laboratory, the Kline Chemistry Laboratory, the Chemistry Research Building and the Bass Center. Electricity is generated when water is heated to turn turbines that produce steam. Yale currently employs several means of energy generation, including natural gas power plants on Tower Parkway and Congress Avenue, wind turbines on the roof of the Becton Engineering and Applied Sciences building and solar panels on Kroon Hall, Fisher Hall and West Campus. The core change in Yale’s energy-generating system involves the replacement of steam-based heating with low‐temperature water — heated to around 120 to 140 degrees Fahrenheit — generated by an electric heat pump that will draw heat from deep underground. “Any move away from using fossil resources (carbon) and toward using renewable resources (i.e., those that don’t move carbon from underground storage to the atmosphere) is a step in the right direction and is a good thing!” Hanno Erythropel, a researcher at the Yale University Center for Green Chemistry and Green Engineering, wrote in an email to the News. “That is certainly the case for the Yale geothermal energy project.” Erythropel added that more specificity regarding the amount of emitted carbon dioxide that could be offset by the project would be helpful. Yale’s move toward geothermal energy follows a trend of universities moving towards cleaner energy. In 2024, The New York Times reported that other universities and colleges, including Princeton, Carleton College and Ball State University, were also tapping into the technology. “I think it’s a good push for Yale as a university and as a whole to make good on their carbon capture initiative while being more sustainable,” Naomi Orcel ’27, the president of Club Geo, an Earth and planetary science student organization, said. Orcel said she was initially irked that the construction on Upper Science Hill took away parking spaces for faculty, but that she changed her mind on the project after hearing its mission. “I think it’s a really good thing that we’re using geothermal energy,” she added, “because it’s not an energy source people usually think to use; people usually think like solar or wind.” The shift toward geothermal energy extends beyond Yale. New Haven received around $9.5 million from the Environmental Protection Agency to build a networked geothermal system to provide clean heating and cooling to the New Haven Union Station. The funding would also support the construction of a mixed-income housing development adjacent to the station. Yale’s Upper Science Hill Development’s new facilities for quantum science, engineering and materials science are designed to hopefully support both new construction and existing Science Hill laboratory buildings by providing electrified thermal energy and reducing the need to add capacity to Yale’s existing power plants and steam distribution system. The Upper Science Hill Development is scheduled to be completed in 2030.

title: New student-built app aims to make dining hall menus more accessible
author: Anya Geist
text: When Yassin Mudawi ’28, a transfer student, arrived on campus last fall, he said he found Yale’s dining system confusing: There were so many dining facilities, all with varying closures, transfer restrictions and menu changes. Mudawi recently led a team at the Yale Computer Society to launch YaleMeals, an app that aims to offer an intuitive, easy-to-use menu guide to Yale’s dining options. The app was downloaded over 700 times by April 7, according to Mudawi. “For a new student coming here, it seemed to be a pain point for me personally, and how I approach software is I find a pain point in my life and then try to fix it,” Mudawi said in an interview, referring to Yale’s dining system. Mudawi wanted to create an easily navigable app. The Yale Hospitality website, which uses Nutrislice to display menus, has a lot of “nested pages,” Mudawi said. Nutrislice requires users to visit new pages to view a dining hall’s menu for each meal on a given day. Mudawi said he brought his idea to Yale Computer Society co-President Ryan Fernandes ’28 last fall, and the club decided to take on YaleMeals as a project. On YaleMeals, Mudawi tried to make it simple to switch between locations, meals and days. The app also allows students to customize its settings, including for their dietary preferences. The app features a “Friends” tab, where users can invite and schedule meals with their peers. “I noticed there’s a big meal culture here at Yale, where everyone wants to grab, like, lunch together or dinner together, and it’s really wholesome. So why not try to promote that and encourage that?” Mudawi said. Mudawi said he and his team hope students can use YaleMeals even in the case that Yale Hospitality’s database changes. He added that he built the YaleMeals code to make it easy to switch to a new interface for a changed database. Yale Hospitality spokesperson Alexa Gotthardt wrote in an email to the News: “We value student innovation and recognize the creativity students bring in developing new tools and platforms. At the same time, we encourage students to rely on Yale Hospitality’s official menus page for the most accurate and up-to-date menu information.” Other students have created similar apps to YaleMeals in the past, including Bulldog Menus, which was founded by Edmundo Chavez Ramirez ’28. Regarding similar apps created outside of the Yale Computer Society, Lily Lin ’27, the club’s co-president, said, “Our goal is not to monopolize the market.” YaleMeals builds on the work of Yale Menus, a previously popular app that also listed each dining hall’s menus. Yale Menus was originally launched by Eric Foster ’20 and his brother David Foster ’23 in 2017, and it was relaunched by David Foster and Erik Boesen ’24 in 2020, Boesen wrote to the News. After Boesen graduated, the app became challenging to maintain. “We negotiated a license to sell Menus to Yale Hospitality over a period of roughly 4-5 years,” Boesen wrote to the News. Ultimately, the sale fell through. Boesen, realizing the app was out of date due to changes to Yale Hospitality’s databases and programming interfaces, passed it on to the Yale Computer Society. While Yale Menus was not a project created through the club, Boesen wrote, he had been a club member as an undergraduate. “Yale Hospitality has previously explored partnership opportunities related to student-developed dining apps, including the ‘Yale Menus’ app,” Gotthardt wrote to the News. “At that time, we determined it was not the right fit, largely due to the operational complexity involved in maintaining a separate platform alongside our existing systems, which serve the same purpose.” Ruby Lytton-Hirsch ’29 frequently uses Yale’s Nutrislice app to look at lunch menus around campus. Looking at the menu in advance allows her to see whether she prefers the Greens and Grains option or the regular offering. However, Lytton-Hirsch said the Nutrislice app can be challenging to use, particularly because she has to scroll to the bottom of the page to see the main options for each meal. Lytton-Hirsch said she thought the YaleMeals app looked “prettier and easier to navigate” than Nutrislice. Similarly, Hrishita Shah ’28 said she found YaleMeals more “intuitive” to use than Nutrislice, though she added that she does not mind using Nutrislice because she has gotten used to it. Shah, however, noted that the YaleMeals app lacks reviews of the menu items. “The old Yale Menus app, it had a lot of reviews, like the Commons cookies had, like, 50 hearts,” Shah said, adding that “not a lot of people” use YaleMeals. “So, like, the s’mores brownies have zero ratings.” Rishi Sankhe ’28 sometimes uses YaleMeals, though he does not always feel the need to check dining hall menus before visiting. He finds the app easy to use, he said. Sankhe noted that he thought the YaleMeals team was continuing to make good changes to the app, including adding pictures of the dishes. Some students have never used YaleMeals. Zach Pan ’27 said he had not heard of YaleMeals or even the Nutrislice website. “I show up at the dining hall and eat what’s there,” Pan said. “I know I’m not going to spend additional funds on food from other sources. So I enter the dining hall, and I eat the food in the dining hall.” The dining halls serve chicken tenders for lunch every other Thursday.

title: Yale study finds link between cancer and living near factory farms
author: Michelle So
text: Earlier this year, Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced a revamp of the familiar food pyramid, with protein sources like red meat and full-fat dairy at the top of the list of recommended foods. Yet, certain studies have found consumption of red meat to be linked to cardiovascular diseases and cancers of the colon . Now, simply living near sites of red meat production may be linked to cancers as well. A Yale study published in the journal Environmental Research examined whether living in areas with higher densities of animal feeding operations and concentrated animal feeding operations is associated with cancer incidence. The feeding operations, commonly known as factory farms, often contain livestock in cramped conditions. Concentrated operations exceed a threshold population of 1,000 beef cattle, 700 dairy cows or 2,500 hogs. “These facilities are known sources of environmental pollution, including air pollutants (e.g., ammonia, particulate matter, hydrogen sulfide) and water contaminants such as nitrates, which may have implications for human health,” Jiyoung Son, a researcher at the School of the Environment and the paper’s first author, wrote in an email to the News. Using county-level data from California, Iowa and Texas between 2000 and 2021, the researchers found that counties with higher factory farm density had higher overall cancer incidence. The increased cancer incidence prevailed despite variations in factory farm makeup; for example, dairy operations are more common in California, swine in Iowa and beef cattle in Texas. Cancer isn’t the only risk associated with living near a factory farm. When animals live in close proximity with one another, disease transmission becomes nearly unavoidable. Farmers often add antibiotics to animal feed to prevent disease-related deaths and bolster livestock growth. Overuse of antibiotics can cause bacteria to develop antibiotic resistance. When runoff from these feedlots enters the environment, these superbugs may infect humans and other animals and remain unresponsive to treatment — a scenario that is dangerous to both livestock and human health. Cole Haynes ’27, the co-president of Plant Forward Yalies, a student group that promotes sustainable food systems, said he was not shocked by the study’s results. “While the findings of this Yale study are deeply concerning, they are unfortunately not surprising. The public health impacts of animal agriculture are widespread and well documented,” Haynes wrote in an email to the News. The study’s researchers still say more detailed research is needed. Son suggested implementing environmental monitoring, especially of air quality and drinking water contamination, and improving waste management and reducing emissions. Consuming red and processed meats is also associated with “pro-inflammatory mechanisms” that increase gastrointestinal and colon cancers and cardiovascular disease, John Dinkler, a cardiologist and the associate head of Timothy Dwight College, said. Instead, Dinkler recommends that people switch to a more plant-based diet. Changing one’s eating habits has marked benefits on cardiovascular disease and reduces cancer risk. A plant-based diet tends to lower blood pressure, improve cholesterol levels, maintain healthy blood sugar levels and reduce inflammation, he wrote in an email to the News.
