All of those changes improve the health of blood vessels throughout the cardiovascular system, reducing the risk of heart attacks and strokes. “I’ve actually had many patients lower doses of cholesterol, blood pressure, and diabetes medications (or come off medications completely) after changing to a plant-based diet,” Dinkler wrote. “It’s impressive to see!” According to Haynes, a plant-based diet has been shown to be one of the most effective ways to reduce an individual’s carbon footprint by 75 percent, while also decreasing reliance on systems that harm human health, animals and the environment. “Even small changes, like replacing meat with plant-based alternatives, opting for plant-based desserts, or swapping dairy milk for oat or soy, can have a significant impact,” Haynes said. An estimated 99 percent of the United States’ farmed animals live on factory farms, according to the Sentience Institute, a think tank.

title: Scrolling through the semester
author: Beatrice Barilla
text: We’ve all been there: your brain turned off at a 9 a.m. lecture, tired from a late night at Bass, the dining hall coffee suspiciously uncaffeinated. Across the room, someone accidentally blasts an Instagram reel with what can only be described as a brainrot audio, and everyone laughs. You groggily pull out your phone to check the time and — is that an email about your late paper? A text about dinner plans? A grade posted on Canvas for that quiz you know you flunked? You gradually get absorbed into the lunch menu at Berkeley, your long-distance best friend’s Instagram story and now Connections. When you look up, everything in lecture is foreign — since when did you start taking Astrophysics 8000?! Technology is bittersweet, and we all know it. Sure, now we can conveniently take notes on an iPad, record and photograph important concepts and subtly stalk our friends on Find My — but our attention spans have suffered as a consequence. In my own biology lectures, I look over the shoulders of those in front of me and see TikTok, not Goodnotes. And as far as notes go, ChatGPT seems to be putting plenty of legwork in. I notice that I can’t even make it through a full lecture without checking my email. History professor Carlos Eire ’79 attended Yale as a graduate student around 50 years ago when typewriters reigned, and he has watched Yale change along with its technology. Back when he first attended in the 70s, Ashley’s Ice Cream was Whitlock’s Typewriter Shop, which Eire says “was essential for everyone’s survival.” “Sometime around 1975 a fellow graduate student in computer science told me that someday ‘soon’ we would all be able to type text onto a TV monitor and produce error-free documents that could be endlessly edited and polished to perfection, and then — best of all — produce the final version on a printing machine,” Eire wrote in an email. “I said something like ‘sure, yeah, and we'll soon be driving flying cars too . . .’ A couple of years later, boom!” Computers were too expensive for common use at that time, but they were becoming more widespread in the collegiate sphere and would spread across Yale over the next few decades. Even in 1996, when Eire returned to Yale to teach, office work was still done on typewriters and paper, and books had to be located in the stacks manually and checked out using cardboard forms. “The printing on those forms was very elegant,” Eire wrote about the check-out forms. “And the libraries were kind enough to provide stubby little pencils to help us all complete this laborious task.” Eire wrote that Yale “fully embraced” computer technology and the Internet around the turn of the millennium. According to him, Yale’s computerized renaissance was fairly rapid: the library and its catalogue became digital, rendering the card catalog shelves in Sterling obsolete, a “reminder of days gone by.” The era of Microsoft Word had dawned, though paper copies of assignments were usually required until about 2010. “And Yale started to provide professors with personal computers,” Eire wrote. We all know the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on digital learning technology, which further cemented remote meetings and virtual education technology as hallmarks of collegiate education. In the words of Eire, we now reside in the “AI Apocalypse,” where nothing can be assumed to be original anymore. Artificial intelligence chatbots can write a paper, create a practice exam, take notes and generate resumes. It takes grit — and time — to ignore its siren song. Technology only continues to change more and more rapidly: The jump from the proliferation of online assignments to the introduction of AI took only a fraction of time compared to the transition from the typewriter to the computer. It’s hard not to wonder what’s next and how quickly it will change education — and our attention spans. This year’s graduating seniors watched educational technology from the pandemic era become the status quo and AI develop from irrelevant to ubiquitous, but remaining students will witness how it continues to shape Yale over the years to come. Will we scroll through the semester or learn to recover our focus? Personally, I’m hoping for the latter.

title: Looking up and slowing down
author: Michael Willen
text: Looking up I have a knack for noticing shoes. They hold space in my mind as a representation and understanding of my friends. I see how they compliment or clash with their personalities and how their choices change with the seasons. I notice when they switch it up, and I know instinctively when they’re debuting a new pair. A scarily good memory is partly to blame. But, in full, it’s a defense mechanism. Instead of focusing on a face, I would find some background fixation: like a baby’s game of peek-a-boo, looking down meant that no one could study me too closely or perceive too much. I spent a lot of my time at Yale afraid of that perception. The school can feel nauseatingly small sometimes: No one is more than one degree of connection away, and if you haven’t met someone, you’ve heard of them. We talk and compare so incessantly. Opinions spread like wildfire, which doesn’t inspire confidence for a people pleaser. In dining halls, I’d stress when conversations got too loud or clam up when neighboring groups got too quiet. Walking through crowded spaces had me on high alert. Overthinking my own racial identity often kept me away from events at the Afro-American Cultural Center. In general, I tried to let any moment of possible judgment pass as quickly as possible. We’re our own harshest critics, in this way. We notice every quirk in a photo and contemplate our actions until every facet has been scrutinized and regretted. Beyond that selfish veil, everyone is living far too much of their own lives to spend more than the briefest moments on you. Across my four bright college years, I’ve come to realize that more and more. And looking back, the moments I’ve enjoyed the most are the moments when I stopped caring. So own the space you take up and stay confident in your ability to make mistakes. Spill your chai latte everywhere in the Elm? Clean it up with a laugh and a smile. Be ready to fall on your ice skates and don’t care who might see — it’ll make you a better skater. Mess up a dance move on stage, or many — shoutout to Danceworks — and no one will notice. Accept that an interview or a date might go badly — the bad ones are the only way you’ll get practice for the good ones. Don’t look back in regret because you’ve let your fear of failure or embarrassment keep you from living Yale to the fullest. If things go wrong? Bounce back. Care about what people think to the extent that it helps you be a better person, but not so much that you live solely for that perception. I still notice shoes. But now I notice faces, too — the eyes, smiles and everything behind them. I let people truly see me, and in doing so I start to truly see them. Slowing down I’m a fast walker. Across two New York summers, I perfected the New Yorker stride that would get me from point A to point B as efficiently as possible without ever showing effort. It’s great when I’m making the crosstown trek from Grand Central Terminal to Penn Station on my way home to Philly or when I’m running embarrassingly late to a class. At Yale, our brains seem trained to work like that, too. We’re a school full of overachievers, overcommitters and overextenders. It’s what got us here in the first place, and here, it only gets worse: Seemingly infinite clubs, classes and events provide never-ending options to fill space in our lives — worsened by an embedded peer pressure to do so. So fill space I did. Each semester, I’d populate my life with every sign-up and every yes. But we have limits. Every new activity removed hours of sleep and added late nights spent in the library. Every new commitment meant less and less blank space in my Google Calendar. I took too long to realize the importance of that blank space. In finding more time for scheduled things, I found less time for myself. I rue the times I said no to friends’ side quests and the times I missed nights of hourslong chatter in our suite’s common room. I spent too many moments with some part of my attention stuck on the next task or deadline instead of what was happening directly in front of my eyes. Always on the lookout for the next thing that needed to be done, I never gave my brain the benefit of a pause. Sometimes, the velocity of keeping up caused me to speed past people. If some adjacent worry took over, I’d accidentally walk faster and leave a friend behind, or I’d space out on important parts of a deep conversation over dinner. I’d forget names or miss important moments. With graduation approaching, I look forward to finally getting a bit of that pause. But taking on so much isn’t in Yale’s graduation requirements. I wish I’d reflected sooner on the intentionality of my commitments, and I wish I’d given myself the grace to question what’s worth it and to drop what wasn’t. Don’t forget the value of slowing down. Have blank space in your schedule — it’s more important than whatever might take its place. It’s okay to say no when you know something is too much, and it’s okay to do less than what it seems like everyone else is doing. It’s okay to take a little bit longer to get from point A to point B. And don’t leave your friends or yourself behind on the way there.

title: Light and truth
author: Madison Butchko
text: On Ivy Day, I put on my lucky purple jacket, a soft lilac almost identical to my bedroom walls, which I had stared at for the 17 years of my life in Michigan. All day, I reassured myself that whatever verdict graced my laptop, I would be okay — though my body knew I was lying. Sitting at my desk, I knotted my fingers together. Before opening my Yale decision, I whispered a series of small prayers. I clicked, and the screen went black briefly before the bulldog appeared, singing, “Bow wow wow.” Tears streamed down my cheeks before I could process a single thought. My hand covered my mouth, muffling a series of “Oh my God, oh my God,” as I sobbed at my desk before running to tell my parents. For a long time, Yale existed in my mind as a lighthouse: distant, bright and almost impossible to reach. I believed education could move me toward a larger life, so I pushed harder into the current — I woke up early, stayed late, studied, worked jobs, joined clubs and sports and kept going, even when no direct sign of progress encouraged me. Somewhere ahead was a fixed point of light. I didn’t know if I would reach it, but it gave me direction when the water was dark and I could not feel the bottom. The following August, the light became a destination. Arriving at the entrance of Jonathan Edwards College with my parents and walking through the doors of Sterling Memorial Library for the first time, I thought I had reached my dream. The cathedral of books enchanted me: the vaulted ceilings, the stone archways, the light falling through tall windows in pale columns. I took photographs of the carved faces in the walls, the floral designs, the small details I wanted to savor in memory. For a long time, I wanted Yale to remain that place in my mind, that place I had dreamed for: all the libraries and courtyards, late nights in the buttery and bright laboratory windows, the stone-bound beauty. I wish I could write about those things as if they belonged to one life — without accepting any connection to his abuse, the court cases, my years of silence and suffering and the irreparable damage I will never have enough words to encapsulate. I still do not know how to separate my experience of Yale from the insurmountable pain I endured here. Lives do not divide themselves that neatly. When I think about graduation, I think about the people around me crying because they will miss this place. I am crying for what I missed — for the version of college I imagined before I knew how much these years would hold. There is no ceremony for that kind of shattering loss. At Yale, achievement is easily recognizable. It has titles, fellowships, publications, neat lines on a resume. Emotional growth doesn’t lend itself to such memorabilia. In high school, my “best” looked obvious: discipline, early mornings, good grades, awards, medals, things other people could see and measure. In college, my “best” became harder to specify. Sometimes, it looked like simply getting out of bed. Sometimes, it looked like answering one email, eating something without feeling sick immediately after, doing far less than the old version of me would have done and trying not to berate myself for it. I don’t want to call what I have been through strengthening. I never wanted to be stronger in this way. Surviving does not mean you are grateful for its so-called lessons, and endurance is not the same as growth. Healing has not felt like rising from the ashes, not like a fire catching again — dramatic and bright. Most of the time, I’ve felt like the embers after a fire: small, hot, exhausted, glowing in places but no longer able to burn the way they once did, with my soul broken into pieces too small to gather once more. I did not choose to keep fighting. I felt caught between my will and my body, between the part of me that wanted to stop and the body that kept moving anyway. Because bodies do that. They fall asleep. They wake again. The sun sets. The sun rises. Seconds pass into minutes, hours, and days, and somehow I remained imprisoned inside time, even when I felt left behind by it. Life moves forward with both cruelty and mercy. The cruelty is that everything continues while you are the one who carries the damage, even though you were not the one who caused it. The mercy is that time carries you too, even when you cannot carry yourself, moving you into another morning, another chance to keep living before you feel ready. Even the small things bring comfort — waking up a little earlier, sleeping without nightmares, meeting friends again after months of absence, walking across campus despite feeling unsafe, writing my truth despite the fear. Healing does not bring peace unilaterally. My friendships, my writing and the small ordinary things that accumulate into a life do not erase the years I’ve lost. They remind me that the harm I suffered can belong to my life without becoming the entirety of it. Healing has become the slow growth of life around the wound. A tree does not heal its wounds. Its trunk thickens around them, laying down new rings over what cannot be undone, with the wound preserved at the center of everything that follows. What happened to me is fixed at the center of these years in the same way: unchangeable, present in everything I have become — the core around which the rest of my life has had to grow. People carry far more than they show. We move beside people whose lives we may never fully understand. Pain has blanketed the world with loneliness, but it has also made me more tender toward the hidden lives of others. If there is anything I will carry beyond Yale, it is that tenderness: a deeper kindness, a greater compassion, a soft heart that reaches toward anyone who has been hurting. I mourn the girl who was seventeen, sitting at a desk in Michigan in a lilac bedroom, crying from pure joy because she was about to attend Yale. She believed the world was beautiful, that all people were good, that her dreams were infinite. I wish I could hold her tightly. I wish I could have protected her. She will go through things she cannot yet imagine, not cleanly, not without cost, but through. I think of “Lux et Veritas” and understand it differently. I came to Yale believing light was a destination, a reward for all that swimming toward the lighthouse. But light is what you learn to see after your eyes have adjusted to the dark. Truth is not a given, either. I used to think truth would be recognized simply because it was real — because it is what happened. I learned that truth can be denied, buried, silenced and made costly to speak. For the past four years, I prayed so desperately to return to my 17-year-old self, to the innocence and possibility she carried in her heart. I cannot. Instead, I protect what remains of her in me: a hope that has changed shape, the way light does, bending around whatever stands in its way and continuing on.

title: The ultimate Yucket list: 100 things to do before you graduate
author: Seth Goldin
text: The list that follows is semi-ordered: 1. Trauma-dumped in a pre-orientation “hometown” 2. Grabbed coffee at the Jitterbus 3. Read “ The Opposite of Loneliness ” 4. Snuck into your friend’s duty night 5. Strolled through the Yale University Art Gallery 6. Explored the Peabody Museum 7. Toured the Yale Center for British Art 8. Attended a recital at the School of Music 9. Explored the “Brain Museum” at the Cushing Center 10. Created a masterpiece in one of the college art studios 11. Peered out of the Observatory telescope 12. Requested texts from the Sterling or Beinecke archives 13. Hissed at a Yale Political Union debate 14. Caught a Dramat theater performance 15. Attended — or sang in — an a capella concert 16. Supported a friend at — or performed in — a dance show 17. Wore a last-minute costume to the Yale Symphony Orchestra Halloween concert 18. Published in — or been quoted in — the YDN 19. Cheered at any Yale sporting event besides The Game 20. Played for an intramural team 21. Visited every residential college library 22. Eaten in every dining hall — yes, that includes Pierson 23. Snacked at a buttery other than your college’s 24. Played ping-pong in a buttery 25. Cooked in a student kitchen 26. Grabbed a bite from a food truck behind Murray 27. Visited the Yale Farm or Wooster Square Farmers’ Market 28. Been to Pepe’s 29. Been to Sally's 30. Been to Modern 31. Argue about which of the above is the best pizza spot 32. Been to Patricia’s 33. Ordered a late-night Wenzel at Alpha Delta 34. Ordered an omelette at JE — if you know, you know 35. Gone to an event just for the free food 36. Said you’ll grab a meal with someone 37. Actually grabbed a meal with someone 38. Used GCal to schedule a meal with someone 39. Answered a tourist’s question 40. Yelled “happy birthday!” at a tour guide 41. Grabbed burgers at the Well 42. Said you “go to school in Connecticut” 43. Pet Handsome Dan 44. Taken the Yuttle 45. Stayed up past midnight talking about religion 46. Hiked East Rock at sunrise or sunset 47. Hiked West Rock 48. Ice skated at “The Whale” 49. Biked the Farmington Canal Heritage Trail 50. Worked out at Payne Whitney 51. Blown giant bubbles on Cross Campus 52. Napped in the Good Life Center 53. Run in the annual Faxon Law New Haven Road Race 54. Gone on a long walk to the Yale Divinity School at night 55. Had a deep, meaningful conversation in Murray’s secret courtyard 56. Retreated with a club 57. Visited a town in Connecticut other than New Haven 58. Rode the Metro North to New York City 59. Visited the Yale Club in New York City 60. Sledded down Science Hill 61. Studied at the Lillian Goldman Law Library 62. Picked a class because of a crush 63. Pulled an all-nighter 64. Fallen asleep in a class 65. Slept through a class 66. Credit/D/Failed a class 67. Cried in front of your dean 68. Requested a dean’s extension 69. Studied in the stacks 70. Changed the aux at Timothy Dwight’s dining hall 71. Split up a suite 72. Slept in a Bass cubicle 73. Eaten at Pasta E Basta 74. Attended a suite party 75. Did everything except drink tea at a college tea 76. Danced in the God Quad 77. Attended first-year Holiday “Dinner” 78. Tailgated at the Yale-Harvard game 79. Crashed the first-year snowball fight 80. Waited in line at the Pierson Inferno 81. Mingled at Senior Masquerade 82. Been to Woads, Soads, Hallowoads, Mistletoads or Valenwoads — bonus: “ Woads Scholar ” 83. Meditated with the Buddhist group at Harkness Tower 84. Heard the Whiffenpoofs sing at Mory’s 85. Sipped from a Mory’s cup 86. Caught the “Yague” 87. Been to GHeav after 2 a.m. 88. “The Tang” or “Caps” 89. Made fun of a friend at a Mellon forum 90. “All Star”-ed Feb Club 91. Polar plunged at Lighthouse Point 92. Attended “Battle of the Bands” 93. Dartied at Spring Fling 94. Picnicked on Cross Campus 95. Done the “Yorkside Challenge” 96. Stumbled upon the third Good Life Center 97. Infiltrated — or joined — a secret society 98. Witnessed — or streaked in — the Bass naked run 99. Attended a party hosted by the Pundits 100. Graduated...

title: The Quiet Architect at Yale
author: Jonas Loesel
text: Last August, Deborah Berke welcomed her newest students to the Yale School of Architecture. Standing atop Rudolph Hall, Yale’s Art and Architecture Building, the dean told them about “seven rules for life”—a creed that belonged not to the dean but to the school’s billionaire donor, Edward Bass ’68. “Ed Bass is truly an extraordinary man,” she recalled telling the new cohort. “It's an honor to have a position that's named after him, and I want to tell you his rules for life.” Nine months prior, a small group led by Bass’s wife, Sasha, had made one of the largest donations in the school’s history to endow Berke’s deanship in honor of Bass, now called the Edward P. Bass Dean at the Yale School of Architecture. Berke told me that she learned these rules over the years of knowing Bass, who is 80 years old, and now keeps them on a note taped to the bottom-left corner of her computer. The note reads: Work hard. Be nice. Have fun. Be humble. Be generous. Be grateful. Everyone deserves your respect. According to former Yale president Richard Levin, Bass has done “probably more philanthropy than any living Yale donor.” Although exact numbers are not public, Bass has quietly transformed the school since the 1990s with gifts totaling at least $300 million. From 2001 to 2013, he served on the Yale Corporation, in addition to a number of other university councils that he’s still on today. His architectural vision, university governance, and philanthropic contributions have significantly shaped Yale’s Science Hill, two new residential colleges, and the transformation of the Yale Peabody Museum. But most students have never heard of him. Bass hasn’t named a major building or program after himself (Bass Library was named after his younger brother, Robert), and he has cut a far slimmer public profile than many of Yale’s megadonors, including his peers in the nine figures. Stephen Schwarzman ’69, to name one, is the namesake of Yale’s Schwarzman Center, and Charles B. Johnson ’54 funded Yale’s field hockey stadium, Johnson Field, and the Brady-Johnson Program in Grand Strategy. Bass did not respond to interview requests made by email, U.S. mail, phone calls, or through the chief of security of one of his companies, Sundance Square. His quiet, “behind-the-scenes” work at Yale begs the question: Why does Ed Bass care about Yale? And how has he shaped the university in the last three decades? Ed Bass sits on a pony with Sid Richardson at the left and Perry Bass at the right. (1947) Photo from Fort Worth Star-Telegram archives. Ed Bass was born in 1945, the second son of Perry Bass ’37, a Fort Worth wildcatter who had worked for his uncle, the legendary oil tycoon Sid Richardson. Ed enrolled at Yale in the fall of 1963 — his mother told the New York Times Magazine in 1991 that his father “so enjoyed his Yale education that he said [the sons] could go to Yale—or they could go to work." When Richardson died in 1959, Bass inherited $2.8 million. Bass’s time at Yale coincided with a major shift in Yale’s demographics. Starting in the mid-60s, Yale president Kingman Brewster, Jr. overhauled the school’s admissions, recruiting “students from a much broader part of the United States,” as opposed to its traditional base of New England private schools, according to longtime Yale administrator Sam Chauncey ’57. “The face of Yale is changing really, very dramatically in the four years [Ed] was here.” Bass’s class of 1967 was the first where private school students weren’t the majority, but their influence still pervaded the school when Bass arrived. Four Yalies who overlapped with Bass recalled the divide on campus between public and private school students, who were then called “preppies.” Bass had come to Yale as a millionaire “preppie” from one of Yale’s feeders, Phillips Andover Academy. But classmates don’t remember him keeping with the Andover crowd. Joe Cohen ’67, a fellow classmate in his residential college Saybrook, recalled that Bass did not fit the prep-school stereotype. “There were many preppies that were very distinctive as preppies and he was not one of them. You know, even his dress, I don't remember him as being a sort of J-Press type guy,” referring to the upscale clothing store typically associated with prep-school students at the time. “I wasn't aware of him being a millionaire. He was very discreet and also open to all students.” Although Bass entered with the class of 1967, he deferred graduation until 1968, according to an entry in the Yale Class of 1967 50th Reunion Class Book. In his senior year, he joined a secret society—so secret, apparently, that fellow member Jack Emmons ’68 wouldn’t tell me its name—primarily composed of engineering and administrative science majors, like Emmons and Bass. “It wasn't quite like Skull and Bones or that sort of thing,” Emmons said. “It was much lower key.” Immediately after graduation, Bass served in the US Coast Guard for two years before returning to Yale for his master’s degree in architecture in 1970. He never finished that degree, dropping out because, according to his entry in his 50th Yale reunion class book, he “embarked on a summer building project from which I never returned.” Ed Bass, left, from a still in the documentary Spaceship Earth, about the members of Synergia Ranch. The summer building project was a house in Cedar Crest, New Mexico, according to Mark Nelson, a former friend who met Bass while doing landscaping for the project. At the time, Nelson lived at Synergia Ranch, a countercultural commune near Santa Fe inspired by the ecological philosopher Buckminster Fuller. Bass “got very turned on about the vision” of Synergia Ranch, said Nelson, and moved there in 1973. At Synergia, Bass worked the ranch’s land, studied its ecology, and toured with the ranch’s experimental theater troupe, the Theater of All Possibilities , in the 1970s and 1980s. In 1973, Bass also funded the Institute of Ecotechnics, a charity which supported Synergia’s ecologically-minded construction projects. Nelson ran the charity until 2024. During the 70s and 80s, Bass and his three brothers all invested their inheritance into billion dollar fortunes, although Bass’s three brothers spent more time investing on Wall Street than he would. Cohen, who knew of the Bass family while on Wall Street in the 1970s, said of their business that Ed Bass was on the “periphery,” and did not play a role in the “core Bass family involvement.” Levin agreed, saying that his brother Sid Bass ’65 “was driving the train on the investments.” Sid Bass declined to comment for this article. A 1984 cover of Newsweek portrayed Ed Bass, second from right, in the background of his family’s investments. In 1991, Bass’s father told the New York Times Magazine that at a young age, Ed “told his mother, ‘I never want to be part of the family business. I want to do something by myself.’” Although spending the intervening decades ranching and funding the members of Synergia, he kept his $2.8 million inheritance with the family’s investment company, Bass Brothers Enterprises, where it grew to more than a billion dollars by the 1980s. In 2024, Forbes ranked the Bass family as America’s 36th richest family, and Ed Bass the third-richest Bass brother. “In 1991,” Bass wrote in his class book, “I became reengaged with Yale after not setting foot on campus for 20 years.” The year prior, in May of 1990, Bass had already donated $20 million to Yale, at the time the single largest gift the university had received since 1966. He first returned to Yale’s orbit at the request of his father, Perry Bass, according to Levin. During Yale’s capital campaign in the ‘90s, Perry Bass gave $20 million to Yale to fund the Perry R. Bass Center for Molecular and Structural Biology, and “agreed to try to get his sons to each come up with $20 million,” Levin said. Ed stepped up first, and his 1990 donation created the Yale Institute for Biospheric Studies, which supports environmental science research. His donation was announced one year before his father’s and months before similar $20 million dollar donations from his brothers Sid and Lee. Ed caught the “Yale bug” after this donation, according to Levin. “Over time, it’s led to just one thing after another. Yale became a huge priority for him.” Even at Yale, finding a repeat megadonor is rare. Most of Yale’s largest benefactors have given only one major gift to the university. Since 2000, six donors have committed more than $100 million to Yale. Only two of those have given two major gifts: Ed Bass and Charles B. Johnson, who donated $250 million toward the construction of Yale’s new residential colleges, Benjamin Franklin and Pauli Murray. And even recruiting these donors for one gift isn’t easy. Yale’s previous Vice President for Development and Alumni Affairs Terry Holcombe told the Yale Daily News in 2018 that, for each nine-figure donor, “there are four other potential donors who—despite the University’s best efforts to sell its projects and mission—never give a gift of the same magnitude.” How does Yale infect its wealthiest alumni with the “Yale Bug?” For most Yale donors, the Yale Office of Development—a nearly 300-person department headquartered at the Connecticut Financial Center—is the main point of contact for their philanthropy. Gift officers research potential donors, identifying interests and potential overlaps with Yale’s needs, before soliciting donations. Yale’s senior leadership, especially the provost and president, have a limited role with these smaller donors, who numbered more than 30,000 in Yale’s 2024-25 fiscal year. Donors “with the potential to give $100,000 or more” are often assigned individual gift officers who act as a direct point of contact to solicit donations, according to Joan O’Neill, Yale’s Vice President for Alumni Affairs and Development. David Richards ’67, who donated his $1.4 million Rudyard Kipling collection to Yale, confirmed that he has a development officer assigned to him, as did Len Baker ’64, a former trustee and major donor to Yale. In Bass’s early days giving to Yale, the Office of Development was his primary contact at the university, according to Levin. But throughout the 1990s, and especially after he joined the Yale Corporation in 2001, he began to form closer friendships with two university leaders: Levin and Alison Richard, the former director of the Yale Peabody Museum who served as university provost from 1994 until 2002. Richard declined an interview. For larger donations, Yale’s president does much more personal fundraising, according to Levin, Yale administrator Chauncey, and two major donors. In addition to the “backup work” that the Office of Development does, such as donor research and compiling donor profiles, Yale’s presidents will frequently visit donors at their homes, keeping them personally engaged in their philanthropy. Levin estimated that, during his presidency, he visited Bass twice a year at his home in Fort Worth, in addition to occasional phone calls. President-emeritus Peter Salovey, who succeeded Levin in 2013, wrote in an email that he counted Bass and his wife as “personal friends,” and said that the Basses had stayed at his home during visits to Yale. Last year, Yale president Maurie McInnis, Salovey, and Levin all attended Ed’s 79th birthday party, where his wife Sasha announced the endowed deanship in his name. Richard Levin, Maurie McInnis, Ed Bass, and Peter Salovey, photographed at Bass’s 79th birthday party. Photograph by Tony Fiorini. As Bass continued donating around Yale—including smaller renovations at the Peabody in the 1990s, an estimated $25 million donation to the Class of 1954 Environmental Science Building in 2001, and $60 million toward Science Hill in 2006—Yale returned the favor, appointing him to councils and committees around the university. David Richards, Bass’s classmate and a major Yale donor, explained that it was common for Yale to name big donors to councils or committees related to their philanthropy. “If you're giving at that level, the big, wet kiss back is, ‘All right, you help us make some of the decisions.’” After donating his Kipling collection, Richards said that Yale made him president of the Yale Library Associates, a donor group for the university’s libraries. However, Ed’s service on university committees has gone far beyond most donors, serving on more committees or councils than any of his peers giving nine-figure donations. During his time at Yale, Bass has chaired committees at the School of Forestry & Environmental Studies, the Yale Institute for Biospheric Studies, the Peabody Museum, the Yale Tomorrow capital campaign, the Yale Corporation’s committees on Development and Buildings & Grounds, as well as serving on the School of Architecture’s Dean’s Council and the University Council, an advisory board to the president. He was named as one of the honorary co-chairs of Yale’s current capital campaign, “For Humanity,” in 2021. Describing his service to the university in his 50th reunion class book, published in 2017, Bass wrote that his “involvement progressed to immersion” since the 1990s. “I have served on a score of committees and councils, chairing several and founding a couple...and still serve today on seven committees/councils. I’d say I simply got very lucky.” Len Baker ’64, Charles B. Johnson ’54, and Ed Bass ’68 participate in the 2017 ribbon-cutting ceremony for Yale’s new residential colleges. Photo by Tony Fiorini. By late September of 2009, Yale was in serious trouble. Caught in the crosshairs of the 2008 financial crisis, Yale’s endowment had lost nearly 25% of its value in just one year, the first time it had posted a negative return in more than two decades. Just a year earlier, the university had announced its plans for two new residential colleges, and had earmarked endowment funds to begin the project, according to Len Baker ’64, the trustee who chaired the Corporation’s finance committee at the time. But when Yale’s endowment lost $5.6 billion in one year, those earmarked funds needed to support university operations, leaving the new colleges in limbo. Realizing this, Bass and another trustee personally donated a total of $20 million “to keep the architectural plan development...alive,” according to Baker. Three-quarters of that $20 million was Bass’s. “He kept that alive, and but for that, I think that the thread would have been lost and we would not have new residential colleges,” Baker said. The episode captures Ed Bass’s tendencies as a donor, characterized by those who worked with him as fueled not by his individual interests, but by his enthusiasm for Yale itself. Eight people who’ve worked with Bass told me that rather than throwing his weight around, Bass’s style is to quietly support the institutions he works with. Bass is known to be generally reserved—Levin called him “not a social animal”—and he rarely puts his name on philanthropic projects. In 2017, when he donated $10 million to the Yale Science Building, he requested that its new, 500-seat lecture hall be named after O.C. Marsh, a paleontologist whose fossil collections are now much of the Peabody Museum’s collection. In the case of Kroon Hall, Levin explained that although Richard Kroon ’64 and Bass donated around the same amount, “Rick Kroon got his name on, because Ed didn’t want his name.” The same thing happened with the Class of 1954 Environmental Science Center, where Ed Bass and members of the Yale class of 1954 donated gifts of “about the same size,” Levin said. At the School of Architecture, where Ed Bass has funded a host of programs, including free summer seminars around the world, two professors guessed that most students would have no idea who Bass is. The professors, Bimal Mentis and Joyce Hsiang, had taught the school’s five week summer workshop in Rome, which Bass named after former dean Robert A.M. Stern. Bass’s largest single gift was $160 million toward the renovation of the Yale Peabody Museum, which was the “lion’s share” of the project’s cost, according to David Skelly, the director of the museum. Skelly said that “if you see [Bass’s] name on anything, he almost certainly had to be talked into it.” He cited Bass Court, a courtyard between the Peabody and Kline Geology Laboratory as an example. “He definitely had to be talked into that.” Bass seems to focus on institutional priorities. Levin recalled how he teased Bass about his wish list of projects at Yale. “All of them were, you know, institutional. None of them were like, ‘Go do this thing for me because it’s something I want,’” Levin said. Baker agreed, saying that Bass “did not assert strong views about the university’s educational priorities, about its financial priorities, [or] about its political or ideological views. He just was ready to, ‘tell me what you want and I’ll try to make it happen.’” Yet, when Bass does express an opinion, university leaders listen. On questions of architecture, Yale administrators told me they relied on his judgement more than many donors. At the School of Architecture, Berke said that when Bass offers his opinion, “you listen carefully because it is knowledgeable and insightful.” Skelly, the Yale Peabody Museum director, echoed this, telling me that he’s learned that “generally, it's not a good idea to ignore [Bass’s] thinking. Because he's usually right.” Skelly recalled one meeting for the construction of the Yale Science Building, where plans for a new outdoor stairway were being discussed. Bass suggested reorienting the stairway away toward the intersection of Peabody Museum, Kline Geology Lab, and the Class of '54 Environmental Science Center. Skelly said that “the VP of Facilities saw that, and he goes, ‘My God, you’re absolutely right.’” During the Yale Corporation’s discussions on Yale’s new residential colleges, Benjamin Franklin and Pauli Murray, Bass disagreed with former trustee and architect Maya Lin on the colleges’ design, according to Baker, a fellow trustee. Bass and Lin both served on the Buildings & Grounds committee, and Baker said that Lin “didn’t like the residential colleges all that much because she would have had a very different, more modern design.” The committee ultimately chose a Gothic style for the new colleges, designed by Robert A.M. Stern, whom Bass had named the architecture school’s summer workshop after. Lin could not be reached for comment. In a 2018 interview with the New York Times , Bass said “I see institutions as having the power to transmit and perpetuate a set of fundamental values, and to do so generation to generation.” He told the Times that Yale’s long history also encourages him: “It’s been more than 300 years, so I have some faith.” Although his fundamental values may be unclear, many of Bass’s donations to Yale have funded environmental projects, including at the Peabody, the Center for Biospheric Studies, and on Science Hill. But Levin said he wasn’t an activist. “He’s less of an environmentalist than you might think. He’s just much more interested in the science.” Outside of Yale, Bass has also donated $30 million to the University of Arizona, which manages Bass’s Biosphere 2, a massive terrarium made famous for eclectic experiments with members of Synergia Ranch in the 1990s. Biosphere 2 is located 30 miles from the university’s campus. The current director of Biosphere 2, Joaquin Ruiz, told me that Bass invests in universities like the University of Arizona and Yale because “he really believes in their ability to operate and drive forward progress in the things he cares about.” Bass’s particular architectural vision is hard to pin down. He told the New York Times in 1991 that two of his major influences were the Synergia Ranchers and Buckminster Fuller, the intellectual forefather of the ranch and a prominent architect of geodesic domes, but he later fell out with many members of Synergia Ranch over disagreements with Biosphere 2. Architecture dean Berke said she couldn’t align him with a particular architectural movement. “Ed cares passionately about good design...Good design is about...the architect having a vision of how the building hits the ground, how it hits the sky, how it connects to its neighbors and its context, how it talks about history, even as it looks forward,” Berke said. When asked about his favorite building in a 2007 interview for the Yale Alumni Magazine, Bass also couldn’t answer. “There’s so many buildings that I appreciate and admire that maybe I don’t like that much. And others that I love but I see all their faults. I couldn't anymore do that than say who is my favorite family member,” he said. When asked in October if Bass had any big donations left, both Levin and School of Architecture dean Berke declined to offer any specifics, but hinted that Bass was not done giving. This April, the News confirmed his next big gift, which has not been previously reported and Yale has not yet publicly announced. This May, the Yale School of Architecture will tell its alumni about the Bass Challenge, a matching gift to the school’s financial aid budget which will allow its students to graduate debt-free, according to Berke. If the school manages to raise $20 million from its alumni for this challenge, Ed Bass and his wife Sasha will donate the remaining $40 million needed to eliminate loans from students’ financial aid packages. Berke said the Bass gift will be the largest that the school has ever received.

title: Under the Lilies
author: Cynthia Medina
text: This piece won 1st place in the fiction category of the 2026 Wallace Prize. The first year, my daughter tried to pick the lilies. She was four and furious with her father for not letting her take anything home from me. She stood on the damp edge of my grave in white shoes already going gray at the toes, tugging at his hand with both of hers as if force alone might loosen him from the silence he had brought there. The morning was bright in that merciless way spring can be, all soft air and wet earth and the smell of things returning. I had been dead eleven months. The lilies were fresh, thick-throated, and opening anyway. “Why can’t we take them?” she asked. Her father looked down at her, then back at the flowers, as if one of us might give him the answer. He had shaved too quickly that morning. There was still a pale line of cream near his jaw. I noticed things like that now. The dead do not leave all at once. First the body goes, then the voice, then the small untidy evidence of living. What remains is stranger: a man with my name still folded inside him like a note he cannot throw away, a little girl who does not remember my face whole and misses me all the same. “They’re for Mom,” he said. She frowned. “But she’s not using them.” He almost smiled. I watched it arrive and fail. A breeze moved through the cemetery and the lilies bent together. I had hated lilies when I was alive. They smelled like flowers for rooms where no one got better. Now he brought lilies because people had brought lilies on the day they lowered me into the ground, and grief is lazy with its symbols. My daughter crouched by the stone and pressed one finger into the mud. One button on her cardigan sat in the wrong hole, and the ribbon at the end of her braid had come half-loose on the walk from the car. He had learned to braid after I died. Badly at first, with the concentration of a man trying to build a bridge out of string. “Can Mom see these?” she asked, tapping the stem of the nearest lily. Her father was quiet. Children notice silence faster than adults do. She lifted her face to him, impatient already, her brow pinched in a way so familiar it seemed for a moment that I had been given back something bodily and impossible. “Daddy?” “Yes,” he said finally, but the word came out rough. “Yes, baby. I think she can.” She leaned closer to the stone. For a long moment she said nothing. Then, very softly, as if she was not sure what volume the dead required, she whispered, “Hi, Mom.” He no longer sang in the kitchen, not even by accident. But he had kept the rituals. He still cut the crusts from her sandwiches because I used to. He still warmed her towel in the dryer after baths. He still kissed the top of her head every time she passed him in the hallway, as though he were blessing something I had left behind. My daughter touched the carved letters of my name with one damp finger. “Was she pretty?” Something inside him seemed to stop. “Yes,” he said. “Very.” “What did she look like?” He knelt in the wet grass so they were eye level. “She had your nose,” he said. This was false, but grief makes revisionists of us. He was trying to give her something. “And your hair,” he added. That was truer. “She laughed loud,” he said. “Louder than anybody.” My daughter smiled. “Like me?” His face changed then. “Yes,” he said. “Like you.” Then she asked, “Do you think she would know me?” The world did not stop, but it ought to have. The breeze went on moving. The lilies went on nodding. Her father lowered his eyes. He pressed his thumb once against the inside of her wrist, where the pulse beats thin and fast. “You are the part of her that stayed,” he said. She did not fully understand, but children know when something matters by the way adults go still around it. She stepped forward and put both arms around his neck, nearly knocking him sideways in the mud. He laughed then, but only once, and held her with such force that for a moment I felt the old animal panic of love, that to care for anyone is to stand forever at the edge of losing them. I could not touch them. This remained the indignity of death. To see and not be seen was one thing. To love and not be able to lay a hand on what one loved was another. Before they left, she picked up a fallen petal and held it in her palm. The knowledge came then, thin and sharp: I had already begun to vanish from the child I loved most, and she would survive me by doing exactly that. The petal stayed in her hand all the way home. Before I died, before lilies and wet earth and my name cut into stone, there was a Tuesday in June when I burned the grilled cheese. Nothing about the day announced itself as worth keeping. Its importance did not arrive in costume. It came in half-sliced fruit, damp dish towels, the sound of someone you love opening the front door and calling your name from the other room. Our daughter was still small enough then to believe every kitchen should belong to her. She sat on the counter in nothing but a diaper and one yellow sock, drumming her heels against the cabinet while I moved between stove and sink, pushing a pan too hot with the side of my hip because both my hands were busy. There were strawberries in a colander. There was a cartoon voice talking nonsense from the next room. There was a glass of water on the windowsill I had forgotten to finish, collecting a crescent of afternoon light. “One day,” he said from the doorway, “I’m going to count them.” “You’ll lose track before the hallway,” I said. He leaned there watching us as if he still could not believe the life was his. Our daughter saw him and shrieked, then reached both arms out with such confidence it did not occur to her he might fail to catch her. He crossed the kitchen in three strides and took her off the counter with a grunt, pressing his mouth to her neck until she kicked and squealed and slapped his cheek with her sticky hand. “You smell like outside,” I said. “You smell like smoke,” he answered. I turned too late. The sandwich had gone dark on one side, black at the corners. “Oh, damn it.” He laughed. “That’s the third one this month.” “It is not.” “It is, and I have witnesses.” Our daughter, traitor that she was, clapped. “Give it here,” he said, reaching for the spatula. “No. You always press too hard.” “It’s a grilled cheese, not open-heart surgery.” “That is exactly why you shouldn’t manhandle it.” He smiled at that, the slow one that began on only one side of his mouth. I loved him most, maybe, in moments like that, not when he was trying, but when affection escaped him by accident. Our daughter had found the wooden spoon and was banging it against his shoulder with all the solemn violence of the very young. He caught it from her and handed her a strawberry instead. She bit into it, then frowned down at the red juice on her wrist. “Sticky,” she said. “That’s the price,” I told her. “For what?” “For wanting something sweet.” The radio by the fridge was murmuring some song I no longer remember, only that it was soft and that my husband knew the words badly and sang anyway. He said, “I have a question.” “That’s never a good opening.” “Would you still love me if I got a boat?” “No.” He looked wounded. “That was immediate.” “A boat is how a man announces he means to become impossible.” “Plenty of reasonable men have boats.” “No,” I said, flipping the sandwich, “they have boating personalities, which is different.” Our daughter laughed because we did, though she understood none of it. He kissed the top of her head. “What if it was a small boat?” “That’s worse.” This time he laughed hard enough to bend forward. Our daughter joined him, delighted, and for a moment the kitchen was full of us. “Mommy,” our daughter said, holding up her sticky fingers. “Wet.” I took the dish towel from my shoulder and wiped her hand clean, each finger one by one. She leaned into me without thinking, warm and trusting and heavy with lunch and afternoon. Later that afternoon she fell asleep on the couch with one sock still on and the other lost somewhere beneath her. I sat beside her and smoothed the damp hair from her forehead. My husband stood in the doorway holding the small abandoned sock between two fingers like evidence from a crime. “Do you think she’ll always be this sweet?” he asked. “No,” I said. “I think she’ll become a person.” “That sounds ominous.” “It is. For us.” He came to stand behind me, resting one hand on the back of my neck. That touch, casual and exact, belonged only to us. He looked down at our daughter sleeping, then at me. “What?” I asked. “Nothing.” “That’s never true.” He smiled. “I was just thinking how lucky I am.” I reached back and took his wrist, kissed the inside of it without looking. “You should be,” I said. He laughed softly through his nose, and for a moment no one moved. Our daughter breathed between us in the long, even rhythm of sleep. If I had known how little of her I would get, I would have lain down beside her. I would have breathed with her until she woke. I would have memorized the exact weight of her against the couch cushion, the soft crease near her mouth, the smell of jam and milk and sun-warmed skin at the back of her neck. I would have turned and looked at my husband until he was embarrassed by it. Instead I rose to find the other sock. I did not understand, while I was alive, how quickly a life could become administrative. There are grand words people use afterward, the ones that stand upright in the mouths of doctors and relatives and priests. Complication. Emergency. Loss. Tragedy. They are not false. They are simply too clean for what happens inside a room when a body begins to fail. The truth is smaller. A nurse moving fast without running. A cup of water left sweating on a tray beside a bed no one is looking at. A husband nodding as if he understands. A pair of socks folded in the hospital bag because someone believed there would still be time for socks. Our daughter was three when I died. That is old enough to have loved me. Not old enough to keep me. She had a cold that week, the harmless kind, all damp cough and hot cheeks and the unreasonable misery of toddlers. On the third morning, when her fever broke, my husband brought her to the hospital in leggings and a sweater with one cuff chewed at the wrist. She came in with my mother’s hand in hers and a stuffed rabbit by one ear, dragging the other ear over the floor. I remember very little in order. The room was too bright. My husband’s face looked unlike itself. “Mommy,” she said, with the terrible ease of someone certain she belongs to you. She climbed carefully onto the bed when he lifted her, one knee, then the other, then both hands pressing into the sheet by my hip to steady herself. “Hi, bug,” I said. My voice sounded wrong to me, as if it were coming from the bottom of a well. She looked at the tape on the back of my hand, the clear line running from it to the bag on the pole. “What’s that?” “Hospital spaghetti,” I said. My husband laughed once, unwillingly. She accepted this answer with the dignity of the barely persuaded. Then she leaned down and put her cheek against my chest. For one second, one merciful, unbearable second, the room righted itself around that weight. Our daughter lifted her head and looked at me closely. “You look weird,” she said. I smiled. “That’s rude.” “You do.” “She’s right,” my husband said softly. I turned my eyes toward him. “Don’t start.” He looked at the floor, smiling despite himself. I think now that this was our last ordinary exchange. “Can you come home now?” she asked. I looked at my husband first. “Not yet,” I said. “When?” “Soon.” It was a lie. There are moments when hope and dishonesty become impossible to separate. She accepted it because she was three. She put the stuffed rabbit on my stomach and patted it there as if leaving me company. My husband came to the bedside then and brushed her hair back from her face. His hand shook once before going still. “You should let Mommy rest,” he said. “No.” “Just for a little.” “No.” He looked at me. Even now, dead as I am, I do not know whether the greater anguish was leaving them or watching him begin to understand that I was. My daughter laid her hand over mine, very carefully, because she had already learned from the room that something about me was breakable. “Mommy?” she said. “Yes?” “When I’m big, will I be big like you?” The question entered me with such force I thought for a moment I had been given back a whole living body just so it could split again. “Yes,” I said. Then the room changed. An urgency in other people’s bodies. A nurse saying my name in a voice I immediately hated. My mother taking our daughter off the bed so quickly she began to cry. “No,” she said, twisting in my mother’s arms. “No, no, no.” “It’s okay,” someone said. Nothing makes me angrier than that phrase. My husband had moved to my side. He was saying something to me, but by then the room had become a place I was already leaving, and his words reached me only in pieces. My name. Stay with me. Look at me. The sort of sentences spoken by people who still think love is leverage. I tried. Then I was above it. Not in the theatrical sense. No bright tunnel, no revelation. Just an angle newly wrong. The room below me, my own body in it, too still now to be mistaken for sleeping. My mother bent almost double around our daughter, who was screaming with all the righteous betrayal of the very young. My husband with one hand braced on the bed rail, the other still reaching toward me as if late were only another form of distance. The dead do not become wise. We become helpless first. Helplessness, I learned, is only rage with nowhere to go. I saw him understand. Then he made a sound I have thought of since as grief’s first true language. Afterward came the administration of loss. Papers. A signature. A woman with a careful voice asking questions no one should have to answer under fluorescent lighting. My husband carrying my bag though there was no reason to carry it anymore. Inside were the folded socks, my toothbrush, a packet of gum, the blue cardigan I had meant to wear home. My daughter asked for me that night just once. Later he stood in the doorway of her room while she kicked free of the blanket and asked, already half asleep, “Where’s Mommy?” He opened his mouth. Nothing came out. She waited, eyelids heavy. “In the hospital?” she guessed. There are losses so complete the body makes a smaller story just to live through them. “Yes,” he said. She nodded. Accepted it. Rolled onto her side with the rabbit against her throat. Then he went into our bathroom and learned, all at once, the full stupidity of objects. My toothbrush by the sink. My damp towel still hooked behind the door. A strand of my hair caught in the teeth of the comb. He sat on the closed toilet lid with my hairbrush in his lap and cried into one of my sweaters until he could not breathe through his nose. The next morning our daughter said, with the serene cruelty of children, “My braid is wrong.” It wasn’t even a braid yet, only two uneven parts and one side clipped back badly. He had tried before daycare and failed. He came around the table, knelt behind her chair, and began again. His fingers moved clumsily through her hair. Too tight, then too loose. She winced. “You’re pulling.” “I know.” “You’re doing it wrong.” “I know.” His voice was gentle, but beneath it I heard the new thing grief had made in him, a rawness always one inch below the skin. Then she lifted one hand and laid it on his wrist. “Okay,” she said. He stopped. Then he finished the braid, badly. One side lower than the other. The part wandering. When he was done, he kissed the top of her head. She let him. That is how the house continued without me. Not nobly. Not beautifully. In cereal and loose clips and lies told at bedtime because the truth was too large to hand a child all at once. In the humiliating little expertise survival demands. But even then, in those first raw days, something of me had already gone forward. In my daughter’s damp head bent over cereal. In my husband’s ruined braid. In the hand she laid on his wrist as if she understood, dimly, that one of them would have to learn how to stay. By the seventh spring, my daughter no longer needed help getting out of the car. She was ten then, all elbows and sharp opinions and the first early signs of privacy, that new and terrible country children enter when they begin to understand that part of their life belongs to them alone. She opened the back door before her father could come around to her side, shouldered her own bag, and stood for a moment in the parking lot squinting into the light with one hand lifted to her brow. But the dead become students of recurrence. That was mine, the squint. Just the motion, brief and unconscious, as if sunlight were an inconvenience she would not grant the dignity of complaint. Her father saw it too. I could tell by the way he stopped with the keys still in his hand. She looked back at him. “What?” “Nothing.” “That means something.” He smiled a little. “You did that like her.” She frowned, then repeated it deliberately, more exaggerated this time, and laughed when he laughed. “Well,” she said, “I guess she had one good idea.” I loved her then with such violence that, if love were a force capable of rearranging matter, the whole cemetery would have split open. The lilies had come again. This year the lilies were taller, their scent thick and overripe. Flowers for rooms where no one gets better. He had told her that story, I knew, because as they walked up the path she said, “I still think that’s dramatic.” “She was dramatic.” “No,” my daughter said. “You’re dramatic when you talk about her. She sounds funny.” He glanced down at her. “She was funny.” “Not the way you say it.” She stepped around a patch of mud, careful without appearing to be. “You always talk about her like she was made of candlelight. But every time you tell a real story, she sounds kind of mean.” He barked out a laugh before he could help it. “I was not mean,” I said, offended in principle. “You know,” she continued, warming to the subject, “the parking thing, and the flowers, and how she said your boat idea was stupid.” His face opened then into something I had not seen on him in years, not only amusement, but relief. “She didn’t say stupid,” he said. “She implied it.” “She did imply it.” At the grave, she did not sit immediately the way she had in earlier years. She stood reading the stone, though by then she knew every word of it. Her father spread the blanket before she could comment. It was the blue one. Faded now at the fold lines. One corner torn and restitched by hand. He had remembered. Then she reached into her bag and took out a book. When she was little, she had wanted toys at the grave, snacks, a ribbon. Now she brought a book. Thick, secondhand, the spine already cracked white in two places. She set it in her lap without opening it. Her father noticed. “What’s that?” She shrugged. “Just something I’m reading.” “Let me see.” “No.” After a minute she sighed and handed it over. He looked at the cover. Then at her. “You brought your mother Anne of Green Gables?” She gave him a look of immediate annoyance. “It’s not your mother Anne of Green Gables. It’s a library book.” “You hated that book when I read it to you.” “I was six.” “And now?” She looked away, out toward the path. “Now I think she would’ve liked Anne.” The sentence entered the world so lightly neither of them seemed, at first, to understand what it had done to me. “What makes you think that?” he asked. She shrugged again, but her voice had gone quieter. “She sounds like someone Mom would’ve liked.” He handed the book back carefully. “She would have,” he said. “I know,” my daughter said. She opened the book then, turning to a page already marked with a receipt, and rested her palm over the paper for a moment without reading. One thumbnail was bitten raw at the corner, exactly as mine used to be. I had not expected inheritance to be so small, or so exact. Just a bitten thumbnail. A dry joke. A girl who picked the books I would have picked without knowing why. After a while she said, still looking at the page, “Sometimes I do something and it feels like I’m remembering for no reason.” Her father did not answer immediately. “What do you mean?” She turned a page she had not read. “Like when I got mad at that florist because the flowers smelled too sweet. Or when Mrs. Bell said I laugh with my whole shoulders. Or when I know what kind of story I’ll like before I read it.” She stopped. “It feels like missing someone and finding them at the same time.” Her father looked out over the cemetery instead of at her. “That sounds right,” he said. She closed the book in her lap. “Sometimes I try to think of her face,” she said. “And I only get the picture by the stairs.” He was quiet. “If I forget her face,” she went on, “is that how she disappears?” He bent his head. “No.” She waited. “No,” he said again, more steadily. “That only means you were too young to keep what should have been yours.” Something in me broke open at that. She looked at him then, her face small and fierce. “Why do I miss her if I don’t remember her?” He closed his eyes. “Because love gets to us before memory does,” he said. She stared at him. Then, with the ruthless clarity children sometimes have, she asked, “Do you miss her all the time?” He smiled in a way that was not a smile. “No,” he said. “Not all the time.” She waited. “But every day.” She placed the book beside her on the blanket and drew one knee up, wrapping both arms around it. “I used to be scared I’d forget everything,” she said. He waited. “And now?” She shrugged. “Now I think maybe remembering everything isn’t the point.” He turned to her then. “What is?” She took longer to answer this than any question before. “Maybe,” she said, “it’s that some of it stays anyway.” He looked at her with the stunned, almost frightened expression of a person who has just watched a child step over some invisible threshold. Then he did something he had not done there in years. He cried. Not violently. Not all at once. Just a sudden ungoverned breaking at the face. He covered his mouth with one hand and turned away, embarrassed even alone with her. She stared. Then, in one movement so familiar I felt it like weather through bone, she shifted closer and put her hand on the back of his neck. Casual. Exact. My touch. He bent forward, laughing once through the tears because the alternative was worse, and she left her hand there, steady and unafraid. “It’s okay,” she said. Nothing, in all the years since my death, had undone me more than those words. He drew a long breath and nodded. “I know,” he said, though of course he didn’t. A breeze moved through the cemetery, and one of the lilies, too open now, gave way. A white petal loosened, then another, then another, falling soundlessly into the grass. When my daughter noticed, she stood. For one moment I thought she would leave it there. But instead she bent over the bouquet. With the grave care of someone tending a wound that cannot be closed, she lifted one broken stem and tucked it gently back among the others, where it would not hold for long but held, for the moment, well enough. Then she looked at my name. Not at the dates. Not at the inscription. Just my name, plain in stone. And she smiled, not sadly, not brightly, just with the quiet recognition one gives something once lost and now found in altered form. When they left, she took the book. I watched them walk back through the gate, not touching now, but moving with the same brief hesitation at the curb. Nothing the earth loves does it keep forever. I had wanted more than this. More years. More mornings. We had not asked for miracles. Only the usual things. To grow older in the same house. To watch our daughter become someone we might not entirely understand. To complain about our knees and forget where we parked. To sit beside each other in the long, unremarkable light of late afternoons. Maybe grandchildren. Maybe separate chairs and the same silence. The kind of future people call ordinary because they have not yet had it taken. Instead I remained this way, in gestures and weather and the borrowed architecture of those who had loved me. Not living. Not gone. Only carried.

title: A Phantom Lineage
author: Yevgeniya Regent
text: This piece won 1st place in the nonfiction category of the 2026 Wallace Prize. We say in Ukraine that children usually grow up with one of two problems: not having a father, or having one. After an amputation, the brain often refuses to acknowledge the loss, creating the sensation that the hand is still present—a condition known as Phantom Limb Syndrome. Though the limb is gone, the brain’s sensory and motor maps remain active, generating sensations of movement, pressure, or pain in the missing part. Some patients describe a phantom fist they can’t unclench, nails digging into a palm that no longer exists, with pain as vivid as if the hand were still there. The brain retains this cortical map for years, sometimes decades, reaching for what’s gone, trying to soothe a part of the self that can no longer respond. *** Dear Dad, there’s something sickening about how we are now. Sometimes I close my eyes and step into your body, walking through my childhood apartment—your home—alone. A place built for four now holding one. Twenty years spent creating a life with two daughters and a wife—then everyone scattered in different directions. My sister hasn’t seen you since she was eight. Her memory of you is evaporating—soon she won’t remember what it was like to have a father at all. Sometimes I wonder if I study the human mind just to understand how forgetting works, or why something still bleeds when it comes to you. You haven’t changed anything in the apartment. My bed is still covered with the blue blanket you spread after I left. You escape the house only at five in the morning or after dark, slipping through the streets so no one can hand you a draft notice. I picture you there, pacing between the same walls, the same chairs, the same silence. And sometimes I cry about you. I don’t cry about other things anymore. Last time we talked, I was in the Czech Republic. With friends you’ve never met, I walked through Lidice—a village erased from the map by the Nazis. So I wouldn’t cry, I told you about the cameras I’d been using, the film work I was doing. You gave me my first camera. You taught me how to see the world through a viewfinder. You always seemed so far ahead of me in your hobby. Standing there, I realized I know more than you now. *** Patronymics—names built from a father’s name—run through ancient Indo-European cultures. Early Slavic societies identified people as “X, son/daughter of Y” centuries before surnames existed. Under the Russian Empire, patronymics became mandatory in church records, censuses, and legal documents. The Soviet Union intensified this insistence: every passport, birth certificate, school file, and military form required a patronymic. When a father was unknown, officials fabricated one, from a name the mother picked out or a bureaucratic placeholder, so the structure itself remained intact. Independent Ukraine retained the tripartite formula: given name, patronymic, surname. *** Dear Dad, I flinch when someone asks if you’re safe. I never know what to say. Why can’t you call? It feels childish to think you don’t care, but I can’t convince myself you do. I’m turning twenty. You last knew me as a fifteen-year-old girl—still soft around the edges, still yours in ways I’m not anymore. I always loved your name. It was the only reason my stubborn, feminist self accepted being called Yevgeniya Vitaliivna. Your name felt like a thread tying us together. Now no one calls me that in New England. *** During the upheavals of the 1920s and 1930s, girls were the first to be sent away. After the 1921–23 famine and civil war, the Soviet state counted between 4 and 7.5 million homeless children. By 1934, after collectivization and the Holodomor, officials registered over 600,000 new juvenile vagrants and famine orphans in a single year, many with living parents. Families could not feed their daughters, so they sent them to shelters, to distant relatives, to cities where the state might keep them alive. Between 1928 and 1938, enrollment of girls in urban technikums tripled. By the late 1930s, girls made up more than 40% of vocational students—thousands lifted from villages and deposited in cities with suitcases, state papers, and no way home. *** My great-grandmother, Anna Yefimovna, was born in 1921, in a village where the house always felt too small for the number of children inside it. She grew up working the fields as collectivization rearranged the country around her. Then came the Holodomor, Stalin’s engineered famine, and her father, Yefim, sold their house for survival. He took a railway job for rations, a line between hunger and death, disappearing for days at a time. At fourteen, Anna was sent to Kharkiv to train as a nurse, an eighteen-hour journey away from everything familiar. She ran away again and again. Each time she returned, her family fed her, let her rest, and in the morning, Yefim woke her gently, walked her to the station, and put her back on the train. She survived the war as a front-line surgical nurse. He fought as a soldier. When the war was finally over, she moved to Kyiv and left her entire family behind. My grandmother, Svetlana Leonidovna, was born in 1945, the daughter of a Red Army general named Leonid. Peace had barely arrived when a wealthy woman in Belarus decided she wanted Leonid for herself. She threatened to have my great-grandmother killed. Not long after, he was reassigned, conveniently, to Belarus. My grandmother met him only once as an adult. The woman greeted her with a strange remorse: God punished me for taking away your father, she whispered. Her own child had severe autism. My mother, Yuliya Vladislavovna, arrived in 1977. She grew up without the softness childhood should have had. She never met her own father—Vladislav, a mathematician, a scientist. She believes her intelligence and analytic mind come from him. When she turned eighteen, they talked on the phone once. She said it felt like talking to a stranger whose voice held no place for her. Half your genes do not make a father. *** Studies in developmental psychobiology suggest attachment isn’t only learned through behavior; it’s also carried through physiology. A parent’s stress biology shapes the intrauterine environment long before birth, altering fetal HPA-axis calibration and producing infants with elevated baseline cortisol and faster sympathetic activation. These physiological settings become the scaffolding for later attachment: bodies tuned for danger often form anxious bonds, while bodies accustomed to passionate scarcity lean toward avoidance. Epigenetic research adds another layer. Chronic stress in one generation modifies DNA methylation in genes regulating stress response and social bonding, and these marks can persist in offspring. A child may therefore inherit not just a parent’s temperament but their vigilance, their difficulty soothing, even their reflex to withdraw. *** Ukrainian fatherhood was shaped by geopolitics long before it was ever allowed to be a choice. From Kyivan Rus onward, this land was crossed, claimed, emptied, and refilled by successive powers: Kyivan Rus campaigns, Mongol invasions, centuries of Polish–Lithuanian rule, Ottoman raids, serfdom, imperial rule under the Habsburgs and Romanovs, Tsarist conscription, two World Wars, Stalinist collectivization, genocide and famine, camps and mass deportations, Nazi forced labor (Ostarbeiter), economic collapse, now full-scale russian invasion. Generation after generation, men left, while women stayed to ensure survival, gradually solidifying as the durable center of the household. Men, by contrast, learned to be temporary. In a structure built to endure absence, attachment is dangerous. Outside the home, masculinity comes with instructions: work, suffer, sacrifice, endure. Inside, there is no inherited language for intimacy or care. Alcohol steps in as a solvent: a cheap amnesia that dulls the shame of powerlessness in a society that insists men must hold power somewhere. What looks like personal failure—a father who withdraws, drinks, vanishes—is often the residue of historical training that taught Ukrainian families to function without a man. ​​*** My mom once told me that growing up, she never wished for a good husband; she wished for a good father for her children. My father wanted to be that. I’d grown up hearing that fathers either vanish or rot in place. I believed he was an exception. He played football with me in the yard, sledded through winters that seemed to last longer than the calendar allowed. He waited outside my dance lessons in the car, letting me play games on his Nokia phone when I cried. We snorkeled for crabs in Crimea. We played chess, and somehow I always won. One day, I begged him to buy a baguette—an unprecedented request in our health-obsessed family. When we got home and realized we’d left it behind at the store, I broke down—full, shameless tears. So he sliced ordinary bread, fried it with garlic and salt, set it in front of me with a small flourish, and declared it Father’s baguette. He never told me what I could or couldn’t be, never forced me to act like a girl, never belittled or shamed me. When I grew too loud, too restless, he pressed his precious camera into my hands. Go photograph something . He helped me build my sails and stood behind me, blowing to create wind. My sister was born, and he did everything: diapers, long stroller walks, his fat-free borscht, and entire afternoons crawling with her around our home. In turn, school consumed me. I aligned myself with my mother’s academic gravity and let my father drift to the edges of the apartment. I practiced independence, telling him I didn’t need help: I could walk to school alone; I could take care of my sister; I could manage without him. He began disappearing on long trips to the grocery store. When he came back, he complained that my mother was too commanding; that she had pushed his friends out of his life; that she had forced him to resign from the army; that she was turning me against him; that we didn’t appreciate him; that my sister was growing up watching a weak male figure. Then came the flask in his pocket. The cognac bottles that migrated across the counter. The arguments. The plates breaking. When my mother and I agreed I should apply to U.S. schools, he didn’t protest; he retreated to his corner of the apartment and disappeared. I was relieved. With him gone, the air felt calmer, the fighting thinner, and the days around them felt easier to survive. After I was accepted, I told them I wouldn’t leave unless they tried to patch their marriage together—at least for my sister. Over winter break, I returned home. Everything felt slightly misaligned, as if the apartment had shifted without me. Now, I spoke a language they didn’t understand, in a country they had never been to; I had already begun living in a world they could not even picture. He took me to the airport at 1 a.m. We played Folklore —the CD I’d forced him to burn. He drove slowly so I could sleep. At the terminal, he hugged me too tightly, then released me. I watched him dissolve into the crowd. A month later, russia launched the full-scale invasion. I haven’t seen him since. These days, my mother and I sit in a kitchen in Germany and talk too much. Martial law keeps my father from leaving Ukraine; he turns off his phone and disappears for weeks at a time, ignoring us. She yells at him—her husband now mostly in name—across weak connections. Text me every morning, so I know you’re alive . She asks if he’s been bombed, if he has electricity. He periodically replies with one word: norm . Between classes and my constant rush of college life, my mom texts me, How are you? My thumbs type automatically: norm. *** Roughly 20.05 million Soviet males were killed in World War II; around 8-11 million were soldiers, almost all of child-raising age. This left 7 to 9 million children fatherless. Post-war instability created a second category: fathers who were alive but absent. In 1945, 24.4% of Soviet births were extramarital.
